File.Name: ia19b_ia18-003.jpg
Notes: 44713342244 I believe this is a Long-bodied Cellar Spider (Pholcus phalangioides) in the family Pholcidae in the bathroom of our house. I usually find these little long-legged spiders in the corners of our windows, or walking across the ceiling, or in the sink, or in my empty coffee cup in the morning just before I pour . . . pretty much everywhere inside! Their legs look like they're made of capillary glass tubing. Apparently they are introduced from Central Europe - but they sure have made themselves at home here. I got lucky with the dim light on this one - my 1/5 second long exposure worked. Arachtober 19b. (San Marcos Pass, 18 October 2018)
ScientificName: Pholcus phalangioides; Pholcidae
associatedTaxa: Pholcus phalangioides; Pholcidae

I believe it's in the family Lauxaniidae, possible species Minettia flaveola - but another possibility is the genus Suilla in the family Heleomyzidae. I get confused by these flies every year. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 18 October 2018)

File.Name: ia19d_ia16-049.jpg
Notes: 45437566301 I believe this is another Stiletto Fly in the family Therevidae, Diptera in a different Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) tree than the similar fly in this photo taken on the same day. This fly is much larger than the other, and I suspect it's a gravid female - full of eggs. It's probably genus Thereva, see this BugGuide info page. I've seen these large and handsome flies before, see this photo from last year, also in October. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 16 October 2018)

File.Name: ia20a_ia20-025.jpg
Notes:
44546082395 The mama Green Lynx Spider (Peucetia, Oxyopidae) I've been showing had a blessed event - actually many of them! These new spiderlings are emerging from her egg sack, ready to explore a brave new world. The mama spiders are truly devoted mothers who guard their egg sacks until the spiderlings disperse, though this one is hiding on the shady side of the egg sac where I can't bother it, along with most of her babies. I have other photos of the same mama spider here and here. I've been concerned for the babies, and I'm glad that they're OK. This is also good timing for the Arachtober group. Arachtober 20a. (San Marcos Pass, 20 October 2018)

Today was another warm day, into the 80s. It was even breezier than yesterday, mostly from the south. There were still a few moments of relative calm to get a few photos. One bit of good news is that all NWS watches and warnings have expired, and it sounds like it will be cooler tomorrow.
ScientificName: Peucetia
associatedTaxa: NA

File.Name: ia20b_ia20-038.jpg
Notes: 31585047498 I'm pretty sure this is the cast-off exuviae - the shed skin after molting - of a Western Black Widow (Latrodectus hesperus, Theridiidae) spider - at least I saw a black female spider on the same cobweb-like web. The spider disappeared when I got close for a photo, but I found these remains of its last molt. I can't explain why it looks brown rather than shiny black like the female spider in this photo. I don't see the spiders very often, but I know they're out there. Arachtober 20b. (San Marcos Pass, 20 October 2018)
ScientificName: Latrodectus hesperus; Theridiidae
associatedTaxa: Latrodectus hesperus; Theridiidae

File.Name: ia20c_ia20-005.jpg
Notes: 43642823200 Here is a neat little Treehopper (family Membracidae) in the order Auchenorrhyncha, Hemiptera on a stem of native Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus vestitus) in the Fabaceae plant family. This one might be the Coast Live Oak Treehopper (Telamona vestita), see BugGuide for more info. The thorax of these insects is expanded to cover the abdomen like a shield. They have remarkable eyes, like all hoppers that have to see where they're hopping. I don't manage to photograph them very often - partly because they hop away! (San Marcos Pass, 20 October 2018)
CommonNames: Treehopper; Wild Sweetpea; Coast Live Oak Treehopper
ScientificName: Membracidae; Auchenorrhyncha; Hemiptera; Lathyrus vestitus; Fabaceae; Telamona vestita

File.Name: ia20d_ia19-002.jpg
Notes: 31584421638 This is a Chalcid Wasp (Chalcidoidea) on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). Yeah, it's not the best photo, but it a very interesting wasp! It was walking constantly, which made it hard to photograph. It was curious that I found (probably) the same wasp on the same branch the day before. I couldn't get a decent photo the first time I saw it, so I got a second chance - but it's still not the best photo. Looking at BugGuide, this wasp might be genus Conura, compare this photo which is not exactly the same but similar. Another possibility is genus Brachymeria. It's tiny - about 980 pixels long in this near 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 3.8 mm, a bit over 1/8 inch. (See here for how I figured.) It has remarkable hind legs. These wasps are parasitoids of other insects - BugGuide says that most attack Lepidoptera pupae. (San Marcos Pass, 19 October 2018)
ScientificName: Chalcidoidea; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Conura; Brachymeria
associatedTaxa: Chalcidoidea; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Conura; Brachymeria

File.Name: ia21a_ia21-014.jpg
Notes:
44758038344 Here is a small spider on its web in a shrub of Southern Bush Monkeyflower (Mimulus aurantiacus var. pubescens, Phrymaceae) in the woods today. It had a little web in the summer-dead shrub and seemed to actually be inside the web. I'm not sure what kind of spider it is, but one possibility is a non-cribellate species of Mesh Web Weaver in the family Dictynidae - at least the eye arrangement seems to match. I think I've seen these spiders before and didn't know what they are then either, see this photo. Arachtober 21a. (San Marcos Pass, 21 October 2018)

Today was much like yesterday but a little bit cooler and a little bit less breezy - both improvements for the better. It was a very nice autumn day.

File.Name: ia21b_ia21-022.jpg
Notes: 31609194208 This is an unusually pale Assassin Bug in the Reduviidae family on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). It's pale because it is teneral - just emerged from its final molt from nymph to adult. I have a separate shot of its exuviae on the right - the shed skin after the molt. Looking close, I can see that the cast-off skin is head-down. I'm sure this is another Leafhopper Assassin Bug (Zelus renardii) - I have recent photos of both adult and nymph. I didn't see it actually emerge, and I couldn't get a decent photo of both adult and husk together. The adult did stick around, as though it had an attachment to the shell of its past. (San Marcos Pass, 21 October 2018)
CommonNames: Assassin Bug; Coast Live Oak; Leafhopper Assassin Bug
ScientificName: Reduviidae; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; teneral; exuviae; Zelus renardii

File.Name: ia21c_ia21-006.jpg
Notes: 43665323940 Here is a relatively large Leafhopper (Cicadellidae, Auchenorrhyncha, Hemiptera) on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods today. It's about 2400 pixels long in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 9.4 mm, about 3/8 inch. (See here for how I figured.) Maybe it's genus Gyponana - e.g. species Gyponana procera which has the right range and is said to be 8.5-10.5 mm which fits. (San Marcos Pass, 21 October 2018)

File.Name: ia21d_ia20-028.jpg
Notes: 45430741172 I found this little Crab Spider (Thomisidae) venturing onto a petal of native California Aster (Corethrogyne filaginifolia aka Lessingia filaginifolia, Asteraceae) yesterday. I didn't notice my magnification, so I can't figure it's size by counting pixels - but the late-season flowers are small, and the spider is tiny. I believe it is genus Mecaphesa with that hairy abdomen. Hah - the spider would creep out onto the petal, notice me, and then hurry back under the petals to hide. A few minutes later it would try again. Arachtober 21b. (San Marcos Pass, 20 October 2018)
ScientificName: Mecaphesa
associatedTaxa: Corethrogyne filaginifolia; Lessingia filaginifolia

File.Name: ia22a_ia22-045.jpg
Notes:
45456687322 Here is a mama Green Lynx Spider (Peucetia, Oxyopidae) watching over her new spiderlings on their egg sac. The spiderlings are only a few days old, I know they weren't emerged before the weekend. I've been watching this spider since September 19, see this photo taken back when she was really green. It's been interesting to watch her slowly turn red. The mama spiders are truly devoted mothers who guard their egg sacks until the spiderlings disperse. Arachtober 22a. (San Marcos Pass, 22 October 2018)

Today was a bit cooler than yesterday and a little more breezy. It felt like a "fog wind" - my name for the turbulent winds that form at the top of the marine layer fog. Sundowner winds were in the forecast tonight, but nothing has happened yet.
ScientificName: Peucetia
associatedTaxa: NA

File.Name: ia22b_ia22-037.jpg
Notes: 45456492852 This is another tiny Crab Spider (Thomisidae) in a folded leaf of Greenbark Ceanothus (Ceanothus spinosus, Rhamnaceae). I believe it is another genus Mecaphesa spider with that hairy abdomen with a v-like pattern. It appears to be a male with those swollen palps. These spiders are typically on flowers, so I'm not sure what it's doing in this shrub that is a long ways from flowering. Hah - somehow I noticed this spider over my head in the shrub. I only got this photo by standing on my tiptoes with tripod fully extended. Arachtober 22b. (San Marcos Pass, 22 October 2018)

File.Name: ia22c_ia22-047.jpg
Notes: 45507165861 Here is another adult Tree Cricket (genus Oecanthus, Gryllidae, Orthoptera) on a plant of dead leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). This one is female with narrow wings, compare this photo of a male with broad wings taken last week. Both male and female crickets work the night shift and try to hide during the day, and only the broad-winged males sing at night, though this one seems to be unusually exposed. I'm still hearing them at night, though its probably getting close to he end of their time. I'll miss their songs. (San Marcos Pass, 22 October 2018)
ScientificName: Oecanthus; Gryllidae; Orthoptera; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae

File.Name: ia22d_ia22-010.jpg
Notes: 31634042798 This is the nymph of a true bug (Heteroptera, Hemiptera) tucked into a new seed capsule of Southern Bush Monkeyflower (Mimulus aurantiacus var. pubescens, Phrymaceae) that's just starting to open. I've seen these nymphs before later in the season, see this photo from last January. I don't know what they will look like as adults. I've also seen other critters use these little boat-like seed capsules for shelter - see this photo and this photo. Pretty neat! (San Marcos Pass, 22 October 2018)

File.Name: ia23a_ia23-043.jpg
Notes:
31656363648 This isn't the best photo, but I reckon the interesting spider makes up for it. It's a small Feather-legged Spider (Uloborus diversus) in the family Uloboridae of Cribellate Orb Weavers aka Hackled Band Orb Weavers. This spider makes a proper orb web that is distinct because it's usually horizontal, see this photo. I noticed this one's web in a tangle of blackberry and snowberry, and then found the small spider off to one side. It's about 900 pixels long in another 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 3.5 mm, about 1/8 inch. (See here for how I figured.) It was a struggle to get this photo in the shrubs, and it's the best I could do today. These spiders resemble the Triangle Web Spiders (genus Hyptiotes) in the same family that I show in this photo. They look similar at rest, but this one doesn't "break the web" like the other one - see my other photo. I found these spiders last summer, but then they seemed to disappear. This is the first one I've seen for a few months. I'll try for a better photo tomorrow. Arachtober 23a. (San Marcos Pass, 23 October 2018)

Today was another pleasant autumn day with one big difference - it was dead calm for most of the day. Whatever brought that condition to be, I like it!
CommonNames: Feather-legged Spider; Cribellate Orb Weavers; Hackled Band Orb Weavers; Triangle Web Spiders
ScientificName: Uloborus diversus; Uloboridae; Hyptiotes
associatedTaxa: Uloborus diversus; Uloboridae; Hyptiotes

File.Name: ia23b_ia23-025.jpg
Notes: 43711360690 I found this handsome Mormon Metalmark (Apodemia mormo) butterfly on the native California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum, Polygonaceae) by our mailbox today. It's probably "Behr's Metalmark" (Apodemia mormo virgulti aka Apodemia virgulti). The flowers are fading, but there are still a few fresh ones with pink anthers on their stamens. This is the first of these butterflies that I've seen near home and only the second that I've seen in the mountains this year. (The first one is here.) They are probably more common down by the river. (San Marcos Pass, 23 October 2018)
CommonNames: Mormon Metalmark; California Buckwheat; Behr's Metalmark
ScientificName: Apodemia mormo; Eriogonum fasciculatum; Polygonaceae; Apodemia mormo virgulti; Apodemia virgulti

File.Name: ia23c_ia23-015.jpg
Notes: 45478036342 Here is another tiny Syrphid Fly (Syrphidae, Diptera) visiting a fuzzy violet flower of (I think) Mexican Bush Sage (Salvia leucantha, Lamiaceae) in the garden. Look close - it's sticking its remarkably long proboscis into the throat of the flower, like an elephant's trunk! I have another recent shot of a similar fly on these flowers in this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 23 October 2018)

File.Name: ia23d_ia22-023.jpg
Notes: 44614715985 Here is another shot of mama Green Lynx Spider (Peucetia, Oxyopidae) watching over her new spiderlings on their egg sac in a plant of native Bird's Beak (Cordylanthus rigidus, Orobanchaceae). I got this photo yesterday, and I'm making a point of not pestering them everyday. The spiderlings are only a few days old, I know they weren't emerged before last weekend. I've been watching this spider since September 19, see this photo taken back when she was really green. It's been interesting to watch her slowly turn red. The mama spiders are truly devoted mothers who guard their egg sacks until the spiderlings disperse. Arachtober 23b. (San Marcos Pass, 22 October 2018)

File.Name: ia24a_ia24-001.jpg
Notes:
44818856154 Here are top and side views of a tiny Trashline Orb Weaver (Cyclosa, Araneidae) spider on its web. You can see a bit of its "trash line" above and below. These spiders decorate their perfect orb webs with the bodies of their prey to make up a line along the midline of the web, like a once-living stabilimentum, that thicker white webbing behind the spider. They always have that odd bump on their abdomen shaped like a bottle gourd. These spiders really are tiny - about 775 pixels long in these 1:1 macro shots, which comes to 3.0 mm, less than 1/8 inch. (See here for how I figured.) I found this spider in place that gets no sun at this time of year. It would be impossibly to get these photos in the low light, but it's been dead calm for the last two mornings. Arachtober 24a. (San Marcos Pass, 24 October 2018)

Today was another lovely autumn day. A breeze kicked up in the afternoon, but not too bad. It might be just the same for a few days, which suits me fine.
ScientificName: Cyclosa
associatedTaxa: NA

File.Name: ia24b_ia24-014.jpg
Notes: 30603014157 I believe this is the composite web of a Labyrinth Spider (genus Metepeira, Araneidae). These are orb-weaving spiders that make a proper orb web (left) and then build a little retreat off to the side held up by cobweb-type webbing (right). I have another shot of both webs here and a photo of the spider in its retreat here. This one is inside a leafless plant of native Deerweed (Acmispon glaber, Fabaceae). One interesting feature of these webs is that there is always a strand of silk connecting the retreat to the orb web that the spider uses to sense activity. In this shot, you can see it running from just above the center of the orb web to the top of the retreat. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 24 October 2018)
ScientificName: Metepeira
associatedTaxa: Acmispon glaber

File.Name: ia24c_ia22-002.jpg
Notes: 45493521722 Here is another Long-bodied Cellar Spider (Pholcus phalangioides, Pholcidae) in the bathroom of our house. This one is hanging from its cobwebby web with a wrapped prey - no doubt one of the large Carpenter Ants (Camponotus) that live in the walls. This is another long exposure in dim light that worked well - it shows the interesting texture of the spider's abdomen. It's also a reminder of how versatile spiders are with their webbing - they can spin different kinds of silk for different purposes. Happy Web Webnesday and Arachtober 24b. (San Marcos Pass, 23 October 2018)
ScientificName: Pholcus phalangioides; Pholcidae; Camponotus
associatedTaxa: Pholcus phalangioides; Pholcidae; Camponotus

File.Name: ia24d_ia24-019.jpg
Notes: 45493357552 This is an interesting Spreadwing Dragonfly in the family Lestidae of the order Odonata that I found on a dead plant stem by our summer-dry creek today. It looks like a damselfly, but it perches with wings open like a proper dragonfly. I believe this one is the California Spreadwing (Archilestes californica), a male with those "claspers" at the end of its abdomen. Great eyes! This is the first one I've seen this year. (San Marcos Pass, 24 October 2018)
ScientificName: Lestidae; Odonata; Archilestes californica
associatedTaxa: Lestidae; Odonata; Archilestes californica

File.Name: ia25a_ia25-017.jpg
Notes:
30618512517 Here is today's photo of the mama Green Lynx Spider (Peucetia, Oxyopidae) watching over her new spiderlings on their egg sac in a plant of native Bird's Beak (Cordylanthus rigidus, Orobanchaceae) that's gone to seed. The spiderlings are less than a week old. The mama spiders are truly devoted mothers who guard their egg sacks until the spiderlings disperse. I've already posted several photos of this mama, like this one, and I'll probably post more. The Arachtober group challenge is to post spider photos every day during October, and I like to treat it like a scavenger hunt to find new spiders every day of the month. I've been successful so far, but spiders are getting scarce as the month comes to an end. Flowers and insects are getting scarce too of course. Expect a few more of these photos until Halloween! Arachtober 25a. (San Marcos Pass, 25 October 2018)

"Breezy days are here again . . ." I've been spoiled after a couple of dead calm days, but they are the exception. There was a breeze today, but it was otherwise another lovely autumn day. It sounds like it will warm up tomorrow.
ScientificName: Peucetia
associatedTaxa: Cordylanthus rigidus

File.Name: ia25b_ia24-011.jpg
Notes: 31686715378 This is the tiny larva of a Neuropteran holding an even tinier nymph of a true bug (Heteroptera, Hemiptera) on an old flower/seed head of native Black Sage (Salvia mellifera, Lamiaceae). The larva may be Brown Lacewing (Hemerobiidae, Neuroptera), compare this photo at BugGuide. Hah - I couldn't even see the prey until I got this photo up on my computer screen, but then I could make out that it has the proboscis and four-segmented antennae of a true bug. I was surprised that the focus is so sharp given that I couldn't see the subject! These critters really are tiny - the predator is about 815 pixels long in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 3.2 mm, about 1/8 inch. (See here for how I figured.) Its prey is about half that size. (San Marcos Pass, 24 October 2018)
CommonNames: Neuropteran; true bug; Black Sage; Brown Lacewing

File.Name: ia25c_ia24-021.jpg
Notes: 43741508600 I'm pretty sure this photo shows the bristly behind of a Tachinid Fly (Tachinidae, Diptera) on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). If you look close, you can see that it has a definite subscutellum - that extra bump below the rear part of its thorax, behind the wings. I'll add a note. See this page at BugGuide which suggest this is a useful character for identifying these flies. I'll post another photo tomorrow that shows the face of this fly for Fly Day Friday, but this shot is for Beautiful Bug Butt Thursday - HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 24 October 2018)

File.Name: ia25d_ia23-006.jpg
Notes: 30617911017 Here is another tiny Trashline Orb Weaver (Cyclosa, Araneidae) spider on its perfect orb web. This one doesn't have much of a "trash line" of body parts of its prey decorating its web, though you can see that what would be the trash line is really a stabilimentum, that thicker white webbing behind the spider. They always have that odd bump on their abdomen shaped like a bottle gourd. These spiders are really tiny, less than 1/8 inch long - see my previous photo of a different spider. I found this spider in place that gets no sun at this time of year on a dead calm morning. I like the way its web stands out despite the low contrast. If you look close, you can see something remarkable about its web - its inner web makes a clockwise spiral, while its outer web makes a counter-clockwise spiral. Compare this photo of a different web in better light. Arachtober 25b. (San Marcos Pass, 23 October 2018)
ScientificName: Cyclosa
associatedTaxa: NA

File.Name: ia26a_ia26-021.jpg
Notes:
30633000427 This is another tiny Crab Spider (Thomisidae) waiting for prey by a faded flower of native California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum, Polygonaceae). There's a fresher flower in the background, but this spider is favoring the older flower. But look at that little pink anther on the faded flower in front of it - could the spider have planted it as bait to attract a pollinator?? (I've wondered that before, see this photo.) I believe this spider is another genus Mecaphesa spider with that hairy abdomen, and it looks like a male with those swollen palps. It seems I've mostly been seeing males lately. It's about 760 pixels long in another 1:1 macro shot that shows it more clearly, which comes to 3.0 mm, less than 1/8 inch. (See here for how I figured.) There were lots of these spiders a couple of weeks ago, but I looked close today and only found two. The other one is in my [Previous] photo. Arachtober 26a. (San Marcos Pass, 26 October 2018)

It was warmer today, up into the low 80s. There was some breeze in the morning, but it seemed to go away as the day progressed. Nice weather, but I'm ready for a swing back to the cool side. It sound like that won't happen for another week at least.
ScientificName: Thomisidae; Eriogonum fasciculatum; Polygonaceae; Mecaphesa
associatedTaxa: Thomisidae; Eriogonum fasciculatum; Polygonaceae; Mecaphesa

File.Name: ia26b_ia26-011.jpg
Notes: 43756601480 Here is another tiny Crab Spider (Thomisidae) surrounded by new flowers of native California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum, Polygonaceae). Hah - I'm thinking of Busby Berkeley musicals! The flowers were pretty much all faded, but they seem to be making a comeback, maybe thanks to the small rain we had a while back. Note that the fresh flowers have pink anthers on the tips of their stamens. I believe this spider is another genus Mecaphesa spider with that hairy abdomen, and it looks like a male with those swollen palps. There were lots of these spiders a couple of weeks ago, but I looked close today and only found two. The other one is in my [Next] photo. Arachtober 26b. (San Marcos Pass, 26 October 2018)

File.Name: ia26c_ia24-023.jpg
Notes: 44659845255 I'm pretty sure this photo shows a bristly Tachinid Fly (Tachinidae, Diptera) on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). This is the front end of the same fly I showed in this photo yesterday showing its rear end. I believe it does have a subscutellum that is visible in that photo, which BugGuide suggests is a useful character for identifying these flies. Maybe it's genyus maybe genus Peleteria which I've found before at this time of year? Whatever it is, it is a handsome fly - hairy as well as bristly. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 24 October 2018)

File.Name: ia27a_ia27-033.jpg
Notes:
44676307275 Here are two shots of an impressive large bottlecap-size Orb-Weaver Spider (Araneidae) that I found in the woods today - showing both top and bottom. This is not the same spider I've been showing e.g. in this photo, but I believe it's the same kind - genus Araneus with those paired humps, probably Araneus gemma or A. gemmoides. (These species are known to cross.) It's a proper Halloween spider, the first I've seen out on its web this year. I was lucky to be able to get on both sides of its web and shoot up seven or eight feet in an oak tree. Sure, I saved another photo for Halloween! Arachtober 27a. (San Marcos Pass, 27 October 2018)

It was warm again today, up into the low 80s. There was some breeze in the morning, but it seemed to go away as the day progressed. Nice weather, but I'm ready for a swing back to the cool side. This is getting monotonous.
ScientificName: Araneidae; Araneus; Araneus gemma; A. gemmoides

File.Name: ia27d_ia27-012.jpg
Notes:
44864737154 Here are two shots of the same remarkable gall on a small tree of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) taken three days apart. I got the photo on the left on October 24 and the photo on the right today. In another three days, it will have fallen away into the leaf litter on the ground. This is the gall of the Live Oak Bud Gall Wasp (Callirhytis quercusagrifoliae) in the family Cynipidae, order Hymenoptera growing on a leaf bud of the tree. These galls are almost too perfectly round! They're small, about the size of a blueberry. This is a unisexual gall, which contains only female wasps. The lady wasps will emerge next spring and parthenogenetically produce a crop of tiny bisexual galls that contain both male and female wasps. The wasps emerge, mate, and induce another crop of unisexual galls that contain only females, and the cycle repeats. Sorry, but galls are complicated like that! (San Marcos Pass, 24 & 27 October 2018)

I'll have a lot more galls to show after I'm done with spiders! Autumn is the time for both.
CommonNames: Coast Live Oak; Live Oak Bud Gall Wasp
ScientificName: Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Callirhytis quercusagrifoliae; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera
associatedTaxa: Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Callirhytis quercusagrifoliae; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera

File.Name: ia28b_ia28-021.jpg
Notes: 43790264250 Here is another shot of what I believe is the composite web of a Labyrinth Spider (genus Metepeira, Araneidae). These orb-weaving spiders make a proper orb web (left) and then build a little retreat off to the side held up by cobweb-type webbing (right). I have another shot of both webs here and a photo of the spider here. This one is inside a leafless (but still green) plant of native Deerweed (Acmispon glaber, Fabaceae). There is always an all-important strand of webbing stretched tight between the retreat and the orb web that the spider uses to sense activity. It's probably not visible at this resolution, but the spider is sticking two feet out of its shelter to monitor the line. I'll make a note. Arachtober 28b. (San Marcos Pass, 28 October 2018)

File.Name: ia29a_ia29-033.jpg
Notes:
44711600845 Here is another shot of the impressive large bottlecap-size Orb-Weaver Spider (Araneidae) that I showed on its web in this photo a couple of days ago. It wasn't on its web today, but it was hiding in its retreat among leaves of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) up above the web. Compare the similar spider's retreat in this photo. I believe this spider is genus Araneus with those paired humps, probably Araneus gemma or A. gemmoides. (These species are known to cross.) It's a Halloween spider, and it will be nice if I can get another shot of it on its web for Halloween in two days, but I do have a backup. Arachtober 29a. (San Marcos Pass, 29 October 2018)

I was expecting wind today, but it hasn't happened . . . yet. We had another pleasant day instead, a few degrees cooler than its been but quite calm. We're under a NWS Wind Advisory tonight and tomorrow, so I reckon the winds may kick up.

File.Name: ia29b_ia29-014.jpg
Notes: 31752498418 This strange-looking creature is a spider - but it looks more like a furry squid! I believe it's a Triangle Web Spider (genus Hyptiotes) in the family Uloboridae of the Cribellate Orb Weavers which make the odd triangular webs that I show in this photo. The spider is probably species Hyptiotes gertschi. Here it is holding one end of its web close to a twig of Southern Bush Monkeyflower (Mimulus aurantiacus var. pubescens, Phrymaceae) today. It's upside down, and you can see one of its eyes underneath it - looking especially squid-like! This is the first one I've seen in our canyon at home since last winter, see this photo from January 1. I also found them in the Oregon woods last summer, see this photo. We also have a close relative, the Feather-legged Spider (Uloborus diversus) in this photo. These spiders are tiny - this one is only 780 pixels long in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 3.1 mm, less than 1/8 inch. (See here for how I figured.) The only way I noticed it is that it was bouncing up and down on it's web. These tiny spiders have become one of my favorites. Arachtober 29b. (San Marcos Pass, 29 October 2018)

File.Name: ia30a_ia30-019.jpg
Notes:
44916798724 This is another tiny Crab Spider (Thomisidae) waiting for prey by a fresh-looking flower of native California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum, Polygonaceae). I like the twinkle in its eye! These spiders have a knack for staking out the fresh flowers in a faded cluster that still have pink anthers on the tips of their stamens. I believe this spider is another genus Mecaphesa spider with that hairy abdomen, and it looks like another male with those swollen palps. File.Name: ia30b_ia30-002.jpg
Notes: 30700701357 Here are two shots of different tiny Trashline Orb Weaver (Cyclosa, Araneidae) spiders - showing their trash lines. Sure, the spiders make these collections vertically on their little orb webs, but I rotated my camera 90 degrees to make better use of my screen space. I was hoping I'd be able to identify more of the "trash," but it's hard. I can see the spider's exuviae on the bottom-right, the shed skin after a molt, but the rest of it is pretty mysterious. It would take patience and a stereoscope to do it right. I got the bottom photo today and the one on top a couple of days ago, both in a place where the sun never shines at this time of year. Hah - I've been taking a photo like this first thing every morning for a while just so I'd have some spider shot to show for each day. The month of Arachtober is just about over, so this time I'm actually using today's photo. Arachtober 30b. (San Marcos Pass, 30 October 2018)

File.Name: ia31a_ia31-025.jpg
Notes:
44743530665 The mama Green Lynx Spider (Peucetia, Oxyopidae) has become a Red Lynx Spider! I've been watching her change colors all this month, and her transformation is just about complete - compare this photo from the beginning of the month and this photo from September. She's still watching over her spiderlings, which are out of sight behind the egg sac on the left. The mama spiders are truly devoted mothers who guard their egg sacks until the spiderlings disperse. She also looks like she's wearing a demon mask for Halloween! Arachtober 31a. (San Marcos Pass, 31 October 2018)

The Arachtober group challenge is to post spider photos every day during October, and I like to treat it like a scavenger hunt to find new spiders every day of the month. I've been successful this year! I've learned a lot too, mostly that there are more spiders in the world than I find at home in October. Now to get back to photographing all the other creatures in the woods.

The second of these photos shows that the male also has a red hourglass-shaped mark under the abdomen, but it's not nearly so impressive as the lady's mark. File.Name: ib01a_ib01-021.jpg
Notes:
43855497800 This is a lovely California Dogface (Colias eurydice) butterfly puddling - taking moisture - at a sandy place near our well where there was some water spilled recently. This is a female that lacks the remarkable "dog face" pattern of the males, but it does have the same "falcate" forewings - curved like a sickle on the trailing edge - which distinguish it from other local Sulphur butterflies. I was surprised to find it today when other butterflies are getting scarce, but I probably shouldn't be. Apparently these butterflies overwinter as adults, see this photo of a male in December. Isn't this one beautiful with the sun behind. (San Marcos Pass, 1 November 2018)

Today I have photos of a butterfly, a wasp, a grasshopper, and a fly - and no spiders! Arachtober has been great fun, but now it's time to get back to everything else in the woods. The weather cooperated today - pleasantly warm and not much breeze, with some high clouds.

File.Name: ib01b_ib01-028.jpg
Notes: 30732613347 I was surprised to find this Velvet Ant (Mutillidae, Hymenoptera) walking across the same sandy place where I found the butterfly in my [Next] photo. I was surprised again when it flew off, but I shouldn't have been since it has obvious wings. That means that it's a male, as the females are wingless - see this photo of what seems to be the same kind. They are not really ants, but wasps that are ectoparasitoids of other immature insects, esp. bees and solitary wasps. This one is possibly species Dasymutilla aureola, compare this photo at BugGuide. Other local species are white in color, see this photo of mine. They are not common, especially these males. They are said to have a very painful sting. (San Marcos Pass, 1 November 2018)
ScientificName: Mutillidae; Hymenoptera; Dasymutilla aureola
associatedTaxa: Mutillidae; Hymenoptera; Dasymutilla aureola

File.Name: ib01c_ib01-016.jpg
Notes: 45622484402 Here is a goofy little Chaparral Monkey Grasshopper (Morsea californica) in the Eumastacidae family of the Orthoptera on a leaf of Greenbark Ceanothus (Ceanothus spinosus, Rhamnaceae). I guess it's less than 1/2 inch long. I'm not sure how to distinguish nymph and adult as both are wingless, but this one is small enough to be a nymph. They are remarkably variable as you can see if you search all my photos for "Morsea californica". This is only the second one that I've seen this year, and it's curious that it's missing the same leg as the first one I found, see this photo. It's the hind leg on the other side, so it's not so obvious in this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 1 November 2018)

It's an unusual gray color. The larvae of these flies parasitize other insects like caterpillars. They seem pretty common right now, and I'm not sure why - most other insects are pretty scarce. HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 31 October 2018)

I know, Arachtober was last month, but I had an errand in the Santa Ynez Valley today. I made my usual stop near Lake Cachuma, and this is what I got. Notes:
30783485207 This is a gorgeous little Tephritid Fruit Fly (Tephritidae, Diptera) on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods today. Those eyes! It also has a remarkable pattern on its wings that reminds me of a Japanese Rising Sun flag, but it's only visible in another photo that I might save for Fly Day Friday - see this photo for something similar. It's tiny, about 1000 pixels long in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 3.9 mm, a bit over 1/8 inch. (See here for how I figured.) Maybe this fly is genus Trupanea of the tribe Tephritini? I reckon it's female with that visible ovipositor. (San Marcos Pass, 4 November 2018)

After some high clouds in the morning, it was another warm day and quite calm. I suppose it was just about another perfect autumn day, but I'd rather see some rain!

Notes: 30783299987 I found this skeletonized leaf on a small tree of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods today. This may be the work of a caterpillar like the one in this photo, but fungi also do this, see this photo. I'm not sure how to tell them apart when a leaf gets to this stage. File.Name: ib04c_ib04-027.jpg
Notes: 45723548801 This swelling is the galled stem of a native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Asteraceae plant family. I believe this is an integral stem gall of the Baccharis Stem Gall Moth (Gnorimoshema baccharisella) in the family Gelechiidae, Lepidoptera), though I know nothing about it. I assume that's a relatively large exit hole where the moth got out - as opposed to some parasitoid getting in. I wonder how the tiny moths chew their way out of the galls - don't they have the wrong kind of mouthparts? Apparently the accumulated frass in the empty galls serves as a culture medium for a number of other insects that take up residence after the moth is gone. Ron Russo in his "Field Guide to Plant Galls" (UC Press, 2006) says "The intricacies of these complex relationships stagger the mind for such a common, yet disregarded, shrub." (San Marcos Pass, 4 November 2018)
CommonNames: Coyote Bush; Baccharis Stem Gall Moth
ScientificName: Baccharis pilularis; Asteraceae; Gnorimoshema baccharisella; Gelechiidae; Lepidoptera

File.Name: ib04d_ib01-017.jpg
Notes: 31851647508 Here's another shot of the goofy little Chaparral Monkey Grasshopper (Morsea californica) in the Eumastacidae family of the Orthoptera that I showed in this photo a couple of days ago. It's resting on a leaf of Greenbark Ceanothus (Ceanothus spinosus, Rhamnaceae). I tried to show it from the other side in this photo, but I was pushed up against the rest of the shrub, and I couldn't get the focus plane right. It's still a nice photo, and it shows that it's missing a hind leg - pretty important for a hopper! This is only the second one that I've seen this year, and it's curious that it's missing the same leg as the first one I found, see this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 1 November 2018)

File.Name: ib05a_ib05-017.jpg
Notes:
30801873077 Here is a beautiful speckled gall of the Beaked Twig Gall Wasp (Disholcaspis plumbella) in the family Cynipidae, Hymenoptera growing on Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia, Fagaceae). The pointed beak of these galls is distinctive. It's about the size of a grape. I've only seen a few of these galls this year. It seems odd to think of galls as having good and bad years, but the wasps that induce them sure do. These galls are particularly beautiful, but why would a gall - the induced home of tiny wasp larvae - be so darned pretty? Doesn't that just attract the attention of predators? I don't think of these as plant pests - they are more like decorations! (San Marcos Pass, 5 November 2018)

Today was about ten degrees cooler than it's been, and there was more of a breeze from the south. That's a change from what its been, but not much of a change.
CommonNames: Beaked Twig Gall Wasp; Scrub Oak
ScientificName: Disholcaspis plumbella

File.Name: ib05b_ib05-021.jpg
Notes: 45742022921 Here are a pair of good-sized galls on a twig of native Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia, Fagaceae). I believe they are the galls of a Dried Peach Gall Wasp (Disholcaspis simulata) in the family Cynipidae. They really do look like little peaches! I've been neglecting galls while I looked for spiders last month, but the time of Arachtober is now past. This is probably the peak time for galls - which is convenient because insects and flowers are getting scarce. There's always something to photograph in our woods, though sometimes you have to hunt for it! (San Marcos Pass, 5 November 2018)

File.Name: ib05c_ib05-002.jpg
Notes:
44827488405 These are last spring's galled flowers on a shrub of Holly-Leaved Redberry (Rhamnus ilicifolia) in the Rhamnaceae plant family. The gall on the left with the hole in it sure looks like something tried to get in rather than a gall insect trying to get out. I'm still not sure what critter makes these galls, as they are not mentioned in my book by Ron Russo, "Field Guide to Plant Galls" (UC Press, 2006). This shrub is related to the California Coffeeberry (Frangula californica) which has similar galls caused by Gall Midge (Cecidomyiidae, Diptera) flies, see this photo. My guess is that the galls in my present photo are caused by something similar.

That said, look at the very base of the gall on the left! (I'll make a note.) I didn't notice this critter until I had my photo up on the computer screen. My first thought was that it's the larva of a tiny Psyllid (Psylloidea, Sternorrhyncha, Hemiptera ) insect - but do they ever have tails? That would be interesting because they cause a different kind of gall on these same shrubs, see this photo. Hah, I think it's more likely the nymph of a Planthopper (Fulgoroidea, Auchenorrhyncha, Hemiptera), and it's just serendipity that it got into my photo - compare this photo. I'll have to go back and look for more of them. (San Marcos Pass, 5 November 2018)

File.Name: ib05d_ib04-023.jpg
Notes: 43923595260 This is a gall on a small leaf of Greenbark Ceanothus (Ceanothus spinosus, Rhamnaceae). Ron Russo in his book Field Guide to Plant Galls (UC Press, 2006) describes two similar galls on different Ceanothus species caused by Gall Midge (Cecidomyiidae, Diptera) flies - the Vein Gall Midge (genus Asphondylia) and the Midrib Fold Midge (genus Contarinia). The latter sounds similar to mine, though he describes it them densely pubescent and mine is quite smooth. I reckon this is something different though related. I don't find a lot of these galls, they are something you have to look for. (San Marcos Pass, 4 November 2018)

File.Name: ib06a_ib06-019.jpg
Notes:
45032777614 Here is another Tree Cricket (genus Oecanthus, Gryllidae, Orthoptera) under an old flower/seed whorl of native Black Sage (Salvia mellifera, Lamiaceae). This one is female with narrow wings, compare this photo of a male with broad wings taken a couple of weeks ago. Both male and female crickets work the night shift and try to hide during the day, and only the broad-winged males sing at night. I'm still hearing them at night, though its probably getting close to the end of their time. All insects are getting scarce as we approach winter. (San Marcos Pass, 6 November 2018)

Today was much like yesterday, cooler with some breeze. Sure, I voted today. Now it's time to catch up on the election results.

File.Name: ib06c_ib05-011.jpg
Notes: 45707340642 These are ripe nuts of native Snowdrop Bush (Styrax redivivus) in the Styracaceae plant family. They are proper tree nuts with a husk on the outside, then the hard shell, which you can crack to expose the nut meat inside. I've tasted them, and they seem edible and good. What a remarkable shrub - it grows in both woodland and chaparral, it has gorgeous white flowers that smell like perfume, the deciduous leaves turn golden in the fall, and it has these interesting edible nuts! File.Name: ib06d_ib05-020.jpg
Notes: 31885833158 Here are the first new flowers of native Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca) in the Ericaceae plant family growing along West Camino Cielo road yesterday - still before most of our winter rains. The flowers are actually late this year - last year I found the first flowers of October 10, see this photo. Some shrubs just do flower early, though most flowers won't appear until after the rains come. Note that the flowers aren't really open yet , they have little caps over their ends - except for that one flower in the middle that some critter has nibbled into. Chaparral Currant (Ribes malvaceum, Grossulariaceae) is also flowering, see this photo from a month ago. I find it so interesting that there's always something flowering in our woods. The late-season flowers like California Fuchsia overlap the early flowers of Chaparral Currant and Bigberry Manzanita in a way that almost seems like design to keep the pollinators alive. (San Marcos Pass, 5 November 2018)

There are still a few of the pretty red flowers on this plant, including one out of focus in the background - see this photo. File.Name: ib07b_ib07-012.jpg
Notes: 43956657210 This is Eastwood Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glandulosa ssp. mollis, Ericaceae) with an infestation of aphid galls. If you look close, you can see the white exuviae (shed skins) of the aphids that live inside the succulent red "pouches" on the leaves. I also find similar galls earlier in the year on Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca), see this photo. For some time I've thought that the red tumor-like growths on this shrub were caused by the Manzanita Leaf Fungus (Exobasidium vacinii) that looks very similar, but I finally opened one up and sure 'nuff there were aphids inside, see my this photo. Maybe the galls on both Manzanitas are caused by the same Manzanita Leaf Gall Aphids (Tamalia coweni, Aphididae), but maybe they are something different? And what about the fungus that looks so similar - is there a connection? (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 7 November 2018)

File.Name: ib08a_ib08-020.jpg
Notes:
30849084287 I reckon the soundtrack for this photo sold be "I Feel Pretty" from West Side Story! This is a Yellowjacket-type wasp grooming itself on an oak leaf, probably a Western Yellowjacket (Vespula pensylvanica, Vespidae, Hymenoptera). This wasp is obviously not from the nest I found dug up about 100 yards away, see this photo from last week. These wasps can become pests in the fall, stealing pieces of your hamburger or landing on your food between plate and mouth! Fortunately they are not a problem in the canyon at home. (San Marcos Pass, 8 November 2018)

Today was much like yesterday, cool with some breeze. There was no proper marine layer fog this morning, but there was a haze layer that came up to our height in the mountains. I suspect that our morning winds are the result of turbulent air at the top of this haze layer - like "fog winds" without the fog. Down south, they're under both a Red Flag Warning and a Wind Advisory. We don't get real Santa Ana winds here in Santa Barbara because there's no path to the desert like the Santa Clara River in Ventura county just down the coast, and there is indeed a wildfire burning south of Camarillo tonight that's shut down Highway 101. That's about all I know about it so far.

These wasps resemble wasps in the genus Polistes, but their abdomen is attached to the thorax by a slender stalk-like "petiole". There's something unnerving about making eye contact with such a tiny critter, but here it is. (San Marcos Pass, 9 November 2018)

There are dangerous wind-driven wildfires burning in both Northern and Southern California tonight. I'm having "There but for fortune" thoughts, but the weather is not explosive here at home, not too many miles from Ventura County where there is both wind and wildfire. We don't get real Santa Ana winds here in Santa Barbara because there's no path to the desert like the Santa Clara River in that county just down the coast. That might be good news for efforts to control the Woosley Fire that is threatening Malibu. I couldn't smell any smoke today, but I could sense a subtle warm shift in the sunlight that was no doubt its effect.

File.Name: ib10c_ib09-002.jpg
Notes: 45095054564 Here are two photos showing different stages of galls of the Live Oak Apple Gall Wasp (Callirhytis quercuspomiformis, Cynipidae). I took both photos yesterday. That's the spring gall of the wasp on the left, and the summer gall on the right. What's missing is any sense of scale. Both galls have circular exit holes, and maybe they are the same size? The gall on the right is about the size of a golf ball, and the gall on the left is about the size (and shape) of a golf tee! These smaller galls are bisexual galls because they will give rise to both male and female wasps that will mate and produce the summer unisexual oak apple galls on the right which contain only female wasps. Those ladies will emerge next spring and produce another crop of bisexual galls like the one on the left, and the cycle of alternating generations repeats. I've also been finding spring galls without exit holes, and I wonder if they can delay emerging for a year or longer e.g. for a drought year - see this photo. Sorry, but galls are complicated like this. It's part of what makes then so interesting. (San Marcos Pass, 9 November 2018)
ScientificName: Callirhytis quercuspomiformis; Cynipidae; bisexual galls; unisexual
associatedTaxa: Callirhytis quercuspomiformis; Cynipidae; bisexual galls; unisexual

File.Name: ib11c_ib11-010.jpg
Notes: 45836057121 I believe these are galls of the Crystalline Gall Wasp (Andricus crystallinus, Cynipidae, Hymenoptera) growing under a leaf of native Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family. Hah, it took me some time to figure out what they are as they are unusually pale. Usually the galls are quite red like in this photo, but I have seen white ones too as in this photo. It's curious that these are just about the only ones I've seen this year, whereas in some years they are quite common - see this photo from 2016 for an extreme case! I don't know what happens when galls fail for a year - is the wasp population gone for good, or do they have a reserve? This isn't the best photo, but it will do. I found these galls in a place where the sun never shines at this time of year, though there is just a hint of sunlight coming through the trees. (San Marcos Pass, 11 November 2018)

File.Name: ib11d_ib10-013.jpg
Notes: 45111419884 Here are ripening seeds of a shrub of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Asteraceae plant family. These shrubs are dioecious with separate male and female plants, and the two look quite different. The male flowers actually seemed to do better than the female ones this year, but even so the dandelion-like seeds are starting to appear everywhere as they are carried by the wind - see this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 10 November 2018)

File.Name: ib12a_ib12-007.jpg
Notes:
44036753810 This is another goofy-looking net-winged Planthopper (Fulgoroidea, Auchenorrhyncha, Hemiptera) that I found today on a leaf of native Goldencup Oak aka Canyon Live Oak (Quercus chrysolepis, Fagaceae). Hah - the leaf was about eight feet off the ground, so I had to fully extend my tripod and stand on tiptoes to get this shot. The little hopper looks sort of like the other ones I've been showing here, here, and here, which I believe are all genus Neaethus in the family Tropiduchidae. But my hopper today looks subtly different - it's smaller, darker, and has patterned wings whereas the others' wings are clear. This one might well be Neaethus maculatus, which BugGuide says is "the only member of this genus with spotted wings." The other ones are something else. (San Marcos Pass, 12 November 2018)

Wildfires continue to burn in California tonight, both to the north and south. I hope the winds are getting better, but the Red Flag Warning in Ventura County (where our fires are) has been extended another day until Wednesday evening. It's been calm at our house. There's even a (remote) rumor of rain for Thanksgiving. I will give thanks for that!

File.Name: ib12b_ib12-028.jpg
Notes: 45804119642 I found this lobster-colored true bug (Heteroptera, Hemiptera) working the fading flowers of California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum, Polygonaceae) in the woods today. At least it seemed to be working the flowers, but looking closer at my photos I think it's actually sticking its proboscis into the spaces between the flowers. I believe that I've seen this critter before, see this photo and this photo from last year near his time. This bug might well be genus Arhyssus, a Scentless Plant Bug in the family Rhopalidae. The bug is almost the same color as the faded flowers of the buckwheat - great camouflage, but becoming less so as the flowers keep fading. (San Marcos Pass, 12 November 2018)

File.Name: ib12c_ib12-013.jpg
Notes: 44036387200 I believe these are galls of the Woollybear Gall Wasp (Sphaeroteras timaculosum aka Atrusca trimaculosa, Cynipidae, Hymenoptera) growing on the underside of leaves of native Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia, Fagaceae). Many galls apparently don't harm their host plant, but these pea-size galls do take a toll on the leaves. This has not been a great year for oak galls, no doubt because it hasn't been a very good year for the wasps that make them. Eight years of drought has made a difference, but I have been finding a few. Autumn is their time, and galls nicely fill the gap between summer insects and winter mushrooms as subjects to photograph. (San Marcos Pass, 12 November 2018)

File.Name: ib12d_ib12-002.jpg
Notes: 44036285420 Here are ripening berries of native Toyon aka California Holly (as in Hollywood), Heteromeles arbutifolia in the Rosaceae plant family. They will ripen in time for the holidays which gives them their other common name of Christmas Berry. The ripe berries are edible, but they are quite astringent (unless you're a bird!) and need cooking. They seem to be having a good year, despite the drought conditions this year. (San Marcos Pass, 12 November 2018)
ScientificName: Heteromeles arbutifolia
associatedTaxa: Heteromeles arbutifolia; Rosaceae

File.Name: ib13a_ib13-021.jpg
Notes:
45869769571 This is another goofy-looking net-winged Planthopper (Fulgoroidea, Auchenorrhyncha, Hemiptera) that I found today on Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). This one is different from the one I showed yesterday in this photo, though I believe they are both in the same genus Neaethus of the family Tropiduchidae. I believe the one I showed yesterday is Neaethus maculatus which is smaller, darker, and has patterned wings - while this one has clear wings. It's still quite small, about 775 pixels long in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 3.0 mm, less than 1/8 inch. (See here for how I figured.) I'm not sure what species this is as it lacks an easy ID feature like spotted wings, but it's still a handsome 'hopper. (San Marcos Pass, 13 November 2018)

The weather changed a bit today with lots of mid-level clouds moving in from the west. I figure any change is good news for the efforts to control the wildfires burning in California, though Ventura County is still under a Red Flag Warning for another day. I'm still hearing about a rumor of rain for Thanksgiving in the NWS Forecast Discussion.

File.Name: ib13b_ib13-025.jpg
Notes: 45145237694 Here are two leaves of native Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) with new and old "leaf roller galls" (my name) on the margins. I suspect these are real galls as they go against the natural downward curve of the leaf, and they're made of "galled tissue" that looks different from normal leaf tissue - see this photo for a look inside. It's not listed in Ron Russo's fine "Field Guide to Plant Galls" (UC Press, 2006). We also seem to have different "leaf roller galls" that roll the leaves under rather than over, see this photo. I opened one up today and found white fluff inside but no obvious insect. I don't like the photo I got, but I'll try again tomorrow. The leaf is lumpy, there's something in there! (San Marcos Pass, 13 November 2018)
ScientificName: Quercus agrifolia
associatedTaxa: NA

File.Name: ib13c_ib13-001.jpg
Notes: 45819928182 This is an odd gall on a leaf bud of a small tree of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). It reminds me of the gall of a Live Oak Bud Gall Wasp (Callirhytis quercusagrifoliae, Cynipidae) that I show in this photo in that it is small and perfectly round, but there are differences as well. The color is different, it seems less hairy, and the way the bracts hold the gall like a claw and ball foot is also different. I first noticed these a couple of days ago, but those galls have already fallen away - another similarity to the other galls that also develop very rapidly. I'll keep an eye on this one, I'm sure it's something different. (San Marcos Pass, 13 November 2018)

File.Name: ib13d_ib13-015.jpg
Notes: 44956001765 Here is another speckled gall of the Beaked Twig Gall Wasp (Disholcaspis plumbella) in the family Cynipidae, Hymenoptera growing on Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia, Fagaceae) in the canyon at home. The pointed beak of these galls is distinctive. I've only seen a few of these galls this year, but this one shrub has lots of them for some reason. I don't think of these galls as plant pests - they are more like decorations! (San Marcos Pass, 13 November 2018)

File.Name: ib14a_ib14-028.jpg
Notes:
44971918835 I guess there will be spiders in the buckwheat as long as there are flowers! Here is another tiny Crab Spider (Thomisidae) waiting for prey by a group of fresh-looking flowers of native California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum, Polygonaceae). Most of the flowers are now faded, but these spiders have a knack for staking out the fresh flowers that still have pink anthers on the tips of their stamens. I believe this spider is another genus Mecaphesa spider with that hairy abdomen, and it's a male with those swollen palps. It's curious that I've been finding mostly males lately, while I was finding mostly females earlier in the year. There were lots of these spiders a couple of weeks ago, but this is the only one I saw today. Time is running out for these flower-loving spiders. (San Marcos Pass, 14 November 2018)

It was warmer today and dead-calm for much of the day. That's got to be good news for the fire-fighting efforts down by Thousand Oaks and Malibu, though the WIFIRE Heat Map still shows some activity on the west end of the fire in Point Mugu State Park. I'm getting a bit distracted - by fires, but also because we're heading off to Oregon this weekend for our usual Thanksgiving time with our kids and grandkids. There's lots to do before we leave. I'll do what I can for new photos.

Notes: 44971821325 Here are new seeds of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Asteraceae plant family - about to leave home. The dandelion-like seeds are starting to appear everywhere as they are carried away by the wind. (San Marcos Pass, 14 November 2018)

Notes: 45835275912 This photo shows webbing at the end of a dead stem of native Black Sage (Salvia mellifera, Lamiaceae). Hah, it looks like rigging on a dusty model ship! I believe this is "cribellate" webbing which is fuzzy and lacks any kind of sticky stuff, see this photo and this photo for more examples. Maybe this is the work of a cribellate species of Mesh Web Weaver in the family Dictynidae. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 13 November 2018)

Notes: 45835203132 Here is webbing between two leaves of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). I like the way the webbing connects to the spines on the leaf below. I guess this is the work of a spider, though I didn't see one nearby. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 9 November 2018)

File.Name: ib15a_ib15-024.jpg
Notes:
32029565808 I found this lovely Stink Bug in the family Pentatomidae of the Heteroptera, Hemiptera inside a seed capsule of native Late-flowered Mariposa Lily (Calochortus fimbriatus) in the Liliaceae plant family. See this photo for one of the fantastic flowers in this place, now gone to seed. The horn-shaped capsule is full of seeds, and I think the bug was "sucking" them with its proboscis. I'm not sure what kind the bug is, but I believe I've seen it before - see this photo and this photo. Its color is remarkable camouflage on these dry seed capsules. (San Marcos Pass, 15 November 2018)

It was partly cloudy again today, cloudy enough to effect my white balance. There were breezy moments, but it was mostly quite calm. The WIFIRE Heat Map is showing no new activity on the Woosley Fire in the Santa Monica Mountains south/east of us - good news. The Camp Fire up north still appears active on the east side above Lake Oroville, so our drive to Oregon this weekend will probably be smoky. We hope to leave on Sunday.

File.Name: ib15b_ib15-015.jpg
Notes: 45850816882 I found this Tree Cricket (genus Oecanthus, Gryllidae, Orthoptera) hiding (in plain sight) under a dead stem of native Black Sage (Salvia mellifera, Lamiaceae). This is another female with narrow wings, and I think it's interesting that I've mostly been finding females lately. (Hah - I've mostly been finding male spiders lately, see my [Previous] photo and this photo yesterday.) Both male and female crickets work the night shift and try to hide during the day, and only the broad-winged males like this one sing at night. I'm still hearing them at night, though its not a deafening chorus, and it's probably getting close to the end of their time. All insects are getting scarce as we approach winter. (San Marcos Pass, 15 November 2018)

Notes: 45900331391 Here are two shots of a small spider hanging from a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods today. I won't try to guess what kind of spider it is, but it definitely has male palps. If you look close at the photo on the left, you can see that the spider is pushed up against one of the leaf spines. I reckon that counts as an interesting "butt shot" - so HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 15 November 2018)

File.Name: ib15d_ib09-017.jpg
Notes: 44987252155 Here are the bristly behinds of a pair of tiny flies on oak leaves. The flies look very similar apart from their colors, and both may be Tachinid Flies (Tachinidae, Diptera) - both are bristly, and the one on the right does seem to have a subscutellum, which BugGuide suggests is a useful character for identifying these flies. These flies are small. From another photo, the one on the right is just 770 pixels long in a 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 3.0 mm, less than 1/8 inch. (See here for how I figured.) File.Name: ib16a_ib16-019.jpg
Notes:
45002871895 Here is a tiny Orb Web Weaver (family Araneidae) spider on an old seed head of native Black Sage (Salvia mellifera, Lamiaceae). If you know the shrub, that's all the scale you need - the spider would fit into one of the little seed pockets! If you don't know the shrub, see this photo with a cricket for scale. The spider is quite small, about 730 pixels long in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 2.9 mm, less than 1/8 inch. (See here for how I figured.) It has a proper orb web off to the side like the one in this photo, which may be the web of the same kind of spider. I believe this is a Labyrinth Spider (genus Metepeira, Araneidae) with that "folium pattern" on its abdomen, compare this photo and this photo. This one seems so tiny, but maybe it's just a kid? It's got a hint of a cat face on its abdomen. (San Marcos Pass, 16 November 2018)

I hit a minor milestone today with 4,000,000+ views of my photos! I was "Treebeard" at Fotolog starting in 2003, and I didn't really start posting at Flickr until 13 February 2012 - so that's four million hits since then. Nobody seems to know how Flickr counts views, it's sometimes more and sometimes less, so I don't take this too seriously - but thanks!

We hope to leave for Oregon on Sunday. I'll be busy with trip stuff on Saturday, but I should still be able to post something in the evening.

I don't know what kind of fly it is, though it's colors aren't quite ordinary. It has an injury or maybe a fungus infection by its right eye. File.Name: ib17a_ib15-033.jpg
Notes:
45930740261 Here is another Western Paper Wasp (Mischocyttarus flavitarsis, Vespidae, Hymenoptera) on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). These wasps resemble wasps in the genus Polistes, but their abdomen is attached to the thorax by a slender stalk-like "petiole". They look threatening enough, but I've never been stung, even by the ones that nest under our eaves at home. (San Marcos Pass, 15 November 2018)

We're off to Oregon tomorrow for Thanksgiving with our kids and grandkids. I'm hoping for wild mushrooms, but it sounds like it's nearly as dry in southeast Oregon as it is here. Fortunately, there is rain in the forecast for Thanksgiving in both Oregon and California - something to be really thankful for! There's more info in the new post at the California Weather Blog, along with interesting thoughts about the recent wildfires. Rain won't keep me indoors next week, and maybe we will find some mushrooms. We'll be off the grid for a couple of weeks.

Maybe it's a Western Striped Cucumber Beetle (Acalymma trivittatum) in the group Chrysomelidae, Coleoptera, but I'm sure there are other possibilities. I like the way the highlight on its compound eye shows the individual simple eyes that make it up. (San Marcos Pass, 15 November 2018)

File.Name: ib17c_ib16-016.jpg
Notes: 44114290860 Here are a pair of different bud galls on leaf buds of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). Both are the work of Cynipid Gall Wasps (Cynipidae, Hymenoptera), but I believe they are different. The green one on the left is the size of a BB, but it will get larger as it matures. This is the gall of the Live Oak Bud Gall Wasp (Callirhytis quercusagrifoliae). The brown gall on the right is larger, about the the size of a pea. I'm not sure whose gall it is, and I can't find it in my books. The colors are different, it's less hairy, and the way the bracts hold the gall like a claw and ball foot is also different. Both galls mature quickly and drop away. The brown gall in this photo was gone the next day! (San Marcos Pass, 16 November 2018)

File.Name: ib17d_ib16-013.jpg
Notes: 44113759800 A few days ago, I posted a photo of rolled leaves on Coast Live Oak - see this photo. Flickr user RandomTruth suggests that it's the work of a leaf roller moth caterpillar, and that sure seems reasonable - the rolled leaves look lumpy like there's something big inside. I've opened a few of these leaves now, and my photo today is what I see - no caterpillar, but there is white fluff, what look like eggs, and what sure looks like an aphid and its shed skins (exuviae)! I've added larger insert of the aphid at the very center of my photo. It's really tiny, about 190 pixels across in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 0.7 mm. (See here for how I figured.) Now just finding the aphid in there doesn't mean that the aphid caused the rolled leaf - it could be an inquiline that shares the space, like a mouse in the walls of our house. But I've opened up a few of these leaves now, and these aphids are all that I've found. And sure enough, a Google search turned up an article on Leaf Roller Aphids on Live Oaks. Maybe these rolled leaves really are galls caused by aphids? I suspect they might be Woolly Aphids or Gall-making Aphids in the aphid genus Stegophylla or subfamily Eriosomatinae. I have found wool-making aphids on oak leaves before, see this photo, and aphids do cause galls on Manzanita and Cottonwood leaves. Like I said, I'm not sure about this, and further research will have to wait until we get back from Oregon in a couple of weeks. (San Marcos Pass, 16 November 2018)
ScientificName: inquiline; Stegophylla; Eriosomatinae
associatedTaxa: inquiline; Stegophylla; Eriosomatinae

File.Name: ic03a_ic01-162.jpg
Notes:
45445127594 Here are geese taking wing at the Sacramento National Wildlife Refuge. They are White Geese - a mix of Snow Geese (Chen caerulescens) and Ross's Geese (Anser rossii) that are hard to distinguish. This wonderful refuge is located south of Willows, California along the Sacramento River. Stopping there is always a highlight of the drive back from our Thanksgiving family time in Oregon - see last year's photos starting here and the previous year's starting here. There are many thousands of birds in the refuge. I got this photo with my 105mm macro lens along the auto tour in the refuge - from inside the car, a reasonable requirement at the refuge. (Sacramento National Wildlife Refuge, Glenn and Colusa Counties, California, 1 December 2018)

When we first got to our cabin in Oregon two weeks ago, the air was full of smoke and the woods felt disturbingly dry. Southwest Oregon has been sharing our California drought this year - actually Douglas County in Oregon and our home mountains near Santa Barbara, California have both been rated in the "D3 (Extreme Drought)" category by the U.S. Drought Monitor. Fortunately, we had good rains in Oregon and at home in California - a real reason for giving thanks. We actually had five inches of rain at home on San Marcos Pass in the time we were gone, and more rain is expected starting tomorrow evening. Thanks!
Locality: Extreme Drought)" category by the U.S. Drought Monitor. Fortunately

File.Name: ic03b_ic01-010.jpg
Notes: 46169068251 This is a soggy Leaf-footed Bug (Coreidae, Heteroptera, Hemiptera) walking along the railing of our deck in the Oregon woods after some rain. It's named for the leaf-like protrusions on its hind legs. It might well be the Western Conifer Seed Bug (Leptoglossus occidentalis). The Western Leaf-footed Bug (L. clypealis) is similar, though it has a small spine at the tip of its head and more of a white line across its wings. I've also found this one at home, see this photo. It looks a bit like a large assassin bug (Reduviidae), but they are strictly herbivorous and use their sharp beaks to suck plant juices instead of other insects. I found another one when we were visiting in August, see this photo. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 23 November 2018)

File.Name: ic03c_ic01-027.jpg
Notes: 45256379225 You can't go to the Oregon woods in autumn without finding at least one banana slug - even in an extereme drought year! This is a large greenish Pacific Banana Slug (Ariolimax columbianus) making tracks in the Oregon woods. You can see its eye-stalks and feelers on the left. These Oregon slugs look quite different from the equally large California Banana Slugs (Ariolimax californicus) that we have at home, compare this photo from last year. There is some confusion about their names, but the difference seems clear to me. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 24 November 2018)

File.Name: ic03d_ic01-132.jpg
Notes: 45255950305 Here is a group of little white mushrooms growing from a fallen cone of Douglas Fir (Pseudotsuga menziesii, Pinaceae) in the Oregon woods. That one mushroom on the left has pinkish colors on the cap, but they are mostly white and probably white-spored. Maybe they are the species Strobilurus trullisatus which only grows on rotting cones? I didn't find a lot of mushrooms on our Thanksgiving trip to Oregon this year, but I did find a few. Southwest Oregon has been sharing our California drought this year - actually Douglas County, Oregon and our home mountains near Santa Barbara, California have both been rated in the "D3 (Extreme Drought)" category by the U.S. Drought Monitor. I hope that will soon change after good rains both here and there. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 27 November 2018)

File.Name: ic04a_ic04-005.jpg
Notes:
32311764358 Trip photos are nice, but today's photos from home are always the best!

Here is a tiny Syrphid Fly (Syrphidae, Diptera) perched for a moment on a patch of liverworts (Marchantiophyta) on a sandstone outcrop in the canyon. There are many different kinds of these flies, so I won't try to guess which one it is. I'm pretty sure the liverwort is the Orobus-seed Liverwort (Targionia hypophylla). It's a remarkable plant that quickly goes from green to dormant as the weather changes, see this photo. It's green now, thanks to good rain last week which soaked the sandstone enough that it's still holding water. With luck and continuing rain, it will stay green all winter. (San Marcos Pass, 4 December 2018)

It was quite windy this morning, so I was happy to find this fly on a rock out of the wind. Rain is in the forecast tonight with "rain likely" through Thursday. I can see it coming on the radar map though it's not here yet. We stand at 6.65 inches of rain for the water year that began on September 1, which comes to 120% of normal - not bad!
ScientificName: Syrphidae; Diptera; Marchantiophyta; Targionia hypophylla
associatedTaxa: Syrphidae; Diptera; Marchantiophyta; Targionia hypophylla

File.Name: ic04b_ic01-147.jpg
Notes: 45271027505 This is a tiny nymph of an Assassin Bug in the Reduviidae family among the needles on a branch of a Douglas Fir (Pseudotsuga menziesii, Pinaceae) sapling in the Oregon woods. It's taking a defensive pose to scare me off, but it didn't work. I don't know if this is the same or different from the ones I find at home in California, but it looks different. I got this photo in light rain on a gloomy day. It shows. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 28 November 2018)
ScientificName: Reduviidae; Pseudotsuga menziesii; Pinaceae
associatedTaxa: Reduviidae; Pseudotsuga menziesii; Pinaceae

File.Name: ic04c_ic01-031.jpg
Notes: 46183323851 Hah, sometimes I take a photo just to see if I can do it! This is a tiny red mite (Acari) with an even tinier prey on a fence post in the Oregon woods. The mite is about 500 across in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 2.0 mm, less than 1/10 inch. (See here for how I figured.) I don't know anything about mites except that they are sometimes predators. The one in my photo seems to be sucking an even tinier prey - maybe a Springtail (order Collembola) insect. Is this large for a mite? (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 24 November 2018)
ScientificName: Acari; Collembola
associatedTaxa: Acari; Collembola

File.Name: ic04d_ic01-066.jpg
Notes: 45459113604 Here are a pair of little Common Puffball mushrooms in the genus Lycoperdon growing in the Oregon woods. The one on the right might be the Dark Puffball (Lycoperdon foetidum) mushroom, with dark color against a yellowish flesh. The one on the left might be the Common Puffball (Lycoperdon perlatum), but I'm not sure. Both of these puffballs have a stem-like base and release their spores through an apical pore. Mushrooms were rather scarce during our time in Oregon, but I did find quite a few puffballs. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 25 November 2018)
CommonNames: Common Puffball; Dark Puffball; Common Puffball
