File.Name: ic06a_ic03-014.jpg
Notes:
45297809005 Trip photos are nice, but more recent photos from home are always the best!

Here is a little Crab Spider (Thomisidae) inside a seed capsule of native Southern Bush Monkey Flower (Mimulus aurantiacus var. pubescens, Phrymaceae). I suspect the spider is genus Xysticus or Ozyptila with those cryptic colors, and maybe a male. I've seen this before, e.g. in this photo and this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 3 December 2018)

I didn't post any photos yesterday because I've been sick. I'm feeling better now, at least not so listless. At least I've picked a good time to feel sick. We've two days of slow drizzly rain - only about 3/4 inch, but it's all soaked in. They're getting more rain in Los Angeles and Malibu where they don't need it. This is being a decent rain year so far, we're still on the sunny side of normal. There must be new mushrooms out there!
ScientificName: Thomisidae; Mimulus aurantiacus; pubescens; Phrymaceae; Xysticus; Ozyptila
associatedTaxa: Thomisidae; Mimulus aurantiacus; pubescens; Phrymaceae; Xysticus; Ozyptila

File.Name: ic06b_ic01-092.jpg
Notes: 32338364418 This is looking at the rear end of a Leaf-footed Bug (Coreidae, Heteroptera, Hemiptera) on the railing of our deck in the Oregon woods. It's named for the leaf-like protrusions on its hind legs. It might well be the Western Conifer Seed Bug (Leptoglossus occidentalis). You can see its very long proboscis folded under its body. I have another recent photo that shows the rest of this interesting bug in this photo. Hah, in this photo it looks like a cannon mounted on a castle wall - so HBBBT! (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 26 November 2018)
CommonNames: Leaf-footed Bug; Western Conifer Seed Bug
ScientificName: Coreidae; Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Leptoglossus occidentalis
associatedTaxa: Coreidae; Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Leptoglossus occidentalis

File.Name: ic06c_ic01-024.jpg
Notes: 32338169808 Here are swollen stems of a Witches' Broom gall on a shrub of Wild Huckleberry (Vaccinium ovatum, Ericaceae) near our cabin in the Oregon woods. Apparently, this abnormal growth is caused by the Huckleberry Broom Rust Fungus (Pucciniastrum goeppertianum). This condition is perennial, and infected stems die after the growing season. It's rather attractive, though not very good for the plant. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 24 November 2018)
CommonNames: Wild Huckleberry; Huckleberry Broom Rust Fungus
ScientificName: Vaccinium ovatum; Ericaceae; Pucciniastrum goeppertianum
associatedTaxa: Vaccinium ovatum; Ericaceae; Pucciniastrum goeppertianum

File.Name: ic06d_ic01-100.jpg
Notes: 32337877958 I took these mushrooms to be some kind of Chanterelle at first, growing on the end of a fallen log in the Oregon woods. They do have depressed funnel-shaped caps with forking decurrent gills like that genus, but I've never seen a Chanterelle with a fibrillose cap before. Now I believe this is a False Chanterelle (Hygrophoropsis aurantiaca). Maybe it's edible and maybe not - it's certainly not choice like its namesake. Hah, there's another odd little Black Cup Fungus just above the mushroom on the right that looks like a pitted olive, compare this photo. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 28 November 2018)
ScientificName: Hygrophoropsis aurantiaca
associatedTaxa: Hygrophoropsis aurantiaca

File.Name: ic07a_ic07-018.jpg
Notes:
32351743838 It's always fun to try to find the smallest mushrooms in the woods . . . these qualify, but I'm not even sure if they are fungi! I reckon they could also be sporangia of a Slime Mold (Myxogastria, Myxomycetes). They don't have gills, they have stems that look like they flowed into shape, there are many of them, and they are tough enough to stand up to my handling. I don't know what they are, but I suspect they are slime molds despite their mushroom-like shape - maybe Diderma floriforme. I found these on a dead grass stem, and another colony of them on a living ground-hugging plant of California Honeysuckle (Lonicera hispidula, Caprifoliaceae). I put my pen in the photo for scale, which has a 0.7 mm ball which is a bit smaller than the size of these whatever-they-are. If you want to be exact, the big one below the pen is I'll keep an eye on them to see what happens next - that should settle what they are. Please let me know if you recognize them. Ain't nature grand! (San Marcos Pass, 7 December 2018)

The sun was out today, though you'd hardly notice at our house on the shady north-facing side of the canyon where we basically loose the sun for the few weeks around the solstice. I got this photo above our retaining wall which puts the micro-world at eye level. I was feeling good enough to get outside today, but I'm still not all there. For some reason, the usual winds we get after a storm haven't happened. The woods are wet, and mushrooms and slime molds and seedlings are just starting to appear. This is no time to be sick!
ScientificName: Myxogastria; Myxomycetes; Diderma floriforme; Lonicera hispidula; Caprifoliaceae
associatedTaxa: Myxogastria; Myxomycetes; Diderma floriforme; Lonicera hispidula; Caprifoliaceae

File.Name: ic07b_ic01-135.jpg
Notes: 46223857231 This is "just a fly" in a conifer needle in the Oregon woods - but it's really not just a fly with those striking colors and features. I don't think I've ever seen a fly like it, but we don't usually notice the features of just a fly. I should add this to the Fly Day Friday group, but I've already posted my two shots for the week - but HFDF anyway! (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 27 November 2018)
ScientificName: just
associatedTaxa: just

File.Name: ic07c_ic01-096.jpg
Notes: 45499945674 I believe from the round head and flattened proboscis that this is a mosquito-sized Dance Fly in the family Empididae. It's on a leaf of Wild Huckleberry (Vaccinium ovatum, Ericaceae) near our cabin in the Oregon woods. I've gotten a lot of photos from this shrub, probably because it's where I take my morning cups of coffee when it's not raining, so I'm in no hurry and looking close. I find these flies at home too, either working the willow catkins by the creek, see this photo - or living as predators, see this photo. They are interesting flies, so HFDF! (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 26 November 2018)
ScientificName: Empididae; Vaccinium ovatum; Ericaceae
associatedTaxa: Empididae; Vaccinium ovatum; Ericaceae

File.Name: ic07d_ic01-052.jpg
Notes: 46172985222 I found this small fly on a leaf of Wild Huckleberry (Vaccinium ovatum, Ericaceae) near our cabin in the Oregon woods. It's not much larger than a mosquito, but I suspect it's some kind of Crane Fly (Tipulomorpha, Diptera) - just not one of the giant ones. Maybe it's a Winter Crane Fly in family Trichoceridae, though I can't see it my photo whether it has ocelli. It's an interesting fly and its shadow, so HFDF! (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 25 November 2018)
CommonNames: Wild Huckleberry; Crane Fly; Winter Crane Fly
ScientificName: Vaccinium ovatum; Ericaceae; Tipulomorpha; Diptera; Trichoceridae

File.Name: ic08a_ic08-015.jpg
Notes:
46237941181 I found this little Stink Bug (Pentatomidae, Heteroptera, Hemiptera) inside a seed capsule of native Late-flowered Mariposa Lily (Calochortus fimbriatus) in the Liliaceae plant family today. What's remarkable is that I found what looks like the same bug inside the same seed capsule a month ago before we went off to Oregon, see this photo! I can't guarantee that it's the same insect, but if it is I wonder it it will sit here all winter? It does look like a secure place to snuggle up for the long haul, with a food source right below in the form of seeds. Hah - like a dragon sitting on its hoard! One possibility for its identity is the Consperse Stink Bug (Euschistus conspersus). I'll check back on it again as winter progresses. (San Marcos Pass, 8 December 2018)

Today was clear and beautiful, with a bit of breeze to keep it interesting. I'm still not feeling quite all there, but I'm getting out.
CommonNames: Stink Bug; Late-flowered Mariposa Lily; Consperse Stink Bug
ScientificName: Pentatomidae; Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Calochortus fimbriatus; Liliaceae; Euschistus conspersus
associatedTaxa: Pentatomidae; Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Calochortus fimbriatus; Liliaceae; Euschistus conspersus

File.Name: ic08b_ic08-003.jpg
Notes: 46237646191 This is another shot of the tiny slime molds that I showed yesterday in this photo. It's pretty obvious now that they are in fact slime molds and not fungi, but at first I wasn't so sure. I apologize for the difference in white balance, it is what it is. These slime molds have an outer layer of lime (calcium carbonate) which is white and smooth at first, but here you can see it breaking up into fragments. They're small, only about 1.0 mm high and across. I believe the presence of lime indicates the order Physarales, and they are probably genus Didymium - thanks Paco! I'll check back on these tomorrow to see what happens next. (San Marcos Pass, 8 December 2018)
ScientificName: Physarales; Didymium
associatedTaxa: Physarales; Didymium

File.Name: ic08c_ic01-086.jpg
Notes: 46237247101 Here are a pair of pretty orange Millipedes (Diplopoda, Myriapoda) twining on and under old fungus and bark of a fallen tree in the Oregon woods. Hah, if you look close to the right of the lower one just below where they cross, you can see just a bit of a third millipede hiding under the bark! They are definitely millipedes with with two pairs of legs on each body segment, but they seem unusual with those smooth scales and bright colors. Looking at BugGuide, I believe they might be the species Octoglena anura. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 26 November 2018)
ScientificName: Diplopoda; Myriapoda; Octoglena anura
associatedTaxa: Diplopoda; Myriapoda; Octoglena anura

File.Name: ic08d_ic01-127.jpg
Notes: 46186334132 Here is a little snail shell that I found in the Oregon woods last month. Aside from its small size, it seems flatter than the usual snails I find in Oregon, compare this photo and this photo. I'm not sure what kind of snail it is, but HSS anyway! (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 26 November 2018)
ScientificName: NA
associatedTaxa: NA

File.Name: ic09a_ic09-032.jpg
Notes:
44435834430 Here is a mating swarm of winged (alate) Termites (order Isoptera) on a long-dead oak stump in the woods near home. The termites weren't taking wing, but they huddled in large groups and vibrated their wings like they were trying to warm up on a cold morning. This is one of the largest swarms I've seen, even larger than this one a few years ago. Rain yesterday, termites today - I guess this is how they synchronize their swarms with other termite colonies so that they'll all emerge at the same time, thereby increasing the chance of mating across swarms to form new colonies. Maybe they are Subterranean Termites (family Rhinotermitidae)? I don't believe these damage houses, at least I hope not. At a time when the news has articles like the "The Insect Apocalypse Is Here" in the New York Times recently, about how insect populations are declining world-wide, it's good to see a real mass of insects - even termites! (San Marcos Pass, 9 December 2018)

Today's photos are all things that the rain brings - from home, not Oregon. There was a chill in the air again, but what a nice day! There were enough high clouds to change white balance. There's been a rumor of a slight chance of rain Monday night, but it sounds like we'll probably only get wind.
ScientificName: Rhinotermitidae
associatedTaxa: NA

File.Name: ic09b_ic09-037.jpg
Notes: 45528899674 I found this California Banana Slug (Ariolimax californicus) in the woods today, the first one I've seen this year! Here it is squeezing under a low dead plant stem which might have slowed it down a bit - not that they are fast, but they move right along and are hard to photograph as a result. I've actually been worried about our slugs in this drought year, so it's good to see this one. Somehow the slugs manage to survive six or more months of hot and dry, and I sure don't know how! There seems to be some confusion about their proper scientific name, but the Oregon Banana Slugs (Ariolimax columbianus) look quite different, with greenish colors and a more pronounced ridge on their back, compare this photo. I found this one with new seedlings. Wikipedia reports that they are the second-largest species of terrestrial slug in the world, growing up to 10 inches long - but this one was smaller, only three or four inches long. I was thinking today how hard it is to see these slugs when there are fallen California Bay leaves on the ground, and I wonder if that's an adaptation? I regard these slugs as environmental indicators of the kind of place I want to live, and I love finding them near home. (San Marcos Pass, 9 December 2018)
ScientificName: Ariolimax californicus; Ariolimax columbianus
associatedTaxa: Ariolimax californicus; Ariolimax columbianus

File.Name: ic09c_ic09-007.jpg
Notes: 46201705752 This is a different slime mold that the one I first showed in this photo. The individual sporangia are only about half the size, and they are sessile, without the stalk of the other ones. They are growing on the cup-like underside of a fallen but still green leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). The while sporagia seem to be swimming in a pool of water. I first thought that was just old rain water, but now I wonder if could be the plasmodium that the sporangia come from? I'm not sure what this one is, but it's still developing, so I can wait and see. (San Marcos Pass, 9 December 2018)
ScientificName: Quercus agrifolia
associatedTaxa: NA

File.Name: ic09d_ic09-003.jpg
Notes: 44435209370 It's always fun to try to find the smallest mushrooms in the woods . . . and I'm sure this one really is a fungus, unlike the "shrooms" in this photo that turned out to be slime molds! I put my pen with a 0.7 mm ball in the photo for scale - about the same size as the mushroom cap. In another photo. I can see that it has white gills that are rather widely spaced. I guess that it's a Mycena mushroom, but it hardly matters. It's growing on an old fallen oak leaf. (San Marcos Pass, 9 December 2018)
ScientificName: Mycena
associatedTaxa: Mycena

File.Name: ic10a_ic10-020.jpg
Notes:
46217276742 This is another shot of our California Banana Slug (Ariolimax californicus) in the woods today, compare this photo from yesterday. I believe these are the same slugs, the only one I've seen in our mountains so far this year. I put the fallen leaf of California Bay (Umbellularia californica, Lauraceae) in the photo - I keep confusing the yellow leaves with the slugs, and I almost wonder if it's an adaptation. (The large slugs in Oregon past the range of California Bay are mostly green or brown, compare this photo.) I've been worried about our slugs in this drought year, so it's good to see this one. Somehow they survive six or more months of hot and dry, and I sure don't know how! It would be nice to find more than one though. (San Marcos Pass, 10 December 2018)

There was no rain today, but there were rain-like clouds moving in from the south, and there were moments when it sure felt like rain. It was calm and pleasant today, but the wind is expected to kick up tomorrow.
ScientificName: Ariolimax californicus; Umbellularia californica; Lauraceae
associatedTaxa: Ariolimax californicus; Umbellularia californica; Lauraceae

File.Name: ic10b_ic10-009.jpg
Notes: 31328703107 Here is today's photo of the large mating swarm of winged (alate) Termites (order Isoptera) on a long-dead oak stump in the woods near home - see this photo from yesterday. I don't know why the termites are still sitting on this stump. Usually they are in a hurry to get away as soon as they emerge from the ground, but these are huddled in large groups and vibrating their wings like they're trying to warm up on a cold morning. Well, it was a chilly morning today, in the low 40s anyway. Maybe these are Subterranean Termites (family Rhinotermitidae)? I don't believe they damage houses, at least I hope not. (San Marcos Pass, 10 December 2018)
ScientificName: Rhinotermitidae
associatedTaxa: NA

File.Name: ic10c_ic09-043.jpg
Notes: 46267347861 This is a small true bug (Heteroptera, Hemiptera) in an empty seed capsule of native Southern Bush Monkey Flower (Mimulus aurantiacus var. pubescens, Phrymaceae). If you look close, maybe you can see a few more nymphs of (probably) this bug on the different "shelves" inside the boat-like capsule. I don't know what kind of little bug this is, but it's rather pretty - and surprising to find in this place. I probably have a shot of their nymph in this photo. These seed capsules seem exposed to me, but they provide an attractive shelter to a number of different spiders and insects. Most recently, I found a little crab spider in one, see this photo. Try a search of my photos to see more. (San Marcos Pass, 9 December 2018)
CommonNames: true bug; Southern Bush Monkey Flower
ScientificName: Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Mimulus aurantiacus; pubescens; Phrymaceae
associatedTaxa: Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Mimulus aurantiacus; pubescens; Phrymaceae

File.Name: ic10d_ic09-023.jpg
Notes: 46216691692 Here is another batch of little slime molds on a dead plant stem, and the streaming plasmodial slime they arise from. I believe these are the same kind of slime molds that I've been showing, see this photo and this photo. I believe these are genus Didymium in the order Physarales as shown by a lime crust on the little mushroom-like sporandia. This new photo shows some of the streaming plasmodium that gives rise to the sporangia. I got this photo yesterday, and I was expecting to find lots of new sporangia this morning - but the colony seems to have dried out overnight, and there was nothing new. The same thing happened to the other slime mold I showed yesterday in this photo. Unfortunately, the woods are starting to dry out. (San Marcos Pass, 9 December 2018)
ScientificName: Didymium; Physarales; sporandia; plasmodium
associatedTaxa: Didymium; Physarales; sporandia; plasmodium

File.Name: ic11a_ic11-020.jpg
Notes:
31342653657 I believe this is a native terrestrial Land Snail (Gastropoda), probably a Shoulderband Snail, genus Helminthoglypta in the family Helminthoglytidae. I found it under loose bark on a log in a woodpile. The snail took off - moving at a "snail pace" of course, but still too fast for the low light at the time. I took a lot of photos, and this one is not too bad. This is a native snail and NOT one of the pesky Garden Snails that are so common in the lowlands. I often find their empty shells (like this), but I rarely find them alive and crawling. I believe they are mostly nocturnal and stay out of sight. (San Marcos Pass, 11 December 2018)

There were no more clouds today, but there were no big winds as expected either. We're still under a NWS Wind Advisory until noon tomorrow. The woods are starting to feel dry, so this may be the last day to photograph what the rain brings . . . at least until our next rain arrives. That may be as soon as Sunday night - but maybe not.
ScientificName: Gastropoda; Helminthoglypta; Helminthoglytidae
associatedTaxa: Gastropoda; Helminthoglypta; Helminthoglytidae

File.Name: ic11b_ic11-007.jpg
Notes: 32409668638 This is just part of a large fungus growing at the base of a dead oak tree, extending from the tree onto the ground nearby and engulfing leaves on its way. I guess this counts as a resupinate fungus since its lying flat on the ground, without any kind of cap. I'm don't know what kind of fungus this is. It's interesting that there is such a difference between new and old growth. I wonder how large it will grow? (San Marcos Pass, 11 December 2018)
ScientificName: resupinate fungus
associatedTaxa: resupinate fungus

File.Name: ic11c_ic11-003.jpg
Notes: 46281029601 Here is a group of tiny mushrooms growing from oak mulch, with my pen for scale. I put my pen with a 0.7 mm ball in the photo for scale - nearly the size of the mushroom caps. I suspect they are Mycena mushrooms, but it hardly matters. I have another shot with my pen for scale in this photo. The dry weather will finish off these mushrooms soon, if it hasn't already. (San Marcos Pass, 11 December 2018)
ScientificName: Mycena
associatedTaxa: Mycena

File.Name: ic11d_ic10-012.jpg
Notes: 31342062537 This is a group of mushrooms growing on and near a bit of dead wood in the oak mulch. Maybe the whitish mushrooms in the center on the wood are Phyllotopsis nidulans which is apparently sometimes called the the Mock Oyster or Orange Oyster. It's in the in the Tricholomataceae family despite having spores colored pink or apricot - you can see a spore deposit if you look close. These small mushrooms are said to has a strong unpleasant odor, but I guess I didn't get close enough to them to notice. I've been thinking uncritically that these are the same as the small white mushrooms that I usually find growing with mosses, see this photo, which I believe are Rimbachia bryophila. But no, there are differences, including the spore color. Flanking them on either side are Fairy Hair (Macrotyphula juncea) mushrooms, as thin as toothpicks. In some wet years we have great fruitings of these, but this year there are just a few - so far. There are also tiny fungi on he wood. It's a good thing I got this photo yesterday, as the mushrooms are shriveling up in the dry air today. (San Marcos Pass, 10 December 2018)
CommonNames: Mock Oyster; Orange Oyster; Fairy Hair
ScientificName: Phyllotopsis nidulans; Tricholomataceae; Rimbachia bryophila; Macrotyphula juncea
associatedTaxa: Phyllotopsis nidulans; Tricholomataceae; Rimbachia bryophila; Macrotyphula juncea

File.Name: ic12a_ic12-020.jpg
Notes:
45381545635 Well, blow me down - as Popeye used to say! I found this Tree Cricket (genus Oecanthus, Gryllidae, Orthoptera) hiding (in plain sight) today on the flower scar at the top of a ripening fruit of a Prickly Pear (Opuntia, Cactaceae) cactus in the garden today. It's a female with narrow wings. I've found these crickets in the cactus patch before, but never so late in the season - try a search on ["Tree Cricket" cactus] to see all my photos. As far as I know, the crickets are done for the year, and the last one I saw in the woods was a month ago, see this photo. This one is an anomaly. (San Marcos Pass, 12 December 2018)

The sun was out today, but it was still chilly and dark at home on the north side of the mountains where we loose the sun this close to the solstice. The winds that were expected last night never happened. The woods are starting to feel dry.
ScientificName: Oecanthus; Gryllidae; Orthoptera; Opuntia; Cactaceae
associatedTaxa: Oecanthus; Gryllidae; Orthoptera; Opuntia; Cactaceae

File.Name: ic12b_ic12-026.jpg
Notes: 44477082480 This is a neat plant of Bigelow's Spike-Moss (Selaginella bigelovii) growing in the woods. Dinosaurs stepped on primitive plants similar to this! It looks like a little conifer, like a Christmas tree, but it's really a fern - at least it's fern-like, a fern ally, because it produces spores rather than seeds. These plants are tough and should be growing in an exposed place like the bare sandstone outcrop in this photo. That's where this one was growing last year, but it slid down an embankment and ended up in this place at the base of the hill. I meant to transplant it to a more suitable place last year, but I never got around to it. Now almost a year later, I have a second chance - and so does the plant. (San Marcos Pass, 12 December 2018)
ScientificName: Selaginella bigelovii
associatedTaxa: Selaginella bigelovii

File.Name: ic12c_ic12-018.jpg
Notes: 46293911101 Here is a little Mycena mushroom growing in the oak mulch above our retaining wall. Hah, the wall puts the micro-world at eye level and saves my back. The mushrooms usually come in troops like in this photo, but we've only had a few solitary specimens this year. The woods are starting to dry out, and these early mushrooms are fading. Today was probably my last chance for such photos until we get our next rain. (San Marcos Pass, 12 December 2018)
ScientificName: Mycena
associatedTaxa: Mycena

File.Name: ic12d_ic11-012.jpg
Notes: 46243608832 This is a tiny Trashline Orb Weaver (Cyclosa, Araneidae) spider in the middle of its web. Even overexposed and at a distance like this, I can recognize these spiders by the hump on their abdomen, see this photo for a close shot. Spiders and webs have been scarce this week, but there are a few. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 11 December 2018)
ScientificName: Cyclosa
associatedTaxa: NA

File.Name: ic13a_ic13-019.jpg
Notes:
45394148985 This is a neat little Treehopper (family Membracidae) in the order Auchenorrhyncha, Hemiptera on a leaf of of native Wood Mint (Stachys bullata, Lamiaceae). I found it above the retaining wall in a place where the sun never shines this close to the solstice, and white balance is what it is. This one might be the Coast Live Oak Treehopper (Telamona vestita), but I really don't know - see BugGuide for more info. The thorax of these interesting insects is expanded to cover the abdomen like a shield. They have remarkable eyes, like all hoppers that have to see where they're hopping. I don't manage to photograph them very often - partly because they hop away as this one did a moment after I got the photo! (San Marcos Pass, 13 December 2018)

The sun was out again today. The woods are starting to dry out, though there's still lots of moisture in the ground after our recent rains. Our next chance of rain comes on Sunday night and Monday - with "rain likely" now in the forecast.
CommonNames: Treehopper; Wood Mint; Coast Live Oak Treehopper

File.Name: ic13b_ic13-023.jpg
Notes: 31367679777 I believe these dark-spored mushrooms are part of the Psathyrella longipes group. You can see a few white veil remnants on the margin of the caps, which is typical. They grow alone or in small groups on debris in the woods under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) trees. The dark spore-covered gills don't turn to ink like the genus Coprinus, and they don't grow on dung like the genus Panaeolus. The caps are "hygrophanous" - changing color as they dry out. They are attractive mushrooms albeit very fragile. (San Marcos Pass, 13 December 2018)

If you look close, you can see that a spider has incorporated the seed into the fine mesh of its webbing. (San Marcos Pass, 13 December 2018)

File.Name: ic13d_ic11-006.jpg
Notes: 46256324502 Here is another shot of the same Tree Cricket (genus Oecanthus, Gryllidae, Orthoptera) as my [Previous] photo (a href="https://www.flickr.com/photos/treebeard/45381545635/">here). Like yesterday's photo, the cricket is hiding (in plain sight) on the scar at the top of a ripening fruit of a Prickly Pear (Opuntia, Cactaceae) cactus in the garden. It's a female with narrow wings. I've found these crickets in the cactus patch before, but never so late in the season. I got this photo the day before yesterday's, and saved it for the Beautiful Bug Butt Thursday group, because here the cricket is turned around with it's butt up in the air. HBBBT! File.Name: ic14a_ic14-003.jpg
Notes:
45595705924 This little Assassin Bug in the Reduviidae family looks like it's rolled over to get its belly rubbed. It's actually deceased - or maybe just playing dead? I found it just like this on a leaf of Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus, Rosaceae) in the woods today. After I got this photo, I rolled it over to get a shot of its other side - it was soft and pliant. Dead or alive, it's an unusual pose showing its long proboscis folded under its head. I believe this is another Leafhopper Assassin Bug (Zelus renardii). I'll probably post a photo of its top-side tomorrow. (San Marcos Pass, 14 December 2018)

I had an errand in the Santa Ynez Valley today, but at least I got this photo before going and my [Next] photo on the way. It was overcast today, but no rain is expected. We might get a bit of rain on Sunday night and Monday. "Rain likely" is still in the forecast, but the NWS is backing off on the amount.

File.Name: ic14b_ic14-012.jpg
Notes:
31380052817 Here are tiny Liverwort (Marchantiophyta) plants growing with mosses on bare ground in once-burned chaparral near Lake Cachuma. I believe there are two different kinds of liverworts in this photo. The large colony in the center is the probably Hairy Crystalwort (Riccia trichocarpa). It's interesting how the "leaves" all seem to be growing from one central place - where the colony started? If you look close, you can also see a different liverwort with flatter darker-green leaves without hairs on either side of my photo. I'm not sure, but this may be a different Riccia species, maybe the Black Crystalwort (Riccia nigrella). I like these secretive little plants that hide in plain sight! (Lake Cachuma, Santa Ynez Valley, 14 December 2018)

(Speaking of hiding in plain sight, there's what looks like a tiny Whitefly just to the left of center. I'll make a note.)

File.Name: ic14c_ic13-031.jpg
Notes: 45595440964 This is a really tiny fly on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) tree in the woods. I believe it's a Frit Fly aka Grass Fly in the family Chloropidae - genus Thaumatomyia in particular, and maybe species Thaumatomyia rubida with those orange rather than yellow colors. Here it is grooming itself - doing some fly yoga with its hind leg! I've briefly seen these flies before, but I only have a few photos, most recently this one from October. This fly is really tiny, only about 1/10 inch long. It's probably the smallest fly I know that's genuinely pretty. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 13 December 2018)

File.Name: ic14d_ic12-043.jpg
Notes: 46318677001 The white specks are tiny Non-biting Midge flies, probably in the family Chironomidae of the order Diptera dancing in the sunbeams above the oak trees. I assume this is a mating thing. The flies are nearly invisible unless the sun is at just the right angle - close but not too close. They form strange columns above the trees that slowly move up and down. I'll keep trying to get a better photo, maybe one like this that shows the light trails of the tiny flies. I may have a shot of one of the tiny flies by itself in this photo. I'd rather have a photo showing the individual flies, but this will have to do for now - HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 12 December 2018)

File.Name: ic15a_ic15-009.jpg
Notes:
44515387410 This is a cute little Jumping Spider watching me from a browning leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods today. It was gazing at me intently from a foot away. There's something unnerving about making eye contact with such a tiny critter, but here it is. (San Marcos Pass, 15 December 2018)

There was no sign of an approaching storm today except a slight breeze from the south - but that is our "rain wind" and hopefully it will live up to its name. "Rain likely" is still in the forecast for Sunday night and Monday, and the NWS seems a little more optimistic about our chances now.

File.Name: ic15b_ic15-013.jpg
Notes: 32459799298 Here are two shots of a rather large mushroom in the woods today with yellowish colors - not as large as a dinner plate, but it is the size of a tea saucer. It's a Cortinarius mushroom growing under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) trees with a ground-hugging plant of California Honeysuckle (Lonicera hispidula, Caprifoliaceae). I'm sure it originally had a viscid cap and a swollen bulb at the base of the stem, but the cap has dried out and the stem fell apart as I dug it up. It does show the rusty-red remnants of the "cortina" hairs on he stem which originally form a veil that protects the gills - see this photo. Maybe this one is Cortinarius xanthodryophilus - see the species page and photos at Mykoweb. I've called this Cortinarius fulmineus in the past, but I reckon it's the same thing. Edibility is unknown. The problem is that all these Cortinarius mushrooms end up looking alike, so there's no good way to tell them apart. Mushrooms are still scarce in our woods, so I was surprised to find this robust specimen. (San Marcos Pass, 15 December 2018)
ScientificName: Cortinarius; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Lonicera hispidula; Caprifoliaceae; Cortinarius xanthodryophilus; Cortinarius fulmineus

File.Name: ic15c_ic15-002.jpg
Notes: 45608383154 This is a tiny Stilt Bug (Berytidae, Heteroptera, Hemiptera) on a still-flowering shrub of non-native hybrid Waverly Sage (Salvia x 'Waverly', Lamiaceae) in the garden this morning. You can see its proboscis folded under its head. It doesn't look very different from the Assassin Bug in my [Previous] photo, but these bugs use their proboscis to suck plants and not other insects as predators. I got this photo in a place where the sun never shines this close to the solstice, and white balance is what it is. The flowers don't seem to mind either the lack of direct sunlight either. (San Marcos Pass, 15 December 2018)

Notes: 45419052015 I went back to the little Assassin Bug in the Reduviidae family that I showed in my [Previous] photo, and found it on the same leaf of Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus, Rosaceae) where I left it. I already flipped it right-side-up yesterday, and I got this fresh photo today. I'm pretty sure it's deceased - if you look close, you can see spider webs draped beyween its legs. It's dead, but it looks alive. It's a nice view of its once-mighty proboscis. (San Marcos Pass, 15 December 2018)

File.Name: ic16a_ic16-013.jpg
Notes:
46296648162 This is a little wild bee (Anthophila, Hymenoptera) working new flowers of Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca, Ericaceae). I'm pretty sure it's the same kind of bee as my [Previous] photo on the right, quite hairy with a yellow patch on its forehead and rather long antennae. Maybe it's genus Andrena, but I really don't know. Quite a few of these dark bees were working the new flowers. If you look close, it looks like that open flower on the right has been chewed open - see my [Previous] photo on the right. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 16 December 2018)

These manzanita flowers have been going since early November, though they are just now really coming into their own and attracting pollinators. I also noticed hummingbirds working the new flowers, like this one a couple of years ago. It was quite breezy today with wind from the south, and there were some clouds moving in. The breeze was frustrating for photos, but I hope it's an early sign of our incoming storm - we still have "rain likely" later tonight and Monday morning. I hope it happens - the woods are starting to dry out, and warmer weather is in the forecast for next week.

associatedTaxa: Anthophila; Hymenoptera; Arctostaphylos glauca; Ericaceae; Andrena

Notes: 45623537004 Here are a pair of different wild bees (Anthophila, Hymenoptera) on new flowers of Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca, Ericaceae). I'm not sure what either of them is, but I suspect at least one of them is genus Andrena. Hah, the bee on the right is chewing an opening into a still-closed flower! (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 16 December 2018)

File.Name: ic16c_ic16-043.jpg
Notes: 31407373857 Here is a tiny caterpillar on unopened flowers of Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca, Ericaceae). By the placement of its legs, I guess the caterpillar might be a looper or inchworm caterpillar of a Geometrid Moth (family Geometridae, Lepidoptera). It's got a couple of extra flaps on its tail end - to the left. The blushing-pink flowers are still closed by a flap of skin at the bottom, but some insects can't wait for them to open - see my [Next] photo, on the right. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 16 December 2018)

File.Name: ic16d_ic16-006.jpg
Notes: 45623247314 This is Eastwood Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glandulosa ssp. mollis, Ericaceae) with an infestation of aphid galls. It's our other manzanita species, different from the Bigberry Manzanita (A. glauca) in my other photos today. I also find similar galls earlier in the year on that shrub, see this photo. For some time I've thought that the red tumor-like growths on this shrub were caused by the Manzanita Leaf Fungus (Exobasidium vacinii) that looks very similar, but I finally opened one up and sure 'nuff there were aphids inside, see this photo. Maybe the galls on both Manzanitas are caused by the same Manzanita Leaf Gall Aphids (Tamalia coweni, Aphididae), but maybe they are something different. And what about the fungus that looks so similar - is there a connection? (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 16 December 2018)

File.Name: ic17a_ic17-011.jpg
Notes:
45449205195 This is a hand-sized California Banana Slug (Ariolimax californicus) munching a seedling of non-native Chickweed (Stellaria media, Caryophyllaceae) in the woods today. Slugs have teeth?? Well, sort of. It does have tooth-like soft mouthparts to help it munch the leaves and mushrooms that it consumes. (They also consume certain unmentionables, see this photo.) They remind me of Dr. Zoidberg from Futurama! I found this one along the road in our canyon today on a day of "banana slug weather". These large slugs are 6-8 inches long. They are something that the rain brings, and it's a mystery to me how they survive our hot dry summers. I regard them as "environmental indicators" of the kind of place I want to live, and I love finding them near home. (San Marcos Pass, 17 December 2018)

We didn't get a lot of rain from our storm last night, but it drizzled all morning - perfect weather for these large terrestrial mollusks. I saw three of them today along the road in our canyon. We ended up with about 2/10 inch of rain, bringing our total to 7.5 inches for the rain year - still on the happy side of normal, but barely. Now it sounds like we're going to dry out for a week or more. The good news is that there's been little wind so far after this storm.
ScientificName: Ariolimax californicus; Stellaria media; Caryophyllaceae
associatedTaxa: Ariolimax californicus; Stellaria media; Caryophyllaceae

File.Name: ic17b_ic17-016.jpg
Notes: 46310567122 We're having Banana Slug weather! We didn't get a lot of rain from our storm last night, but it drizzled all morning - perfect weather for these large terrestrial mollusks. I saw three of them today along the road in our canyon. I assume that this one is a California Banana Slug (Ariolimax californicus) which seems different from the Oregon Banana Slugs (A. columbianus) like the one in this photo. This one was in a hurry to get somewhere - sure, they move at a "snail pace" but still pretty fast for the low light in the kind of weather they prefer. You can clearly see it's "pneumostome" - that hole in its side - which leads to its single lung. They only have this hole on the slug-right side. These large slugs are 6-8 inches long. I regard them as "environmental indicators" of the kind of place I want to live, and I love finding them near home. (San Marcos Pass, 17 December 2018)
ScientificName: Ariolimax californicus; A. columbianus
associatedTaxa: Ariolimax californicus; A. columbianus

File.Name: ic17c_ic17-004.jpg
Notes: 44544639950 Here is a little Mycena mushroom growing in the oak mulch above our retaining wall - with a perfect raindrop balanced on its cap! What are the odds? (San Marcos Pass, 17 December 2018)
ScientificName: Mycena
associatedTaxa: Mycena

File.Name: ic17d_ic17-031.jpg
Notes: 46361201771 This is an odd galled flower of native California Coffeeberry (Rhamnus californica aka Frangula californica, Rhamnaceae). That odd "crook in the neck" is typical. I'm pretty sure this is the gall of a Gall Midge (Cecidomyiidae, Diptera), see this photo of an emerging adult - at least the husk of one. I've seen these galls before at this time of year, see this photo from last January. But there's a mystery here. These shrubs flower in June, so why do I find the galls in mid-winter? I guess the answer is that the galls stay on the shrub until the next flowering time so that the cycle can repeat, and they're only now starting to develop. (San Marcos Pass, 17 December 2018)
ScientificName: Rhamnus californica; Frangula californica
associatedTaxa: Cecidomyiidae

File.Name: ic18a_ic18-033.jpg
Notes:
32503442198 I believe this is a tiny Mycena acicula (or kin) mushroom growing from oak leaf mulch. It's probably growing from buried wood, but they have remarkably long stems. I included my pen with a 0.7 mm ball, so the caps are only a few millimeters across. This is the smallest beautiful mushroom that I actually have a name for - the reddish caps and yellowish stems are quite handsome. I have more photos here and here. You have to get down on your hands and knees to see this little mushroom - or have a retaining wall at a comfortable height for photography, lucky me and lucky for my back and knees! (San Marcos Pass, 18 December 2018)

We're under a NWS Wind Advisory today, but it was dead calm this morning and not too bad after that. It sounds like it will be dry for the next week, but there is a "slight chance" for rain on Christmas Day. Rain for both Thanksgiving and Christmas - I like that!
ScientificName: Mycena acicula; beautiful
associatedTaxa: Mycena acicula; beautiful

File.Name: ic18b_ic18-011.jpg
Notes: 32503211618 These are tiny sporangia of slime molds on a green leaf of California Honeysuckle (Lonicera hispidula, Caprifoliaceae). They are covered with raindrops like jeweled bonnets. These are the same kind of slime molds that I showed a couple of weeks ago in this photo and this photo and this photo. I believe they are genus Didymium in the order Physarales which contain lime crystals in their caps. Maybe that's why they're so tough - these are almost two weeks old, unlike some species that disappear overnight. Is it unusual that they are on living leaves and not dead stems? (San Marcos Pass, 18 December 2018)
ScientificName: Lonicera hispidula; Caprifoliaceae; Didymium; Physarales
associatedTaxa: Lonicera hispidula; Caprifoliaceae; Didymium; Physarales

File.Name: ic18c_ic18-014.jpg
Notes: 44558121370 Here is a mushroom with a fungus infection. This no doubt started as a Bolete mushroom, probably the Oak-loving Bolete (Boletus dryophilus aka Xerocomellus dryophilus). It has become unrecognizable as it has been engulfed by the "Bolete Eater" (Hypomyces chrysospermus) fungus that is particularly attracted to these mushrooms. The first stage of an infection is white mold as in my photo. This is followed by a yellow crust which turns into a final reddish-brown final stage which is visible in this photo. The funny thing is that I haven't found any uninfected Bolete mushrooms in the woods yet this year - the Bolete Eater found this one first! (San Marcos Pass, 18 December 2018)
ScientificName: Boletus dryophilus; Xerocomellus dryophilus; Hypomyces chrysospermus
associatedTaxa: Boletus dryophilus; Xerocomellus dryophilus; Hypomyces chrysospermus

File.Name: ic18d_ic17-027.jpg
Notes: 32502784848 These mushrooms have me puzzled. They look just like the beautiful but deadly poisonous Destroying Angel (Amanita ocreata) mushrooms - EXCEPT that they seem to have no "death cup" or volva at the base of the stem, compare this photo and this photo. I was careful, and I'm pretty sure I didn't leave it in the ground. You can even see a little "button mushroom" at the base of the pair I dug up that has no sign of a universal veil. On the other hand, they are pure white, with a smooth cap, free gills, no striations on the cap margin, and a skirt-like ring on the stem. I found them growing under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) near the Banana Slugs I showed yesterday, but they were also on the edge of a large pile of wood chips that's a few years old. I'm not sure if these are the Destroying Angel because they have no volva, but I sure wouldn't want to eat one for dinner! Any help with the ID of these mushrooms will be much appreciated. (San Marcos Pass, 17 December 2018)
ScientificName: Amanita ocreata; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae
associatedTaxa: Amanita ocreata; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae

File.Name: ic19a_ic19-004.jpg
Notes:
46388145941 I believe this is the Powdered Orange Bush Lichen (Teloschistes flavicans). It resembles the more common Golden-Eye Lichens (Teloschistes chrysophthalmus) in this photo, but it lacks the ciliate apothecia - those small orange disks which are the fruiting bodies of the fungus part of the lichen. I found this one on a dead stem of native Chamise (Adenostoma fasciculatum, Rosaceae) growing with other lichens. This one is not common in our mountains, and I've only found it in one other place, see this photo. It really looks like something that belongs in the ocean! To be honest, I could have gotten this photo at pretty much any time in the past year, these lichens don't change much even when they dry out - compare this photo from a year ago that is very similar. (San Marcos Pass, 19 December 2018)

Today was mostly sunny and quite calm. It sounds like it will be dry for the next week, but there is a "slight chance" for rain on Christmas Day. Rain for both Thanksgiving and Christmas holidays - I like that!

File.Name: ic19b_ic19-002.jpg
Notes: 32515934068 I found these Agaricus mushrooms growing in the oak mulch on the edge of our garden today. The cracked pattern on the cap is handsome though not really typical. I don't know what kind they are, and I find our woodland Agaricus mushrooms to be a confusing group at best. Mushrooms are still scarce in the woods, and I'm not sure why - we have had decent rain so far this year for a change. (San Marcos Pass, 19 December 2018)

Notes: 45475421635 Here is the web of a tiny Orb Web Weaver (family Araneidae) spider on an old seed head of native Black Sage (Salvia mellifera, Lamiaceae). If you look close, you can see the spider in the lower-left of the web. It might actually be a Labyrinth Spider (genus Metepeira, Araneidae), see this photo from the same shrub a month ago. Spiders and webs have been scarce after recent rains, but there are a few. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 19 December 2018)

Notes: 46387687791 This tiny critter is as small as some of the mushrooms and slime molds I've been showing lately! I believe it's a Slender Springtail in the family Entomobryidae of the class Collembola - possibly genus Entomobrya. It's so tiny that this is a 1:1 800x1066 crop from my 6000x4000 original photo taken as a 1:1 macro shot - about 450 pixels long, which comes to about comes to about 1.7 mm, about 1/16 inch. (See here for how I figured.) It's on a green leaf of California Honeysuckle (Lonicera hispidula, Caprifoliaceae), and it didn't hop away from me. It's kind of cute for being so tiny! (San Marcos Pass, 18 December 2018)

File.Name: ic20a_ic20-029.jpg
Notes:
44583726360 This is another speckled gall of the Beaked Twig Gall Wasp (Disholcaspis plumbella) in the family Cynipidae, Hymenoptera growing on Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia, Fagaceae). These galls are monothalamous which means they contain just one wasp larva,and I believe there is just a single generation. The pointed beak is distinctive. If you look close, you can see an exit hole on the right side where the tiny wasp got out to complete its destiny. Or did another predator get inside?? I suspect it really is an exit hole since November/December is when the adults are said to emerge. I've only seen a few of these galls this year. I don't think of these galls as plant pests - they are more like decorations! (San Marcos Pass, 20 December 2018)

(I just realized after posting that this is probably the same gall I show in this photo taken on November 5. Well, it's a nice one! Note how the leaves are turning yellow in this photo compared to the other. Scrub oaks aren't properly deciduous, but they are considered semi-deciduous - they pretty much drop all their old leaves at once and get new ones at the same time. This shrub is heading in that direction.)

The woods feel like they're drying out already after our small rain a few days ago - the ground is damp, but the air feels dry. We've fallen below normal rainfall for the first time in a while - though 97% of normal to date is not bad! Winter officially starts tomorrow with the winter solstice. There is still a "slight chance" for rain on Christmas Day, and I hope it happens.
Locality: I just realized after posting that this is probably the same gall I show in this photo taken on November 5. Well

Notes: 31461121347 Here are the first new flowers of native Veatch Silk-Tassel (Garrya veatchii) in the Garryaceae plant family. They are nearly open but not quite - their stamens are starting to poke out of their coverings. These shrubs are dioecious with separate male and female plants, and this photo shows a male, pollen-bearing, staminate plant. Both male and female flowers are organized into catkins. They are handsome and unusual when in full flower like this photo from last year shows. (San Marcos Pass, 20 December 2018)

File.Name: ic20c_ic20-045.jpg
Notes: 32528376858 Here are cheerful new flowers of native Holly-leaved Cherry (Prunus ilicifolia) in the Rosaceae plant family. These early-bloomers have actually been flowering since early October near Arroyo Burro, though those plants are highly unusual. I guess they are starting to flower for real now. This is my first photo of them for the year, taken along West Camino Cielo today. They are still plenty early, before the solstice! (San Marcos Pass, 20 December 2018)

File.Name: ic20d_ic16-001.jpg
Notes: 32528287528 This is a little wild bee (Anthophila, Hymenoptera) working new flowers of Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca, Ericaceae). I suspect the bee is genus Andrena, see this photo and the one [Previous] to that for more similar bees from the same day. I like the way this bee is sticking its bee-hind up in the air, so HBBBT! File.Name: ic21a_ic21-037.jpg
Notes:
32541453908 Here is a garland of flowers for the winter solstice! The fresh-looking flowers belong to native California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum, Polygonaceae). All the other flowers of these shrubs are long gone, but this one cluster has persevered. I figure that deserves a photo. (San Marcos Pass, 21 December 2018)

The winter solstice happened today at 2:23 p.m. PST. It's the official first day of winter, the "dark night of the year" in the Northern Hemisphere. The days will start getting just a bit longer tomorrow, and the sun will rise just a bit higher in the sky tomorrow. It's a time of new hope for the future. Happy solstice everyone!

associatedTaxa: NA

File.Name: ic21b_ic21-026.jpg
Notes: 44596369530 This is the tiny nymph of a Planthopper (Fulgoroidea, Auchenorrhyncha, Hemiptera) perched on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). It's only about 1/8 inch long. I took a few photos of this critter and glanced at my camera display and realized that my exposure was way too low, but the little hopper got away from me as I fixed it. I did what I could to make the photo presentable. I think it's still a nice shot of this unusually dark planthopper, see this photo of what might be the same kind taken about twenty feet away from this one. I believe that it's a Tropiduchid Planthopper in the family Tropiduchidae, probably genus Danepteryx. It's cute - with a front end that looks like the grill of a truck and a hind end that looks like jet exhaust! (San Marcos Pass, 21 December 2018)
CommonNames: Planthopper; Coast Live Oak; Tropiduchid Planthopper
ScientificName: Fulgoroidea; Auchenorrhyncha; Hemiptera; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Tropiduchidae; Danepteryx

File.Name: ic21c_ic21-033.jpg
Notes: 46362288302 Here is another Flesh Fly in the family Sarcophagidae on a sandstone outcrop that is covered with little liverwort (Marchantiophyta) plants. I'm pretty sure the liverwort is the Orobus-seed Liverwort (Targionia hypophylla). It's a remarkable plant that quickly goes from green to dormant and back again as the weather changes, see this photo. I didn't notice at the time, but it appears to be developing reproductive structures. I'll have to get back for a closer shot to examine them. The fly has the combination of reddish eyes and red-tipped tail that seems to be a pretty good field mark for this type. This isn't the first fly I've found on the liverworts - see this photo. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 21 December 2018)
ScientificName: Sarcophagidae; Marchantiophyta; Targionia hypophylla
associatedTaxa: Sarcophagidae; Marchantiophyta; Targionia hypophylla

File.Name: ic21d_ic15-004.jpg
Notes: 44596091350 This isn't quite "just a fly" (Diptera) with those golden hairs on its thorax. It's perched for a moment on a sandstone outcrop with moss brought out by recent rains. I'm not sure, but the fly might be a Cluster Fly in the genus Pollenia, Calliphoridae. If I'm right, they usually lose their fur as they age, compare this photo. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 15 December 2018)

File.Name: ic22a_ic22-022.jpg
Notes:
46375077122 This is a female Anna's Hummingbird (Calypte anna) getting nectar from a new flower of native Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca) in the Ericaceae plant family. I agree, the flowers are overexposed - but who cares! Each of these early-flowering shrubs seems to have a resident hummingbird or two - and they seem to do raids on each other's shrubs. Manzanita is not a classic hummingbird flower with a long red floral tube, but it's one of the first flowers to appear in autumn, and a bridge between summer and spring for the hummers. I reckon they are important for keeping the hummingbirds alive during this harsh time of year. There is a bit of pollen stuck to the hummer's bill, so I believe they are pollinators in return. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 22 December 2018)

I was hoping for more photos of wild bees like I found in this place a week ago, see this photo. I saw them, but I had no luck getting photos. I also saw a Painted Lady (Vanessa cardui) butterfly on the flowers, the first I've seen all year - but no photo. There was a breeze today that got in the way, but this photo of the hummingbird makes up for it!
CommonNames: Anna's Hummingbird; Bigberry Manzanita; Painted Lady
ScientificName: Calypte anna; Arctostaphylos glauca; Ericaceae; Vanessa cardui
associatedTaxa: Calypte anna; Arctostaphylos glauca; Ericaceae; Vanessa cardui

File.Name: ic22b_ic22-025.jpg
Notes: 46374896012 These are tiny thrips (order Thysanoptera) insects on an unopened flower of native Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca) in the Ericaceae plant family. I believe they are Predatory Thrips in the genus Aeolothrips of the family Aeolothripidae, see this photo for a whole lot of them a few years ago. They're not so common this year, but maybe more are still coming. These critters are tiny, about 425 pixels across in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 1.7 mm, about 1/16 inch (See here for how I figured.) They seem to spend most of their time chasing each other away - like little roosters in the chicken yard. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 22 December 2018)
ScientificName: Thysanoptera; Arctostaphylos glauca; Ericaceae; Aeolothrips; Aeolothripidae
associatedTaxa: Thysanoptera; Arctostaphylos glauca; Ericaceae; Aeolothrips; Aeolothripidae

File.Name: ic22c_ic22-002.jpg
Notes: 44608614260 I found this mysterious orb in an old flower cluster of native California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum, Polygonaceae). It's small, about 630 pixels across in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 2.5 mm, less than 1/8 inch (See here for how I figured.) Maybe it's an egg or a pupal case? It looks like its wrapped in silk, so maybe it's the work of a spider? It reminds me of craft projects I've seen with string or paper mache wrapped around a balloon with glue, then the balloon is popped after the glue dries - like this. I wonder how it was made without a balloon?? I love finding these little mysteries! (San Marcos Pass, 22 December 2018)
ScientificName: Eriogonum fasciculatum
associatedTaxa: NA

File.Name: ic22d_ic22-030.jpg
Notes: 44608477500 Here are the first flowers of Woolly Paintbrush (Castilleja foliolosa) in the Orobanchaceae plant family that I've seen this year, but they've obviously already been flowering for a while. I first found them last year on February 18, and before that on November 16, so they are quite variable. This is a small woody sub-shrub, usually just a foot high or less, that grows in dry rocky areas in the mountains. This one was growing up a steep embankment so I couldn't get any closer. I assume there are real tubular flowers on it, but it's the brilliant red bracts that first catch your eye. It's a time of year when I can find old summer flowers like the buckwheat in my [Previous] photo as well as new flowers like this. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 22 December 2018)
ScientificName: Castilleja foliolosa; Orobanchaceae
associatedTaxa: Castilleja foliolosa; Orobanchaceae

File.Name: ic23a_ic23-019.jpg
Notes:
45716236254 This is a California Tortoiseshell (Nymphalis californica) butterfly perched for just a moment on a mossy sandstone outcrop in the canyon today. These butterflies overwinter as adults and are active on warm winter days like we've been having lately, though this is the first one I've seen for some time. I don't usually find these at a other time of year, but there's always an exception like this fine specimen from last June. I also found a horde of caterpillars on a Ceanothus shrub a couple of years ago that are probably these, see this photo. Other butterflies that overwinter as adults are the Mourning Cloak (here), Red Admiral (here), and the California Dogface (here). It's always a treat to find one of these butterflies on a winter day. (San Marcos Pass, 23 December 2018)

The NWS has bumped up our chance of rain to 50% on Christmas Eve - but even so, it sounds like we'll get more wind than rain from this small storm. I'm hoping that Santa will surprise us and bring a wet Christmas!
ScientificName: Nymphalis californica
associatedTaxa: Nymphalis californica

File.Name: ic23b_ic23-003.jpg
Notes: 31499720777 Here is a tiny Tick (Ixodoidea, Arachnida) waiting on the end of an old grass stem. Hah - it's waiting for you or me to walk by so it can fall away and attach itself! It's probably a Western Black-legged Tick (Ixodes pacificus), which is the principal vector of Lyme Disease on the West Coast. This one really is tiny, about 790 pixels across in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 3.1 mm, about 1/8 inch. (See here for how I figured.) I only noticed it because I was looking for spiders on a sandstone outcrop - that nice neutral color in he background. I did an abrupt shift of focus and saw the tick right before my eyes. *Sigh* I've already been ticked at least once this year - I still feel it on my side. What, me worry? (San Marcos Pass, 23 December 2018)
ScientificName: Ixodoidea; Arachnida; Ixodes pacificus; What, me worry?
associatedTaxa: Ixodoidea; Arachnida; Ixodes pacificus; What, me worry?

File.Name: ic23c_ic22-018.jpg
Notes: 31499489187 These appear to be undescribed galls on a flowering branch of native Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca) in the Ericaceae plant family. There are brown leafy ones on the left, smaller brown ones that might be the same in the upper-right, and the swollen stem of the flowers in the lower-right. None of these is described in Ron Russo's fine "Field Guide to Plant Galls" (UC Press, 2006). Based on appearance, I suspect that all three are the work of Gall Midges (Cecidomyiidae, Diptera) - but I don't really know. To get an idea on the diversity of their galls, try a Flickr search on "gall midge Cecidomyiidae". I'll keep an eye on these. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 22 December 2018)
ScientificName: Arctostaphylos glauca; Ericaceae; Cecidomyiidae; Diptera
associatedTaxa: Arctostaphylos glauca; Ericaceae; Cecidomyiidae; Diptera

File.Name: ic24a_ic24-024.jpg
Notes:
44635785410 Here are blushing pink flowers of native Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca) in the Ericaceae plant family. Usually the new flowers are pure white, but occasional shrubs are more interesting like this. I guess its just individual variation. If you look close, maybe you can spot a little wild bee down and left of center. It's the same kind of bee I show in this photo, probably genus Andrena. It was too windy today to even try to get a closer shot of the bee, and I was busy trying to get photos appropriate to the holiday anyway. I think I did! (San Marcos Pass, 24 December 2018)

I wish you all the best for the holidays,
Peace on earth, goodwill to all.

File.Name: ic24b_ic24-016.jpg
Notes: 45729349124 Here are ripe Christmas berries on native Toyon aka California Holly (as in Hollywood), Heteromeles arbutifolia in the Rosaceae plant family. The ripe berries are edible, but they are quite astringent (unless you're a bird!) and need cooking. They are beautiful and appropriate for the holidays. They seem to be having a good year, despite the ongoing drought conditions. (San Marcos Pass, 24 December 2018)

File.Name: ic24c_ic24-012.jpg
Notes: 31512746947 These are ripe berries at the top of a native Madrone (Arbutus menziesii, Ericaceae) tree in the woods. Unfortunately, the berries are all too high in the trees this year to get a closer shot. The berries are edible, though a bit mealy, and they have an interesting bumpy texture, see this photo. Many trees don't have berries in this drought year, and I hope there will be enough for the Band-Tailed Pigeons (Columba fasciata) who seem particularly fond of them, see my Christmas photo from a few years ago. Yes, this tree goes look a bit ratty - it is a drought year. I have been seeing our winter flock of the pigeons lately in our oak trees. Compare my [Next] photo of native Toyon berries - both could be called Christmas berries, though the Toyon is a deeper color. (San Marcos Pass, 24 December 2018)

File.Name: ic24d_ic24-005a.jpg
Notes: 44635465990 Here are pretty new flowers of native Chaparral Currant (Ribes malvaceum) in the Grossulariaceae plant family. These were the first flowers of any native plant this year, starting to flower by October 5 as shown in this photo of a different plant. These later flowers have more color, though maybe that's just individual variation. In some years I've found the flowers as early as the autumnal equinox in September - see this photo from 2013. File.Name: ic25a_ic25-009.jpg
Notes:
45552778405 This is a tiny Sheet Web Weaver spider in the family Linyphiidae hanging under its web. I believe it's a Bowl and Doily Spider (Frontinella communisaka Frontinella pyramitela), though its web was not so well-defined as some I've seen, like this one. The spider is tiny, about 800 pixels across in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 3.1 mm, about 1/8 inch. (See here for how I figured.) The spider was in a shrub of Eastwood Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glandulosa ssp. mollis, Ericaceae). It was really too windy today for this photo, but here it is. (San Marcos Pass, 25 December 2018)

Today was even windier than yesterday, and that's saying something! We've had 30-40 mph gusts since yesterday, and we're under a NWS High Wind Warning tonight. With so much wind, I wasn't expecting much rain, but we actually got 4/10 inch early this morning from our Christmas storm. Thanks Santa!

File.Name: ic25b_ic25-017.jpg
Notes: 46465372931 I believe this is the pupal cocoon of a Western Tussock Moth (Orgyia vetusta, Lymantriidae, Lepidoptera) on a stem of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). The cocoon feels rough, like excelsior - apparently the caterpillars pluck the hairs from their hairy bodies to make them. There's an excellent photo essay on "Life History of Western Tussock Moths, Orgyia vetusta" by Joyce Gross here. I'm not sure what this one is doing out now, as I usually find the caterpillars in late spring. Does it contain eggs? I guess it's been here for a while, and I just happened to notice it today. I'm also not sure the that white fuzz is at the top of the cocoon. At first I thought it might be the remains of a female moth, see this photo. Looking again, it looks more like the work of Woolly Oak Aphids - see this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 25 December 2018)
CommonNames: Western Tussock Moth; Coast Live Oak; Woolly Oak Aphids
ScientificName: Orgyia vetusta; Lymantriidae; Lepidoptera; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae
associatedTaxa: Orgyia vetusta; Lymantriidae; Lepidoptera; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae

File.Name: ic25c_ic25-016.jpg
Notes: 46465217951 Rain last night and new mushrooms in the woods today? It must be a coincidence, but here they are. I believe these are "Poison Pie" (Hebeloma crustuliniforme) mushrooms growing under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). They have brown spores and viscid caps when fresh like this. The slime dries out in time, and I usually find them covered with debris and tiny Springtail (order Collembola) insects that get stuck in the slime - or are they feeding on it? I can see some on the upper cap, but they are probably too small to show up here. I consider this a field mark! See this photo for another shot showing the springtails. Like the common name of this mushroom suggests, don't eat it. They are said to cause severe gastrointestinal distress. I remember these being common, but seven years of drought has taken a toll. I've only seen a few in recent years. (San Marcos Pass, 25 December 2018)
CommonNames: Poison Pie; Coast Live Oak; Springtail
ScientificName: Hebeloma crustuliniforme; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Collembola
associatedTaxa: Hebeloma crustuliniforme; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Collembola

File.Name: ic25d_ic23-005.jpg
Notes: 44648036670 This is a little beetle (Coleoptera) that had fallen prey to an entomopathogenic fungus - a fungus that can act like a parasite that kills its host. This fungus looks like Beauveria bassiana which is the asexual form of an uncommmon Cordyceps fungus. If this is what it is, it has some use as a biological control for a number of insect pests, see Wikipedia for more info. I'm not sure what the beetle is. It looks like a Junebug (Melolonthinae, Scarabaeidae), but it seems the wrong time of year for them. (San Marcos Pass, 23 December 2018)
ScientificName: Coleoptera; entomopathogenic fungus; Beauveria bassiana; Cordyceps; Melolonthinae; Scarabaeidae
associatedTaxa: Coleoptera; entomopathogenic fungus; Beauveria bassiana; Cordyceps; Melolonthinae; Scarabaeidae

File.Name: ic26a_ic26-005.jpg
Notes:
46428608202 Here is another hand-sized California Banana Slug (Ariolimax californicus) making its way through the woods. These large slugs are 6-8 inches long. They are something that the rain brings, and they seem to disappear between storms. I don't know where they hide, and it's a mystery to me how they survive our hot dry summers. Do they dehydrate into almost nothing like pipe cleaners, or do they find a burrow and go into a big sleep? I suspect the latter, though I have yet to find one in the dry time of the year. I regard these slugs as "environmental indicators" of the kind of place I want to live, and I love finding them near home. I want to photograph every one I find. There's also another "mystery orb" in this photo, right above the back edge of the slug's mantle. Is it an egg? I'll make a note. (San Marcos Pass, 26 December 2018)

There was some residual wind today after our wind event yesterday, and our NWS High Wind advisory has been extended until early tomorrow morning. It sounds like tomorrow will be windy as well, and that last little rain will have to last us for a while. As the NWS Forecast Discussion has it: "No sign of any precip for this area through the end of next week at least.
ScientificName: Ariolimax californicus
associatedTaxa: Ariolimax californicus

File.Name: ic26b_ic26-009.jpg
Notes: 44662690760 I believe these are the mushrooms formally known as the Oak-Loving Collybia (Collybia dryophila), now more properly called Gymnopus dryophilus - though I suspect the common name will stick. They are growing in an unlikely place, with weedy grass instead of under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) like their name suggests. There used to be an oak tree here, but it came down a couple of years ago - so maybe the mushrooms made do? These mushrooms also typically have copious white mycelium with root-like rhizomorphs at the base, though these mushrooms seem to have none. Maybe that is a consequence of not growing in oak mulch - or maybe I'm wrong about what they are? Some mushrooms are starting to appear, but they are taking their time. (San Marcos Pass, 26 December 2018)
ScientificName: Collybia dryophila; Gymnopus dryophilus; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae
associatedTaxa: Collybia dryophila; Gymnopus dryophilus; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae

File.Name: ic26c_ic25-004.jpg
Notes: 32606700468 I found these brown-spored Agaricus mushrooms growing in the mulch under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) and California Bay (Umbellularia californica, Lauraceae) trees. I'm not sure what kind they are, but they have a sweet odor like almonds which is not at all phenolic. One possibility is the Amber-Staining Agaricus (Agaricus albolutescens aka A. moronii). I didn't notice any stains from my handling, but notice how the exposed ring of the mushroom on the right is stained amber. I'll try to get back to this one tomorrow for a closer look. I find our woodland Agaricus mushrooms to be a confusing group. (San Marcos Pass, 25 December 2018)

File.Name: ic26d_ic25-001.jpg
Notes: 45755602754 This is a tiny gall on the underside of a fallen leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) - with raindrops from the night before. I'm pretty sure it's the gall of a Two-horned Gall Wasp (Dryocosmus dubiosus in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera. These galls hold the unisexual generation of wasps that are all female. Adults will emerge in the spring and lay eggs to male the bisexual generation of wasps that have their own galls. They will emerge in summer to mate and create a new generation of unisexual galls. Sorry. but galls are complicated like this. The galls really are tiny, about 850 pixels across in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 3.3 mm, about 1/8 inch. (See here for how I figured.) They have little Micky Mouse ears! (San Marcos Pass, 25 December 2018)
CommonNames: Coast Live Oak; Two-horned Gall Wasp
ScientificName: Dryocosmus dubiosus
associatedTaxa: Quercus agrifolia

File.Name: ic27a_ic27-016.jpg
Notes:
44677132430 Here is a little Bee Fly (family Bombyliidae, Diptera) standing up in a mulch of fallen oak leaves in the woods. I like its shadow. I believe the fly is genus Bombylius, probably the Greater Bee Fly (Bombylius major) which is said to appear early in the year. It does look slightly different from the similar fly in my [Previous] photo, but maybe it's a different exposure or gender or just individual differences? I've been waiting for these flies which usually appear in our woods around New Year's. Like the swallows are said to return to Capistrano, the Bee Flies have returned to San Marcos Pass! I'll have another shot for Fly Day Friday tomorrow. (San Marcos Pass, 27 December 2018)

The chilly winds kicked up again today. Not that it wasn't windy yesterday, but today was worse with 25-30 mph gusts. We're still under an extended NWS Wind Advisory again tonight. It's chilly too. The Santa Ynez Valley is under a Freeze Warning tonight. We haven't had any frost in the mountains yet this year, but I brought a few plants inside tonight just in case.

File.Name: ic27d_ic27-003.jpg
Notes: 45769985844 This is a Bolete mushroom with pores instead of gills. I found it growing in leaf mulch under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) trees. Hah, there also a second mushroom in the photo! That patch of white mold on the left is another Bolete that has been engulfed by the "Bolete Eater" (Hypomyces chrysospermus) fungus, see this photo for more info. Before the mold gets them, these mushrooms have minutely velvety caps that start brown, like baking potatoes, see this photo. The brown fuzz seems to wear off, revealing reddish caps underneath. They have yellow and red stems with a "pinched" base, and rather large yellow pores which stain blue when bruised. I guess these are Boletus dryophilus aka Xerocomellus dryophilus, but there are a number of +/- similar species - B. chyrsenteron, B. subtomentosus, B. truncatus, B. bicolor, and no doubt more. All of these have new scientific names besides "Boletus". All of these are also said to be edible but not choice - but they have their fans, see Jeff's comment on this photo. I haven't tried them, mostly because they are nearly always parasitized by the Bolete Eater which is not appetizing! (San Marcos Pass, 27 December 2018)
CommonNames: Bolete; Coast Live Oak; Bolete Eater
ScientificName: Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Hypomyces chrysospermus; Boletus dryophilus; Xerocomellus dryophilus; B. chyrsenteron; B. subtomentosus; B. truncatus; B. bicolor
associatedTaxa: Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Hypomyces chrysospermus; Boletus dryophilus; Xerocomellus dryophilus; B. chyrsenteron; B. subtomentosus; B. truncatus; B. bicolor

File.Name: ic28a_ic28-020.jpg
Notes:
32634021568 Here are new flowers of Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca) in the Ericaceae plant family with what I guess are two undescribed galls. The brown ones on the left that look kind of gooey appear to be galled flower buds - maybe the oversized ovaries of the flowers, compare the normal ones to the right exposed where the outer flower has slipped away. See this photo for something similar. These might be the work of Gall Midges (Cecidomyiidae, Diptera), but I don't really know. The other possible gall is that squat reddish flower on the right. I've also found these on Eastwood Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glandulosa) which is a different but related species, see this photo. I'll have to keep an eye on these squat flowers to see if they ever open. Aphid galls are common on manzanita, but both of these are something different that's not described in Ron Russo's "Field Guide to Plant Galls" (UC Press, 2006) book. (San Marcos Pass, 28 December 2018)

I went to a place today where I should have found lots to photograph, but the pickings were slim - no mushrooms, no insects, few of these new manzanita flowers. The winds finally died down today, but it continues to be cold. I don't think it ever froze last night, but it must have been close. One result of three days of wind is that it already feels dry. We now stand at 8.02 inches of rain for the water year, which comes to 91% of normal. Could be better.

I've been waiting for these flies which usually appear in our woods around New Year's. Like the swallows are said to return to Capistrano, the Bee Flies have returned to San Marcos Pass! HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 27 December 2018)

File.Name: ic29a_ic29-004.jpg
Notes:
46520599941 This is looking closer at the dragonfly in my [Previous] photo. I believe it is our usual winter dragonfly, a Variegated Meadowhawk (Sympetrum corruptum) in the family Libellulidae, Odonata, perched on a dead stem of Southern Bush Monkey Flower (Mimulus aurantiacus var. pubescens, Phrymaceae). I got this photo in the morning, and the other photo in the afternoon - in the same place. I usually find these dragonflies hanging onto plants on cold days in winter like today, grounded by the weather. Except for the days when they are grounded, these predators seem to be active all winter long looking for prey in sunny places. Despite its bright colors, it's well camouflaged. I almost bumped into this one while looking for something else, and it was cold enough that I could get close. (San Marcos Pass, 29 December 2018)

We had another day without much wind, but it was still cold - not quite freezing, but still cold enough that this dragonfly was not out hunting. Cold winds are expected down south into the new year, and maybe here too.

File.Name: ic29c_ic29-017.jpg
Notes: 46468115132 This golden Syrphid Fly (Syrphidae, Diptera) has just come in for a landing on this leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). Usually they fold heir wings up when they land, but this one still has them open. Maybe it's the species Syrphus opinator which is said to be the common species in central California, see BugGuide - but there are any different kinds of these flies. I usually see these flies hovering in the air just a little too far away to photograph, but it was a chilly morning so I could get close to this one - for a moment at least.
