File.Name: ic29d_ic29-040.jpg
Notes: 32646819958 I was surprised to find this early caterpillar of a Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly on a new leaf Climbing Penstemon aka Heart-leaved Keckiella (Keckiella cordifolia, Plantaginaceae) today. I guess I shouldn't be too surprised as I found the first one last year on January 13 on a plant of Southern Bush Monkey Flower (Mimulus aurantiacus var. pubescens, Phrymaceae), see this photo. Despite being in different families, both plants are considered to be "scrophs" because they were both in the old Scrophulariaceae plant family. That family has now been split up, but there is still some affinity that these caterpillars recognize. It's interesting that I found the earliest caterpillars last year on the Monkey Flower because the Penstemon hadn't leafed out yet, but it's earlier this year, and I believe it is the preferred host for these caterpillars. These are the same web-spinning caterpillars that I find in summer, for example in this photo from last August. Apparently they form their creepy webs on the host plants in the summer, then form tough skins and drop to the forest floor to over-winter. They re-emerge in the following spring, get fat on their host plants, and then pupate into butterflies. It's a strange cycle, but it works for them. (San Marcos Pass, 29 December 2018)
CommonNames: Variable Checkerspot; Climbing Penstemon; Heart-leaved Keckiella; Southern Bush Monkey Flower
ScientificName: Euphydryas chalcedona; Keckiella cordifolia; Plantaginaceae; Mimulus aurantiacus; pubescens; Phrymaceae; Scrophulariaceae
associatedTaxa: Euphydryas chalcedona; Keckiella cordifolia; Plantaginaceae; Mimulus aurantiacus; pubescens; Phrymaceae; Scrophulariaceae

File.Name: ic30a_ic30-003.jpg
Notes:
31594556137 This is looking close at the back of the head of the same dragonfly as my [Previous] photo. It's also the same dragonfly I showed yesterday in this photo - but I got this photo today. I went back to the same dead stem of Southern Bush Monkey Flower (Mimulus aurantiacus var. pubescens, Phrymaceae), and there it was, still hanging on the plant on this cold morning. I believe this is our usual winter dragonfly, a Variegated Meadowhawk (Sympetrum corruptum) in the family Libellulidae, Odonata. I believe it will stay here as long as it stays this cold. Like all dragonflies, it has amazing eyes. There's a distinct boundary between the top and bottom based on the size of the ommatidia that make up its compound eyes. You can also see this difference in this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 30 December 2018)

Yeah, I photographed this dragonfly again today - and why not? It was another cold day, the dragonfly was hanging in the same place, and other insects are scarce because of the chill. It's supposed to get even colder over the next couple of days, so we might finally get some frost. There's a NWS Hard Freeze Watch in place for the Santa Ynez Valley tomorrow night. It was also quite breezy today, and we're under a Wind Advisory tonight. Gusty cold Santa Ana winds are expected down south into the new year, but we usually miss those because there's no direct path to the desert in Santa Barbara. Here's hoping.

File.Name: ic30c_ic30-015.jpg
Notes: 45621046245 This is a new gall on a leaf of native Black Sage (Salvia mellifera) in the Lamiaceae plant family. I believe it is the gall of a Sage Leaf Gall Midge (Rhopalomyia audibertiae) fly in the family Cecidomyiidae, Diptera. It's hard to see in this photo, but the galls are open on top, with an opening filled with hairs - see this photo where this feature is more obvious. This is the earliest I've found these galls. File.Name: ic30d_ic30-014.jpg
Notes: 31594029677 Here are liverworts and lichens on a sandstone rock outcrop in the canyon. If you look close, you can see some moss too, e.g. in the upper left. The lichen is crustose - that's all I know about it. The liverworts are interesting because there are actually two different kinds in this one photo, larger ones on the left and smaller ones on the right. I'm pretty sure the ones on the left are the California Liverwort (Asterella californica), and the smaller ones on the right are Orobus-seed Liverwort (Targionia hypophylla). The ones on the left are developing sexual sporophytes with will look like little palm trees or umbrellas when they are mature. There's also one plant on the right that has a bumpy structure on its "leaves" (properly a thallus) that will become the flat asexual buds known as gemmae which splash out in the rain. You can see both kinds of reproductive structures in this photo. The smaller liverwort isn't so obvious, but I believe it also has tiny dark gemmae on the ends of its leaves. It needs a closer photo to see. (San Marcos Pass, 30 December 2018)

File.Name: ic31a_ic31-019.jpg
Notes:
32675585558 This is a neat colony of Sulfur Tuft aka Clustered Woodlover (Hypholoma fasciculare aka Naematoloma fasciculare) mushrooms growing from a stump in the woods that is probably Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). The gills of these fresh mushrooms are pale yellow, but they will turn dark as they mature with purple-gray spores. (There is a bit of the dark color on the vestigial partial veil on the stem.) The mushrooms are said to be poisonous - with a bitter taste to keep you away. I wish all poisonous mushrooms were so obliging! These are the first ones I've seen in a couple of years, thanks to our recent rains. The green leaves in the photo are a handsome native Sedge in the Cyperaceae plant family, see this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 31 December 2018)

It was a pleasure to find some mushrooms in the woods today, but at home and in nearby Kinevan Canyon. I'm not surprised - the woods feel dry, but there is plenty of moisture in the soil. There is now a chance for more rain this weekend, which should keep the mushrooms coming. Fortunately, it hasn't been windy, but it continues to be cold. The Santa Ynez Valley is under a NWS Freeze Warning again tonight.

File.Name: ic31b_ic31-032.jpg
Notes: 46496784892 Here are two shots of a remarkably pallid Cortinarius mushroom growing under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) and California Bay (Umbellularia californica, Lauraceae) trees in the woods along Kinevan Road. It clearly has a bulbous base that may even count as "rimmed" - see my [Previous] photo - which places it in the subgenus Bulbopodium. You can see the cobwebby remains of the partial veil (the cortina) on the stem, stained rusty-brown from falling spores. One mushroom that fits the description is Cortinarius regalis, but I really don't know. (San Marcos Pass, 31 December 2018)

File.Name: ic31c_ic31-010.jpg
Notes: 46496520662 This is a Cortinarius mushroom growing under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) trees. This photo shows the rusty-brown remnants of the "cortina" hairs on the stem which originally form a veil that protects the gills and catch falling spores. Maybe this one is Cortinarius xanthodryophilus - see the species page and photos at Mykoweb. This one is considered to be subgenus Phlegmacium with a swollen base and not subgenus Bulbopodium with a "rimmed base" - a distinction that I don't understand. I've called this one Cortinarius fulmineus in the past, but I reckon it's the same thing. Edibility is unknown. The problem is that all these Cortinarius mushrooms end up looking alike, so there's no good way to tell them apart. (San Marcos Pass, 31 December 2018)

File.Name: ic31d_ic31-004.jpg
Notes: 31607821757 I believe this is a Boletus dryophilus aka Xerocomellus dryophilus mushroom on the left, next to the same kind of mushroom that has been engulfed by the "Bolete Eater" (Hypomyces chrysospermus) fungus, see this photo for more info. If you look close at the mushroom on the left, you can see that it is indeed infected with the mold, visible as a white fuzz over the pores near the stem. I found these growing in leaf mulch under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) and California Bay (Umbellularia californica, Lauraceae) trees. Before the mold gets them, these mushrooms have minutely velvety caps that start brown, like baking potatoes, see this photo. The brown fuzz seems to wear off, revealing reddish caps underneath. They have yellow and red stems with a "pinched" base, and rather large yellow pores which stain blue when bruised. They are said to be edible but not choice. I haven't tried them, mostly because they are nearly always parasitized by the Bolete Eater which is not very appetizing! (San Marcos Pass, 31 December 2018)

File.Name: h101a_h101-051.jpg
Notes:
32040012235 I happened to notice this small earthworm (Oligochaeta, Annelida) in the woods today, crawling over the oak mulch. This worm is small, an inch or two long depending on how stretched out it is. It's also somewhat transparent, and you can see future "worm castings" lined up along its body! I don't know anything about our local worms. Is it native? (San Marcos Pass, 1 January 2017)

It was a treat to start the new year with a walk after yesterday's rain! I was expecting our usual post-storm winds today, but it wasn't too bad. Our next chance of rain comes on Wednesday, and there is a rumor of a good storm next weekend! We still only stand at about 3/4 of normal rainfall, though it feels better after five years of drought already.

File.Name: h101b_h101-033.jpg
Notes:
31198392224 Here are two shots of a fresh Cortinarius mushroom in the woods today. This is one of the blue ones that looks superficially like the Blewit (Clitocybe nuda) mushrooms in my [Previous] photo, though this photo shows the color better. I first thought this one was a Blewit until I lifted it up. This mushroom has cobwebby cortina hairs stained rusty-brown from falling spores, see this photo. It also has the viscid cap and bulbous stem of the subgenus Bulbopodium. Maybe it's Cortinarius sodagnitus or C. glaucopus or something similar? This one is growing under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). (San Marcos Pass, 1 January 2017)

There are dozens or hundreds or maybe gazillions of Cortinarius species of mushrooms in our woods. The old ones all look the same, but I love the fresh ones for their color and character. Edibility unknown, mainly because they are so difficult to distinguish.

Notes: 31229093193 These Blewit (Clitocybe nuda) mushrooms all start violet-blue, but they soon fade like these. They still have blue colors underneath like the one I turned over, but in time that will fade too. Compare this photo. They are edible and tasty, but be careful - we also have several inedible blue Cortinarius mushroom in the woods that sometimes look very similar, see my [Next] photo. We have quite a few of these mushrooms in the canyon right now, growing under the oaks. (San Marcos Pass, 1 January 2017)

File.Name: h101d_h101-028.jpg
Notes: 31999943976 Here are seedlings of native Miner's Lettuce (Claytonia perfoliata) in the Portulacaceae or Montiaceae plant family getting backed into a corner by moss and a bolt on a concrete utility cover in the canyon. Note the raindrops - we had a bit of rain yesterday, and everything looks fresh and clean today. Yeah, Miner's Lettuce has round leaves that encircle the stems, but they start with these narrow leaves. This is also my photo for the Macro Mondays group, with the theme of "corners". Not every corner we find ourselves in has straight edges, so HMM! (San Marcos Pass, 1 January 2017)

File.Name: h102a_h102-007.jpg
Notes:
31256272593 I believe this is a tiny mosquito-like Fungus Gnat in the family Mycetophilidae of the Diptera. I like the family name, it's a mushroom lover like me! Compare this photo of a genus Boletina at BugGuide. The larvae of these flies feed on different fleshy mushrooms and are apparently dependent on particular families of mushrooms. The fly is about 570 pixels long in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to about 2.2 mm, less than 1/10 inch. (See here for how I figured.) (San Marcos Pass, 2 January 2017)

Today was overcast, but it was also a lovely photo day with no wind, "cloudy bright" light, and no harsh shadows. These have become my favorite photo days, and it's kind of a shock to go out on a contrasty sunny day! Our next chance of rain comes tomorrow night, and there is still a rumor of a good storm next weekend!

File.Name: h102b_h102-035.jpg
Notes:
32026475516 I don't often find living native terrestrial Land Snails (Gastropoda), but I do find their shells. I believe this is (or was) a Shoulderband Snail, genus Helminthoglypta in the family Helminthoglytidae. It's NOT one of the pesky Garden Snails that are so common in the lowlands. The shell seems to have some nacre (aka mother of pearl), but Wikipedia says that "the inner layer in the great majority of mollusc shells is porcellaneous, not nacreous." Either way, it's rather pretty. I have found living snails in the mountains, see here and here, and here! (San Marcos Pass, 2 January 2017)

There is an interesting web page on Ventura County Terrestrial Snails.

Notes: 31255128723 These look like little tiny insect eggs, but they are really Slime Molds (Myxomycetes or Myxogastria). Something different comes from their spores, see this photo for a bit more info. They are growing on the edge of a piece of bark on a large fallen branch of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods. I believe the photo shows sessile (without a stalk) sporangia or plasmodiocarps. In the past I've called them Diderma effusum, but I'm really not sure. I guess these white ones are an immature form of the orange ones in this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 2 January 2017)

File.Name: h102d_h102-040.jpg
Notes:
31223620574 Here are two shots of a handsome yellow Cortinarius mushroom growing under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods today. They have a slimy cap and a swollen base which places them in subgenus Bulbopodium. They also show the rusty-red remnants of the "cortina" hairs which originally form a veil that protects the gills and catch the falling spores. I call all these yellow-orange cortinarius mushrooms "Cortinarius fulmineus", but I believe that name is just a placeholder for whatever the mushrooms really are. Compare this blue Cortinarius mushroom that I found yesterday. (San Marcos Pass, 2 January 2017)

There are dozens or hundreds or maybe gazillions of Cortinarius species of mushrooms in our woods. The old ones all look the same, but I love the fresh ones for their color and character. Edibility unknown, mainly because they are so difficult to distinguish.

File.Name: h103a_h103-047.jpg
Notes:
31971611901 This is our usual winter dragonfly, a Variegated Meadowhawk (Sympetrum corruptum) in the family Libellulidae, Odonata, in the canyon near our still-dry creek. I look for them every time I walk the canyon in winter, but I don't always find them. This one is hanging onto a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). They seem to be quite invisible - until one catches your eye and they jump out at you! (San Marcos Pass, 1 January 2017)

I also found a new caterpillar of a Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly on a shrub of native Southern Bush Monkey Flower (Mimulus aurantiacus var. pubescens, Phrymaceae) today, the first I've seen this year. I tried to get a photo, but the breeze got in the way. Hopefully, that breeze in a early sign of the storm coming in tonight or tomorrow. *Sigh* Now the word from the NWS is that the new storms will mostly be to the north of us. We'll see what happens.

File.Name: h103b_h103-033.jpg
Notes:
32089275605 I believe these are Woodland Agaricus (Agaricus silvicola or A. abruptibulbus) mushrooms growing in the woods today. The mushroom caps stain yellow from age and handling, but the base of the stem does not stain yellow. It has a sweet almond smell. They have a "cogwheel" pattern on the partial veil ring, see this photo. They look and smell delicious, and maybe they are, but I'm wary of all our woodland species. Agaricus hondensis in my [Previous] photo and A. xanthodermus (here) are the main culprits, with a nasty phenol-like taste . The one in this photo lacks that taste, but "once bitten, twice warned!" (San Marcos Pass, 1 January 2017)

We have quite a few Agaricus species in our woods. I find them to be a confusing group.

File.Name: h103c_h101-001.jpg
Notes: 32088996695 I believe these are the Felt-Ringed Agaricus (Agaricus hondensis) mushrooms growing in deep oak mulch in the woods. They have a thick flaring ring on the stem, and slowly develop pink stains. (Should they stain yellow?) They look edible, but they have a nasty phenol-like taste if you nibble a bit or crush the base of the stem. It's definitely not edible, but it is a large, attractive mushroom. (San Marcos Pass, 1 January 2017)
ScientificName: Agaricus hondensis
associatedTaxa: Agaricus hondensis

File.Name: h103d_h101-023.jpg
Notes: 31247476074 I believe this is an Agaricus bitorquis mushroom growing along the edge of the road in our canyon. Sometimes they push up through the chipseal of our road, see this photo! If you read Michelle's comment to that other photo, you'll learn that Agaricus bernardii is another possibility, and this one does seem to stain reddish from handling. Whatever it is, it's a large hefty mushroom that is considered edible and choice. I have another photo of a similar mushroom here. The solid heft and the basal ring seem distinctive, but I'm wary of all our woodland agaricus species - "once bitten, twice warned!". (San Marcos Pass, 1 January 2017)

File.Name: h104a_h104-011.jpg
Notes:
32111514655 Here are new flowers of native Milk Maids aka Toothwort (Cardamine californica) in the Brassicaceae plant family, with raindrops from the gentle storm today. (I got this photo in the rain.) They are beautiful flowers, more elegant than our usual yellow "wild mustards" that are non-native. That happens to be the foliage of a native Southern Bush Monkey Flower (Mimulus aurantiacus var. pubescens, Phrymaceae) shrub behind the lower flowers. (San Marcos Pass, 4 January 2017)

We only had light rain today - but it rained all day, adding up to about 7/10 inch so far, and still coming. San Luis Obispo to our north has been doing much better, but this gentle rain has to be good for the woods.

File.Name: h104b_h104-004.jpg
Notes: 31962933862 I believe this is a tiny Mycena acicula (or kin) mushroom growing from oak leaf mulch - with rain drops. It's probably growing from buried wood, but they have remarkably long stems. I included my pen with a 0.7 mm ball, so the caps are only a few millimeters across. This is the smallest beautiful mushroom that I actually have a name for. I got this photo in light rain this morning, holding an umbrella over my camera. (San Marcos Pass, 4 January 2017)

File.Name: h104c_h103-032.jpg
Notes: 32071850696 This is a cluster of Western Jack-O-Lantern (Omphalotus olivascens) mushrooms growing from the base of a dead Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) tree. (*Sigh* The tree is probably drought-dead.) I put my 3-1/2 inch pocket knife in the photo for scale. Look how there are fruiting bodies of the fungus trying to push out between cracks in the bark on the right! This mushroom is quite poisonous, so don't eat it. I can't imagine why anyone would confuse this with an edible chanterelle mushroom when they are massed like this, but I've been fooled for a moment by solitary specimens growing from buried wood. The greenish colors, rubbery texture, and sharp gill edges should settle it. These mushrooms are slightly bioluminescence - see this interesting photo by Michelle C. Torres-Grant. I want to try that! (San Marcos Pass, 3 January 2017)
ScientificName: Omphalotus olivascens
associatedTaxa: Quercus agrifolia

File.Name: h104d_h101-014.jpg
Notes: 31269478284 Raindrops on a spider web ... what else can I say? The disorganized web is probably the work of a Cobweb Spider (Theridiidae). The web was close against a sandstone outcrop. The color difference is just the reflected sunlight when I looked at the web just so. Happy Web Wednesday! (San Marcos Pass, 1 January 2017)

File.Name: h105a_h105-038.jpg
Notes:
31291405764 Here are head and tail shots of our usual winter dragonfly, a Variegated Meadowhawk (Sympetrum corruptum) in the family Libellulidae, Odonata. I had the notion to get a "butt shot" of this critter for the Beautiful Bug Butt Thursday group, but it disappeared from the place where I last saw it, on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). I walked on, and I spotted it on my way back home - about a foot away from where I first found it. I'm not sure if this one is male or female. It has cerci, but does it also have a paraprocts? It seems to have *something* below, and it also has red colors, so I guess this is a male. A side-view would be helpful, but this dragonfly was hiding in the tree in a way that made this impossible. Half of this is a butt shot, so HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 5 January 2017)

We ended up with about an inch of slow rain from our storm yesterday. This kind of rain doesn't go deep, like the oaks need after five years of drought, but it helps. Our next chance of rain comes this weekend, but not the "atmospheric river" that was in the forecast a couple of days ago. Que L·stima.

File.Name: h105b_h105-029.jpg
Notes:
31322224863 Here are the first caterpillars of a Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly that I've found this year, on new leaves of native Southern Bush Monkey Flower (Mimulus aurantiacus var. pubescens aka Diplacus longiflorus, Scrophulariaceae or Phrymaceae) in the canyon today. They seem early this year - I didn't find them last year until March 23, but I found the first one on December 21 the year before. (Photos here and here.) These are the same web-spinning caterpillars that I find in summer, for example this photo. Apparently they form their creepy webs on the host plants in the summer, then form tough skins and drop to the forest floor to over-winter. They re-emerge in the following spring, get fat on their host plants, and then pupate into butterflies. It's a strange cycle, but it works for them. (San Marcos Pass, 5 January 2017)

This caterpillar is on Bush Monkey Flower, where I've been finding them for the last few years. It's curious because I used to find them mostly on native Climbing Penstemon (Keckiella cordifolia, Scrophulariaceae or Plantaginaceae) which hasn't leafed out yet. Have they changed host plants? The old Scrophulariaceae plant family has been split up, but I wonder if the butterflies know something that the botanists have missed?

I've only seen a few of these pretty galls this year. It seems odd to think of galls as having good and bad years, but the wasps that induce them do. (San Marcos Pass, 5 January 2017)

File.Name: h105d_h105-015.jpg
Notes: 32093102666 This is the bowing head of a dark-spored Inky Cap mushroom. It actually shows the underside of the mushroom's cap, but the gills "deliquesce" - they literally digest themselves into black slime! There are many different kinds of these "coprinoid mushrooms". They all used to be considered genus Coprinus, but now they have been split into Coprinus, Coprinopsis, Coprinellus, and Parasola. I don't know what it is, but it is quite beautiful - in a black slime sort of way. (San Marcos Pass, 5 January 2017)

File.Name: h106a_h106-001.jpg
Notes:
31777856010 I believe this is another tiny mosquito-like Fungus Gnat in the family Mycetophilidae of the Diptera. I like the family name, it's a mushroom lover like me! Compare this photo of a genus Boletina at BugGuide. The larvae of these flies feed on different fleshy mushrooms and are apparently dependent on particular families of mushrooms. The fly is about 570 pixels long in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to about 2.2 mm, less than 1/10 inch. (See here for how I figured.) Hah, you can see the ommatidia that make up its compound eyes! Compare this photo. It's probably an unusual fly to photograph, so HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 6 January 2017)

It sounds like San Luis Obispo will get most of the strong storm on Saturday, but we still have a 90% chance of something here in Santa Barbara. Then we have another 90% chance of rain on Sunday night. We're still at just 78% of normal rainfall, but it's looking better with every new storm.

File.Name: h106b_h106-029.jpg
Notes: 32152561855 These are new plants of native Indian Warrior (Pedicularis densiflora) in the Orobanchaceae plant family, coming up mushroom-like under a shrub of Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca, Ericaceae). Sometimes entire plants have red foliage, but these plants look bronze and green.The red color belongs to leafy bracts where the real flowers will appear - but there are no flowers yet. I found the first true flowers last year on February 14, but I believe they'll be earlier this year. Sometimes entire plants have red foliage, but these plants look bronze and green. These plants are root-parasites of several different chaparral shrubs, surely manzanita in this place. Apparently, the plants form haustoria - like mistletoe - to connect to their host species. There seem to be fewer plants this year, but the year is young. (San Marcos Pass, 6 January 2017)

File.Name: h106c_h106-022.jpg
Notes: 32113976066 I happened to notice this "thing" when I was down on my hands and knees getting the photo of the Lactarius mushroom in my [Previous] photo, under a shrub of Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca, Ericaceae). I never would have noticed it otherwise, as it's only an inch or so across, and barely rises above the leaf litter. I had WTF thoughts, and I thought it might be a different Coral Mushroom. Then I remembered the Oregon mushroom in this photo. Maybe it's a Fetid False Coral (Thelephora palmata) mushroom? It's said to have a garlic-like odor that becomes fetid (unpleasant) in age, but I'm sorry I didn't smell it. It's still there, and I'll get back to it when I can. (San Marcos Pass, 6 January 2017)

Notes: 32003454602 I believe this is the Yellow-Staining Milk Cap (Lactarius xanthogalactus) mushroom, formerly known as Lactarius vinaceorufescens or Lactarius chrysorheus. (Those darn mycologists!) This was the only one I found today, so here are two shots of it. If you slice the gills, they produce a white "milk" that turns yellow almost immediately. It has a acrid taste and is said to be poisonous. This one was growing under Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca, Ericaceae). (San Marcos Pass, 6 January 2017)

File.Name: h107a_h107-023.jpg
Notes:
31333608004 You might have to squint at this photo to see it ... here is a row of slime mold (Myxomycetes or Myxogastria) sporangia across the top, with a tiny Harvestman (Opiliones) spider just below. These spiders only have one body part and two eyes, unlike true spiders that have two and eight respectively. I'm not sure what the slime molds are - they have stalks, and colors like tiny Easter eggs. I found this scene under the bark of a fallen branch of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). (San Marcos Pass, 7 January 2017)

I went to a memorial for Lynn Watson (aka NatureShutterbug on Flickr) this afternoon. Lynn passed away last month. I'll miss her many comments on my photos. I reckon Lynn left us with a good example - share what you know, and get it right!

We've had active weather tonight, with enough thunder and lightning that I shut down my computer for a while! Despite the activity, we've only had modest rain, about 6/10 inch so far today. Hah, the city of San Luis Obispo to the north has Flood Warnings, but we only have Wind Warnings here.

File.Name: h107b_h107-021.jpg
Notes: 32174510065 I believe these two photos show different stages in the development of a tiny genus Stemonitis slime mold (Myxomycetes or Myxogastria) growing on dead oak wood in the woods. The shot on the left shows the mucous-like plasmodia still streaming together into clumps, before forming the more-structured sporangia on the right. They are about 1500 pixels high in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to about 5.8 mm, a bit less than 1/4 inch. (See here for how I figured.) These sporangia seem unusually hairy - maybe they have a fungus infection? Compare this photo. I'm pretty sure these two photos show similar slime molds, but I might be wrong. They were about an inch away from each other, and they are about the same size at the base. (San Marcos Pass, 7 January 2017)

File.Name: h107c_h107-012.jpg
Notes: 32174165205 I was in the dark about what this photo shows. I found it on under bark from a forgotten pile of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) wood. My first thought was eggs, since they look a bit like frog eggs with embryos inside. But they are all different sizes, and wouldn't eggs be all the same size? Thanks to Jeff Goddard (see below), I believe it is Myxarium nucleatum, a jelly fungus. what's most striking is the presence of whitish nodules inside the bits of jelly, which Wikipedia claims are mineral inclusions made of calcium oxalate. Hah, I'm glad I can still find some mysteries in the woods! (San Marcos Pass, 7 January 2017)

It's busy with life, including several odd Crampball (Daldinia grandis or kin) fungi that look and feel like lumps of charcoal , and a pretty orange Crust Mushroom (Stereaceae or kin) that might be Stereum hirsutum. In addition there are the usual mosses and lichens. A fallen log is a feast for many organisms! (San Marcos Pass, 7 January 2017)

File.Name: h108a_h108-028.jpg
Notes:
32051549092 After I dug out the Agaricus hondensis mushroom in my [Previous] photo, I noticed this native terrestrial Land Snail (Gastropoda) trying to escape! Usually when I find them (rarely) they withdraw, but this one ran for the exit. I believe this is a Shoulderband Snail, genus Helminthoglypta in the family Helminthoglytidae. I don't often find the living snails. Both Wikipedia and iNaturalist say that these "snails create and use love darts as part of their mating behavior." Sounds interesting - cupid is a snail?? (San Marcos Pass, 8 January 2017)

Today was cloudy, but I'm hoping for rain tonight, and the NWS agrees. We're under a Flash Flood Watch tonight. This might be a good storm!

File.Name: h108b_h108-020.jpg
Notes: 32081507251 This Agaricus mushroom looks delicious, like large portabello ... and it tastes horrible! This is a Felt-Ringed Agaricus (Agaricus hondensis) mushroom growing in deep oak mulch in the woods. They have a thick flaring ring on the stem, and slowly develop pink stains. They look edible, but they have a nasty phenol-like taste if you nibble a bit or crush the base of the stem. It's definitely not edible, but it is a large, attractive mushroom. If you look close, you can see a little fly on the stem above the ring. I believe this is a Heleomyzidae fly in the genus Suillia, see this close photo. These flies seem to specialize in laying eggs that produce maggots that destroy mushrooms, see this photo! (San Marcos Pass, 8 January 2017)

I have another photo of a different mushroom from a week ago here. It's more of a blue-gray than an blue-violet, but it's my best shot at an indigo mushroom. Compare my [Previous] photo. This is my photo for the Macro Mondays group, with the theme "Inspired by a Song" - the song being "Mood Indigo" by the great Duke Ellington - listen here. HMM! (San Marcos Pass, 8 January 2017)

31823518200 This is looking close under the cap of a fantastic Violet Cortinarius (Cortinarius violaceus) mushroom growing under conifers in the Oregon woods ... a few years ago. It's so blue/violet that it's almost black. It's said to be edible but not choice. It's principal appeal is its beauty. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 25 November 2011)

You see, the theme for the Macro Mondays group this week is "Inspired by a Song," and I had the notion of doing the song "Mood Indigo" by the great Duke Ellington - listen here. Unfortunately this fine mushroom doesn't grow around here, and the group has the proper guideline that photos should be taken during the week of the challenge. (Change the EXIF and cheat?? Nah!) So I looked for other blue mushrooms this week. The blewits were past their prime. Finally I settled on the different Cortinarius mushroom in my [Next] photo. 32100270501 I was surprised to find this Crab Spider (Thomisidae) on a narrow leaf of native Southern Bush Monkey Flower (Mimulus aurantiacus var. pubescens, Phrymaceae) in the canyon yesterday. I believe it's genus Mecaphesa. I'm surprised that it's out now - but it has been rather warm, and there are lots of tiny flies out in the woods like this one. (San Marcos Pass, 8 January 2017)

Just two photos today. I'm computer-weary after working all day on the newsletter for the Santa Ynez Valley Natural History Society. But I did get it mostly done today, so I just have some finishing touches before I send it out to them society Board for review and comments. What can I say, the volunteer work we do for our non-profits is important.

We got 1-3/4 inch of good rain last night! I was bound to my computer all day, but Julie reports that the creek was even running a bit. We stand at 11 inches for the rain year, about 90% of normal. There's more rain in the forecast, but I don't want to queer it! After five years of drought, this is good. I'll try to get out with my camera tomorrow.

Notes:
32238365505 Here is a new flower of native Milk Maids aka Toothwort (Cardamine californica) in the Brassicaceae plant family, with raindrops. I got this photo in the light rain today. They are beautiful flowers, more elegant than our usual yellow "wild mustards" that are non-native. Hah - they've hardly had a chance to fully open with all the rain we've been having lately! Just two photos again today. I'm computer-weary after working on the newsletter for the Santa Ynez Valley Natural History Society. I'm done! I sent it out to a few society board members for review and comments, and I'll get it to the printers on Thursday. What can I say, the volunteer work we do for our non-profits is important.

I did get outside today for a short rain walk with my camera. It was light rain, less than 1/4 inch - but welcome after five years of drought! The forecast is for heavy rain tonight - and again tomorrow night! This is not a dramatic "drought buster" - but it is a step in the right direction!

File.Name: h110b_h110-014.jpg
Notes: 31862398910 Here are a pair of Bolete mushrooms growing in leaf mulch under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) trees. These have minutely velvety caps that start brown, like baking potatoes. The brown fuzz seems to wear off, revealing reddish caps underneath, visible on the right. They also have yellow and red stems, and rather large yellow pores which slowly stain blue. I guess that these are Boletus dryophilus aka Xerocomellus dryophilus, but there are a number of +/- similar species - B. chyrsenteron, B. subtomentosus, B. truncatus, B. bicolor, and no doubt more. All of these have new scientific names besides "Boletus". All of these are also said to be edible but not choice. Compare this photo of probably the same mushrooms - you can see the remnants of a brown fuzz on the red caps. (San Marcos Pass, 10 January 2017)

File.Name: h111a_h111-011.jpg
Notes:
32258796565 Here is the tiny nymph of a "true bug" (Heteroptera, Hemiptera) on a dead leaf of Southern Bush Monkey Flower (Mimulus aurantiacus var. pubescens, Phrymaceae) in the canyon today. There are new insects in the woods right now, but they are mostly larvae like this. I guess that's why there are so many parasitic Tachinid Flies (Tachinidae, Diptera) in the woods right now, see here and here. (San Marcos Pass, 11 January 2017)

Sorry, no spider webs or mating bugs this week. It's been rainy, and I've been busy with my newsletter. I'll deliver it to the printers tomorrow. We had another 1-3/4 inch of rain last night, bringing our rain-year total up to 12.73 inches - 98% of normal! But one or two storms are not enough to break our five year run of drought years. Last year we had almost 4 inches of rain on January 31, and we still ended up with just 60% of normal for the year. Everything depends on what happens next - and there happens to be another storm coming in early tomorrow morning. I like this!

File.Name: h111b_h111-006.jpg
Notes: 31882440480 This is looking close at the veil of an unusually pallid member of the Banded Cortinarius (Cortinarius collinitus ) group - maybe Cortinarius trivialis? I found it barely pushing up a "mushrump" under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) trees when I was looking at a different mushroom. Both cap and stem are slimy, which puts it in the subgenus Myxacium. It's actually difficult to pick up because its so slimy! These mushrooms have a "slime veil" along with the "cortina" hairs which also form a veil that protects the gills and catch the falling rusty-orange spores. This one has bands of slime of its stalk. It's quite beautiful when fresh after a rain, less so when old and dried out - see this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 11 January 2017)

Hah, if you look close, you can spot a different Brown Witch's Butter, probably Tremella foliacea. The orange ones are interesting because they apparently grow as parasites on the Stereum. I certainly find them together almost always. The traditional name for these is Tremella mesenterica, but it sounds like Tremella aurantia is a better name. The two differ microscopically and in host preference. Is there a difference between the yellow and orange mushrooms? (San Marcos Pass, 11 January 2017)

File.Name: h112a_h112-009.jpg
Notes:
32237492696 Here are two surprisingly similar mushrooms in the rain today - across a road from each other. Both have bluish colors, but both have colors that wash out in the rain. On the left is a Cortinarius mushroom, with the viscid cap and bulbous stem of the subgenus Bulbopodium. Maybe it's Cortinarius sodagnitus or C. glaucopus or something similar? One important clue to its identity is the presence of "cortina" hairs on the stem, stained rusty-orange by the falling spores. Look close, you can see them. Also note the woodland debris that sticks to the slimy cap. The Blewit (Clitocybe nuda) mushroom on the right starts deep violet-blue, but soon fades like this one. It does not have a slimy cap, though it gets slippery in the rain, and the stem lacks any sign of rusty-red hairs. The base might be enlarged, but it's doesn't have a bulb like the Cortinarius. The Blewit is edible, the Cortinarius is unknown. Learning to identify mushrooms is a lot like detective work, reading the evidence, and musing about what should be and what is and what is not. Good hunting! (San Marcos Pass, 12 January 2017)

I delivered my newsletter to the printers today, so my work is done. I wanted to get some photos on the way home, but it was raining! I'll get more new photos tomorrow. We got another 2-1/4 inches of rain last night and today. I know they got much more rain further north, but I'm not complaining. It actually very nice for the woods that we got our 6 inches of rain in three separate storms rather than all at once, because most of it has soaked in. I suppose that's not so good for Cachuma Lake, but there was a stream going into it.
ScientificName: Cortinarius; Bulbopodium; Cortinarius sodagnitus; C. glaucopus; Clitocybe nuda; what should be; what is; what is not
associatedTaxa: Cortinarius; Bulbopodium; Cortinarius sodagnitus; C. glaucopus; Clitocybe nuda; what should be; what is; what is not

File.Name: h112b_h110-006.jpg
Notes: 31433895254 This photo shows new leaves of native Fairy Lantern (Calochortus albus, Liliaceae) growing with a native grass (Poacea). Both plants have linear leaves with parallel veins, but the grass has sheathed leaves coming out along the stems, whereas the lily has no side leaves at all. My photo shows one native grass plant coming from the lower left and arching to the right, and four separate Fairy Lantern plants. In this place, the native grass is probably the short-awned California Brome (Bromus carinatus), see this photo, but who knows? (Grasses all look pretty similar before they flower, to me at least.) I like this photo. It's a slice of life in the woods. (San Marcos Pass, 10 January 2017)
ScientificName: Calochortus albus; Liliaceae; Poacea; Bromus carinatus
associatedTaxa: Calochortus albus; Liliaceae; Poacea; Bromus carinatus

It's perched on a narrow leaf of native Southern Bush Monkey Flower (Mimulus aurantiacus var. pubescens, Phrymaceae) in the canyon. I believe it's genus Mecaphesa. HBBBT! File.Name: h112d_h108-002.jpg
Notes: 31465041973 This is another odd one. I first found a tiny Cricket (Tettigoniidea, Orthoptera) on December 30, see this photo. I found it again this week on the same flower scar of a cactus fruit in our garden. Well, I always check the tops of the cactus fruit for surprises, see this photo of a Tree Cricket, and I'm pretty sure it wasn't there on any days in between. It's a tiny critter, an immature Chaparral Camel Cricket (Gammarotettix genitalis, Raphidophoridae). It's also a decent butt shot for how small the critter is, so HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 8 January 2017)

File.Name: h113a_h113-025.jpg
Notes:
31452374784 Here is another photo of our usual winter dragonfly, a Variegated Meadowhawk (Sympetrum corruptum) in the family Libellulidae, Odonata. This time it's perched in a small tree of Arroyo Willow (Salix lasiolepis, Salicaceae) by the creek. Hah, I've been playing Hide-and-seek with this dragonfly on different plants near our creek: Coast Live Oak, Arroyo Willow, and Bush Monkeyflower. I reckon the dragonfly comes out on warm days, and then settles down again when the weather turns stormy. It will keep hiding, and I'll keep trying to find it! (San Marcos Pass, 13 January 2017)

It was breezy this morning, but then it became sunny and calm - but chilly. It sound like we'll have a few clear days for a chance to dry out, and then maybe more rain on Thursday. It's interesting, according to the U.S. Drought Monitor, much of California is out of the drought, and only a small piece of California - including us! - is still in category D4, Exceptional Drought. If the rains keep coming, that will change. Or not. BTW, don't believe any hype about the end of the drought. We're now at 110% normal rainfall, but the creek is barely running, which means that the ground is far from saturated. Lake Cachuma is still only at 9% capacity. It will take years of good rain to recharge the watersheds.
ScientificName: Sympetrum corruptum; Libellulidae; Odonata; Salix lasiolepis; Salicaceae
associatedTaxa: Sympetrum corruptum; Libellulidae; Odonata; Salix lasiolepis; Salicaceae

File.Name: h113b_h113-012.jpg
Notes: 32174397571 Here is an early caterpillar of a Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly snuggled into a boat-like seedpod of native Southern Bush Monkey Flower (Mimulus aurantiacus var. pubescens aka Diplacus longiflorus, Scrophulariaceae or Phrymaceae). They seem early this year, but I found them on December 21 two years ago. These are the same web-spinning caterpillars that I find in summer, for example this photo. Apparently they form their creepy webs on the host plants in the summer, then form tough skins and drop to the forest floor to over-winter. They re-emerge in the following spring, get fat on their host plants, and then pupate into butterflies in May and June. It's a strange cycle, but it works for them. (San Marcos Pass, 13 January 2017)
CommonNames: Variable Checkerspot; Southern Bush Monkey Flower
ScientificName: Euphydryas chalcedona; Mimulus aurantiacus; pubescens; Diplacus longiflorus; Scrophulariaceae; Phrymaceae
associatedTaxa: Euphydryas chalcedona; Mimulus aurantiacus; pubescens; Diplacus longiflorus; Scrophulariaceae; Phrymaceae

File.Name: h113c_h113-007.jpg
Notes: 31917303890 This is probably the California Liverwort (Asterella californica) growing on a sandstone outcrop with lichens and mosses. It's interesting, because it shows two different reproductive strategies. The liverworts on the left have the bumpy tongue-like structures on their "leaves" (properly a thallus) that will become the flat asexual buds known as gemmae which splash out in the rain, while the liverworts on the right have umbrella-like sporophytes for sexual reproduction. I've never found both kinds so close together. Compare this photo from a few weeks ago. (San Marcos Pass, 16 December 2016)
ScientificName: Asterella californica; thallus; gemmae; sporophytes
associatedTaxa: Asterella californica; thallus; gemmae; sporophytes

File.Name: h113d_h113-023.jpg
Notes: 32293502295 This large portabello-size Agaricus mushroom looks delicious, and maybe it is. I found a number of these growing in deep oak mulch in the woods today. I put my 3-1/2 inch pocket knife in the photo to show scale. It has the sweet almond-like smell of the Woodland Agaricus (Agaricus silvicola or A. abruptibulbus), but it also has a flaring ring like the ill-tasting Felt-Ringed Agaricus (Agaricus hondensis) mushroom in this photo. I nibbled a bit, and it doesn't have any phenol-like taste, so I suspect it's the first one - but "once bitten, twice warned!" I find our woodland Agaricus mushrooms to be a confusing group. (San Marcos Pass, 13 January 2017)
ScientificName: Agaricus; Agaricus silvicola; A. abruptibulbus; Agaricus hondensis
associatedTaxa: Agaricus; Agaricus silvicola; A. abruptibulbus; Agaricus hondensis

File.Name: h114a_h114-001.jpg
Notes:
31503608333 This isn't the best photo, but it's an interesting insect to find in the middle of winter - the nymph of an Assassin Bug (Reduviidae, Heteroptera, Hemiptera) on a leaf of Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus, Rosaceae). It looks different from the nymph in this recent photo. This critter is small, about 950 pixels across in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to about comes to about 3.7 mm, a bit over 1/8 inch. (See here for how I figured.) This was a difficult photo to get - in a place where the sun never shines at this time of year, it was my first photo of the day, and the bug was moving. (San Marcos Pass, 14 January 2017)

Did you notice that my photos today feature an insect, a flower, a lichen, and a fungus - all taken today, nice diversity! There are new mushrooms in the woods, but I reckon they were already here in the mulch before our good rains came last week, and they are quite soggy now. I'm trying to get a spore print for this week's Macro Mondays group, but the soggy mushrooms I've been collecting have few spores. We might have more rain on Thursday to keep up our wet spell.
ScientificName: Reduviidae; Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Rubus ursinus; Rosaceae
associatedTaxa: Reduviidae; Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Rubus ursinus; Rosaceae

File.Name: h114b_h114-023.jpg
Notes: 32314070815 These are the first actual flowers I've seen this year on native Indian Warrior (Pedicularis densiflora) in the Orobanchaceae plant family. This plant is coming up mushroom-like under a shrub of Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca, Ericaceae), producing flowers almost as soon as it appears. Most of the red color belongs to the leafy bracts from which the real flowers emerge. I found the first true flowers last year on February 14, but they're earlier this year. These plants are root-parasites of several different chaparral shrubs, surely Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca, Ericaceae) in this place. Apparently, the plants form haustoria - like mistletoe - to connect to their host species. There seem to be fewer plants this year, but the year is young and the recent rain might make a difference. (San Marcos Pass, 14 January 2017)
CommonNames: Indian Warrior; Bigberry Manzanita; Bigberry Manzanita
ScientificName: Pedicularis densiflora; Orobanchaceae; Arctostaphylos glauca; Ericaceae; Arctostaphylos glauca; Ericaceae; haustoria
associatedTaxa: Pedicularis densiflora; Orobanchaceae; Arctostaphylos glauca; Ericaceae; Arctostaphylos glauca; Ericaceae; haustoria

File.Name: h114c_h114-009.jpg
Notes: 31472092894 This is a Pelt Lichen in the genus Peltigera, maybe the Field Dog Lichen (Peltigera rufescens), growing on a sandstone rock with mosses. This lichen has a few "dog teeth" which are the apothecia (spore-bearing structures) of the fungal part of the lichen partnership. I believe these lichens have Nostoc as a photobiont, which is a cyanobacterium rather than an algae. These pelt lichens are common in the Oregon woods, but they are quite uncommon in our mountains. I only know them from this one trail, but they almost disappear into a dark crust when dry, so they would be easy to overlook. (San Marcos Pass, 14 January 2017)
ScientificName: Peltigera; Peltigera rufescens; apothecia; Nostoc; cyanobacterium
associatedTaxa: Peltigera; Peltigera rufescens; apothecia; Nostoc; cyanobacterium

File.Name: h114d_h114-025.jpg
Notes: 32313235895 I believe this interesting mushroom is a Sand Amanita, Amanita baccata sensu Arora - as described by David Arora in Mushrooms Demystified (2nd Ed., 1986). There's some discussion of it at Amanitaceae and Mushroom Observer. The latter site in particular has photos that are a ringer for mine. I found this mushroom in sandy soil under Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca, Ericaceae) with (I believe) a shrub of Shreve Oak (Quercus parvula var. shrevei) nearby. The mushroom has a smooth dry cap with veil remnants hung along the margin, look in the back of my photo. It has a poorly defined partial veil ring on the stem, and no volva to speak of, but the stem does have a long tapered base below a distinct bulb. I believe this is Amanita baccata sensu Arora because the name "Amanita baccata" is already taken by a different (though similar) species from Europe. Is this right? (San Marcos Pass, 14 January 2017)
CommonNames: Sand Amanita; Bigberry Manzanita; Shreve Oak
ScientificName: Amanita baccata sensu Arora; Mushrooms Demystified; Arctostaphylos glauca; Ericaceae; Quercus parvula; shrevei; Amanita baccata sensu Arora

File.Name: h115a_h115-018.jpg
Notes:
31960473710 This is new foliage of native Chaparral Orchid aka Denseflower Rein Orchid (Piperia elongata) in the Orchidaceae plant family growing in the woods. These are uncommon plants. The large basal leaves will completely shrivel up by the time they flower in summer, leaving a bare asparagus-like stalk about two feet tall, see this photo for the flowers. It's interesting that I find what I believe is the same orchid species in Oregon in mid-summer, see this photo. I found these orchids today by an old homestead site along West Camino Cielo. There are no structures, but there is a rather large non-native Fir tree nearby. You can see old needles on the ground in this photo. The tree sure looks like a White Fir (Abies concolor) tree, which grow in the backcountry. I'd like to know the story of this tree! (San Marcos Pass, 15 January 2017)

I went for a walk in shady Kinevan Canyon today, a place where the sun never shines at this time of year. It seems the woods should be full of mushrooms, but I only found a few, mostly old soggy ones. Oh well - the creek was flowing after recent rains, and it was a fine walk.
CommonNames: Chaparral Orchid; Denseflower Rein Orchid; Fir; White Fir
ScientificName: Piperia elongata; Orchidaceae; Abies concolor
associatedTaxa: Piperia elongata; Orchidaceae; Abies concolor

File.Name: h115b_h115-007.jpg
Notes: 32217239341 These are more tooth-sized white mushrooms growing on a mossy sandstone rock in Kinevan Canyon. I've considered genus Marasmius and genus Calyptella, but I think Jacob Kalichman nailed it in his comment to this photo - this is Rimbachia bryophila. As the species name suggests, It is closely associated with the mosses - very likely as a parasite. I plucked one cap and turned it over so that Jacob could see the underside. Mykoweb says that "Fieldmarks include a small, laterally attached, thin white cap, lack of stipe, and gills reduced to intervenose folds" - sounds right. It's interesting that this one is NOT marasmioid. (San Marcos Pass, 15 January 2017)
ScientificName: Marasmius; Calyptella; Rimbachia bryophila
associatedTaxa: Marasmius; Calyptella; Rimbachia bryophila

File.Name: h115c_h115-030.jpg
Notes:
32297796096 This is a spore print of the plain white Russula mushroom in my [Previous] photo, here. Individual fungus spores are too small to see, but you can see a few million of them! You make a spore print by removing the stem of a mushroom and placing it gills-down on a piece of plain white paper to catch the falling spores. The color of a spore deposit is a key characteristic for determining what your mushroom is, pale buff/orange in this case. This is also my photo for the Macro Mondays group, with the theme "Just White Paper". I cheated a bit by including mushroom spores and a thin slice of the mushroom itself. I thought this would be an easy one, but it's been raining much of the week, and most of the mushrooms I've been finding are soggy and spore-less. Oh well, HMM! (San Marcos Pass, 15 January 2017)

Why do I post "Macro Mondays" photos on Sunday night? Well, the group goes by GMT/UTC time - Flickr time, here. That means that the start of the day (GMT) is 4:00 pm PST, and the end of the day is 4:00 pm tomorrow. I usually post my photos in the evening (PST), so here I am on Sunday night (PST) - aka Monday morning (GMT). Yep, it's clear as mud!
ScientificName: Russula
associatedTaxa: Russula

File.Name: h115d_h114-016.jpg
Notes: 31959396700 This is a plain white Russula mushroom growing under (mostly) Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) trees. Maybe they are the Boring White Russula (Russula albidula), as David Arora calls it? Another possibility is the Short-Stemmed Russula (Russula brevipes), though the stems aren't particularly short and the mushrooms aren't especially large. They're pretty common in the woods right now. This is the mushroom I used for my spore print in my [Next] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 14 January 2017)

File.Name: h116a_h116-020.jpg
Notes:
32207388702 I found this beautiful Mourning Cloak (Nymphalis antiopa) butterfly perched in a shrub of native Chamise (Adenostoma fasciculatum, Rosaceae) - a perch in the sun on a chilly day. The butterfly looks black and white when it flies, but it is really rich chocolate brown with creamy yellow wing margins. The blue spots are wonderful. I don't see them very often, sometimes in midsummer (like this), but usually in midwinter. This completes a trio of the butterflies that I usually find in midwinter, including the Red Admiral (Vanessa atalanta) and the California Tortoiseshell (Nymphalis californica). A couple of days ago, I spotted a little Blue (Polyommatinae, Lycaenidae) butterfly flying by. The years also fly by! (San Marcos Pass, 16 January 2017)

I had an appointment in town his morning, so I got a late start for new photos today. I took my usual walk in the canyon, and had some luck finding critters! I also found some mushrooms, and I'm falling behind with posting photos. We do have more rain expected on Wednesday night and Friday and again on Sunday, which might give me a chance to catch up. It's a good problem to have.
CommonNames: Mourning Cloak; Chamise; Red Admiral; California Tortoiseshell; Blue
ScientificName: Nymphalis antiopa; Adenostoma fasciculatum; Rosaceae; Vanessa atalanta; Nymphalis californica; Polyommatinae; Lycaenidae

File.Name: h116b_h116-007.jpg
Notes: 32318120566 This is a cute little furry Bee Fly (Bombyliidae, Diptera) perched on an oak leaf on the sunny side of our canyon, probably genus Bombylius. This is the first one I've seen this year, and I just happened to find the first one on the same date last year. It's perched on a fallen oak leaf in a place that gets morning sun in our canyon. You can see the outline of the real fly underneath its vestiture of thick hairs. There are both larger flies and smaller flies, which I guess are female and male respectively. (I'm not sure which one this is.) Sometimes I see the big and little ones flying together, see this photo. These flies have a pretty loud whine when they fly, and when the fly together, they make a chord! I guess this patch of oak leaves that gets morning sun is their "hookup" place. (San Marcos Pass, 16 January 2017)
ScientificName: Bombyliidae; Diptera; Bombylius
associatedTaxa: Bombyliidae; Diptera; Bombylius

File.Name: h116c_h116-038.jpg
Notes: 31514611444 Here is a cute little Jumping Spider (Salticidae) on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) - staring at me! There's something unnerving about making eye contact with such a tiny critter, but here it is. There are lots of these little jumpers that I can't tell apart, and this is one of them. This is the first one I've seen for a couple of months. (San Marcos Pass, 16 January 2017)
ScientificName: Salticidae
associatedTaxa: Salticidae; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae

File.Name: h116d_h114-007.jpg
Notes: 31514371374 Here is the first new flower of native Indian Paintbrush (Castilleja affinis) in the Orobanchaceae plant family that I've found this year. You can see one of tubular real flowers poking out on the left at about the 10 o'clock position. If you look close, you can see that the real flower is green and the rest of it is showy red bracts. It seems early to find this, but I checked my records and darned if I didn't find the first one on January 29 for the past two years. That's close enough. (San Marcos Pass, 14 January 2017)
ScientificName: Castilleja affinis; Orobanchaceae
associatedTaxa: Castilleja affinis; Orobanchaceae

File.Name: h117a_h117-003.jpg
Notes:
31999737690 I was surprised to find this Western Spotted Cucumber Beetle (Diabrotica undecimpunctata, Chrysomelidae, Coleoptera) on a leaflet of native Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus vestitus, Fabaceae) on the wild edge of our garden today. You can see a bit of frass in the lower-right. Do these beetles overwinter as adults, or is this just an early one? It's a common garden pest, but it also shows up in the woods. Sure the white balance is off, but check the EXIF info. I got this photo in the dark shade above our retaining wall this morning, and this is just what it looked like. We won't get any sunlight here until after the vernal equinox. (San Marcos Pass, 17 January 2017)

It was a nice day today, but that's expected to change tomorrow. The forecast is for a trio of storms to come in, starting tomorrow night - then again on Friday, and again on Sunday! Each storm is expected to be a bit stronger than the one before. I hope this forecast pays out, but we've been burned before.

File.Name: h117b_h117-040.jpg
Notes: 32256736561 Here are the first new flowers I've seen this year of Canyon Gooseberry aka Hillside Gooseberry (Ribes californicum) in the Grossulariaceae plant family. These deciduous shrubs are beautiful as they start to leaf out and flower at the same time. The flowers remind me of ballet dancers "en pointe" with tight ballet shoes and petticoats and skirts in the air. The flowers of Fuchsia-flowered Gooseberry (Ribes speciosum) are starting to open, but not quite there yet. The glandular Bitter Gooseberry (Ribes amarum) will be the next to flower. It's interesting how our native gooseberries usually start flowering so close together - though the Chaparral Currant (Ribes malvaceum) has already been flowering for months, and it's still going strong! (Near Arroyo Burro Saddle, Santa Ynez Mountains, 17 January 2017)
CommonNames: Canyon Gooseberry; Hillside Gooseberry; Fuchsia-flowered Gooseberry; Bitter Gooseberry; Chaparral Currant
ScientificName: Ribes californicum; Grossulariaceae; en pointe; Ribes speciosum; Ribes amarum; Ribes malvaceum

File.Name: h117c_h117-008.jpg
Notes: 32376486915 This is looking close at new flowers of native Veatch Silk-Tassel (Garrya veatchii) in the Garryaceae plant family. This shrub has long dangly catkins that make for interesting (but odd) "string-of-pearl" flowers hanging like ornaments from the shrub. These shrubs are dioecious with separate male and female plants, and this photo shows the male, pollen-bearing, staminate plants. I haven't seen any of the female, seed-bearing, pistillate flowers which appear on different shrubs yet this year. I found the first flowers a month ago on December 14 growing higher in the mountains, see this photo. In a reversal of my expectations, they are just starting to flower down lower. (San Marcos Pass, 17 January 2017)
ScientificName: Garrya veatchii
associatedTaxa: NA

File.Name: h117d_h117-014.jpg
Notes: 32337119826 This looks like a fruit on this tree branch - but it's an oak, and oaks have acorns instead of fruit! I can't identify this gall on a branch of Canyon Live Oak (Quercus chrysolepis) in the Fagaceae plant family. Possibly it's the early stage gall of the Potato Gall Wasp (Andricus truckeensis)? It seems to be a stem gall, but it could also be coming from a hidden bud. This is an interesting evergreen oak with very different foliage from our usual live oaks. It has different galls too. (San Marcos Pass, 17 January 2017)
CommonNames: Canyon Live Oak; Potato Gall Wasp

File.Name: h118a_h118-014.jpg
Notes:
31584272493 I believe this photo shows three different Liverwort (Marchantiophyta) species at once, growing with a moss. I found them on bare ground in once-burned chaparral near Lake Cachuma. I believe the two large colonies in the center are Riccia trichocarpa. If you look close, maybe you can see that they are producing the flat "bumpy tongue" asexual buds known as gemmae which splash out in the rain, see this photo. There is a different liverwort with flat leaves around the sides of my photo, e.g. at the bottom-center. (I'll add a note.) I'm not sure, but I think this is a different Riccia species, maybe Riccia nigrella. Finally, if you look at the large colony on the left, you can see something that looks like moss - but isn't. I believe that is a genus Fossombronia liverwort. They have a ruffled thallus that looks like lettuce, and sometimes produce tiny lollypop-like sporophytes. I didn't realize the bare ground in this place was so rich in species. I'll have to get back on a day that's not so gloomy - and look closer. (Lake Cachuma, Santa Ynez Valley, 18 January 2017)

I had I had an errand in the Santa Ynez Valley today, so I stopped by Lake Cachuma today to see what I could find. It was pretty gloomy, not really a good photo day, but I did what I could. It feels a good storm coming in, with my two most reliable rain signs: wind from the south, and a cloud cap on the mountains. I know, "wind from the south" sounds wrong, but the storms come from the north and pull clouds from the south - which meet our east-west mountains head on, producing an "orographic enhancement". At least that's what I hope will happen tonight!
ScientificName: Marchantiophyta; Riccia trichocarpa; gemmae; Riccia; Riccia nigrella; Fossombronia; thallus
associatedTaxa: Marchantiophyta; Riccia trichocarpa; gemmae; Riccia; Riccia nigrella; Fossombronia; thallus

File.Name: h118b_h118-021.jpg
Notes: 31584029503 This is new foliage of a perennial California Peony (Paeonia californica) in the Paeoniaceae plant family, arising mushroom-like from bare ground. They remind me of new rhubarb stalks. These plants are fire-followers, but they sometimes linger for decades, as at this place near Lake Cachuma. I only noticed one flower bud, but I reckon it won't be long before we have open flowers. The flowers are worth waiting for, but you have to get low to appreciate them - see this photo. (Lake Cachuma, Santa Ynez Valley, 18 January 2017)
ScientificName: Paeonia californica; Paeoniaceae
associatedTaxa: Paeonia californica; Paeoniaceae

File.Name: h118c_h117-016.jpg
Notes: 32355519516 Here are a mating pair of little flies (Diptera) on a leaf of California Bay (Umbellularia californica) in the Lauraceae plant family. I suspect these flies are Tachinid Flies (Tachinidae), though they are not as large and bristly as some. Happy hump day! (San Marcos Pass, 17 January 2017)

Notes: 32244337592 Here are dew drops on a spider web in the grass along my trail yesterday. The drops vanish quickly when the sun hits the web, so you have to catch it right when the sun first gets to it. Maybe this web belongs to a little Funnel Web Weaver (Agelenidae) spider, though I can't see any funnel-like entrance. Happy Web Wednesday! (San Marcos Pass, 17 January 2017)

File.Name: h119a_h119-020.jpg
Notes:
32413566375 This is a pretty little diurnal (daytime) moth perched for a moment on a stem of native Chamise (Adenostoma fasciculatum, Rosaceae). I'm pretty sure this is Pyrausta dapalis - a Crambid Snout Moth family (Crambidae) of the order Lepidoptera, the first I've managed to capture this year. It has brilliant red "underwings" that make them easy to spot in the air. They are quite small, less than 1/2 inch long. For some reason I found one last year on January 25 in the same place. I seen a few flying past this winter, but this is the first one I've managed to photograph. I regard these moths as almost butterflies. (San Marcos Pass, 19 January 2017)

We had over an inch of rain last night, bring us up to almost 16 inches of rain this year, still above normal. Our next storm is expected on Friday, then another even bigger storm on Sunday. I like the way this is going.

File.Name: h119b_h119-028.jpg
Notes: 32413359705 Here is the first new flower I've found this year of native Chaparral Nightshade (Solanum xanti) in the Solanaceae plant family. This is the common form in our mountains with white-hairy stems and petals, but compare the var. hoffmannii with nearly glabrous stems in this photo from near Lake Cachuma. These are the kind of small differences that keep our local botany interesting year after year. I found the first flowers last year on January 14, so they're right on schedule. These flowers are "shakers" - the pollen-bearing stamens clasp the female style, and pollen falls out as wind and critters jiggle the flowers. The flowers usually point down, so I had to prop this stem up a bit for this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 19 January 2017)
ScientificName: Solanum xanti; Solanaceae; hoffmannii
associatedTaxa: Solanum xanti; Solanaceae; hoffmannii

File.Name: h119c_h119-040.jpg
Notes: 32292755781 I found this fantastic little coral mushroom today among fallen leaves of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) in a place where the sun never shines this time of year. This one is small, the entire cluster is only a couple of inches across. One possibility is the Meadow Coral (Clavulinopsis corniculata), but maybe it's genus Ramaria. This is probably the same kind of mushroom I show in this photo from 2012, the last time I've seen these fungi. I'm glad they survived the drought years! I expect to find coral mushrooms in Oregon, but we do have a few species here on the edge of Southern California. It's beautiful, like it belongs in the ocean. (San Marcos Pass, 19 January 2017)

File.Name: h119d_h117-004.jpg
Notes: 32035449640 This is a "beautiful bug butt" shot of a Western Spotted Cucumber Beetle (Diabrotica undecimpunctata, Chrysomelidae, Coleoptera) on a leaflet of native Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus vestitus, Fabaceae) on the wild edge of our garden, the same beetle I showed in this photo. This is a pretty neat delivery system for the "packages" its been delivering. Oh frass, and HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 17 January 2017)

File.Name: h120a_h120-014.jpg
Notes:
32390948616 This is looking at the catkins and cones on a small native White Alder (Alnus rhombifolia, Betulaceae) tree, with rushing San Jose Creek in the background. I got this photo today from the bridge on West Camino Cielo Road after good rain this morning. (There was still light rain when I got this photo.) You can also see an Incense Cedar (Calocedrus decurrens, Cupressaceae) tree on the right. I believe the cedar was introduced long ago, though they've been seeding and proliferating in Kinevan Canyon, and they are native in the backcountry. (San Marcos Pass, 20 January 2017)

We've had 3-1/4 inches of rain so far from our storm today. That's not huge, but much of it came down in a short period around 9 am when the rain rate was over 2 inches/hour, and I got this photo at 11 am when the creeks were still high. We had another inch of rain from our storm on Thursday night. Our next storm is due on Sunday, and that one might be the biggest of the three. I like the way this is going!
ScientificName: Alnus rhombifolia; Betulaceae; Calocedrus decurrens; Cupressaceae
associatedTaxa: Alnus rhombifolia; Betulaceae; Calocedrus decurrens; Cupressaceae

File.Name: h120b_h120-021.jpg
Notes: 31618914523 The Santa Ynez River is flowing! It's not a flood, but there is something there. I like that muddy side creek coming in below the center on the right. That's Paradise Creek which helps drain San Marcos Pass, and it looks like it's packing some water. We've had 3-1/4 inches of rain so far from our storm today. That's not huge, but much of it came down in a short period around 9 am when the rain rate was over 2 inches/hour. If you look close, you can see trees that burned during the Rey Fire last summer above the bluffs along the river in the back. That wildfire started just around the river bend in the distance, burned to the left (west) for a while, and then doubled back and burned east again into the backcountry. I wonder which had the name first - the Paradise area along the river or Paradise creek? (San Marcos Pass, 20 January 2017)
ScientificName: NA
associatedTaxa: NA

File.Name: h120c_h120-005.jpg
Notes: 32390375136 This waterfall along our driveway rarely flows, but here it is! It falls about 20 feet total, more than this photo shows. It's a small drainage and it takes some heavy rain to get it working. We've had 3-1/4 inches of rain so far from our storm today. That's not huge, but much of it came down in a short period around 9 am when the rain rate was over 2 inches/hour! The woodland around our waterfall shows Coast Live Oak and California Bay trees, with an understory of Coastal Wood Fern (Dryopteris arguta) and Wild Blackberry and still-dormant Nuttall's Snowberry. This is about as bare as I've ever seen the woods, because the snowberry is still leafless and the blackberry has been dying back during our five year drought. That makes it easier to get around in the woods, but I hope the good rain we've been having brings it back. (San Marcos Pass, 20 January 2017)
CommonNames: Coast Live Oak; California Bay; Coastal Wood Fern; Wild Blackberry; Nuttall's Snowberry
ScientificName: Dryopteris arguta
associatedTaxa: Dryopteris arguta

File.Name: h120d_h116-011.jpg
Notes: 32278887492 Here is another photo of a cute little furry Bee Fly (Bombyliidae, Diptera) perched on an oak leaf on the sunny side of our canyon, probably genus Bombylius. Compare this photo of a different fly on the same day. These are the first such flies I've seen this year. This one is perched on a fallen oak leaf in a place that gets morning sun in our canyon. There are both larger flies and smaller flies, which I guess are female and male respectively. (I'm not sure which one this is.) Sometimes I see the big and little ones flying together, see this photo. These flies have a pretty loud whine in the air, and when the fly together, they make a chord! I guess this patch of oak leaves that gets morning sun is their "hookup" place. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 16 January 2017)

associatedTaxa: Bombyliidae; Diptera; Bombylius

File.Name: h121a_h121-034.jpg
Notes:
32449776835 I found this stunning Russula mushroom in the woods today under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). It is surely in the "Russula emetica" group, probably Russula silvicola with that white stem. I didn't nibble this one, but it no doubt tastes "acrid" - hot and spicy, like slightly "off" wasabi, not quite pleasant. It's probably poisonous, but it sure is beautiful. We have other Russula's in the woods with reddish caps, but this one stands out. It's one of our prettiest mushrooms. (San Marcos Pass, 21 January 2017)

A hard rain's a-gonna fall ... at least that's the forecast for tomorrow! We've had two good storms so far in this series - storm #1 brought an inch of rain on Thursday night, and storm #2 brought us 3-1/4 inches on Friday. We had a break today. Now storm #3 is coming tonight and tomorrow, said to be the biggest of the bunch. I liked this message on the NWS Area Forecast Discussion this morning, after predicting 3-6 inches in the mountains: "There will likely be local amounts much greater than 6 inches above the city of SBA..." That's us! As of this morning, we stand at 19.45 inches of rain, 129% of normal. This new rain will get the creeks moving!

File.Name: h121b_h121-013.jpg
Notes: 32409180746 This is a small Harvestman (Opiliones) spider on the gills of a mushroom that I turned over in the woods. These arachnids only have one body part and one pair of eyes, unlike true spiders that have two and four respectively. The mushroom is a pink-spored Entoloma mushroom, probably in the Entoloma rhodopolium sensu Arora group, though I don't know its actual name. This one has a slight "farinaceous" smell (like linseed oil) and does not smell like bleach. You can see the pink spores starting to make a deposit on the gills. (San Marcos Pass, 21 January 2017)

Notes: 32328024501 I believe this is the nymph of an immature Chaparral Camel Cricket (Gammarotettix genitalis, Raphidophoridae). It's snuggled into the spent flower on the end of a cactus fruit in our garden. What's odd is that I actually found two of them on different flowers on his day, and I've also found them twice before - photos here and here. I don't know what the attraction is, but crickets like these flowers - see this photo of a different Tree Cricket on the fruit underneath. (San Marcos Pass, 19 January 2017)

I found them on a near-vertical sandstone cliff in the woods today. Liverworts also reproduce with the asexual buds known as gemmae, see this photo to see both strategies. File.Name: h122a_h121-004.jpg
Notes:
32431379256 This is the trunk of a sapling native Madrone (Arbutus menziesii, Ericaceae) tree showing the pretty "peeling bark" that these trees have - with a green sprout of native California Honeysuckle (Lonicera hispidula, Caprifoliaceae) for a highlight. I first thought this theme would be easy, but I actually had a hard time finding peeling bark in the woods, so maybe summer is a better time to look - see this photo. It's also been hard because of the rainy weather this week, wahoo! This is my photo for the Macro Mondays group, with the theme of "It's a-peeling to me". I didn't realize until I was ready to post that this photo may not quite be a macro, but I suspect the sapling is about 3 inches across. It might qualify - barely - so HMM! (San Marcos Pass, 19 January 2017)

We had a good rain today, but not the deluge I've been hoping for. We've had about 2-1/4 inches so far. Rain is still expected tonight and tomorrow, and there is another band of rain showing on the radar map right now, so maybe we'll make the low end of the 3-6+ forecast?

File.Name: h123a_h123-008.jpg
Notes:
32340486672 The photo on the right shows the first fully open flower I've found this year of native Bitter Gooseberry (Ribes amarum) in the Grossulariaceae plant family. I got that photo today, with a neat raindrop at the bottom. I got the photo on the left two days ago of a not-quite-open flower that is exposed because that one sepal has opened or been eaten. It's the same flower, look at that little stick in the upper-right of each shot! The red "petals" are actually sepals, the outermost whorl of parts that form a flower. The true petals are the inner whorl of white parts that form the flower. The photo on the left clearly shows how glandular these flowers are, compare this photo of Canyon Gooseberry (R. californicum). I even drove up a ways to check today, and the Fuschia-flowered Gooseberry (Ribes speciosum) is still not quite flowering. (San Marcos Pass, 23 January 2017)

We ended up with almost 3 inches of rain in the gauge today - not bad, but still on the low end of the 3-6+ inches that was forecast. Now the weather is expected to dry out until at least the end of the month. I'm not sorry, and a dry spell will give the mushrooms a chance to come out. hat brings us up to 22.5 inches since September 1, and 144% of normal. After five years of drought, this is very nice. Of course, the rain has to continue...

Notes: 32370227141 Mushrooms sure get remarkable shapes and textures - especially when they've been sitting out in the rain for a week! This one is a Bolete - maybe the Cracked-cap Bolete (Boletus chrysenteron aka Xerocomellus chrysenteron) or kin. I didn't want to pick it when it's so perfect as it is, but you can see just a hint of blue-stained yellow pores in the lower-left. But the real question is - what does this remind you of? My first thought is a perfectly baked muffin or maybe a scoop of parmesan mashed potatoes. Any ideas? (San Marcos Pass, 23 January 2017)

File.Name: h123c_h123-006.jpg
Notes:
32113104910 This is a group of pink-spored Entoloma mushrooms in the woods under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) trees. You can see the pink spores starting to show on the gills. This one is dark-colored and doesn't smell like bleach - which distinguishes it from the mushroom in my [Previous] photo that is light-colored and don't smell like bleach. It does have a slight "farinaceous" smell, like linseed oil. I don't feel any particular need to give these a name, and why guess? Many species of this genus are toxic, and there's no good way to tell them apart. (San Marcos Pass, 23 January 2017)

The mushroom on the right happens to be the same mushroom with the spider that I show in this photo. In fact, if you look close you can still see it huddled against the stem! It hasn't been sitting exposed to the rain - whenever I pick a mushroom to photograph, I "replant" it in the same whole to give the mycelium a chance to reattach. I don't know if that does any good, but it's a courtesy to other mushroom hunters that may come along.
