File.Name: h124a_h124-045.jpg
Notes:
31699824553 Here is the first new flower of native Bigpod Ceanothus (Ceanothus megacarpus) in the Rhamnaceae plant family, exploding like popcorn from the buds!

Nah, it's not really that dramatic, there was a whole shrub full of flowers right next door, but they are just starting to flower in the mountains. I've been waiting for them. Last year I found the first flowers on (by sheer coincidence) January 25, the year before on December 31, and the year before that on January 7. We have several different species of Ceanothus shrubs in our mountains, and this one is first. It has mostly alternate leaves and large seed pods. In a good year, the shrubs cover the south-facing slopes of the mountains in flowers, like snow. The individual flowers are intricate, like little origami boxes. I got this photo on the way back from town today, near the intersection of Highway 154 and Painted Cave Road at about 1500 feet elevation, right on the lower edge of what I consider to be "the pass" - the area from the Trout Club to the San Marcos Christian Camp, and Lizard Mouth to Knapp's Castle. (San Marcos Pass, 24 January 2017)

It was rainy last week, but it's cold today! There's snow in the backcountry and frost on the windshields this morning. That makes me appreciate how warm it's been through our recent series of storms. I'm not complaining, but it is curious.

(Our power will be off again for "scheduled maintenance" tomorrow from 8 to 6 - they say. If I don't post tomorrow, that's why.)

File.Name: h124b_h124-017.jpg
Notes: 32132396290 This is pretty new red foliage of Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca, Ericaceae) with new tumor-like galls caused by the Manzanita Leaf Gall Aphid (Tamalia coweni) in the family Aphididae of the Sternorrhyncha, Hemiptera. The specialized aphids mostly live inside the red pockets on the leaf, but they do occasionally come out - see this photo. It's curious that there are also galls of the Manzanita Leaf Fungus (Exobasidium vacinii) that look very similar, see this photo. I seem to find the aphid galls on this Bigberry Manzanita and the fungus galls on Eastwood Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glandulosa ssp. mollis). It's remarkable that the aphid galls and the fungus galls look so much alike, though I suppose they are both building on the same manzanita genome. (San Marcos Pass, 24 January 2017)

File.Name: h124c_h124-027.jpg
Notes: 32388827941 Here are different galls on leaves of Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia, Fagaceae). Both are the work of different tiny Cynipid Wasps (Cynipidae, Hymenoptera). On the left are a pair of striking "saucer galls" which may be work of the the Disk Gall Wasp (Andricus parmula) - but cp. the Saucer Gall Wasp (Andricus gigas) which is similar. I didn't recognize the gall on the right at first, but I believe it is the work of a Yellow Wig Gall Wasp (Andricus fullawayi) with hair faded, see this photo. These are both "detachable galls" which typically fall off the leaves and disappear into the leaf litter before the adults emerge later in spring. These galls are hanging on, but they will soon fall with the old leaves. These oaks are "semi-deciduous" - dropping their old leaves and getting new ones all at once in the spring, soon - so new leaf galls won't appear until that happens, maybe sired by the wasps that emerge from these galls! (San Marcos Pass, 24 January 2017)
CommonNames: Scrub Oak; Cynipid Wasps; Disk Gall Wasp; Saucer Gall Wasp; Yellow Wig Gall Wasp
ScientificName: Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Andricus parmula; Andricus gigas; Andricus fullawayi
associatedTaxa: Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Andricus parmula; Andricus gigas; Andricus fullawayi

File.Name: h124d_h124-011.jpg
Notes: 32510610995 Here is another photo of (I believe) the Yellow-Staining Milk Cap (Lactarius xanthogalactus) mushroom, formerly known as Lactarius vinaceorufescens or Lactarius chrysorheus. (Those darn mycologists!) I've been finding quite a few of these lately. If you slice the gills, they produce a white "milk" that turns yellow almost immediately. It has a acrid taste and is said to be poisonous - but it is solid and attractive. This one was growing under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) trees. If you look close below the overturned mushroom on the right, you can see that it's sitting on a clump of tiny-leaved Bedstraw - probably the California Bedstraw (Gallium californicum ssp. flaccidum, Rubiaceae). That's not important, it's just a plant group that I enjoy. (San Marcos Pass, 24 January 2017)
CommonNames: Yellow-Staining Milk Cap; Coast Live Oak; Bedstraw; California Bedstraw

File.Name: h125a_h125-046.jpg
Notes:
32148956380 Here is another photo of our usual winter dragonfly, a Variegated Meadowhawk (Sympetrum corruptum) in the family Libellulidae, Odonata. I was sitting in my chair down by the creek, and this dragonfly suddenly landed at my feet. Here it is on a fallen oak leaf. I got a quick photo, and it was gone. Usually I play hide-and-seek with this dragonfly resting on different plants near our creek, but this time I caught it on the prowl! (San Marcos Pass, 25 January 2017)

Our "scheduled maintenance" power outage didn't happen this morning, and I had to check the webpage to discover the fact. As I said once before, this is no way to run a business. The weather continues dry and chilly. In a week I'll be wondering where the rain is, but for now it's a pleasure to be outside.

This one is a native Collar Lupine (Lupinus truncatus, Fabaceae) growing in our canyon at home, with those distinctive skinny truncate leaflets. I think of it as a fire-follower, but it's happy on disturbed roadsides as well. It's actually one of my favorites, though it's less dramatic than some lupines. (San Marcos Pass, 25 January 2017)

File.Name: h125d_h125-042.jpg
Notes: 32148436710 This Cortinarius mushroom has both a slimy cap and stem, so I reckon it's subgenus Myxacium. It's actually difficult to pick up because its so slimy! This is probably a Slimy Purple Cortinarius in the Cortinarius cylindripes group - probably not that species, but something similar. The cap has blue/gray colors, and looks a little like a Blewitt, but the slime distinguishes it. It also looks a bit like the equally slimy Banded Cortinarius in this photo, but it lacks the distinct bands of slime. Sure, this one is growing under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). I found several of these today, all fresh and slimy from the weekend rain. They become less attractive when they dry out. (San Marcos Pass, 25 January 2017)

File.Name: h126a_h126-034.jpg
Notes:
32165621970 Here is the first new flower I've seen this year of the well-named native Padre's Shooting Star (Primula clevelandii aka Dodecatheon clevelandii) wildflower in the Primulaceae plant family. (I'm confused about varieties, so leave it at that!) I got this photo in a place where the sun never shines at this time of year, the white balance isn't quite right, there was a breeze ... but I've been looking for this flower ever since Jeff Goddard posted his first flower from the other side of the Santa Ynez Valley - two weeks ago on January 8! This is still early for us. I didn't find the first flowers last year until February 14 - but it was January 18 the year before. I reckon the exact dates don't matter much - it's the order that matters. (San Marcos Pass, 26 January 2017)

Hurrah, the new U.S. Drought Monitor report came out today, and we are no longer in the "D4 (Exceptional Drought)" category! We are still in the "D3 (Extreme Drought)" category, just a small piece of California that includes us. A little more rain might even change that, and the next rumor of rain comes next Wednesday or Thursday.
ScientificName: Primula clevelandii; Dodecatheon clevelandii; Primulaceae
associatedTaxa: Primula clevelandii; Dodecatheon clevelandii; Primulaceae

File.Name: h126b_h126-017.jpg
Notes: 32165376340 Here are the first new flowers I've seen of native Eastwood Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glandulosa ssp. mollis) in the Ericaceae plant family - with (I'm pretty sure) a galled flower at the top. Aphid galls are common on manzanita, but this is something else that's not described in Ron Russo's "Field Guide to Plant Galls" (UC Press, 2006) book. The "ssp. mollis" in the shrub's name refers to the long white non-glandular hairs on the petioles and stems. This is our other native manzanita, different from the Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca) that I show in in many other photos, e.g. here. A big difference is that these make burls, while the other one doesn't. There are more differences in the flowers and fruit. (San Marcos Pass, 26 January 2017)

File.Name: h126c_h126-004.jpg
Notes: 32503355016 I believe these fine mushrooms are Zeller's Bolete (Xerocomellus zelleri aka Boletus zelleri). They have a brilliant color combo - dark "fuscous" cap with a frosted look and not at all cracked, yellow pores that do not stain blue, and a red stem with only hints of yellow. I found them growing under a mix of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) and Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca, Ericaceae). They are quite beautiful - though these specimens are a little the worse for being out in the rain last weekend. (San Marcos Pass, 26 January 2017)

File.Name: h126d_h124-016.jpg
Notes: 32421625591 Here is another photo of a pretty little diurnal (daytime) moth perched for a moment on a dead stem. I have another recent photo here - curiously, in close to the same place. For some reason, they like it here. I'm pretty sure this is Pyrausta dapalis - a Crambid Snout Moth family (Crambidae) of the order Lepidoptera. It has brilliant red "underwings" that make them easy to spot in the air. They are quite small, less than 1/2 inch long. This is also a pretty nice butt shot, so HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 24 January 2017)

File.Name: h127a_h127-012.jpg
Notes:
31717992764 This is a clutch of tiny insect eggs on the underside of a leaf of Goldencup Oak aka Canyon Live Oak (Quercus chrysolepis) in the Fagaceae plant family. They are stacked in neat layers, like hexagons, with six neighbors each. The eggs are tiny, about 120 pixels across in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to less than 1/2 mm. (See here for how I figure.) I suspect these are the eggs of true bugs in the order Heteroptera, Hemiptera. I had to check my records because I had the vague feeling that I've seen this before. Hah, see this photo from 24 January 2015, almost exactly two years ago! I don't know whose eggs they are, but they are consistent! (San Marcos Pass, 27 January 2017)

We're being invaded by ants right now in our house, mostly after the cat's food. I fighting back with the vacuum cleaner, duct tape, and "Bar Keeper's Friend" cleanser which they don't like! I'm pretty sure the ants are the California Velvety Tree Ant (Liometopum occidentale, Dolichoderinae, Formicidae, Hymenoptera) with a strong unpleasant smell. These are the same ants that devil us later in the springtime, see this photo. But isn't it early for that??

File.Name: h127b_h127-020.jpg
Notes: 31749930253 Here are two different flies (order Diptera) in the woods that are both "just a fly" - but there are differences. The fly on the right is the same one I showed in my [Previous] photo - maybe a Blue Blowfly (Calliphora vicina), though it's curious that it's in the woods rather than in the trash. This fly is on a leaf of of native Eastwood Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glandulosa ssp. mollis) in the Ericaceae plant family, with flowers nearby. I'm not sure about the fly on the left. I think I see a hint of a subscutellum, so maybe it's a Tachinid Fly (Tachinidae)? It has an interesting face and a hairy abdomen that doesn't look especially bristly. It's on a leaf of not-quite-flowering California Bay (Umbellularia californica, Lauraceae). So many flies! The Fly Day Friday group has a two photo limit, so I won't post this one, but HFDF anyway! (San Marcos Pass, 27 January 2017)

File.Name: h128a_h128-040.jpg
Notes:
31767785723 Here is another photo of a Mourning Cloak (Nymphalis antiopa) butterfly in the canyon today - sticking out its tongue after taking moisture from under the oak leaves. See my [Previous] photo for a group shot. Their food plants include willow and cottonwoods, so they should be common in our canyon, but I don't often find them - mostly in midwinter. This is one of a trio of large butterflies that I mostly see on warm days in the middle of winter. For some reason, I managed to photograph all three today, including the Red Admiral and the California Tortoiseshell! (San Marcos Pass, 28 January 2017)

We've had a few frosty mornings recently, but it felt like spring today. It was a butterfly day - but I've still only seen a few new butterflies, and none wanted to pose. Groundhog Day happens in a few days on February 2, marking the halfway point between winter and spring. We're still past normal rainfall with 22.70 inches for the water year, but the rain has to continue if we're going to make our 34 inch average. Our next chance of rain comes on next Thursday or Friday.

File.Name: h129a_h128-044.jpg
Notes:
31788732873 I found this nymph of an Assassin Bug (Reduviidae, Heteroptera, Hemiptera) on a leaf of Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus, Rosaceae) in the woods yesterday. You can see its mighty proboscis folded under its head, and what interesting antennae it has. It doesn't seem the right time of year to be finding nymphs, but here it is - and compare this photo and this photo. The first one is surely a match, though I don't see any sign of thoracic spurs. (San Marcos Pass, 28 January 2017)

My usual custom is to post today's photos first, but I made an exception today for these good shots from yesterday. It was nice enough today, but I'll admit I'm getting distracted by national events. They are getting in the way of my photos. I'm pissed.

Notes: 32221296940 Here is another photo of a cute little Bee Fly (Bombyliidae, Diptera) perched on an oak leaf on the sunny side of our canyon, probably genus Bombylius. It's got its snoot up in the air! This fly is perched on a fallen oak leaf in a place that gets morning sun in our canyon. There are both larger flies and smaller flies, which I guess are female and male respectively. Sometimes I see the big and little ones flying together, see this photo. These flies have a pretty loud whine in the air, at a different pitch for the different size flies. When the fly together, they make a chord! I guess this patch of oak leaves that gets morning sun is their "hookup" place. (San Marcos Pass, 28 January 2017)

File.Name: h129c_h129-011.jpg
Notes:
31756383674 Here are new flowers and foliage of native Wild Cucumber aka Chilicothe or Manroot (Marah macrocarpus) in the Cucurbitaceae plant family growing over dead stems in the woods. You can see a spring-like tendril in the background, featured in my [Previous] photo. The vines have exuberant new growth, coming from the enormous "man-root" of the plant. See Gerry Queener's photo (of a related species) here! Many years ago they had a large root on display at the Santa Barbara Botanic Garden that flowered for several years despite being out of the ground! (San Marcos Pass, 29 January 2017)

We also have this plant's cousin Marah fabaceus which grows more in the backcountry and has smaller more yellow flowers. That one also grows as a fire-follower higher in the mountains, and it's confusing where they intersect. But M. macrocarpus has 8-12 smaller seeds in its spiky fruit, and M. fabaceus has just four larger ones. See this photo for the difference. Both of these plants are monoecious with separate male and female flowers on the same plant. My photo shows the plentiful male staminate flowers. The female pistillate flowers are more solitary, there's one visible in the center at the bottom.
ScientificName: Marah macrocarpus; Cucurbitaceae; Marah fabaceus; M. macrocarpus; M. fabaceus; monoecious

You can see a bit of the growing plant below. The coiled tendrils are neat. If you start at that flat place in the center of the tendril, you can see that they twist clockwise to the left and counterclockwise to the right. You can see more of the plant in my [Next] photo, here. This is my photo for the Macro Mondays group, with the theme of "contraptions". Is this spring-like coil really a contraption? Well, it's certainly a mechanism, so HMM! (San Marcos Pass, 24 January 2017)

32466953382 I found this gorgeous Mourning Cloak (Nymphalis antiopa) butterfly perched on a shrub in the canyon today. It was catching some rays in the sun. This is one of a trio of large butterflies that I mostly see on warm days in the middle of winter, like today. For some reason, I managed to photograph all three a couple of days ago and again today. Compare this photo from two days ago. I saw at least six of them today, more than I've seen before. They look black in the air, but what a rich brown they are at rest. It's hard to complain about such a lovely day - it was 70 degrees today, and we even have some sun on our upstairs porch. We do have a good chance of more rain later this week.

The moth has a pair of long antennae, but it also has what look like shorter antennae between them. I believe these are enlarged and recurved labial palps. File.Name: h131a_h131-003.jpg
Notes:
31826675373 Here is a cluster of new plants of native Indian Warrior (Pedicularis densiflora) in the Orobanchaceae plant family. Most of the red color belongs to the leafy bracts from which the flowers arise. These plants are root-parasites of several different chaparral shrubs, surely Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca, Ericaceae) in this place. Apparently, the plants form haustoria - like mistletoe - to connect to their host species. There seem to be fewer plants this year, but they are doing well. (San Marcos Pass, 31 January 2017)

It was breezy this morning with winds from the south - what I think of as our rain wind. I know, "wind from the south" sounds wrong, but the storms come from the north and pull clouds from the south. We have "rain likely" on Thursday and Friday, though it doesn't sound like much of a storm. The woods are already starting to feel dry, so I hope it happens.

See this photo of the flower. The bees are small, not tiny, and they look like little Bumble Bees. I don't know what they are, and I would appreciate advice. I'll keep trying to get a clear photo of a bee on the flowers, but they move fast! (San Marcos Pass, 31 January 2017)

File.Name: h131c_h130-040.jpg
Notes: 32515440731 Here is a pair of furry Bee Flies (family Bombyliidae, Diptera) perched for just a moment on a leaf of Mugwort (Artemisia douglasiana, Asteraceae). I believe they are genus Bombylius. I often see them hovering together in the air, but here they are together. I say they are perched, but you can see some flutter in their wings. I guess they are a courting pair with the larger female in the back and the smaller male up front. But I'm confused because isn't the female hounding the male? Maybe I have their genders reversed? Courtship is never easy! This isn't a fluke, see this photo of a similar scene. These flies have a pretty loud whine in the air, at a different pitch for the different size flies. When the fly together, they make a chord! This is near the patch of oak leaves where I usually find these flies. I guess this place that gets morning sun is their "hookup" place. Maybe I should save this for Wednesday hump day, but they aren't actually "doing it". Happy hump day anyway! (San Marcos Pass, 30 January 2017)

Notes: 32637689175 Maybe this odd mushroom is the Spreading Brown Cup Fungus (Peziza repanda) or kin, but I really don't know. I've been finding a number of these under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) trees in the woods lately. I didn't have my knife for scale, but they are three or four inches across. There is no stalk to speak of and no ribs on the outside, unlike the Brown Ribbed Elfin Cup (Helvella acetabulum) in this photo. Sometimes when you touch them, they release a cloud of spores from the inside fertile surface. I've tried to get a photo of that, but not yet. (San Marcos Pass, 31 January 2017)

File.Name: h201a_h201-013.jpg
Notes:
32278442790 Here are photos our three common Gooseberry (Ribes) shrubs in the Grossulariaceae plant family that grow in the mountains. These shrubs all have nodal spines, but we also have two other spineless Currant species of Ribes in our mountains.

On the left is a flower of Bitter Gooseberry (Ribes amarum) which is distinguishable by its glandular petiole and ovary - and leaves too. In the middle is the Canyon Gooseberry aka Hillside Gooseberry (Ribes californicum) which is distinguished by its glabrous petiole and ovary - and foliage too. There are other differences between these two, like the color of the anthers. (Is that consistent?) On the right is the first fully-open flower I've seen this year of Fuchsia-flowered Gooseberry (Ribes speciosum). This one is easy to recognize, though the key difference is that it has "erect sepals" rather than "reflexed sepals" of the other two. The word "reflexed" sounds wrong at first, but they do point along the axis of the flower rather than backwards.

I got the first and last photos today, and the middle one a week ago. All are from different places on San Marcos Pass. It's pretty neat how these related shrubs all flower at about the same time. (San Marcos Pass, 1 February 2017)

Tomorrow is Groundhog Day, midway between solstice and equinox. The days are getting longer and the sun is a bit higher, we are starting to get a bit of sun in the afternoon on our upstairs porch. The old story goes that if a groundhog sees it's shadow on this day, then we're due for six more weeks of winter - until the equinox. (I did a stumper about this many years ago.) We don't have groundhogs in California, but I'll report if any gopher sticks its head out tomorrow. The forecast is for rain and wind.
Locality: I did a stumper about this many years ago.) We don't have groundhogs in California
CommonNames: Gooseberry; Currant; Bitter Gooseberry; Canyon Gooseberry; Hillside Gooseberry; Fuchsia-flowered Gooseberry; Groundhog Day
ScientificName: Ribes; Grossulariaceae; Ribes; Ribes amarum; Ribes californicum; Ribes speciosum
associatedTaxa: Ribes; Grossulariaceae; Ribes; Ribes amarum; Ribes californicum; Ribes speciosum

File.Name: h201b_h201-008.jpg
Notes: 31814626304 These are the remarkably intricate new flowers of a native California Bay (Umbellularia californica) tree in the Lauraceae plant family. The flowers are beautiful and strange, related to sassafras and avocado. There's a hard-to-see pistil in the center, surrounded by an inner-circle of three fat stamens with four anthers each, then six orange glands at the base, surrounded by an outer-circle of six fat stamens with four anthers each, with a base of (usually) six petals/sepals. They are strange and wonderful for such a common tree in our woods. I found the first flowers back on December 28, but they are really just starting to come into their own. (San Marcos Pass, 1 February 2017)
ScientificName: Umbellularia californica
associatedTaxa: NA

File.Name: h201c_h130-033.jpg
Notes:
32616761336 This is the first new flower I've seen this year of native Prickly-Phlox (Leptodactylon californicum) in the Polemoniaceae plant family. The shrubs looked quite dead a month ago, but they've come back to life. You can see more buds waiting their turn. (San Marcos Pass, 30 January 2017)

(I've heard a few people suggest that this will be a memorable wildflower year, but I'm not convinced. Yes, we've had good rain this year after five years of drought. But many shrubs and perennials form their buds last year, and they will not benefit from the rain this year. I also worry that the non-native weeds will have a field day. Time will tell.)
Locality: I've heard a few people suggest that this will be a memorable wildflower year

File.Name: h201d_h131-021.jpg
Notes: 32657060805 This is the inner circle of a tiny Trashline Orbweaver (genus Cyclosa, Araneidae) spider, with the spider hanging upside down in the center. That odd bump on its abdomen is quite distinctive. The whole web is small, about the size of a CD, and this inner circle is like the hole in the CD. If you try to follow the webbing around and around, you find it's a spiral. The thick white silk at the top and bottom are the stabilimentum of the spider. These spiders typically decorate their stabilimentum with the body parts of their prey, but this one is just getting started. Happy Web Wednesday! (San Marcos Pass, 31 January 2017)
ScientificName: Cyclosa
associatedTaxa: NA

File.Name: h202a_h202-023.jpg
Notes:
32296151520 This is a very small Orb-Weaver Spider (Araneus, Araneidae) on a a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). It looks like one of the large bottlecap-size spiders (photo here) that collect under the eaves of our house in the fall, though it's much smaller. I got this macro photo at 1:1 magnification, so I can measure the spider by counting pixels. The spider is about 600 pixels across in my original photo, which comes to 2.4 mm - less than 1/10 inch, about the size of a match head. (See here for how I figure.) I believe this one is Araneus bispinosus or Araneus montereyensis. In a comment to another photo, Alice Abelia says to look at "the way the abdomen dips between the two humps" - but I can't really see if there's a dip or not. I didn't see any sign of a web, but I was lucky to see this spider at all! (San Marcos Pass, 2 February 2017)

I got this photo in very light rain today - just a whisper of rain, like the damp edge of virga, that rain that doesn't quite make it to the ground. The rain was no problem, but there was also a pretty steady breeze. I took lots of photos to get this keeper. If any gopher happened to stick his head out of the ground today on Groundhog Day, he didn't see his shadow - which means an early spring? Today is midway between solstice and equinox. We'll see what happens - without asking the gopher!
CommonNames: Orb-Weaver Spider; Coast Live Oak; Groundhog Day

It's a wasp with no sign of a "wasp waist" - like all sawflies. There are many different kinds. Some of their larvae become real pests of our oak trees, see this photo, but I don't know which ones. File.Name: h202c_h202-020.jpg
Notes: 32552701231 Here is a group of early caterpillars of Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterflies munching foliage of native Southern Bush Monkey Flower (Mimulus aurantiacus var. pubescens aka Diplacus longiflorus, Scrophulariaceae or Phrymaceae). These are the same web-spinning caterpillars that I find in summer, for example this photo. They form their communal webs on their host plants in the summer, then form tough skins and drop to the forest floor to over-winter. They re-emerge in the following spring and get fat on their host plants. I don't believe they're actually communal when they re-emerge, but I often find groups of them. They pupate into butterflies in May and June. It's a curious cycle, but it works for them. (San Marcos Pass, 2 February 2017)
CommonNames: Variable Checkerspot; Southern Bush Monkey Flower
ScientificName: Euphydryas chalcedona; Mimulus aurantiacus; pubescens; Diplacus longiflorus; Scrophulariaceae; Phrymaceae
associatedTaxa: Euphydryas chalcedona; Mimulus aurantiacus; pubescens; Diplacus longiflorus; Scrophulariaceae; Phrymaceae

File.Name: h202d_h127-013.jpg
Notes: 32634174366 Here are two photos of a small Crab Spider (Thomisidae) on a leaf of Greenbark Ceanothus (Ceanothus spinosus, Rhamnaceae) a few days ago. I guess I shouldn't be too surprised to find this spider in midwinter - see this photo of another one taken a few weeks ago. I believe this spider is another genus Mecaphesa. Half of this photo is a good butt shot, so HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 27 January 2017)

associatedTaxa: Thomisidae; Ceanothus spinosus; Rhamnaceae; Mecaphesa

File.Name: h203a_h203-024.jpg
Notes:
32313918020 Here is real California gold - Chanterelle (Cantharellus californicus) mushrooms! They are large mushrooms, the size of your open hand, with blunt ridges instead of proper gills. These are the first ones that I've collected since 2013, except for this oddball that my son found last year. That's actually a good sign. It means that the mushroom mycelium has survived five years of drought in the ground as mycorrhiza on the roots of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). This photo is a bit blurred because I got it in the rain today after wiping my lens with a pocketful of damp paper towel. The rain is also a good sign! The mushrooms are edible and delicious. This is one time us knowledgeable naturalist peasants can eat like kings, see this photo. I foresee that these mushrooms will meet chicken and garlic and pasta in their immediate future... (San Marcos Pass, 3 February 2017)

Our rain today has been better than expected - over 1-1/4 inch so far. It's been "slow rain" - the best for soaking in. This gives us 24.05 inches for the rain year, still 133% of normal. We might see the sun tomorrow, then even more rain on Sunday night and Monday!
ScientificName: Cantharellus californicus; mycelium; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae
associatedTaxa: Cantharellus californicus; mycelium; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae

File.Name: h203b_h203-011.jpg
Notes: 32313662180 This is our first new flower of native Hummingbird Sage (Salvia spathacea) in the Lamiaceae plant family in the rain today, with green raindrops reflecting the woods. This new flower seems unusually pale, but the deep maroon anthers make up for it. Hah, I got almost the same photo last spring, see this photo. I reckon it's a classic pose. (San Marcos Pass, 3 February 2017)
ScientificName: Salvia spathacea
associatedTaxa: NA

File.Name: h203c_h203-006.jpg
Notes: 32692699675 I found this little Chaparral Camel Cricket (Gammarotettix genitalis, Raphidophoridae) floating in our full plastic trash can/rain barrel today. I moved it to a garden table nearby and got this photo in the rain today. It didn't want to pose, and I had to chase it for a while before I got this photo. Some gratitude! Here it is, with raindrops. They lack wings, so I'm not sure if this is an adult or nymph. I've been finding a few nymphs on the cactus fruit in our garden not too far away from this one, see here and here - but why? (San Marcos Pass, 3 February 2017)
ScientificName: Gammarotettix genitalis
associatedTaxa: Gammarotettix genitalis

File.Name: h203d_h130-016.jpg
Notes: 32313130470 This is another photo of "just a fly" (order Diptera) on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) by the creek. This one looks bristly enough to be another Tachinid Fly (Tachinidae). I've been finding a lot of these lately, and that makes sense as their young are internal parasitoids (that kill their host) of caterpillars and other insects that are just starting to appear now. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 30 January 2017)
ScientificName: Tachinidae
associatedTaxa: Quercus agrifolia

File.Name: h204a_h204-031.jpg
Notes:
31871999484 I believe this fine mushroom is the Jonquil Amanita (Amanita gemmata), at least it's in this group - the species may not be the true Amanita gemmata of Europe. Another possibility is Amanita aprica, see this discussion at Mushroom Observer. It's interesting that it is growing under a native Madrone (Arbutus menziesii) tree in the Ericaceae plant family rather than a conifer. This mushroom has an attractive yellow cap with powdery white "warts" which are the remnants of the universal veil, an obscure skirt-like ring on the stem that disappears with handling, and a thick collar-like volva at the base that is easy to break. It looks a bit like a yellow Amanita muscaria, but the volva is different. It's an attractive mushroom, but definitely not edible. This is the first one I've seen since 2013. Five years of drought has taken a toll, but hopefully this means that the fungusmycelium has survived the drought in the ground as mycorrhiza on the tree roots. This is the only one I found, but I hope there will be more. They are beautiful. (San Marcos Pass, 4 February 2017)

The chanterelle mushrooms last night were delicious - so firm and solid, see this photo from Oregon. May there be many more! It was mostly cloudy today, though the sun came out in full glory - and contrasty light - just as I found this mushroom. Rain is likely again on Sunday night and on into next week. I like this weather!
ScientificName: Amanita gemmata; Amanita aprica; Arbutus menziesii; Ericaceae; Amanita muscaria; mycelium
associatedTaxa: Amanita gemmata; Amanita aprica; Arbutus menziesii; Ericaceae; Amanita muscaria; mycelium

File.Name: h204b_h204-038.jpg
Notes: 32591642781 Here are two shots of what I believe is a "Poison Pie" (Hebeloma crustuliniforme) mushroom in the woods today. They have brown spores and viscid caps when fresh, but the slime dries out and I usually find them covered with debris and tiny Springtail (order Collembola) insects that get stuck in the slime - or are they feeding on it? I consider this a field mark! Most of the springtails are too tiny to see, but you can make out a couple of larger Globular Springtails (Symphypleona, Collembola). Like the common name of this mushroom suggests, don't eat it. They are said to cause severe gastrointestinal distress. I remember these being common, but five years of drought has taken a toll. This is the first one I've seen in a few years. (San Marcos Pass, 4 February 2017)
ScientificName: Hebeloma crustuliniforme; Collembola; Symphypleona; Collembola
associatedTaxa: Hebeloma crustuliniforme; Collembola; Symphypleona; Collembola

File.Name: h204c_h204-027.jpg
Notes: 31871257994 These large portabello-size Agaricus mushrooms looks delicious, but I believe they are the ill-tasting Felt-Ringed Agaricus (Agaricus hondensis). I put my 3-1/2 inch pocket knife in the photo for scale. Look at the felt-like partial veil ring on the exposed mushroom on the right, and compare the less substantial ring of the mushroom in my [Previous] photo. I nibbled a piece, and it starts good, but soon gets a nasty phenol-like taste that's hard to spit out. This one is toxic, causing gastrointestinal upset. Compare my [Previous] photo of (I think) a tasty mushroom growing in similar leaf mulch under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). I find our woodland Agaricus mushrooms to be a confusing group! (San Marcos Pass, 4 February 2017)
ScientificName: Agaricus; Agaricus hondensis; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae
associatedTaxa: Agaricus; Agaricus hondensis; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae

File.Name: h204d_h202-025.jpg
Notes: 32591080551 This large portabello-size Agaricus mushroom looks delicious, and maybe it is. I found these growing in deep leaf mulch under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). They have the sweet almond-like smell of the Woodland Agaricus (Agaricus silvicola or A. abruptibulbus), and the ring seems to be less substantial than that of the ill-tasting Felt-Ringed Agaricus (Agaricus hondensis) mushroom in my [Next] photo. I nibbled a bit, and it doesn't have any phenol-like taste, so I suspect it's the good one - but "once bitten, twice warned!" Compare my [Next] photo. I find our woodland Agaricus mushrooms to be a confusing group. (San Marcos Pass, 2 February 2017)
CommonNames: Coast Live Oak; Woodland Agaricus; Felt-Ringed Agaricus
ScientificName: Agaricus; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Agaricus silvicola; A. abruptibulbus; Agaricus hondensis
associatedTaxa: Agaricus; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Agaricus silvicola; A. abruptibulbus; Agaricus hondensis

File.Name: h205a_h205-028.jpg
Notes:
32696209806 I found this large Carpenter Ant (Camponotus, Formicidae, Hymenoptera) swimming in our full plastic trash can/rain barrel today. I moved it to a nearby cactus fruit and got a photo. Maybe it's Camponotus semitestaceus? They look so robotic! I find these ants in the woodpile when I split firewood, and we have them in our home. BugGuide says that they "Do not eat wood, only nest in it, and usually only after fungi have softened it." I'm not sure I believe that. (San Marcos Pass, 5 February 2017)

It was gloomy all day as our new storm starts to move in. We have a 100% chance of good rain later tonight, then showers through Wednesday. There's more rain next weekend! There might be some wind with our next storm. All this recent weather is a pleasure!
ScientificName: Camponotus; Formicidae; Hymenoptera; Camponotus semitestaceus
associatedTaxa: Camponotus; Formicidae; Hymenoptera; Camponotus semitestaceus

File.Name: h205b_h205-017.jpg
Notes: 32356533580 Here is a neat cluster of dark-spored Sulfur Tuft aka Clustered Woodlover (Hypholoma fasciculare aka Naematoloma fasciculare) mushrooms growing from buried wood in the woods. The gills of these fresh mushrooms are pale yellow, but they will turn dark as they mature. The mushrooms are said to be poisonous - with a bitter taste to keep you away. I wish all poisonous mushrooms were so obliging! These are the first ones I've seen in a few years, thanks to our recent rains. (San Marcos Pass, 5 February 2017)
ScientificName: Hypholoma fasciculare; Naematoloma fasciculare
associatedTaxa: Hypholoma fasciculare; Naematoloma fasciculare

File.Name: h205c_h205-013.jpg
Notes: 32695711666 Here are some kind of "pleurotoid" mushrooms growing on the dead trunk of (I believe) a White Alder (Alnus rhombifolia, Betulaceae) tree growing along San Jose Creek in Kinevan Canyon. The mushrooms were too high in the tree to get close to, but look how fanciful that one in the upper-left is - like the hands of Shiva! Maybe they are older specimens of the Oyster Mushroom (Pleurotus ostreatus), but maybe not. (San Marcos Pass, 5 February 2017)
ScientificName: Alnus rhombifolia; Betulaceae; Pleurotus ostreatus
associatedTaxa: Alnus rhombifolia; Betulaceae; Pleurotus ostreatus

File.Name: h205d_h201-024.jpg
Notes: 32695287856 I put a small piece of pepper jack cheese on a branch of native Greenbark Ceanothus (Ceanothus spinosus, Rhamnaceae) that was inhabited by ants and aphids to see what would happen. The ants literally dropped their aphids to go for the cheese! Look, you can see a couple of stranded aphids just below the center. I believe the ants are the California Velvety Tree Ant (Liometopum occidentale, Dolichoderinae). I'm not sure what kind of aphids these are, but I'm pretty sure the become the black aphids in this photo. I don't really approve of baiting for photos, but this is my photo for the Macro Mondays group, with the theme of "Say Cheese!" This was really just a quick shot to test my idea, but the weather turned funky this week with rain and gloom, so here it is. HMM! (San Marcos Pass, 1 February 2017)
CommonNames: Greenbark Ceanothus; California Velvety Tree Ant
ScientificName: Ceanothus spinosus; Rhamnaceae; Liometopum occidentale; Dolichoderinae
associatedTaxa: Ceanothus spinosus; Rhamnaceae; Liometopum occidentale; Dolichoderinae

File.Name: h206a_h206-031.jpg
Notes:
32714938636 I found this native terrestrial Land Snail (Gastropoda) crawling on our porch today. I moved it to the hood of my white truck for a photo. Hah, you'd think that all the rain would do a better job of washing my truck! Usually when I find them (rarely) they withdraw, but this one relaxed and started moving. I actually had to use my knife to ease it off my truck so I could release it in the woods! I believe this is a Shoulderband Snail, genus Helminthoglypta in the family Helminthoglytidae. I don't often find the living snails. There seem to be lots of regional species, and I won't try to guess what this is. (San Marcos Pass, 6 February 2017)

We had good rain last night - 2.65 inches on the pass according to the Santa Barbara County rain page, a bit less by my gauge. The rain was winding down today, and I did a rain walk in the drizzle to get my photos today of neat things that the rain brings. It sound like we'll have more rain later tonight and tomorrow, and again on Friday. Our total is now 26.58 inches, 142% of normal. Looking good!
ScientificName: Gastropoda; Helminthoglypta; Helminthoglytidae
associatedTaxa: Gastropoda; Helminthoglypta; Helminthoglytidae

File.Name: h206b_h206-008.jpg
Notes: 32375738420 Here are tiny Slime Molds (Myxomycetes or Myxogastria) growing on a piece of bark from Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods. These look like little insect eggs, but something different will come from their spores, see this photo for a bit more info. I believe the photo shows sessile (without a stalk) sporangia or plasmodiocarps. You can see some slime on the bark, especially on the right, that may be the plasmodial slime that they grow from. In the past I've called these Diderma effusum, but I'm really not sure. We have white ones too, maybe an immature form of these - see this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 6 February 2017)
ScientificName: Myxomycetes; Myxogastria; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Diderma effusum
associatedTaxa: Myxomycetes; Myxogastria; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Diderma effusum

File.Name: h206c_h206-004.jpg
Notes: 32632148521 Here is a fresh cluster of Honey Mushrooms (Armillariella mellea) growing from a stump of native California Bay (Umbellularia californica, Lauraceae). I thought these mushrooms were all done for the year, but apparently not. The white dusting on the caps isn't snow, but a deposit of white spores falling from the mushrooms above. (San Marcos Pass, 6 February 2017)
ScientificName: Armillariella mellea; Umbellularia californica; Lauraceae
associatedTaxa: Armillariella mellea; Umbellularia californica; Lauraceae

File.Name: h206d_h206-022.jpg
Notes: 32601929332 Here is another new flower of native Milk Maids aka Toothwort (Cardamine californica) in the Brassicaceae plant family, with raindrops. I got this photo in the light rain today - after real rain last night. They are beautiful flowers, more elegant than our usual yellow "wild mustards" that are non-native. They've been flowering since the end of December, but they've hardly had a chance to fully open with all the rain we've been having lately! It's hard to get both raindrops and the reflections inside in focus at the same time - like getting the frame of a mirror in focus as well as the image it holds. And why do the drops only sometimes reflect the woods? (San Marcos Pass, 6 February 2017)
ScientificName: Cardamine californica; Brassicaceae
associatedTaxa: Cardamine californica; Brassicaceae

File.Name: h207a_h207-004.jpg
Notes:
32621003472 This is "just a fly" (order Diptera) on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the rain today. I guess that this is another bristly Tachinid Fly (Tachinidae), the same kind of fly as this photo. Yep, I saved another shot of the same fly from the side for Fly Day Friday. (San Marcos Pass, 7 February 2017)

We've had another 6/10 inch of rain today - drizzle, but it's been drizzly all day, so it adds up. It's expected to rain much of tonight, then we have some time to dry out before our next storm on Friday.
ScientificName: Tachinidae
associatedTaxa: Quercus agrifolia

File.Name: h207b_h207-009.jpg
Notes: 32733194566 This is looking close at tiny sexual sporophytes of (I believe) the California Liverwort (Asterella californica, Marchantiophyta). I found them on a sandstone bank in the woods, near the location of this photo which shows a lot more of them. Those tiny hyaline points that stick out under the green points of the sporophytes are interesting, like a classy lady showing her slip. (I'm thinking of Claudette Colbert hitchhiking in the movie It Happened One Night (1934), though she's not showing her slip.) (San Marcos Pass, 7 February 2017)
ScientificName: sporophytes; Asterella californica; Marchantiophyta; It Happened One Night
associatedTaxa: sporophytes; Asterella californica; Marchantiophyta; It Happened One Night

File.Name: h207c_h205-026.jpg
Notes: 31959755563 This is a whole plant of native California Maiden-Hair (Adiantum jordanii) fern in the Polypodiaceae plant family growing on a sandstone bank in Kinevan Canyon, some distance from San Jose Creek. The black stems are so smooth you can wear them as earrings if you have pierced ears! They are related to the common Maidenhair Fern (Adiantum capillus-veneria) that grow in wet places, see this photo, but these grow in the woods nearby. The botanical difference is that the lobes of these are cut less deep, and converge at the base at a wider angle. Compare my photos to appreciate this difference. (San Marcos Pass, 5 February 2017)
ScientificName: Adiantum jordanii; Polypodiaceae; Adiantum capillus-veneria
associatedTaxa: Adiantum jordanii; Polypodiaceae; Adiantum capillus-veneria

File.Name: h207d_h206-015.jpg
Notes: 32393451690 These tiny yellow "chiclets" don't look too different from the Slime Molds I show two photos back, but I believe they are fungi. I don't know what kind. For one reason, they were here a couple of weeks ago, and slime molds don't usually last that long. (Though the ones I've been calling Diderma effusum do last quite some time.) For another reason, they are all different sizes, and slime molds are usually all the same size when they get around to fruiting. These are growing on a fallen Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) on the ground. (San Marcos Pass, 6 February 2017)
ScientificName: Diderma effusum; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae
associatedTaxa: Diderma effusum; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae

File.Name: h208a_h208-009.jpg
Notes:
32793598455 This is a little Woodlouse (Oniscidea, Isopoda, Crustacea) high-walking a grass stem. This is the kind that cannot roll up into a ball, maybe the genus Porcellio? I call these "sow bugs" - as distinct from the "pill bugs" in this photo. There are many different kinds, so I'll leave it at that. These are critters that the rain brings out, at least it makes them more bold and easier to spot. (San Marcos Pass, 8 February 2017)

We've had another 1/2 inch of rain since yesterday, bringing our total to 27.56 inches, about 142% of normal. We're getting closer to our average of 33.95 inches, great news after five years of drought. I got a few photos this morning, but I had something to do in town later in the day. I'll do better tomorrow, which is expected to be partly sunny before our next storm (!) comes in on Thursday night and Friday. Then we might dry out for a while.
ScientificName: Oniscidea; Isopoda; Crustacea; Porcellio
associatedTaxa: Oniscidea; Isopoda; Crustacea; Porcellio

File.Name: h208b_h208-008.jpg
Notes: 32670227321 Here are raindrops on a plant of native Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus vestitus, Fabaceae) on the wild edge of our garden. Why do some plants like lupines and these hold rain drops so well, while others shed them so fast? It's a proper stumper! (San Marcos Pass, 8 February 2017)
ScientificName: Lathyrus vestitus
associatedTaxa: NA

File.Name: h208c_h207-014.jpg
Notes: 31949930624 This is another very small Orb-Weaver Spider (Araneus, Araneidae) on a partially skeletonized leaf (see here) of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). It might even be the same spider I show in this photo from a week ago. It's the same small tree, but a different leaf a few feet away. You gotta love the raindrops! In my previous photo, I figured the size as 2.4 mm - less than 1/10 inch, about the size of a match head. Really tiny! I believe this one is Araneus bispinosus or Araneus montereyensis, and I'll leave it at that. It's got one leg holding a bit of webbing - like using your index finger so sense the line while fishing. It's not much of a web for an orb weaver, but happy Web Wednesday anyway! (San Marcos Pass, 7 February 2017)
ScientificName: Araneus bispinosus; Araneus montereyensis
associatedTaxa: Quercus agrifolia

File.Name: h208d_h206-020.jpg
Notes: 31949709284 I guess this is the web of an Orb-Weaver Spider (Araneidae) in a shrub of Climbing Penstemon (Keckiella cordifolia, Plantaginaceae) that is just starting to leaf out. I had to stare at this web for a few seconds until I noticed what's so odd about it - the web has absolutely no cross-webbing. Maybe the spider was interrupted before it finished? Happy Web Wednesday! (San Marcos Pass, 6 February 2017)
ScientificName: Araneidae; Keckiella cordifolia; Plantaginaceae
associatedTaxa: Araneidae; Keckiella cordifolia; Plantaginaceae

File.Name: h209a_h209-003.jpg
Notes:
32658375022 This is a goodbye "butt shot" of a little Woodlouse that can roll up in a ball, so I guess it's genus Armadillidium, probably Armadillidium vulgare. Compare the woodlouse that can't roll up in this photo yesterday and this photo a year ago. One difference is the yellowish color swirls on the scales. These critters are terrestrial crustaceans in the suborder Oniscidea of the order Isopoda, Crustacea. They are known as pill bugs, sow bugs, roly-polys and many more common names. These are critters that the rain brings out, and we've been having lots of rain lately. HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 9 February 2017)

It was surprising warm today in the mountains, at least 70∞, but there was pea soup fog on the coast. I'm glad I didn't have to go into town today. There is a new storm moving in tonight that should bring us good rain late tonight and tomorrow. The NWS is starting to waffle a bit about this storm, but it sounds like we'll get something. Our next chance of rain comes in a week.
ScientificName: Armadillidium; Armadillidium vulgare; Oniscidea; Isopoda; Crustacea
associatedTaxa: Armadillidium; Armadillidium vulgare; Oniscidea; Isopoda; Crustacea

File.Name: h209b_h209-007.jpg
Notes: 32658213462 This is looking close at the new flowers of native Bigpod Ceanothus (Ceanothus megacarpus) in the Rhamnaceae plant family that are starting to flower in our mountains. They are surprisingly intricate, like little origami boxes, with a blue nectar disk at the base. The flowers typically have five petals and stamens, but there is the occasional oddball like the middle one on top with six stamens. In a good year, the shrubs cover the south-facing slopes of the mountains in flowers, like snow. I guess in all those gazillions of individual flowers, a few oddballs are to be expected! I hope this will turn out to be one of those good years. (San Marcos Pass, 9 February 2017)
ScientificName: Ceanothus megacarpus
associatedTaxa: NA

File.Name: h209c_h209-011.jpg
Notes: 32811299895 This is a crustose lichen on a sandstone outcrop, but that's all I understand about this photo. It looks like several different lichens with a fungus growing on top. Lichens do typically produce apothecia which are the fruiting bodies of the fungus part of the lichen, and maybe that's what the brown bits are. Or are they a different lichen? I'm still not sure. (San Marcos Pass, 9 February 2017)
ScientificName: apothecia
associatedTaxa: apothecia

File.Name: h209d_h205-001.jpg
Notes: 32810975045 Here is another photo of not one but two tiny crickets snuggling "butts up" in the flower scar at the end of a cactus fruit in the garden. I believe they are the Chaparral Camel Cricket (Gammarotettix genitalis, Raphidophoridae). I guess they're nymphs because they're so small, but the adults lack wings, so I can't be sure if these are adults or nymphs. What's odd is that I've found them here before, photos here, here, and here. I don't know what the attraction is, but crickets like it here - see this photo of a different Tree Cricket on the fruit. I have another photo of a cricket out in the open here. This is a cute butt shot, so HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 5 February 2017)
ScientificName: Gammarotettix genitalis

File.Name: h210a_h207-007.jpg
Notes:
32014518603 This is another photo of "just a fly" (order Diptera) on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the rain a couple of days ago. It's the same fly on the same oak leaf as this photo - just taken from the side rather than straight on. I guess that this is another bristly Tachinid Fly (Tachinidae), the same kind of fly as this photo. I like the raindrops - especially the one balanced on top of its head. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 7 February 2017)

Rain, rain, rain ... it never rained hard today, but the light rain never stopped either. We've got about 6/10 inch in our rain gauge. I did go out to the front porch today with my camera on my tripod ... but it was too wet even for a rain walk. On the other hand, the photos I posted today are all shots I could have gotten in the rain. Tomorrow should be clear, but the wind might pick up too.
ScientificName: Diptera; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Tachinidae; could
associatedTaxa: Diptera; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Tachinidae; could

File.Name: h210b_h208-006.jpg
Notes: 32675400572 I found this soggy little beetle (Coleoptera) on a soggy cactus fruit in the garden. I'm not sure, but it might be a Broad-nosed Weevil (subfamily Entiminae, Curculionidae). The antenna placement seems right for a weevil, and the first antennae segment is quite long - see BugGuide for more info. It's a Prickly Pear (Opuntia, Cactaceae) cactus, and those little glochid hair-like spines are particularly nasty if you brush against them, much worse than the larger cactus spines! (San Marcos Pass, 8 February 2017)
ScientificName: Coleoptera; Entiminae; Curculionidae; Opuntia; Cactaceae; glochid

File.Name: h210c_h208-004.jpg
Notes: 32675259372 This is a new leaf of native Canyon Lupine (Lupinus latifolius) in the Fabaceae plant family in the canyon with raindrops. Lupines catch raindrops better than any other plant I know, except maybe the Canyon Lupine (Lupinus latifolius, Fabaceae) in this photo. Why do some plants hold rain drops so well, while others shed them so fast? It's a proper stumper! (San Marcos Pass, 8 February 2017)

File.Name: h210d_h207-003.jpg
Notes:
31985556404 Here are flower buds of Arroyo Willow (Salix lasiolepis, Salicaceae) by the creek - with raindrops. I found the first open catkins last year on February 13, and it won't be long before these open as well. The flowers attract many early insects. (San Marcos Pass, 7 February 2017)

I was wondering why this photo has getting more views than usual, and then I realized why. Hah - I originally described them as "p*ssy willows". Yep, that p-word was coming up in remarkably many searches. C'mon...
ScientificName: Salix lasiolepis
associatedTaxa: NA

File.Name: h211a_h211-041.jpg
Notes:
32034740453 Here is a caterpillar of a Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly - with raindrops. Look on top - it's surprising that such a large drop could form on such a spiky caterpillar! That big drop is reflecting the woods, and probably me too. The scene is a shrub of native Southern Bush Monkey Flower (Mimulus aurantiacus var. pubescens, Phrymaceae). (San Marcos Pass, 11 February 2017)

We had another bout of rain last night, bringing our storm total to just over an inch. I was expecting some sun today, but it stayed cloudy and we even had a few showers. There was some breeze today, but the warnings are all to the south/east of us. Now we have a few days to dry out, with our next chance of rain on Friday. I like this weather!
CommonNames: Variable Checkerspot; Southern Bush Monkey Flower
ScientificName: Euphydryas chalcedona; Mimulus aurantiacus; pubescens; Phrymaceae
associatedTaxa: Euphydryas chalcedona; Mimulus aurantiacus; pubescens; Phrymaceae

File.Name: h211b_h211-003.jpg
Notes: 32006087414 Here are a couple of Fluted Elfin Saddle (genus Helvella) mushrooms in the woods today under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) trees. I just assumed at the time that they were the Fluted Black Elfin Saddle (H. lacunosa), but the caps actually look more brown, so they actually be the Fluted Brown Elfin Saddle (H. maculata). I don't really care, but I think of these as a sign that our mushroom season is winding down, even if rain continues. There are still many mushrooms that I haven't seen yet, so I hope that's wrong! (San Marcos Pass, 11 February 2017)
CommonNames: Fluted Elfin Saddle; Coast Live Oak; Fluted Black Elfin Saddle; Fluted Brown Elfin Saddle
ScientificName: Helvella; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; H. lacunosa; H. maculata
associatedTaxa: Helvella; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; H. lacunosa; H. maculata

File.Name: h211c_h211-022.jpg
Notes:
32724921621 This is a strange Coral Mushroom (family Clavariaceae) growing in the leaf mulch under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) trees. It's small, only two or three inches across. I find coral fungi hard to identify. I doubt that this one is genus Ramaria, but another possibility is the Crested Coral (Clavulina cristata) mushroom. I expect to find coral mushrooms in Oregon, but we do have a few species here on the edge of Southern California. I show another kind in this photo and another here. They are beautiful and strange, like they belongs in the ocean. (San Marcos Pass, 11 February 2017)

I almost forgot ... there's a tiny Fungus Gnat in the family Mycetophilidae of the Diptera visible to the left of center. I'll make a note. I have a close photo here. I like the family name of these flies, they are mushroom lovers like me! The larvae of these flies feed on different fleshy mushrooms and are apparently dependent on particular families of mushrooms.
CommonNames: Coral Mushroom; Coast Live Oak; Crested Coral; Fungus Gnat
ScientificName: Clavariaceae; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Ramaria; Clavulina cristata; Mycetophilidae; Diptera
associatedTaxa: Clavariaceae; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Ramaria; Clavulina cristata; Mycetophilidae; Diptera

File.Name: h211d_h211-029.jpg
Notes: 32807253316 My first thought was that these are white-spored Tricholoma mushrooms growing in deep leaf mulch under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) trees. My second thought is that they are a pink spored Entoloma mushrooms, maybe Entoloma medianox. I guess time will tell as the spores ripen and stain the gills. I'll check back in a few days. They are large solid mushrooms, with a "farinaceous" smell like linseed oil, notched or adnexed gills, and a radially streaked cap that is not violet and doesn't seem at all viscid, even after our rain last night. (San Marcos Pass, 11 February 2017)
ScientificName: Tricholoma; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Entoloma; Entoloma medianox
associatedTaxa: Tricholoma; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Entoloma; Entoloma medianox

File.Name: h212a_h212-021.jpg
Notes:
32747754551 I found this handsome Brown Elfin (Callophrys augustinus) butterfly in the woods today, working new flowers of native Eastwood Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glandulosa ssp. mollis) in the Ericaceae plant family. (That's botanist Alice Eastwood, not Clint!) At first the butterflies look plain brown, but when you look closer you see that they really have a palette of many colors. Up close it looks more like a pointillist painting - many color swatches, including violet and gold, combine to make the eye-catching brown. It's a Hairstreak (family Theclinea) butterfly, but you can hardly tell by looking at it with that stubby little tail. This is the first one I've managed to photograph this year. Last year I found the first one in February 21, so this is close. (San Marcos Pass, 12 February 2017)

Yesterday was gloomy and breezy, but today was sunny and calm. I also saw a Sara Orangetip butterfly on the same manzanita flowers! It's only February, but today feels like spring. We have more rain on the plate starting Thursday night.

(I've been posting my photos for the Macro Mondays group on Sunday night, but it bugs me not to be posting on Mondays as the name says. You see, I usually post in the evening, but the group want you to post on Monday by UTC/GMT/Flickr time, so I always miss the deadline. This time I'll try posting on Monday morning.)
Locality: I've been posting my photos for the Macro Mondays group on Sunday night
CommonNames: Brown Elfin; Eastwood Manzanita; Hairstreak; Sara Orangetip
ScientificName: Callophrys augustinus; Arctostaphylos glandulosa; mollis; Ericaceae; pointillist; Theclinea
associatedTaxa: Callophrys augustinus; Arctostaphylos glandulosa; mollis; Ericaceae; pointillist; Theclinea

File.Name: h212b_h212-016.jpg
Notes: 32489933920 Here is a California Tortoiseshell (Nymphalis californica) butterfly working new flowers of native Eastwood Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glandulosa ssp. mollis) in the Ericaceae plant family. I've been seeing more of these than usual this winter. Usually I find them taking moisture or sun, and this is the first time I can recall seeing one actually working flowers. (San Marcos Pass, 12 February 2017)
ScientificName: Nymphalis californica; Arctostaphylos glandulosa; mollis; Ericaceae
associatedTaxa: Nymphalis californica; Arctostaphylos glandulosa; mollis; Ericaceae

File.Name: h212c_h212-030.jpg
Notes: 32747110381 I guess for sheer gnarliness that this is a another bristly Tachinid Fly (Tachinidae, Diptera). I found several of these working the new flowers of native Eastwood Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glandulosa ssp. mollis) in the Ericaceae plant family. At first I thought of the fly in this photo, genus Belvosia in the subfamily Exoristinae. But that one has a golden butt and a black thorax, while this one has a mostly black butt and a golden thorax. Looking around on BugGude, I think this one might be in the Archytas analis complex, maybe species Archytas apicifer, of the subfamily Tachininae - though that spot on its butt gives me pause. Whew! It's an impressive fly. If my ID is right, the larvae are parasitoids of moths that overwinter inside their hosts. Think of the movie Alien.
Locality: Arctostaphylos glandulosa ssp. mollis) in the Ericaceae plant family. At first I thought of the fly in this photo
ScientificName: Tachinidae; Diptera; Arctostaphylos glandulosa; mollis; Ericaceae; Archytas analis; Archytas apicifer; Tachininae; Alien

Notes: 32746765401 Here are the first new flowers of California Saxifrage (Saxifraga californica) in the Saxifragaceae plant family, growing in their usual habitat on a shady sandstone bank in a place where the sun never shines. I did the best I could in the deep shade. They are not common in our mountains. (San Marcos Pass, 12 February 2017)

File.Name: h213a_h213-009.jpg
Notes:
32047392094 This is another small Crab Spider (Thomisidae) on a flower bud of Greenbark Ceanothus (Ceanothus spinosus, Rhamnaceae). I believe this spider is another genus Mecaphesa. I've found a few of these this winter - see this photo and this photo and this photo. I reckon these spiders overwinter as adults. This one seems attached to a bit of webbing, maybe a nest, though it's interesting that it has one foot on the web like the Orb-Weaver Spider in this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 13 February 2017)

Today was another beautiful day, warm and almost dead calm. We'll have another day or two of this, then rain is likely starting on Thursday. This might be a significant rain event. ;=)

File.Name: h213b_h213-005.jpg
Notes: 32765683731 I found a lot of these little beetles (Coleoptera) doing some damage to this new leaf of Climbing Penstemon (Keckiella cordifolia, Plantaginaceae). I also saw a few on other plants, but they were ganging up on this plant. I guess they are Flea Beetles in the group Alticini. If you look close at my photo, you can see that these beetles have enlarged femurs on their hind legs - yep, they can jump like fleas! The beetles are small and shiny. At first I thought they were oil drops on the leaves. The larger one on the right is about 740 pixels long, a bit over 1/10 inch. (See here for how I figured.) BTW, I did a web search on "beetle large femur" and this one popped right up as a BugGuide image. It's a useful tool. (San Marcos Pass, 13 February 2017)
ScientificName: Coleoptera; Keckiella cordifolia; Plantaginaceae; Alticini
associatedTaxa: Coleoptera; Keckiella cordifolia; Plantaginaceae; Alticini

File.Name: h213c_h212-037.jpg
Notes: 32046699994 Several of these pretty diurnal (daytime) moths were working the new flowers of native Eastwood Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glandulosa ssp. mollis) in the Ericaceae plant family yesterday. I believe it is a Litocala Moth (Litocala sexsignata) in the Noctuoidea group, Hodges#8597 at BugGuide. The species name "sex-signata" refers to the six white/yellow spots on the hindwings, three spots on each wing. The spots are quite distinctive. They didn't like posing, and I was lucky to get this shot! Happy Moth Monday! (San Marcos Pass, 12 February 2017)
ScientificName: Arctostaphylos glandulosa; mollis; Ericaceae; Litocala sexsignata; Noctuoidea
associatedTaxa: Arctostaphylos glandulosa; mollis; Ericaceae; Litocala sexsignata; Noctuoidea

File.Name: h213d_h209-021.jpg
Notes: 32882568685 This is looking close at a new flower of native Canyon Gooseberry aka Hillside Gooseberry (Ribes californicum) in the Grossulariaceae plant family - showing its heart-shaped anthers. The red "petals" at the top are actually sepals, the outermost whorl of parts that form a flower. The true petals are the inner whorl of white parts that form the flower. This is my photo for the Macro Mondays group, with the theme of "Heart". HMM! (San Marcos Pass, 9 February 2017)

associatedTaxa: Ribes californicum; Grossulariaceae; sepals; petals

File.Name: h214a_h214-036.jpg
Notes:
32754503972 Here is a striking Mourning Cloak (Nymphalis antiopa) butterfly perched on a stem of native Arroyo Willow (Salix lasiolepis) in the Salicaceae plant family by the creek today. You can see the buds on this willow branch, and other catkins on the same staminate shrub are open, see my [Previous] photos. The butterfly looks downright haughty in this photo. For some reason, I've been seeing more of them than usual this winter. (San Marcos Pass, 14 February 2017)

It was just a bit cooler and breezier today, maybe a sign of our next weather system approaching on Thursday and Friday. I don't want to get too excited and queer the deal, but it's said that this might be the most significant storm of the season.

File.Name: h214b_h214-011.jpg
Notes: 32064935824 I believe this is a tiny mosquito-like Dance Fly in the family Empididae, probably genus Empis. It's on a new leaf bud of Arroyo Willow (Salix lasiolepis) in the Salicaceae plant family by the creek today. It's been working the male pollen-bearing staminate catkins, see my [Previous] photo. Apparently they are mainly predators, but they are also known to take nectar with that long proboscis. I guess the pollen is a by-product? It's a dirty job, but someone's gotta do it! This is too deep of a crop, but it works. The flowers are just beginning, so I'll get more chances. (San Marcos Pass, 14 February 2017)

File.Name: h214c_h214-030.jpg
Notes: 32783608651 Here are two shots of new flower catkins on native Arroyo Willow (Salix lasiolepis) in the Salicaceae plant family by the creek today, showing the separate female seed-bearing pistillate catkins on the left and male pollen-bearing staminate catkins on the right. These shrubs are dioecious with male and female flowers on different plants, and the ladies look quite different from the gentlemen. Both are the same species, and they need each other. These catkins look wind-pollinated, but I'm sure that both male and female catkins produce nectar and are host to many insects - look at the ants on the left and see my [Next] photo for an example. (San Marcos Pass, 14 February 2017)
ScientificName: Salix lasiolepis; Salicaceae; dioecious
associatedTaxa: Salix lasiolepis; Salicaceae; dioecious

File.Name: h214d_h214-006.jpg
Notes: 32526992790 Here is a trio of native plants in the canyon that all form a rosette - a radiating cluster of leaves growing from a common center, usually at or close to the ground. The largest plant in the photo is Chaparral Yucca (Yucca whipplei, Liliaceae aka Hesperoyucca whipplei, Agavaceae). This particular plant has unusually thin leaves, maybe a sign that it is being shaded out by the oak trees. Above and to the left is a rosette of California Thistle (Cirsium occidentale var. californicum, Asteraceae). This is the one with violet flowers, on the right in this photo. These plants seem to be true biennials which form leaves in their first year, then flower in their second year. (Then die?) Below the yucca, are two rosettes of succulent Southern California Dudleya (Dudleya lanceolata, Crassulaceae) on the sandstone outcrop. There's one on the left and another hiding under the leaves directly below the yucca. That one's my favorite, and it already has flower buds! (San Marcos Pass, 14 February 2017)
CommonNames: Chaparral Yucca; California Thistle; Southern California Dudleya
ScientificName: rosette; Yucca whipplei; Liliaceae; Hesperoyucca whipplei; Agavaceae; Cirsium occidentale; californicum; Asteraceae; Dudleya lanceolata; Crassulaceae

File.Name: h215a_h215-041.jpg
Notes:
32926225145 Here is a Brown Elfin (Callophrys augustinus) butterfly ovipositing - laying eggs - on new flower buds of Blue Elderberry (Sambucus mexicana) shrub in the Caprifoliaceae plant family aka Sambucus nigra ssp. caerulea in the Adoxaceae. They seem to lay their eggs on many different plants, including dodder, madrone, ceanothus, and ... elderberry. At first the butterfly looks plain brown, but when you look closer you see that it really has a rich palette of many colors. Up close it looks like a pointillist painting - many color swatches, including violet and gold, combine to make the eye-catching brown. It's a Hairstreak (family Theclinea) butterfly, but you can hardly tell by looking at it with that stubby little tail. I'm just starting to find them. (Lake Cachuma, Santa Ynez Valley, 15 February 2017)

Our next weather system is still on track to start bringing rain tomorrow on Thursday. The NWS discussion sound almost ominous: "The second and most dynamic storm system SoCal has seen in a while will then begin to approach from the west with a classic Atmospheric River component. We are expecting 2-4 inches for coast and valleys and between 4-8 inches across the foothills and mountains. Highest rainfall totals are expected across the Santa Ynez Mtn Range." That's us. And this evening, the NWS is saying "The southerly winds will provide a significant orographic enhancement factor for south facing slopes and it`s possible the 8" rain amount might even be conservative for some of the more favored south facing spots." Yipes!

Well, we've been let down before when projected big storms didn't materialize. We'll see what happens. And yes, I'm ready to be isolated for a few days if it really happens!
ScientificName: Callophrys augustinus; Sambucus mexicana; Caprifoliaceae; caerulea; Adoxaceae; pointillist; Theclinea
associatedTaxa: Callophrys augustinus; Sambucus mexicana; Caprifoliaceae; caerulea; Adoxaceae; pointillist; Theclinea

File.Name: h215b_h215-009.jpg
Notes: 32802316671 This is looking into a new flower of California Peony (Paeonia californica) in the Paeoniaceae plant family, showing a troop of fat stamens and three reddish pistils looking out from the large carpels. This is the first open flower that I've seen this year, though I reckon they've been flowering for a while. Usually the flowers point down towards the earth, but I propped this one up with a stick. They are one of my favorite wildflowers, probably long-lasting fire-followers in this place that burned about 15 years ago. I've read that the perennial roots were once collected for export to China as a medicinal herb. I'd rather find the flowers. (Lake Cachuma, Santa Ynez Valley, 15 February 2017)
ScientificName: Paeonia californica; Paeoniaceae
associatedTaxa: Paeonia californica; Paeoniaceae

I did see the fairly large spider - maybe a Filmy Dome Spider (Neriene radiata)? Like so many of my photos, this is in a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) tree. I dig all those color swatches in the web - HWW! (San Marcos Pass, 14 February 2017)

File.Name: h216a_h216-036.jpg
Notes:
32790738922 The things we see as naturalist photographers ... all those moments will be lost in time, like tears in rain; but photos can capture the moments, for a while at least, if we have skill and luck...
(after Blade Runner.)

This ant (Formicidae, Hymenoptera) has a mosquito-like Dance Fly (Empididae, Diptera) by a leg on a female pistillate catkin of Arroyo Willow (Salix lasiolepis, Salicaceae). I've never seen this behavior before. They struggled back and forth for some time. If you follow the leg up from the ant to the fly, you can see that the leg is about to go at the top joint. The fly took off a moment later - without a leg. I don't know what happened, but the ants do seem to control certain catkins, see this photo on the left. The ant may be another California Velvety Tree Ant (Liometopum occidentale, Dolichoderinae). They seem to be everywhere. (San Marcos Pass, 16 February 2017)

We're finally starting to get some rain after several false starts today. We had some rain before 11:00 am - and then it stopped. We had some more rain about 2 pm - and then it stopped. Now we have light rain again, but the real rain is still coming later tonight and tomorrow. We've been let down before when projected big storms didn't materialize. We'll see what happens. And yes, I'm ready to be isolated for a few days if it really does happen!

File.Name: h216b_h216-055.jpg
Notes: 32563338040 Here are separate photos of (I believe) male and female Dance Flies in the family Empididae, probably genus Empis. They might even be different species, I'm not sure. The fly on the left is a female with that wide abdomen that is large with eggs. If you look close at her head, you can see a distinct bare area between her eyes. The fly on the right is a male that happens to be grooming his proboscis. He has a narrow abdomen that is more visible in my [Previous] photo. He also has "holoptic eyes" that meet in the middle of his head unlike the lady. Both these flies are working female pistillate flowers on a catkin of Arroyo Willow (Salix lasiolepis) in the Salicaceae plant family by the creek today. It was gloomy today with a breeze and rain threatening, but the flies were active and I persisted. I took hundreds of photos of these flies and kept a few. (San Marcos Pass, 16 February 2017)

Notes: 32100245714 This is a "butt shot" of the same gnarly Tachinid Fly (Tachinidae, Diptera) that I showed a few days ago in this photo. File.Name: h217a_h216-026.jpg
Notes:
32920678346 This is a different mosquito-like Dance Fly in the family Empididae on a male staminate catkin of Arroyo Willow (Salix lasiolepis, Salicaceae) by the creek. This one is visibly different from the ones I show in this photo, smaller and blacker. It's dusted with pollen from the flowers. These tiny flies do a great job of transporting pollen as they go back and forth between the separate male and female plants of this dioecious tree. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 16 February 2017)

"Oh, the wind and rain..." (listen)
We've had more than 8-1/2 inches of rain since last night, and it's still coming as I type this! It was raining so hard today that I never got out, and the highway was closed from both ends anyway. I'll get out tomorrow to inspect the damage, but the creeks drop fast after the rain stops. I would like to see the lake. The river peaked today at 13,200 cfs - the highest in 69 years of record, after the previous max of 4700 cfs in 1980! I can't find any news, but the lake might actually be full after being down below ten percent capacity during the drought - just a month ago!

(Hah - three of us foolish friends carried a raft down to Gibraltor Dam and took it down the Santa Ynez River to the first crossing after the "March Miracle" rains of 1991 when we got almost 20 inches of rain in one week. We got to the end after dark to be greeted by the Search & Rescue people that Julie had called who were wondering what to do about us. I'm glad I did it, but never again!)

File.Name: h217c_h212-014.jpg
Notes: 32807071262 Here are two shots of what I'm pretty sure are a Thick-headed Fly (family Conopidae) in the order Diptera, maybe subfamily Myopinae. They are on new flowers of native Eastwood Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glandulosa ssp. mollis, Ericaceae). The shape of the flies is distinctive, especially the crooked shape of their slender proboscis. I couldn't get a photo that shows these features better, but I saw them clearly. So many flies! The Fly Day Friday group has a two photo limit, so I won't post this one, but HFDF anyway! (San Marcos Pass, 12 February 2017)

File.Name: h217d_h213-001.jpg
Notes: 32836115861 I first thought this tiny fly (Diptera) was another Fungus Gnat in the family Mycetophilidae, see this photo. But looking closer at the photos, I can see that this one has more ornate antennae. Maybe it's a female Midge fly in the family Chironomidae? It's only about 500 pixels long in this 1:1 macro shot, less than 1/10 inch. (See here for how I figured.) It's perched on a leaflet of Common Bedstraw (Galium aparine, Rubiaceae), which may make a better ruler if you know the plant. So many flies! The Fly Day Friday group has a two photo limit, so I won't post this one, but HFDF anyway! (San Marcos Pass, 13 February 2017)
