File.Name: h218a_h218-026.jpg
Notes:
32826607162 Just a dead twig on the Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus, Rosaceae)? Look again, especially on the ends of the "twig"! This is a looper or inchworm caterpillar of a Geometrid Moth (family Geometridae, Lepidoptera) with its butt end up in the air. It's also the damndest camouflage I've ever seen - except maybe the different looper in this photo. It's a perfect stick. I'm not sure how I recognized it, on a rainy day at that, but I was looking for a photo. (San Marcos Pass, 18 February 2017)

We ended up with a bit over 9-1/4 inches of rain from our storm yesterday! The highway was closed yesterday, but it opened this morning and I went for a drive to see what I could see. Sure, the creeks dropped when the heavy rain stopped, but there was still light rain today. I reckon my best photo was this caterpillar when I got home and looked small rather than big!
CommonNames: Wild Blackberry; looper; inchworm; Geometrid Moth
ScientificName: Rubus ursinus; Rosaceae; Geometridae; Lepidoptera
associatedTaxa: Rubus ursinus; Rosaceae; Geometridae; Lepidoptera

File.Name: h218b_h218-006.jpg
Notes: 32981121135 This is looking up San Jose Creek today from the bridge on West Camino Cielo road. Looking good! There was still some light rain when I got this photo. Th creek looks respectable, but I bet it was pretty massive at the height of the rain yesterday! It's remarkable how quickly the water in the creeks drops when the heavy rain stops, but watch out for the floods! That's a native California Bay (Umbellularia californica, Lauraceae) on the left, and an unusual Incense Cedar (Calocedrus decurrens, Cupressaceae) tree on the right. I believe the cedar was introduced long ago, though they've been seeding and proliferating in Kinevan Canyon, and they are native in the backcountry. (San Marcos Pass, 18 February 2017)
ScientificName: Umbellularia californica; Lauraceae; Calocedrus decurrens; Cupressaceae
associatedTaxa: Umbellularia californica; Lauraceae; Calocedrus decurrens; Cupressaceae

File.Name: h218c_h218-024.jpg
Notes: 32136447204 This is the muddy Santa Ynez River flowing around a bend near Manzanita Lane today. It's not cranking like it was yesterday in the heavy rain, when it got to 13,000 cfs (cubic feet per second), but it's still respectable at about 4,000 cfs. It's remarkable how the water in all the creeks drops almost immediately when the heavy rain stops. That's still a lot of water going down to Lake Cachuma. The reservoir was about 32.5% full this morning, no doubt more now. I was thinking the lake might actually fill overnight, but it's got a long ways to go after being down below ten percent capacity during the drought - just a month ago! The city of Santa Barbara might have squeaked by some serious water issues! If you look close along the right side of the river, you can see winter-bare willow and cottonwood tree. Behind them, you can see a group of bare non-native Eucalyptus trees. These trees are bare because they were burned by the Rey Fire last summer. You can see the hills greening up with fire-follower plants in the background. Hah, it will be a while before I can get up there to check it out! (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 18 February 2017)
ScientificName: Eucalyptus
associatedTaxa: Eucalyptus

File.Name: h218d_h218-018.jpg
Notes: 32939192366 This is a lovely native Valley Oak (Quercus lobata) in the Fagaceae plant family standing winter-bare in light rain today. These deciduous trees will start leafing out in the next few weeks unless the weather delays them. True to their common name, these fine trees stick to the valleys and foothills and don't normally grow in the mountains. (A friend in Painted Cave has a small tree which grew from an acorn, it's doing fine!) I was looking for rain photos today, and while this may not seem to qualify, I don't think I could have gotten the photo in any other weather. There's something about the light. (Santa Ynez Valley, 18 February 2017)
ScientificName: Quercus lobata; Fagaceae
associatedTaxa: Quercus lobata; Fagaceae

File.Name: h219a_h219-039.jpg
Notes:
32159850784 I found this native terrestrial Land Snail (Gastropoda) on a blushing cap of "The Blusher" (Amanita novinupta aka Amanita rubescens) mushroom in the woods today. I believe it is a Shoulderband Snail, genus Helminthoglypta in the family Helminthoglytidae. I guess the holes in the mushroom cap are the snail's work. I don't often find the living snails, though I've been finding more than usual in this wet year. There seem to be many regional species, and I won't try to guess what this is. See my [Previous] photo for more of a different mushroom of the same kind. (San Marcos Pass, 19 February 2017)

I wasn't expecting to find new mushrooms so soon after a big rain, but I had good luck today - three different Amanita mushrooms. Despite many species being poisonous, I've been looking for Amanitas, and I found several today. They are our most elegant mushrooms, and finding them today is a sign that the mushroom's mycelium has survived the drought. The snail was a bonus!

Darned if it isn't raining again! This might be a big rain maker for northern California, but probably less so for us. Just what they don't need in NorCal, I wish the best for everyone who lives below a dam.

(That said, looking at the Vandenberg radar map, it looks like it's coming right at us!)
CommonNames: Land Snail; The Blusher; Shoulderband Snail
ScientificName: Gastropoda; Amanita novinupta; Amanita rubescens; Helminthoglypta; Helminthoglytidae; Amanita
associatedTaxa: Gastropoda; Amanita novinupta; Amanita rubescens; Helminthoglypta; Helminthoglytidae; Amanita

File.Name: h219b_h219-024.jpg
Notes: 33004493925 Here are two shots of what I'm pretty sure is "The Blusher" (Amanita novinupta aka Amanita rubescens) mushroom in the woods today. I found several of these growing under growing under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) trees like most of our Amanita, but not all. This mushroom doesn't have a ring or any kind of volval cup on the base. I also find these mushrooms in the fall, which confirms what David Arora says (in Mushrooms Demystified) that they usually fruit twice - "a small flush after the first fall rains, and a larger crop in the late winter and spring." The fall mushroom look different, compare this photo. That hole in the stem on the right is a sign that something's been nibbling - see my [Next] photo! The stems do "blush" pink with handling, but they also blush pink on their own with age. Despite many species being poisonous, I've been looking for Amanitas, and I found several today. They are our most elegant mushrooms, and finding them today is a sign that the mushroom's mycelium has survived the drought. (San Marcos Pass, 19 February 2017)
ScientificName: Amanita novinupta; Amanita rubescens; Quercus agrifolia; Mushrooms Demystified
associatedTaxa: Amanita novinupta; Amanita rubescens; Quercus agrifolia; Mushrooms Demystified

File.Name: h219c_h219-047.jpg
Notes: 32158968714 My first thought was that this fine mushroom is the Pine Cone Amanita (Amanita magniverrucata), growing under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) trees in the woods today. Or maybe it is A. cokeri which is said to have less impressive warts on the cap? Or maybe it is Amanita sponsus (here)? The warts on the cap are not as impressive as the typical pyramidal warts in the photos at Mykoweb, so it may well be something else. Apart from the warts, this mushroom has a short ring on the stem and a scaly volva on the bulbous base. Edibility is unknown, "to be avoided". Despite many species being poisonous, I've been looking for Amanitas, and I found several today. They are our most elegant mushrooms, and finding them today is a sign that the mushroom's mycelium has survived the drought. (San Marcos Pass, 19 February 2017)
CommonNames: Pine Cone Amanita; Coast Live Oak
ScientificName: Amanita magniverrucata; Quercus agrifolia; A. cokeri; Amanita sponsus
associatedTaxa: Amanita magniverrucata; Quercus agrifolia; A. cokeri; Amanita sponsus

File.Name: h219d_h219-053.jpg
Notes: 32158632024 I found this small Amanita mushroom growing under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) trees in the woods today, but what kind is it? My guess is that this is a Panther Amanita (Amanita pantherina) mushroom with the veil remnants (warts) washed off by the heavy rains we've had. You can see some wart-like material around the edge of the cap. Except for the warts, it's a dead ringer for the mushroom in this photo taken two years ago, the last time I found them. If I'm right about the ID, this mushroom is quite poisonous. They contain the same interesting toxins as Amanita muscaria, but in higher concentrations. They should not be used for recreational purposes. My book says it's one of the most common causes of mushroom poisoning in the Pacific Northwest, but they are not dangerous to handle or photograph! Despite many being poisonous, I've been looking for Amanitas, and I found several today. They are our most elegant mushrooms, and finding them today is a sign that the mushroom's mycelium has survived the drought. (San Marcos Pass, 19 February 2017)
ScientificName: Amanita; Quercus agrifolia; Amanita pantherina; Amanita muscaria
associatedTaxa: Amanita; Quercus agrifolia; Amanita pantherina; Amanita muscaria

File.Name: h220a_h220-011.jpg
Notes:
32209484283 I found this fine Chanterelle (Cantharellus californicus) mushroom in the woods today during my walk in the drizzle and gloom. It's remarkable how they stay so firm in the rain. They are large mushrooms, the size of your open hand, with blunt ridges instead of proper gills. I'm happy to find these mushrooms - for the table of course, but also because they are a sign that the fungus mycelium has survived five years of drought in the ground as mycorrhiza on the roots of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). The mushrooms are edible and delicious, a local favorite. This is one time us knowledgeable naturalist peasants can eat like kings, see this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 20 February 2017)

We got an inch of rain last night, better than I expected, and today was drippy and gloomy with some showers. That brings us up to 39.18 inches for the rain year, beating our nearly 34 inch average - and we still have another month to go. That's better than the miserable 12.05 inches we got in 2013-2014, but it's still a ways from our record 87.95 inches in 1982-83!
ScientificName: Cantharellus californicus; mycelium; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae
associatedTaxa: Cantharellus californicus; mycelium; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae

File.Name: h220b_h220-018.jpg
Notes: 32179199894 I believe this is a Brown Ribbed Elfin Cup (Helvella acetabulum) under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). You can see the ribs on the outside of the cap, which extend almost to the cap margin. As David Arora says, "this species looks like a cross between a cup fungus and an elfin saddle." I have recent photos of an elfin saddle in this photo and a Peziza cup fungus in this photo, and you can see the similarity. But the ribs (and buried stem) of this elfin cup are distinctive. (San Marcos Pass, 20 February 2017)
CommonNames: Brown Ribbed Elfin Cup; Coast Live Oak
ScientificName: Helvella acetabulum; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Peziza

File.Name: h220c_h219-031.jpg
Notes: 32982532816 This is another shot of a native terrestrial Land Snail (Gastropoda) on a blushing cap of "The Blusher" (Amanita novinupta aka Amanita rubescens) mushroom in the woods. I believe it is a Shoulderband Snail, genus Helminthoglypta in the family Helminthoglytidae. If you look close, you can see that it's taking a dump! Wow, I really don't understand snail anatomy - just where is that poo coming from?? This is the same snail I showed in this photo yesterday. It's a different mushroom than the specimen in this photo, and it shows a bit of the mushroom's ring which was lacking in that other photo. (San Marcos Pass, 19 February 2017)

File.Name: h220d_h219-002.jpg
Notes: 32172199684 This is the foliage of native White Sage (Salvia apiana, Lamiaceae), with raindrops. Making a black and white image of this white-leaved plant on a gray drizzly day wasn't much of a stretch! This is my photo for the Macro Mondays group, with the theme of "B&W". HMM! (San Marcos Pass, 19 February 2017)

associatedTaxa: NA

File.Name: h221a_h221-018.jpg
Notes:
32228440883 This is a thick root of Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca, Ericaceae) emerging for a moment while growing through a sandstone outcrop. There's a small forest of moss and lichen. This is no doubt the same manzanita that belongs to the branch in my [Previous] photo, about 15 feet away. Roots travel! (San Marcos Pass, 21 February 2017)

I got all my photos today in light rain. The rain started sometime last night and continued all day. It never did rain particularly hard, but it rained all day, bringing us another 8/10 inch of rain that I wasn't expecting. It's interesting that there was not much showing on the radar map today, and the rain pretty much hugs the south-facing mountains. We've had Oregon weather lately, but it sounds like we'll have a break for the next few days. Hah, we're under a Wind Advisory tomorrow rather than a Flood Advisory! It might rain again next weekend. I guess our climate just is drought punctuated with deluge.
ScientificName: Arctostaphylos glauca
associatedTaxa: NA

File.Name: h221b_h221-021.jpg
Notes: 32916916761 Here is a lovely little garden of native Indian Warrior (Pedicularis densiflora, Orobanchaceae) plants growing in the lee of a thick branch of Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca, Ericaceae) along with lichens, mosses, and ferns. This is no doubt the same manzanita that belongs to the root in my [Next] photo, about 10 feet away. Usually this place is lost in deep shade, but a drizzly day like today opens it up. Most of the red color of the Indian Warrior belongs to leafy bracts, but you see a few real flowers if you look close. These plants are root-parasites of several different chaparral shrubs, surely the manzanita in this place. Apparently they form haustoria - like mistletoe - to connect to their host species. (San Marcos Pass, 21 February 2017)
ScientificName: Pedicularis densiflora; Orobanchaceae; Arctostaphylos glauca; Ericaceae; haustoria
associatedTaxa: Pedicularis densiflora; Orobanchaceae; Arctostaphylos glauca; Ericaceae; haustoria

File.Name: h221c_h221-011.jpg
Notes: 33001561576 Here are the first new flowers I've see this year of native Madrone (Arbutus menziesii) in the Ericaceae plant family. These are wonderful trees, though this one is half dead and looks a little ratty. I hope the abundant rain this year will revive it some. Sure, I got this photo in the light rain today -- all my photos. (San Marcos Pass, 21 February 2017)
ScientificName: Arbutus menziesii
associatedTaxa: NA

File.Name: h221d_h221-001.jpg
Notes: 32197682984 We had steady light rain last night and today which is just right to stick to the lupine leaves. If it rains any harder, all the drops get knocked off. I know this is almost a photo clichÈ, and I'm a sucker every time! The leaf belongs to native Canyon Lupine (Lupinus latifolius) in the Fabaceae plant family. Lupines catch raindrops better than any other plant I know. Why do some plants hold rain drops so well, while others shed them so fast? (San Marcos Pass, 21 February 2017)
ScientificName: Lupinus latifolius
associatedTaxa: NA

File.Name: h222a_h222-015.jpg
Notes:
33062744315 I found this lovely brown Stink Bug in the family Pentatomidae of the Heteroptera, Hemiptera on a leaf of a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) tree this morning. This one has five-segment antennae unlike the four-segment antennae of some other bugs, hence the family name Penta-tomidae. There are many different kinds, so I won't try to guess what it is. (San Marcos Pass, 22 February 2017)

Today started gloomy and calm, and then turned sunny and windy - quite windy, gusts to 40 mph. It's nice to see the sun today - it's been awhile! On the other hand, I'll take the gloom any day if it comes without the wind, which makes my kind of photography impossible.
ScientificName: Pentatomidae; Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Penta-tomidae
associatedTaxa: Pentatomidae; Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Penta-tomidae

File.Name: h222b_h222-032.jpg
Notes: 32935847661 Here are the first new flowers of a (I believe) native dry-land Roundfruit Sedge (Carex globosa) in the Cyperaceae plant family growing in our canyon. These are the shaggy male staminate flowers, and the more delicate female pistillate flowers will appear later underneath them. This is a remarkably tough grass-like plant that grows in the shaded woods, but also in glaring sun. It's not at all like a typical wet-land sedge, except for the flowers. I really admire the toughness of this plant. (San Marcos Pass, 22 February 2017)
ScientificName: Carex globosa; Cyperaceae
associatedTaxa: Carex globosa; Cyperaceae

File.Name: h222c_h222-010.jpg
Notes: 32935575401 This is a spider web in the making. It's the web of a tiny Trashline Orbweaver (genus Cyclosa, Araneidae) spider. The spider itself is visible in the 7:00 o'clock position going around and around to make its spiral web. If you look very close, you might be able to see a thin strand of webbing coming from the spider. It's interesting that the new webbing isn't visible in the first sector. Does it have to harden in the air before it catches the light? It's also interesting that the spider makes the thick white silk stabilimentum before it makes the web. This should be a great place for this little spider - right in the middle of a male staminate tree of Arroyo Willow (Salix lasiolepis, Salicaceae) by the creek that is full of tiny flies that are just the right size, see this photo. Happy Web Wednesday! (San Marcos Pass, 22 February 2017)
ScientificName: Cyclosa; Araneidae; stabilimentum; Salix lasiolepis; Salicaceae
associatedTaxa: Cyclosa; Araneidae; stabilimentum; Salix lasiolepis; Salicaceae

File.Name: h222d_h219-020.jpg
Notes: 32246503043 This is the web of a little Funnel Web Weaver (Agelenidae) spider - with lots of tiny raindrops and the actual spider sitting in the opening of the funnel! These are the webs that are so common in grassy areas, see this photo. The webs stand out with the early morning dew, then almost disappear as the day warms up. Finding the spider was a happy bonus, so Happy Web Wednesday! (San Marcos Pass, 19 February 2017)
ScientificName: Agelenidae
associatedTaxa: Agelenidae

File.Name: h223a_h223-005.jpg
Notes:
33038771176 I found this tiny Jumping Spider (Salticidae) on a succulent leaf in the garden today. It sure looks like the picture of a female Pelegrina aeneola in my main spider book, but with 119 described salticids in California, who knows! This spider didn't want to show me its face, but I got a decent butt shot, so HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 23 February 2017)

It was really too windy today to get any photos, with pretty steady 30+ mph winds. I managed to get this one photo on a succulent plant close to the ground that wasn't dancing too much. Hopefully the winds will behave tomorrow. The new U.S. Drought Monitor report came out today, and we are now in the "D2 (Severe Drought)" - a step down from the "D3 (Extreme Drought)" category for the last month, and a big step down from the "D4 (Exceptional Drought)" category for the last couple of years. Hurrah!
Locality: Extreme Drought)" category for the last month

File.Name: h223b_h222-002.jpg
Notes: 32698285610 This is a tiny Katydid nymph (Tettigoniidae, Ensifera, Orthoptera) on a leaf of Hummingbird Sage (Salvia spathacea) in the Lamiaceae plant family. It can't be much more than 1/8 inch long - without those long antennae of course. This is the first one I've noticed this year. I guess this is the nymph of a California Angle-winged Katydid (Microcentrum californicum). We also have Bush Katydids in the genus Scudderia, but those nymphs appear later in the summer and are much more ornate, compare this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 22 February 2017)

File.Name: h223c_h222-023.jpg
Notes: 32698123370 I found this handsome Green Stink Bug in the family Pentatomidae of the Heteroptera, Hemiptera on foliage of native Prickly-Phlox (Leptodactylon californicum, Polemoniaceae) in the woods yesterday. There are lots of different kinds, maybe this one is genus Chinavia or Thyanta? (San Marcos Pass, 22 February 2017)

File.Name: h223d_h216-007.jpg
Notes: 32953131811 This is a spider with prey on its dome-like web. I guess from the web that the spider is a Sheet Web Weaver spider in the family Linyphiidae, but it also looks like a Cobweb Spider (Theridiidae) with that speckled abdomen. There are spines on the legs that suggests the former. I believe its prey is an unfortunate Common Sawfly (family Tenthredinidae, "Symphyta", Hymenoptera), maybe the same kind as this photo. I found this spider in a small tree of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). Whatever the spider is, this is a pretty good butt shot, so HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 16 February 2017)

File.Name: h224a_h224-026.jpg
Notes:
32253751254 This is another liverwort (Marchantiophyta) in our canyon, but I believe it is different from the usual California Liverwort (Asterella californica) that I've been showing. For one thing, it's growing in a sunnier place. The sporophytes are taller and smoother and less divided, and the creeping "leaves" ("thallus") are smaller, compare this photo. This one might be a Riccia species, maybe the Black Crystalwort (Riccia nigrella). Yeah, this is pretty obscure, but it is a neat bryophyte! (San Marcos Pass, 24 February 2017)

Today was sunny but rather chilly, in the mid-30s last night, but not quite frosty. It started calm, but then got windy. Our next chance of rain is Sunday, but the odds have been shrinking as we get closer. We started at 80% of rain a few days ago, but now we're down to a 30% chance. Hey, we're already good for the year!
ScientificName: Marchantiophyta; Asterella californica; sporophytes; Riccia; Riccia nigrella
associatedTaxa: Marchantiophyta; Asterella californica; sporophytes; Riccia; Riccia nigrella

File.Name: h224b_h224-004.jpg
Notes: 33057262706 This is new growth of native Southern Bush Monkey Flower (Mimulus aurantiacus var. pubescens) in the Phrymaceae plant family. Look how the stem on the right is swollen compared to the normal stem on the left. I believe it's been galled by an insect, possibly the Monkey Flower Stem Gall Midge (Neolasioptera diplaci) in the family Cecidomyiidae, Diptera. It's interesting how the leaves appear different on the galled and ungalled stems. I can tell at a glance which stems are galled. (San Marcos Pass, 24 February 2017)

File.Name: h224c_h219-064.jpg
Notes: 32253313684 This is an odd one. My photo shows "just a fly" (order Diptera) on the woven back of a chair on our porch - carrying a load of sand? Well, I found this fly the day after our big storm a week ago that brought us 9-1/4 inches of rain in mostly one day, and I reckon the fly was caught in the flood. Look how the space under the bristles between its eyes (the frontorbital bristles?) is filled with sand! The fly seemed healthy enough and took off when I got too close. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 19 February 2017)
ScientificName: Diptera; frontorbital
associatedTaxa: Diptera; frontorbital

File.Name: h224d_h216-046.jpg
Notes: 33098474525 This is a tiny mosquito-like Dance Fly in the family Empididae, probably genus Empis, working female pistillate flowers on a catkin of Arroyo Willow (Salix lasiolepis) in the Salicaceae plant family by the creek. I'm pretty sure this fly is a gravid female, large with eggs. Compare this photo of a male fly with a much slimmer stature, and this photo showing both. The willows are dioecious with male and female flowers on different plants, see this photo from a week ago. The flies are going for nectar which is hidden under those dark flaps at the base of both the pistils and the stamens. One thing I can't explain is the proboscis which appears to have two layers, white and dark. I got this photo a week ago. I tried to replace it with something fresher today, but the flies didn't cooperate. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 16 February 2017)
ScientificName: Empididae; Empis; Salix lasiolepis; Salicaceae; dioecious
associatedTaxa: Empididae; Empis; Salix lasiolepis; Salicaceae; dioecious

File.Name: h225a_h225-023.jpg
Notes:
32736081570 This isn't the best photo, but it shows an uncommon insect in our mountains - a "Timema" insect, no doubt Timema cristinae in the family Timematidae of the Phasmida clan. They are atypical walkingstick insects. This one is quite small, so I reckon it's immature and/or a male. I found it in a shrub of native Chamise (Adenostoma fasciculatum, Rosaceae), one of their preferred host plants. It did not want to pose for me. I had to chase it for a while, and this is the best photo I got. This might be the first one I've found outside of their mating season in late March and April, when they seem to become bolder - see this photo. I'm not sure how uncommon these insects actually are, as they are very well camouflaged and certainly hard to spot. Photos of them are certainly uncommon! (San Marcos Pass, 25 February 2017)

There were moments when it felt like rain today ... but nothing has happened yet. Rain is likely later tonight and tomorrow. It doesn't sound like this will be a big rain-maker, but drizzle is always welcome.

File.Name: h225b_h225-038.jpg
Notes:
33118175185 This is the first of our native Sanicles in the genus Sanicula of the Apiaceae plant family that I've seen in flower this year. I believe this one is the Coast Sanicle (Sanicula laciniata). The species name "laciniata" sound like "lacerate" - and the leaves look like someone went at them with scissors. The Sanicles are kind of a "collector's genus". Clifton Smith list nine species in his Santa Barbara flora. The flowers all look the same - little yellow balls - but the foliage is different. This one has sharp points on its simple palmate leaves, which distinguishes it from the similar Pacific Sanicle (S. crassicaulis). The leaves are also much thinner. (San Marcos Pass, 25 February 2017)

If you look close in the upper-right, you can see an interesting little plant that I'm pretty sure is Small-flowered Miner's Lettuce (Claytonia parviflora, Montiaceae or Portulacaceae) as opposed to regular Miner's Lettuce (Claytonia perfoliata). You can also see some fertile stalks of a moss.

I'm not sure of my ID, but they sure look different from he ones on my [Previous] photo. We have more different kinds of liverworts in our woods than I previously thought. There's a checklist of different species at iNaturalist, but I haven't yet found a proper key. They seem to be having a good year with all the rain. (San Marcos Pass, 25 February 2017)

I reckon this one is a large, a couple of inches long, much larger than this different fly I posted earlier today, though it also has patterned wings. I'll post another photo of the whole fly tomorrow. (San Marcos Pass, 26 February 2017)

We did get another 2/10 inch of rain last night. It was cloudy today and I was hoping for more, but nothing doing. There's still a 30% chance of more rain tomorrow. I'm more concerned about the wind, which might pick up.

Here it is fully exposed on a leaf of Greenbark Ceanothus (Ceanothus spinosus, Rhamnaceae), but maybe it got waylaid by cold weather today and didn't spend the night here? If there were a group of "big-headed bees", then I'd know what this one is! I first thought this might be a Long-horned Bee (genus Melissodes) with those long antennae and hairy legs - but apparently it is genus Andrena, see Bernhard's comment below. 33118415676 This is another extremely well-camouflaged looper or inchworm caterpillar of a Geometrid Moth (family Geometridae, Lepidoptera) on a branch of native Chamise (Adenostoma fasciculatum, Rosaceae). It's interesting that both caterpillars are on plants in the Rosaceae plant family. Small world - that gray lichen below the caterpillar's rear end has rhizines like the tiny lichens on our deck in this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 27 February 2017)

File.Name: h227d_h225-052.jpg
Notes: 32997119852 This is a tiny forest of lichens growing in the gap between the boards of our upstairs deck. For scale, the gap is only about 3/8 inch. The gray lichens have root-like rhizines growing underneath which will serve to hold the lichen to its substrate. The lichens are tiny, a millimeter or less across. Between the rains, they dry out and shrivel to a gray dust. They quickly revive in the next rain, whether it's tomorrow, next week, or next year. Rather than wait, I splashed a glass of water on the deck waited a short time to get my shot. This is my photo for the Macro Mondays group, with the theme of "The Space In Between". HMM! (San Marcos Pass, 25 February 2017)

I was expecting some wind today, but it was sunny and calm. I didn't find many insects today, so maybe they've been slowed down by recent rains and cool weather? I'm distracted tonight, listening to the president while trying to post these photos...

Notes: 32334599114 Again, this isn't the best photo, but it shows an uncommon insect in our mountains on an unusual host plant. It's another "Timema" insect, no doubt Timema cristinae in the family Timematidae of the Phasmida clan, on leaves of native Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia, Fagaceae). They are atypical walkingstick insects. I tried to nudge it into better position, but it got away from me. I believe their preferred host plants are Chamise (Adenostoma fasciculatum, Rosaceae) and Greenbark Ceanothus (Ceanothus spinosus, Rhamnaceae), so this is an interesting plant to find it on. This one is quite small, so I reckon it's immature and/or a male. I'm not sure how uncommon these insects actually are, but photos of them are certainly uncommon! (San Marcos Pass, 28 February 2017)

I didn't find any yesterday until I got home ... and found this one walking on the arm of my shirt! I transferred it to our upstairs deck railing, but it was too active to photograph. I had a brainstorm and put my cold can of beer on top of it. The can has a domed bottom, so it didn't crush the little critter, and the coldness slowed it down for this photo. This one is quite small, so I reckon it's a male, see this photo. I'm not sure how uncommon these insects actually are, as they are well camouflaged and hard to spot. I apologize for not posting last night. We had yet another maintenance power outage yesterday, which was supposed to run from 8 am to 6 pm. The power still hadn't come on at 9 pm, so we went to bed. The power finally came back around 9:30. As I've said before, this is no way to run a business.

Darn, I'm coming down with a cold. *Blah* Today was another lovely day - surprisingly warm, with temps in the mid-70s and little breeze. It sounds like the temperature will drop back to normal tomorrow, and our next rain will come on Sunday. This won't be a big rain-maker, but anything is welcome.

File.Name: h304a_h304-011.jpg
Notes:
33212894886 Here are the first new flowers of Snowball aka Hoary-leaved Ceanothus (Ceanothus crassifolius) in the Rhamnaceae plant family. You can make out all the key characters in this photo. The leaves are opposite and toothed, white-tomentose underneath, with persistent corky stipules, and white flowers. We have several different white-flowered Ceanothus species that cover the mountains, at least in a wetter year - Ceanothus megacarpus along the south-facing slopes, Ceanothus cuneatus on the north-facing slopes, and this Ceanothus crassifolius on the summits. They all have white flowers that look similar, but the foliage is different. (San Marcos Pass, 4 March 2017)

*Blah* I've got a cold. Today was cooler and breezier and cloudier than yesterday, all early signs of our storm coming in later tonight. Actually, it doesn't sound like much of a storm, just a 1/4 inch or so if we're lucky. I figure anything is welcome!

I'm still feeling under the weather, but we did have some real weather this morning to perk me up! We actually got more rain than expected, about 1/3 inch. One of these storms will be our last storm of the season, but hopefully not for another month. Then we begin our usual "summer drought" which is to be expected as part of our summer-dry Mediterranean climate. The drought that's been a problem for the last five years is something else again.

File.Name: h305c_h305-013.jpg
Notes: 33233556006 I found this small group of "The Blusher" (Amanita novinupta aka Amanita rubescens) mushrooms in the woods today, growing under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) trees. That grass-like plant in the upper-right is the dry-land Roundfruit Sedge (Carex globosa, Cyperaceae), see this photo. See this photo for a look under the mushroom caps. The stems do "blush" pink with handling, but they also blush pink on their own with age, like these. It's a striking color. Despite many species being poisonous, I've been looking for Amanitas, and finding them today is a sign that the mushroom's mycelium has survived the drought. I am concerned that I haven't found the beautiful but deadly Destroying Angel (Amanita ocreata) mushroom for two years, see this photo. I used to find them regularly in our woods, and it bothers me that they are missing. Sure they are poisonous, but they're not dangerous to touch or admire, and they are probably the most elegant mushroom in our woods, very beautiful. It's still possible that I'll find one, but it's becoming less likely. (San Marcos Pass, 5 March 2017)
CommonNames: The Blusher; Coast Live Oak; Roundfruit Sedge; Destroying Angel

File.Name: h307b_h307-027.jpg
Notes: 32472266184 Here are ants tending a large winged aphid that is giving live birth to a little aphid that will join its older siblings on this willow stem. I assume that's an aphid with the orange-striped abdomen, but it's not like any aphid I've seen before. (Could it be something else??) This aphid is a "fundatrix" for this new colony - the first aphid from the spring hatch that will produce new aphids parthenogenetically and viviparously. These aphids are all female, their daughters will produce more daughters, and so on all summer long. Basically they are born pregnant. Males don't come on the scene until autumn. Sexual females and males mate, and females lay eggs that endure the winter and emerge as winged females the following spring. I'm pretty sure the ants are the California Velvety Tree Ant (Liometopum occidentale, Dolichoderinae) which is the most common ant in our woods - and sometimes in our house also, see this photo! The willow is Arroyo Willow (Salix lasiolepis, Salicaceae). This staminate tree is still flowering, but it will be done soon. Getting this photo was a challenge. There was a breeze, and this was inside the tree to the extent that I couldn't get my shot without my head bumping other branches. I did my best. (San Marcos Pass, 7 March 2017)
CommonNames: ants; aphid; willow; California Velvety Tree Ant; Arroyo Willow
ScientificName: fundatrix; Liometopum occidentale; Dolichoderinae; Salix lasiolepis; Salicaceae
associatedTaxa: fundatrix; Liometopum occidentale; Dolichoderinae; Salix lasiolepis; Salicaceae

File.Name: h307c_h306-030.jpg
Notes: 33314929365 This is something that looks like moss - but isn't. I believe that is a genus Fossombronia liverwort. They have a ruffled thallus that looks like lettuce, and sometimes produce tiny lollypop-like sporophytes like on the left. I found these growing on thin bare soil over sandstone. It's interesting that I almost always find this one growing with other liverworts, maybe genus Riccia. I don't often find this Fossombronia liverwort. It's tiny and really looks like a moss, so it's easy to overlook. I suspect it shrivels into nothing when it dries out, so these last few drought years have been a disadvantage. This is a tiny world that I know almost nothing about. (San Marcos Pass, 6 March 2017)

The temperature jumped almost 15∞ today, up to almost 80 degrees! Spring is still two weeks away by the calendar, but it felt like it was here today.

Notes: 33177041602 This is a very small Orb-Weaver Spider (Araneus, Araneidae) in the curl of a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). It had a web nearby. File.Name: h309a_h309-011.jpg
Notes:
32507639504 This is a flower head of California Bay (Umbellularia californica, Lauraceae) that is nearly done with flowering. (Other trees are just starting or completely done, a nice variability this year.) Look close! The strange fruit is another stick-like caterpillar hanging down just to the left of center. This is the well-camouflaged looper or inchworm caterpillar of a Geometrid Moth (family Geometridae, Lepidoptera). I can see some webbing in there. Are these caterpillars web-spinners? Today was a gorgeous day - into the 80s and nary a breeze. Despite the fine weather, spiders seem to be more common than insects right now up in the mountains. Maybe I'm just not looking close enough, but maybe I need to go further afield. I can see poppies beginning to appear on the burn of the Rey Fire last summer, but I'm not sure yet how to get to them - on private ranch land, across the river.

You can tell by the robust palps that one is a male, probably genus Mecaphesa. That little yellow bit on its screen-left palp is interesting. I've noticed that these spiders sometimes carry bits of pollen on their pedipalps as bait, see this photo. That's doubly interesting because this spider must have carried that pollen some ways from a nearby California Bay tree, as there are no other flowers nearby. For some reason, I've been finding lots of spiders lately. (San Marcos Pass, 9 March 2017)

File.Name: h310a_h310-040.jpg
Notes:
33212934632 This is a gorgeous little Labyrinth Spider (genus Metepeira, Araneidae) posed at the entrance to it's retreat. These orb-weaver spiders build a proper orb web with a separate little house suspended by a tangle of cobwebs off to the side. They decorate their houses with the bodies of their prey, pirate fashion. This one seems uncommonly small, not much more than 1/4 inch across. It's smaller than the one in this photo from last November. This one is in a not-yet-flowering shrub of native Black Sage (Salvia mellifera, Lamiaceae). (San Marcos Pass, 10 March 2017)

I suggested yesterday that spiders are more common than insects right now up in the mountains. Well, probably not when you consider numbers, but in terms of interesting species? I did a tally of my photos today, and I found 3 plants worth photographing, 5 interesting insects, and 7 different spiders. The spiders win! (Yes, I take many more photos than I post.) It was another lovely day with some some high cloud cover, a couple of degrees cooler, but still quite calm.

Notes: 32553610533 Here are a pair of mosquito-like Dance Flies in the family Empididae, probably genus Empis, on a male staminate catkin of Arroyo Willow (Salix lasiolepis, Salicaceae) by the creek. They are making their living in different ways. The fly on the right is dipping for nectar under the scales at the base of each stamen. But the fly on the left is being a predator with a tiny midge for prey. The answers one question I've had about these flies, that they are described as primarily predators, while I find them mostly on flowers. Notes:
33387767905 Here are two shots of a tiny wild bee (Anthophila, Hymenoptera) making a nest in last year's stem of Mugwort (Artemisia douglasiana, Asteraceae). Actually, a pair of the bees was involved in making this their home. Once I noticed this one, I saw others on all the other stems nearby. These bees are tiny and have a white patch on their snout. They're not hairless, but are they a Masked Bee in the genus Hylaeus? Whatever they are, they are tiny. The stems are only about 1/4 inch in diameter. (San Marcos Pass, 11 March 2017)

The new U.S. Drought Monitor report came out today, and we are now in the "D1 (Moderate Drought)" - a step down from the "D2 (Severe Drought)" category for the last month, and a big step down from the "D4 (Exceptional Drought)" category for the last couple of years. Hurrah! Of course the effects of the five-year drought will linger for years. Looking at the woods right now, I wonder if they will ever recover. On the other hand, the chorus frogs are going at it tonight so something is right!

Notes: 33231776312 Some critter was impatient waiting for this flower of native Eastwood Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glandulosa ssp. mollis, Ericaceae) to open, and made a hole to get inside! Lots of insects practice nectar theft, entering a flower through the calyx or a hole rather than through the throat so that pollination might happen, see this photo of a different manzanita. This insect blasted out the whole wall of the flower! I don't know why the flower turned red around the opening, but it's attractive, like lipstick. (San Marcos Pass, 11 March 2017)

File.Name: h311c_h311-020.jpg
Notes: 33231491412 Yeah, just a weed - but this is a nice photo of the two odd flowers cozying up together. The plant is non-native English Plantain (Plantago lanceolata) in the Plantaginaceae plant family. It's interesting how the stamens of the flower form down at the bottom and the pistils are at the top. As the season progresses, both will move on up the flower axis. The big mystery to me is how the Plantaginaceae plant family has gobbled up most of the Scrophulariaceae. There some info in this PDF from the Fremontia journal, but I still don't like it! (San Marcos Pass, 11 March 2017)

File.Name: h312a_h312-016.jpg
Notes:
33253133032 Here is a closer shot of the lovely Sonoran Blue (Philotes sonorensis) butterfly in my [Previous] photo. She's been ovipositing - laying eggs - on different succulent plants of Southern California Dudleya (Dudleya lanceolata, Crassulaceae) on this sandstone outcrop, hopping from plant to plant to spread her eggs around. The lady butterflies are even more brilliantly colored than the gentlemen (see this photo) which reverses the usual pattern with butterflies and ducks that the males are more brightly colored than the females. This is actually the first of any kind of Blue (Polyommatinae, Lycaenidae) butterfly that I've managed to photograph this year. See my [Previous] photo. It was a good day for butterflies! (San Marcos Pass, 12 March 2017)

It was a bit breezy in the morning, but it turned into another beautiful day. It actually got up to 84∞ today! If this keeps up, I'll be back to moaning about how dry it is! There's still some moisture in the ground, and it's interesting there have been (almost) no mushrooms. I guess their time is done.

File.Name: h312b_h312-036.jpg
Notes: 32565583554 Here is a lovely Brown Elfin (Callophrys augustinus) butterfly on unopened flowers of native Eastwood Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glandulosa ssp. mollis, Ericaceae) . At first the butterfly looks plain brown, but when you look closer you see that it really has a rich palette of many colors. Up close it looks like a pointillist painting - many color swatches, including violet and gold, combine to make the eye-catching brown. It's a Hairstreak (family Theclinae) butterfly, but you can hardly tell by looking at it with that stubby little tail. This isn't the first one of the year, but they are still pretty scarce. (San Marcos Pass, 12 March 2017)

Notes: 33252348342 This is a Propertius Duskywing (Erynnis propertius) butterfly getting water from the rock - a sandstone outcrop in this case. It's a kind of vertical puddling! The rock holds a lot of rain water for a surprisingly long time. This is the first one I've seen this year. It's one of our two duskywings that lacks the white trailing edge of species like this one. I might have also photographed our other plain duskywing, the Afranius Duskywing (Erynnis afranius) which is smaller and darker. I'll post a photo tomorrow. It was a good day for butterflies! File.Name: h313a_h313-002.jpg
Notes:
33428085475 This is the first new flower of Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus, Rosaceae) that I've seen in the woods this year. Look at that tiny little wild bee (Anthophila, Hymenoptera) flying over the flower. This plant is close to the wild bee nest I show in this photo, and it may well be the same white-faced bee! The blackberries have really taken a hit during our drought years. We used to have a patch the size of a house lot under the oaks behind our home, but now they are mostly gone. It's not like the blackberries are scarce, but don't take anything for granted! I'm waiting for new growth to see whether those blackberries are really dead or just resting. (San Marcos Pass, 13 March 2017)

It was a bit breezy again this morning, but it turned into another beautiful day. It actually got up to 85∞ today, a degree warmer than yesterday. Looking down from my eyrie in the mountains, I could see a dense fog layer hugging the coast. It's a good time to be up in the mountains.

File.Name: h313c_h307-009.jpg
Notes: 33044608580 Here are the shrubs that got me to using a vernier caliper to measure seedpods in my [Previous] photo. (Sure I could have used a ruler, but the caliper fit the bill for the Macro Mondays challenge.) Both of these native shrubs are in the Rhamnaceae plant family, but they usually grow on different sides of the mountains. I believe the first photo shows Bigpod Ceanothus (Ceanothus megacarpus) with opposite leaves, and the middle photo shows the same shrub with alternate leaves. As the species name suggests, both of these plants have large fruit, 8-12 mm. On the right is Buck Brush (Ceanothus cuneatus) with opposite leaves and small fruit just 4-6 mm. Hah - the key difference is whether the leaves are alternate (megacarpus) or opposite (cuneatus), but the key goes on to say that megacarpus has "lvs on single plant alternate and sometimes opposite". I've been finding shrubs that seem to have wholly opposite leaves, and I was wondering if they could show some influence with C. cuneatus. Probably not, at least on the basis of the fruit which are about 8.4 mm across. They are compatible with C. megacarpus, though on the low end. (San Marcos Pass, 12 March 2017 and Lake Cachuma, Santa Ynez Valley, 15 February 2017)
ScientificName: Rhamnaceae; Ceanothus megacarpus; Ceanothus cuneatus

File.Name: h313d_h312-054.jpg
Notes: 32578930964 This is using a vernier caliper to measure an old seedpod of (probably) native Bigpod Ceanothus (Ceanothus megacarpus) in the Rhamnaceae plant family. It's 8.4 mm across, which is compatible with that species which is said to have fruit from 8-12 mm. I've been curious because these shrubs are flowering now in the mountains, and their leaves are quite opposite in arrangement. There is a different species Buck Brush (Ceanothus cuneatus) with fruit 4-6 mm. Hah - the key difference is whether the leaves are alternate (megacarpus) or opposite (cuneatus), but the key goes on to say that megacarpus has "lvs on single plant alternate and sometimes opposite". I've been finding shrubs that seem to have wholly opposite leaves, and I was wondering if they could show some influence with C. cuneatus. Probably not. The fruit are compatible with C. megacarpus, though on the low end. Since my caliper is also metal, this is my photo for the Macro Mondays group, with the theme of "Made of Metal". HMM! (San Marcos Pass, 12 March 2017)

It was a bit cooler today, though it still got up to 82∞. It was quite a bit breezier as well - not huge gusts, but enough steady wind to make getting photos difficult. I have errands tomorrow. I'll do my best for new photos.

Notes: 33064048330 Here are new leaves and flower buds of native Poison Oak (Toxicodendron diversilobum) in the Anacardiaceae plant family. It looks positively dripping with skin-irritating oil! This shrub or vine isn't really poison, but many people are sensitive to it. It really is a beautiful plant, and important for wildlife. It's pretty to look at, but don't touch it if you are sensitive! For some reason, I don't get it. I hike through Poison Oak constantly, and it doesn't bother me unless I really ask for it. File.Name: h314d_h313-015.jpg
Notes: 33291377112 Here are the first new flowers I've seen in the mountains of Greenbark Ceanothus (Ceanothus spinosus) in the Rhamnaceae plant family. I've been seeing them along the highway on the way to town, but these are the the first flowers at home. If you look very close at the flowers, maybe you can see some of the hundreds of tiny insects that are there. If you hold your hand out under the flowers and whack them with a stick, you can sometimes get a handful of tiny insects. It's a good naturalist trick when you're taking kinds into the woods. This species is in the subgenus Ceanothus with thin, alternate leaves and deciduous stipules. It's different from the other white-flowered species in the subgenus Cerastes that I been showing lately, with mostly opposite leaves (except for C. megacarpus!) and persistent stipules. The species of this group I've shown so far are C. megacarpus, C. cuneatus, and C. crassifolius, see this photo and this photo. There are more species to come! (San Marcos Pass, 13 March 2017)

File.Name: h315a_h315-003.jpg
Notes:
33465186955 This is a little "Timema" insect, no doubt Timema cristinae in the family Timematidae of the Phasmida clan, on a flower of native California Peony (Paeonia californica) in the Paeoniaceae plant family. Truth to tell, I found it on a peony leaf in the shade. I tried to nudge it into the sun, and it crawled onto my hand! I let it crawl onto this flower of the same plant and got the photo. This is an atypical walkingstick insect. I believe their preferred host plants are Chamise (Adenostoma fasciculatum, Rosaceae) and Greenbark Ceanothus (Ceanothus spinosus, Rhamnaceae) - and there was a chamise shrub hanging over the peony. Notes:
33308828682 The oaks are starting to flower! This is a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) tree with new leaves and staminate catkins. And it's not just this oak as all our oaks seem to be going off at about the same time. It's remarkably beautiful - but I expect some hay fever coming up. It's interesting that the new leaves aren't always red like this. Sometimes they are pale yellow, and the combination of red and yellow trees is wonderful. (San Marcos Pass, 15 March 2017)

Now it's time to worry about the Sawfly Wasp ("Symphyta", Hymenoptera) larvae which devour the buds, see this photo. I'll be watching for them on the oaks at home - not like there's much I can do about them!

They are said to be a fire-follower, and this place did burn about 15 years ago. They are small plants, just 2 or 3 inches high, but very pretty. They look like an escaped garden ground cover plant, but they are native. These are plants I always forget about and then am surprised to find again. File.Name: h315d_h310-043.jpg
Notes: 33423912086 Here is another caterpillar of a Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly with its webbing on a shrub of native Southern Bush Monkey Flower (Mimulus aurantiacus var. pubescens, Phrymaceae). It seems this caterpillar just went through a molt, and you can see a bit of its old skin on the side. Does the webbing help it wiggle out? I can also make out a bit of frass in the web under the caterpillar, so this is more than a temporary retreat. Compare this photo. These are the same web-spinning caterpillars that I find in summer, for example this photo. They form their communal webs on their host plants in the summer, then form tough skins and drop to the forest floor to over-winter. They re-emerge in the following spring and get fat on their host plants before they pupate into butterflies in May and June. I don't believe these spring caterpillars are actually communal, but I do find groups of them. It's a curious cycle, but it works for them. This webbing isn't made by a spider, but Happy Web Wednesday anyway! (San Marcos Pass, 10 March 2017)
CommonNames: Variable Checkerspot; Southern Bush Monkey Flower

File.Name: h316a_h316-011.jpg
Notes:
32639989334 Look at those antennae! I found this medium-sized Scarab Beetle (Scarabaeidae, Coleoptera) on the chipseal road in our canyon today. A moment after I got this photo, it retracted its antennae - making this the keeper photo. I believe it is an Anteater Scarab Beetle in the genus Cremastocheilus. It has an odd face with those moose-like antennae and that odd mouth that I don't understand. (The photos at BugGuide don't really help!) Apparently the larvae are "covered with long, silky hairs; in nests of mound-building harvester ants, where they live as scavengers." (Powell and Hogue, California Insects, UC Press, 1979) Hah - I had too many decent "beautiful bug butt" photos this week, so this is something different. (San Marcos Pass, 16 March 2017)

It was cooler and breezier today. Now we have "rain likely" for next Tuesday. That's still four days away, so who knows, but I'm rooting for the rain!

It has a web nearby, and it's keeping in touch with its web with that one leg, like using your index finger to sense the line while fishing. I believe this spider is either A. bispinosus or A. montereyensis, and the way to tell them apart is to look at the way the abdomen dips between the two humps. (See Alice Abelia's comment to this photo.) This one seems straight as a board, so maybe it's Araneus bispinosus - but I figure "one of those" is good enough for me! HBBBT! Notes: 32657638544 Here is a beautiful new flower head of native Wavyleaf Indian Paintbrush (Castilleja applegatei ssp. martinii aka Castilleja martinii) in the Orobanchaceae plant family. The brilliant red color is mostly from the bracts, but the actual flower tubes are also quite red - whereas the flower tubes of the more common Castilleja affinis are greenish, compare this photo. Flowers and bracts are quite glandular, even below the flowers - the leaves feel sticky, almost slimy. See this photo for a whole group of plants. These handsome plants are growing on a shady hillside by our mailbox. They seem late this year, flowering a month later than last year. No doubt all the rain last month slowed things down. File.Name: h318a_h318-025.jpg
Notes:
33519862515 This is a Pale Swallowtail (Papilio eurymedon) butterfly on new flowers of native Prickly-Phlox (Leptodactylon californicum, Polemoniaceae). I've seen a few of these butterflies this year - including a single Western Tiger Swallowtail (P. rutulus) flying down our canyon - but this is the first one I've managed to photograph this year. I got better photos photos today, but I like this one because it shows the butterfly's pollen-encrusted proboscis. These flowers have deep throats, and this is how the butterflies move pollen - and pollination happens. (San Marcos Pass, 18 March 2017)

It was windy again this morning. It occurs to me that these might be the annoying "fog winds" that come with the "June Gloom" fog that we usually get later in the year. The marine layer fog has been creeping higher and higher into the mountains. I had to go back to the Santa Ynez Valley today, and I entered the cloud layer near Lake Cachuma. So much for photos. There were real clouds as well. It still sounds like we will get some weather next week.

File.Name: h318b_h318-005.jpg
Notes: 33390796501 Here are lots of fantastic tiny wasp galls on staminate catkins of a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree in the Fagaceae plant family. I believe they are the spring bisexual galls of a Two-horned Gall Wasp (Dryocosmus dubiosus in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera, which will make different summer unisexual galls after they mature - see this photo. These spring bisexual galls contain both male and female wasps in different galls. The wasps will emerge, mate, and induce a summer crop of unisexual galls that contain only female wasps. The lady wasps will emerge next spring and parthenogenetically produce another crop of tiny bisexual galls ... and the cycle repeats. Sorry, but galls are complicated like that! These galls are only a few mm long. Catkins seem such an ephemeral place to build a house, but these galled catkins usually stay on the tree long past blooming. I believe the tiny gall insects change the behavior of the abscission as well as the stamens. I haven't seen many of these galls this year, but the oaks are just beginning to flower. (San Marcos Pass, 18 March 2017)
CommonNames: Coast Live Oak; Two-horned Gall Wasp
ScientificName: Dryocosmus dubiosus
associatedTaxa: Quercus agrifolia

Notes:
33384555712 I found this plump little caterpillar on an old leaf of Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus, Rosaceae) today. It was actually on an emerging new leaf, but I moved it into the open for a photo. It has a racing stripe! I have no idea what kind of Lepidoptera it will become. There are also green caterpillar-like larvae of Sawflies (Tenthredinidae, "Symphyta", Hymenoptera), see this photo, but their eyes are different, and they have at least six pairs of prolegs. The vernal equinox happens tonight at 3:30 AM PDT, and tomorrow is the first official day of spring. Happy equinox! The seasons change, life goes on...

File.Name: h319c_h319-035.jpg
Notes: 33412104701 Here are three different lupines flowering in the canyon right today. They all have palmate "lupine leaves" like a hand with outstretched fingers, they all have pea-like flowers, and they are all in the genus Lupinus of the Fabaceae plant family. It's the differences that are most interesting. These photos are not to scale, but they go in order from larger to smaller. On the left is the perennial Canyon Lupine (Lupinus latifolius), the most common of all these and the largest, usually 3 or 4 feet tall. In the middle is the annual Collar Lupine (Lupinus truncatus). This one is a common fire-follower that also grows in disturbed places like our road. It get a foot or two tall, but often smaller. In this photo, it's growing with native Bird's Foot Fern (Pellaea mucronata, Polypodiaceae). The hairy lupine on the right is the smallest of these, and I really don't know what it is. I've called it the south coast variant of Coulter's Lupine (Lupinus sparsiflorus ssp. inopinatus) in the past, but I really don't know what it is. It's only a few inches tall. It grows in disturbed places along the road in our canyon. Help will be appreciated! (San Marcos Pass, 19 March 2017)
CommonNames: Canyon Lupine; Collar Lupine; Bird's Foot Fern; Coulter's Lupine

File.Name: h319d_h319-009.jpg
Notes: 33156788460 I haven't been thinking about mushrooms much lately, but I stumbled across this one today along our road. It's an odd one that looks more like a discarded cookie than a fungus! I guess it's the Spreading Brown Cup Fungus (Peziza repanda) or kin, but I really don't know. They start out more cup-shaped like this when young and then flatten out with age. This one is growing with Miner's Lettuce (Claytonia perfoliata, Montiaceae) under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) trees. I didn't have my knife for scale, but they are two or three inches across. There is no stalk to speak of and no ribs on the outside, unlike the Brown Ribbed Elfin Cup (Helvella acetabulum) in this photo. It's curious how there is still moisture in the ground, but there have been (almost) no mushrooms. I guess their time is done, but maybe the rain coming later this week will bring up a few more. (San Marcos Pass, 19 March 2017)
CommonNames: Spreading Brown Cup Fungus; Miner's Lettuce; Coast Live Oak; Brown Ribbed Elfin Cup
ScientificName: Peziza repanda; Claytonia perfoliata; Montiaceae; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Helvella acetabulum

File.Name: h320d_h315-030.jpg
Notes: 33554249045 This is a well-named native Padre's Shooting Star (Primula clevelandii aka Dodecatheon clevelandii) wildflower in the Primulaceae plant family - looking like a basket of real shooting stars exploding in all directions. This is my photo for the Macro Mondays group, with the theme of "Happy 10 Years!" File.Name: h321a_h320-005.jpg
Notes:
33580012635 This is a cute little Broad-nosed Weevil (subfamily Entiminae, Curculionidae, Coleoptera) munching on a leaf of Hummingbird Sage (Salvia spathacea, Lamiaceae). There are many different kinds of these weevils, so I'll leave it at that. This one is small, about 845 pixels across in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to about 1/8 inch. (See here for how I figure.) (San Marcos Pass, 20 March 2017)

It started raining while it was still dark this morning, and never stopped all day - though it slowed down to a drizzle this afternoon. We have 2+ inches in the rain gauge, and it's still coming down! These late-season rains are important for our summer flowers, so I'm happy to see this one. It was also breezy today, so I was never tempted to go outside. I'm pretty distracted anyway. I'm going in for outpatient surgery tomorrow for a prostrate condition. I'll be back home tomorrow afternoon, but I'm assuming I won't feel like posting photos. Hah, I'd love to be wrong about that!

File.Name: h321b_h317-038.jpg
Notes:
32736660664 This is a mating pair of small beetles (Coleoptera) on a new leaf of Arroyo Willow (Salix lasiolepis, Salicaceae) by the creek. They are ladybug-like, but different. I believe they are Leaf Beetles (genus Chrysomela, Chrysomelidae), probably subgenus Macrolina - and maybe species Chrysomela schaefferi. My first thought was that the color difference is a gender difference, but they seem to be quite variable. Other individuals are orange with black spots, so the color difference might not mean anything. Happy hump day! (San Marcos Pass, 17 March 2017)

(Actually hump day is tomorrow. I won't be able to post on Wednesday so I'm posting to the groups early on the night before. I figure that it really is Wednesday in Flickr/UTC/GMT time right now.)

File.Name: h321d_h316-015.jpg
Notes:
33579069085 This is the web of a Sheet Web Weaver spider in the family Linyphiidae, likely a Bowl and Doily Spider (Frontinella communis. As I understand it, the web consists of three main parts. The main part is "the bowl" of non-sticky webbing. The spider hangs below this and grabs its prey from underneath. Hanging below this is "the doily" which seems to be like an oil pan to catch falling prey. Above the bowl is a fine network of random webbing that knocks flying insects into the bowl. I found this web in a not-quite-flowering native shrub of Black Sage (Salvia mellifera, Lamiaceae). This is a pretty neat structure for Web Wednesday! (San Marcos Pass, 16 March 2017)

(I won't be able to post tomorrow on Wednesday, so I'm posting to the groups early. I figure that it is Wednesday in Flickr/UTC/GMT time.)

File.Name: h323a_h323-008.jpg
Notes:
33486047901 Here is a handsome male Sonoran Blue (Philotes sonorensis) butterfly perched on a twig in the woods today. It's interesting that the lady butterflies in this photo> are even more brilliantly colored than the gentlemen like this one. This reverses the usual pattern with butterflies and ducks that the males are more brightly colored than the females. It's interesting that these are still the first of any kind of Blue (Polyommatinae, Lycaenidae) butterfly that I've managed to photograph this year. Usually I've photographed Echo Azure (Celastrina echo) and Silvery Blue (Glaucopsyche lygdamus) butterflies by now. (San Marcos Pass, 23 March 2017)

We ended up with 2-1/2 inches of rain in the last couple of days, better than expected. My surgery went well yesterday, but I had some difficulties after I got home. I guess sometimes you have to get worse before you can get better - tough with prostrate issues! I felt better today and went for a walk with my camera - and got three of my four photos today. That's recovery.
CommonNames: Sonoran Blue; Blue; Echo Azure; Silvery Blue

File.Name: h323b_h323-027.jpg
Notes: 32772100864 This is a Common Sawfly (family Tenthredinidae, "Symphyta", Hymenoptera) ovipositing - laying eggs - on budding new leaves of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). The eggs will hatch into larvae which devour the new leaves and catkins, see this photo. Really, these little devils can defoliate the trees if they get started early enough when the new leaves are still tender. I've been watching for the larvae on the oaks at home, and I haven't seen them so far. The trees do come back eventually, but they don't need the stress. (San Marcos Pass, 23 March 2017)
ScientificName: Tenthredinidae; "Symphyta"; Hymenoptera; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae

This is a female spider, and I have another photo of the male spider here. I got this photo down by our creek today, with a steel bridge railing as a backdrop. HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 23 March 2017)

Notes: 33614243915 If you didn't see this photo of almost-mating Propertius Duskywing (Erynnis propertius) butterflies last week, go look at it now. This is the same female butterfly a moment later in the same tree, when their union failed. Uh, she's exposing her genitalia, no doubt trying to lure the male back after I got too close. After I got this photo, I let them be, hopefully to finish what they started. The tree is a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) - which happens to be their host plant. HBBBT! File.Name: h324a_h324-012.jpg
Notes:
32818495963 These are the first new flowers of native Hairy Ceanothus (Ceanothus oliganthus) in the Rhamnaceae plant family growing along West Camino Cielo road. The flower clusters are deeper blue and in tighter clusters than the flowers of Greenbark Ceanothus (Ceanothus spinosus) in this photo. Look close - the leaves are also 3-veined from the base rather than 1-veined. This shrub is kind of a slow fire-follower that comes up on burns and clearings and starts to flower ten years later. This is no doubt the most handsome Ceanothus species in our part of the mountains, though they get deeper colors out west towards Vandenberg AFB and Point Sal - see Alice Abelia's photo of Mount Tranquillon Ceanothus in this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 24 March 2017)

It was sunny and breezy today. It really felt more like the end of a storm than the beginning of a new one, but we still have "rain likely" in the forecast for later tonight, and a band of rain is starting come into the area of our radar image.

File.Name: h324b_h324-032.jpg
Notes: 33248426070 Here are brand new flowers of native Fremont's Star Lily aka Chaparral Death Camas (Zigadenus fremontii var. inezianus) in the Liliaceae plant family - now called Toxicoscordion fremontii in the Melanthiaceae plant family. These are the first flowers I've seen this year. The variety inezianus isn't recognized in Jepson, but the plants do seem distinct - larger, with green rather than yellow glands at the base of the petals. Very beautiful. They are a fire-follower, though these are growing in a place that was cleared - not burned - a few years ago. Sometimes that's enough. There's a little true bug to the right of center that I guess is a Plant Bug in the family Miridae of the order Heteroptera, Hemiptera. (San Marcos Pass, 24 March 2017)

Notes: 33590760576 This is a Large Crane Fly (Tipulidae,Tipulomorpha, Diptera) hanging in the leaves of a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) tree. This is a lady with that pointy abdomen that's large with eggs, see this photo for both male and female. This one is a proper Large Crane Fly (family Tipulidae) with the "terminal (fourth) segment of maxillary palpus elongate, longer than first three segments combined." They may seem ungainly when they are trapped in your house, but they are quite at home in the woods hanging from the leaves - even missing a hind leg. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 20 March 2017)

File.Name: h325a_h325-010.jpg
Notes:
33610261336 I found this wonderful insect on a leaf of Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus vestitus, Fabaceae) in the woods today. This is a new one for me. After looking around BugGuide, I believe this is the San Francisco Lacewing (Nothochrysa californica, Chrysopidae, Neuroptera). Hah, it's a "green lacewing" that's not at all green - but it has similar eyes, compare this photo. It's called the lacewing from San Francisco, but they are found all along the coast up to British Columbia. (San Marcos Pass, 25 March 2017)

Our storm last night fell apart, leaving us with less than 1/10 inch of rain. Today was cloudy and sunny by turns, and increasingly breezy. The wind will likely be a problem over the next few days.

These handsome flies are not common, but I find them now and then in the woods. I don't know much more about them. Getting these photos every day is like casting a net into the sea and bringing in a fish. That tells you nothing about the creature except that it was here, today. The real challenge is to understand their lives! (San Marcos Pass, 25 March 2017)

It's confusing because we also have Arroyo Willow (Salix lasiolepis) growing about 20 feet away, compare this photo. I don't really pay much attention to willows except when they flower. This tree flowers later in the year and has longer staminate catkins that stand out more from the stems. It's quite striking. (San Marcos Pass, 25 March 2017)

File.Name: h326a_h326-053.jpg
Notes:
33543262321 This is a green "Timema" insect, probably Timema cristinae in the family Timematidae of the Phasmida clan, on a leaf of Hairy Ceanothus (Ceanothus oliganthus) in the Rhamnaceae plant family. Other individuals like the one in my [Previous] photo are brown. As far as I know, color doesn't matter with species identity. Most of the ones I find are green, but sometimes they have a white stripe down their back like this one. This color variability is interesting. I found more than a dozen of these insects today, both brown and green, mostly on this one Ceanothus shrub, more than I've ever seen before. I haven't seen any actually mating going on yet, but that must be getting close. Timemas are atypical walkingstick insects. There's some discussion of them in Smithsonian Magazine, but it's better to go to the source of Christina Sandoval - the "cristinae" of the specific name. The Timema page at BugGuide is excellent. (San Marcos Pass, 26 March 2017)

We had a little dry cold front move in today, bringing no rain but some wind. *Sigh* We're expecting big winds Monday and Tuesday, and the NWS forecast says something about 50 mph gusts. We're under both a High Wind Watch and a Wind Advisory. I got a few extra photos today just in case!

Notes: 32858797893 This is a brown "Timema" insect, probably Timema cristinae in the family Timematidae of the Phasmida clan, on a leaf of Goldencup Oak aka Canyon Live Oak (Quercus chrysolepis, Fagaceae). Other individuals like the one in my [Next] photo are green. As far as I know, color doesn't matter with species identity. Most of the ones I find are green, but this might have to do with camouflage more than anything else. I found more than a dozen of these insects today, mostly on Ceanothus, more than I've ever seen before. I haven't seen any actually mating going on yet, but that must be getting close. Timemas are atypical walkingstick insects. There's some discussion of them in Smithsonian Magazine, but it's better to go to the source of Christina Sandoval - the "cristinae" of the specific name. The Timema page at BugGuide is excellent. I've always thought their preferred host plants are Chamise and Ceanothus, but I've found them on oak before, see this photo. Or is this species Timema chumash which do favor oaks? (San Marcos Pass, 26 March 2017)

File.Name: h326c_h326-040.jpg
Notes: 33630815746 Thanks to Jeffrey Caldwell and Paul Johnson, I now believe that this is a horde of the caterpillars of California Tortoiseshell (Nymphalis californica) butterflies. See the comments below. They are devouring the leaves and flowers of a shrub of native Hairy Ceanothus (Ceanothus oliganthus) in the Rhamnaceae plant family. My first thought was "oh no, tent caterpillars" - but I don't see any signs of their silken shroud-like tents. My second thought was some kind of Checkerspot butterfly. With some help, I finally got it right. I've been seeing more of the adult butterflies than usual this year, see this photo. I reckon we'll have a lot more of them in a couple of months! (San Marcos Pass, 26 March 2017)

File.Name: h326d_h326-030.jpg
Notes: 32828169344 Here are two shots of a tiny Orb-Weaver Spider (Araneus, Araneidae) under a leaf of Goldencup Oak aka Canyon Live Oak (Quercus chrysolepis, Fagaceae). It looks like one of the large bottlecap-size spiders that collect under the eaves of our house in the fall, though this one can't be much more than 1/4 inch long. I believe it is a male with those swollen palps. This spider is either A. bispinosus or A. montereyensis, and the way to tell them apart is to look at the way the abdomen dips between the two humps. (See Alice Abelia's comment to this photo.) This one does seems to have a dip, so maybe it's Araneus montereyensis - but I figure "one of those" is good enough for me! (San Marcos Pass, 26 March 2017)

File.Name: h327a_h326-048.jpg
Notes:
32878492873 Here is a green "Timema" insect (Timema cristinae, Timematidae, Phasmida) on a leaf of Hairy Ceanothus (Ceanothus oliganthus) in the Rhamnaceae plant family - with flowers. Only there's not just one timema, but two! The other one is vertical on the left side of the new leaf in the center of my photo. These are both green, but other individuals in the same shrub are brown. As far as I know, color doesn't matter with species identity. Sometimes they have a white stripe down their back like this one. This color variability is interesting. I found more than a dozen of these insects today, both brown and green, mostly on this one Ceanothus shrub, more than I've ever seen before. I haven't seen any actually mating going on yet, but that must be getting close. Timemas are atypical walkingstick insects. There's some discussion of them in Smithsonian Magazine, but it's better to go to the source of Christina Sandoval - the "cristinae" of the specific name. The Timema page at BugGuide is excellent. The shrub is also interesting, see this photo for more info. (San Marcos Pass, 26 March 2017)

The wind did kick up today as expected, with gusts to 40 mph so far according to the NWS station about 1/2 mile away. We're currently under a High Wind Warning. I never went out for photos today, though I did take my photo for Macro Mondays. It will be nice if the wind dies down tomorrow, but I wouldn't bet the farm on it!

File.Name: h327b_h325-034.jpg
Notes: 33650580166 I found this small (but not tiny) Harvestman (Opiliones) spider crawling along our gravel road in the canyon. More properly, it's not a spider at all but an arachnid. It has just one body part and two eyes. I guess this one is superfamily Phalangioidea of the suborder Eupnoi, but I won't guess beyond this. I don't find then very often, especially out in the open like this. When I first saw it, I thought it might have prey, but no - those are very large palps. I'm curious why they have them, since according to BugGuide: "Uniquely among the arachnids fertilization is direct: males of most taxa possess a penis (also referred to in the literature as pene, aedagus or intromittent organ)." Interesting! That means that the palps are not sexual organs like they are with true male spiders, so why are they so large? (San Marcos Pass, 25 March 2017)
ScientificName: Opiliones; Phalangioidea; Eupnoi
associatedTaxa: Opiliones; Phalangioidea; Eupnoi

One possibility is genus Coriarachne, see this photo, but it doesn't look quite right. A more likely possibility is a Ground Crab Spider in the genus Xysticus. (San Marcos Pass, 24 March 2017)

File.Name: h327d_h327-007.jpg
Notes: 32844389034 Here is an early foxtail stuck in my sock. It's the seed of either Foxtail Barley (Hordeum jubatum) or Ripgut Grass (Bromus diandrus), probably the former with those relatively short awns. Both are non-native weeds that are now ubiquitous, I have a photo here. It's really too early for this, and the seeds are still tight on the plants, but I picked a few of each kind and kept them on the dashboard of my truck for a few days to dry out. That worked. Then I took a clean sock and exposed it to the foxtails. Hah, they almost jump into the sock! I try to remember to wear gaiters when I have to walk through bristly grasses like foxtail or ripgut, but sometimes I forget. And sometimes I have to throw the socks out afterwards! This is my photo for the Macro Mondays group, with the theme of "Cloth/Textile". HMM! (San Marcos Pass, 27 March 2017)
ScientificName: Hordeum jubatum; Bromus diandrus
associatedTaxa: Hordeum jubatum; Bromus diandrus

File.Name: h328a_h328-015.jpg

It's the first one I've seen this year, and it looks absolutely fresh - like it just emerged from its chrysalis this morning! Today started windy and ended up almost calm. It was curious that the sun was warm this morning but the winds were cold. It was warm between the gusts, and then cooled down again when the wind came back. It got into the mid-70s today, and tomorrow might get into the 80s. No wonder so many butterflies are out!

File.Name: h328c_h328-031.jpg
Notes: 33326149000 This is a plain Echo Azure aka Pacific Azure (Celastrina echo) butterfly "puddling" - taking moisture - on damp soil in the canyon. This butterfly was formerly known as the Spring Azure (Celastrina ladon) but that name is now restricted to butterflies from the Appalachian and Ozark ranges. These small butterflies have no strong markings below, but they are real Blues (subfamily (Polyommatinae, Lepidoptera), and this one looked quite blue when it took wing. You can see hints of blue here - and see this photo for the whole deal. I've been seeing fewer and fewer of these butterflies over the last few years, and I hope they rebound with this year's rain. This is the first one I've managed to photograph this year, and I've found them as early as January in past years. (San Marcos Pass, 28 March 2017)

Notes: 33710052745 This is a Mournful Duskywing (Erynnis tristis) butterfly "puddling" - taking moisture - from a sandstone rock in the canyon. The Funereal Duskywing (E. funeralis) butterflies are also similar, but they are more black than brown, see this photo. File.Name: h329a_h329-018.jpg
Notes:
33688622256 This is a tiny Lace Bug (Tingidae, Heteroptera, Hemiptera) on a leaflet of Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus vestitus, Fabaceae). This is where I usually find them, though we seem to have a different species on shrubs of Trask's Yerba Santa (Eriodictyon traskiae, Boraginaceae) - see this photo. You can see a bit of the skinny little bug under the remarkable net-like lattice-work on their body that reminds me of shogun-era samurai armor - but also looks like bubble-wrap! This is the first one I've seen this year, and I wonder if these insects actually over-winter as adults. Hah, there's not enough DoF at f/128 to photograph these bumpy critters! (San Marcos Pass, 29 March 2017)

It was warm and not too breezy today, so I went back to the creek to get a closer shot of a Sonoran Blue butterfly for my Macro Mondays photo. But It was odd. Yesterday, I got photos of five different butterflies, and today I couldn't get one. Maybe it was just cool enough yesterday to slow them down enough to photograph?
