Notes: 32886405444 Here are two shots of a remarkable debris-carrying larva of a Green Lacewing (ChrysopidaeNeuroptera) on a new leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). You can see the critter inside in the photo on the right. BugGuide has a nice page about these with lots of photos if you click the [Images] button. Apparently the debris-carrying larvae cover themselves with bits of litter, perhaps to deter predators. It is great camouflage. The next time you see a bit of lint moving along a leaf, look closer! (San Marcos Pass, 28 March 2017)

File.Name: h329c_h326-020.jpg
Notes: 33572611132 Here is a pair of mating pairs of Flea Beetles in the group Alticini, Chrysomelidae, Coleoptera. If you look close at my photo, you can see that these beetles have enlarged femurs on their hind legs - yep, they can jump like fleas! The beetles are so small and shiny that I first thought they were oil drops on the leaves. They are a bit over a bit over 1/10 inch, see this photo. The little beetles have been destroying the new leaves of some shrubs of Climbing Penstemon (Keckiella cordifolia, Plantaginaceae). I'm not sure if the plants will recover. Happy hump day! (San Marcos Pass, 26 March 2017)

File.Name: h330a_h330-026.jpg
Notes:
32933834123 "That feels *so* good!"

This is a handsome Syrphid Flower Fly (Syrphidae, Diptera) scratching its back on a twig in the woods today. Well, it's grooming its abdomen anyway. Maybe this fly is the species Scaeva pyrastri in the tribe Syrphini? It's also a pretty good butt shot, so HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 30 March 2017)

Today started gloomy and breezy. I figured it was the "fog winds" that come with the marine layer fog. Then the fog cleared out about noon just as I got this photo, and the real winds kicked up with gusts to 40 mph. Mostly I looked close to the ground today for mushrooms and slime molds that weren't blowing in the wind. This fly was a bonus! I didn't really mean to photograph it at f/20. That's what happens when you try to shoot an insect just after shooting mushrooms - and forget. It worked out OK in this case.

File.Name: h330b_h330-018.jpg
Notes:
33706220556 Here are tiny Slime Molds (Myxomycetes or Myxogastria) growing on rotting wood from a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) tree in the woods. These look like little insect eggs, but something different will come from their spores, see this photo for a bit more info. I believe the photo shows sessile (without a stalk) sporangia or plasmodiocarps. In the past I've something like this Diderma effusum, see this photo, but the bodies in this photo are much more closely aggregated. I don't know what this is. (San Marcos Pass, 30 March 2017)

(Hah - is that a tiny insect egg on a stalk on the bottom of my photo?)

File.Name: h330c_h330-008.jpg
Notes: 33590313872 I believe this is a Panther Amanita (Amanita pantherina) mushroom growing under a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree in the woods today. It has warts on the cap, a ring on the stem, and a collar-like volva that adheres to the base except for a free rim. This one may have some influence from the Jonquil Amanita (Amanita gemmata). These mushrooms are quite poisonous. My book says it's one of the most common causes of mushroom poisoning in the Pacific Northwest, but they are not dangerous to handle or photograph! Aren't they elegant and graceful. It's getting late in the season to find any mushrooms, but I was happy to find several of these today. They are a sign that the mushroom mycelium has survived our five year drought. (San Marcos Pass, 30 March 2017)
CommonNames: Panther Amanita; Coast Live Oak; Jonquil Amanita

associatedTaxa: Amanita pantherina; Quercus agrifolia; Amanita gemmata

File.Name: h330d_h330-011.jpg
Notes: 33705873266 I guess these are the Yellow-Staining Agaricus (Agaricus xanthodermus) mushrooms in the woods under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) and Tanbark Oak (Lithocarpus densiflorus) trees. They look beautiful and edible, but they taste terrible - with a strong phenolic odor and taste, like an old fashioned doctor's office. Don't eat them! All parts of the mushroom stain bright yellow as you handle it, including the very base of the stem. They also have a thick skirt-like ring on the stalk, with patches of tissue on the underside. This mushroom and the Felt-Ringed Agaricus (Agaricus hondensis, photo here) are the reason I'm wary of all our woodland Agaricus mushrooms. (San Marcos Pass, 30 March 2017)
CommonNames: Yellow-Staining Agaricus; Coast Live Oak; Tanbark Oak; Felt-Ringed Agaricus

File.Name: h331a_h331-026.jpg
Notes:
33724037036 Starting from the left side, here is a normal new flower of Holly-Leaved Redberry (Rhamnus ilicifolia) in the Rhamnaceae plant family. Then in the center is a galled flower on the same shrub. I'm not sure what critter makes these galls, as they are not mentioned in my book by Ron Russo, "Field Guide to Plant Galls" (UC Press, 2006). On the side of the gall is a tiny "parasitic wasp" (Hymenoptera) - a predator on the larvae inside! I believe the wasp is a Chalcid Wasp (Chalcidoidea), maybe in the family Torymidae which favor galls. See this photo of a similar wasp on a different gall. If you look close, you can see the wasp's ovipositor inserted into the galled flower and into the gall-inducing insect within. It seems odd that the wasp's ovipositor comes from the front of the abdomen, but I believe they fold it under their belly to the middle like this. The galled flower has a parasite inside, so I reckon this wasp is a hyperparasite - a parasite whose host is a parasite. I suppose this isn't the best photo, but I was excited to capture this scene! (San Marcos Pass, 31 March 2017)

There was still some residual breeze today, but the big winds subsided this morning. It was pretty terrible yesterday and last night with 30-40 mph gusts, that's a lot of air going somewhere! I'm looking forward to getting out with my camera tomorrow.

(Apparently the [Post a Flickr Reaction] is an April Fools joke - see Flickr Help Forum. Good joke, but please don't!)
Locality: Apparently the [Post a Flickr Reaction] is an April Fools joke - see Flickr Help Forum. Good joke

File.Name: h331b_h331-015.jpg
Notes: 33723797356 This is a tiny gall on the upper side of a new leaf of Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family. It's beautiful and strange - and quite small, only a few mm high. I'm not sure whose gall this is, but I've seen them before, here and < a href="https://www.flickr.com/photos/treebeard/13918836451/">here. It might be the spring sexual generation gall that will produce the summer unisexual galls of the Crystalline Gall Wasp (Andricus crystallinus, Cynipidae, Hymenoptera), see this photo and this one. On the other hand, I find lots of these galls, but this new one is unusual. I don't know what it is. (San Marcos Pass, 31 March 2017)

Notes:
33742610836 This Silvery Blue (Glaucopsyche lygdamus) butterfly seems to be "puddling" - taking moisture - from a dead leaf! It was warm (not quite 80∞) and not too breezy today, and tomorrow will hopefully be more of the same.

File.Name: h402a_h402-027.jpg
Notes:
33765382006 Here are beautiful new flowers of the uncommon Ojai Fritillary (Fritillaria ojaiensis) in the Liliaceae plant family. The plants have whorls of very skinny leaves, like a lily on a diet. There are only a few plants with flowers in this place, but they are hanging on after our drought years. These plants are growing in a place where the sun rarely shines - but I happened to get there at just the right time today to catch it in a beam of sunlight! This plant is included in the CNPS Inventory of Rare and Endangered Plants on list 1B.2 - "rare, threatened, or endangered in CA and elsewhere." I'm going to "stealth" the location of this wonderful flower, but I'll show the dirt road to walk down. (I also "stealth" our home, though I show the canyon.) (San Marcos Pass, 2 April 2017)

Today was a fine spring day with temps in the 70s and not too much breeze. May there be many more days like this!

File.Name: h402b_h402-038.jpg
Notes: 33764738236 This Timema (Timematidae, Phasmida) insect sure looks different from our usual Timema cristinae insects in my [Previous] photo and in this one. It's on a different host plant as well, a Chaparral Currant (Ribes malvaceum, Grossulariaceae). On the other hand, it sure looks like Alice Abelia's photo at BugGuide which is identified as Timema cristinae. They look different, but are they? The genus page at BugGuide says that "dependable species ID requires study of the shape of externally visible structures of the terminalia, especially of the male (for non-parthenogenetic spp.)..." and that's beyond me, though I believe that this one is male with those asymmetric "claspers". Timemas are atypical walkingstick insects. I have some more info and links in my [Previous] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 2 April 2017)

File.Name: h402c_h402-048.jpg
Notes:
32961236274 Here is a green "Timema" insect (Timema cristinae, Timematidae, Phasmida) curled up in new leaf of native California Ash (Fraxinus dipetala) in the Oleaceae plant family. I've been finding more Timemas than usual this year, on a greater variety of host plants than usual. Note that this one has a green stripe down its back and sides while this one doesn't. Timemas are atypical walkingstick insects. There's some discussion of them in Smithsonian Magazine, but it's better to go to the source of Christina Sandoval - the "cristinae" of the specific name. The Timema page at BugGuide is excellent. (San Marcos Pass, 2 April 2017)

I think of Timemas as occurring on Chamise (Adenostoma fasciculatum and Greenbark Ceanothus (Ceanothus spinosus), but I've also been finding them on Hairy Ceanothus (Ceanothus oliganthus), California Peony (Paeonia californica), Canyon Live Oak (Quercus chrysolepis, and Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia). Strangest of all might be the one I found last year on Snowdrop Bush (Styrax redivivus, Styracaceae), see this photo. And this not to mention the odd Timema in my [Next] photo on Chaparral Currant (Ribes malvaceum)!

It's a beautiful combination. The ash is a shrub or small tree that growing (untypically) in the mountains along East Camino Cielo road - with Figueroa Mountain/Ranger Peak in the background across the Santa Ynez Valley to establish it's provenance. They are beautiful winter-deciduous shrubs with odd-pinnate leaves and interesting flowers, see my this photo. I usually find these shrubs along the river, but there are a few up here in the mountains. (San Marcos Pass, 2 April 2017)

File.Name: h403a_h402-006.jpg
Notes:
33012355173 Here is a Blue-belly Lizard aka Western Fence Lizard (Sceloporus occidentalis) perched on our retaining wall yesterday morning. It has blue mating colors - and Ticks (Ixodoidea, Arachnida)! I guess the "sweet spot" (for ticks) is the lizard's ear tympanum, down from it's eyes. But there's another "sweet spot" down lower just above it's front leg that I don't understand. Apparently these are immature forms of the same ticks that usually get me in the summer, though I've already been "ticked" a few times this year. The host lizards may actually kill the bacteria that carry Lyme disease. Our mountain ticks have a complicated life-cycle that moves from wood rat (rodent) to lizard (reptile) to human (me!). I don't quite understand the details, but I'm glad the lizards act as a filter. There's more info on the web. Sure, I seem to get this photo every year. Here's my photo from last year. I find this fascinating. (San Marcos Pass, 2 April 2017)

It was sunny with a slight breeze this morning. Sometime after noon, I had an errand in town and *whoa* the marine layer fog was on the verge of coming over the pass! I guess it's early "June gloom" weather - but what's surprising is that there were none of the "fog winds" that usually come with the fog. I don't understand.

ScientificName: Sceloporus occidentalis; Ixodoidea; Arachnida

This one is taking moisture from a cup-like leaf of Miner's Lettuce (Claytonia perfoliata, Montiaceae). I don't think I've ever seen these wasps visit flowers. (San Marcos Pass, 2 April 2017)

File.Name: h404a_h404-012.jpg
Notes:
33846724025 This is a brand new Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly on brand new open flowers of native Sugar Bush (Rhus ovata) in the Anacardiaceae plant family. I'm sure I'll be seeing many more of them this year, because their caterpillars are happily munching the leaves of (mostly) the shrub Southern Bush Monkey Flower (Mimulus aurantiacus var. pubescens, Phrymaceae). Hah - it's kind of a local naturalist-photographers' secret that these butterflies are no doubt the easiest of all our butterflies to get close to and photograph. They sure are pretty though! (San Marcos Pass, 4 April 2017)

Today was sunny with no marine layer fog, but there was some breeze this morning that felt like the "fog winds" that usually come with fog/stratus. It's curious because yesterday we had fog but no wind. Go figure.

File.Name: h404b_h404-054.jpg
Notes:
33033151603 Here are the first new flowers of native Snowdrop Bush (Styrax redivivus) in the Styracaceae plant family fully open along West Camino Cielo road in the mountains today! This deciduous shrub has everything - gorgeous white flowers like this, edible nuts (here), and pretty autumn leaves (here)! The flowers have a strong, sweet fragrance like perfume. For the few weeks that they flower, there is no finer wildflower in our mountains. They are pretty common on here San Marcos pass - both in woodland and chaparral. They are stunning flowers, worth hunting for. Last year I found them on March 16, so they are late this year. (San Marcos Pass, 4 April 2017)

The shrubs are unusual, a singleton in their family here in California. The specimen map at Calflora shows them all around the central valley in California, but I suspect their distribution is highly localized. They are common here on the pass, but I don't think I've seen them anywhere else in Santa Barbara County. There are a few records from San Luis Obispo county to the north and none from Ventura county to the south.
ScientificName: Styrax redivivus
associatedTaxa: NA

File.Name: h404c_h404-033.jpg
Notes:
33690174442 Another day, another Timema...

Really, I've been finding more of these than ever before lately. Either I've finally learned what to look for, or they are having a very good year. This "Timema" insect (Timema cristinae, Timematidae, Phasmida) is on a native shrub of Chamise (Adenostoma fasciculatum, Rosaceae) - where it should be. It is green with white stripes, but other individuals are green without stripes (here) or completely brown (here. I found more than a dozen of them today in this one shrub of Chamise, and they were of all different color types. Timemas are atypical walkingstick insects which are endemic to California and neighboring states. They're cute! I have a bit more info in my comments on this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 4 April 2017)

File.Name: h404d_h404-018.jpg
Notes: 33689928902 I believe this iridescent beetle grub will become a genus Trirhabda beetle (Chrysomelidae, Coleoptera). It's on a furry leaf of Trask's Yerba Santa (Eriodictyon traskiae) in the Boraginaceae plant family. The beetles are in the subfamily Galerucinae of this family, the Skeletonizing Leaf Beetles, which describes what the larvae do to the foliage. They definitely inflict some damage! As far as I know, there are only two of the beetles that feed on Yerba Santa, so it's Trirhabda diducta and Trirhabda eriodictyonis. I suspect it's the latter since I've found them before on the same plant, see this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 4 April 2017)

File.Name: h406b_h406-025.jpg
Notes: 33070932193 This is a Pinacate Beetle (genus Coelocnemis, Tenebrionidae, Coleoptera) showing its rear end. The genus Eleodes is similar, but this one has two rows of fine, golden brown hairs on the lower half of the inside margin of the hind tibiae, which are barely visible on the left. It's also known as a Stink Beetle or Stinkbug or Acrobat Beetle or Clown Beetle, but I prefer the old name. Wikipedia says that "the name pinacate is Mexican Spanish, derived from the Nahuatl (Aztec) name for the insect, pinacatl..." Kids like to torment these large beetles - don't let them! The beetles put their heads on the earth, think of them as wise. These beetles are said to release a brew of noxious chemicals from their rear ends, but I can't vouch for that. HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 6 April 2017)

File.Name: h407a_h407-024.jpg
Notes:
33089308763 This is another tiny Orb-Weaver Spider (Araneus, Araneidae) with prey under a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). The spider's prey is a small Syrphid Flower Fly (Syrphidae, Diptera) - which makes a good ruler for the spider. It looks like one of the large bottlecap-size spiders that collect under the eaves of our house in the fall, though this one is not much more than 1/4 inch long. I've been finding more of these spiders than usual this year, but maybe I'm just learning to find them. This one is curious because of those two white spots on its abdomen which I haven't seen before. This spider is either A. bispinosus or A. montereyensis, and the way to tell them apart is to look at the way the abdomen dips between the two humps. (See Alice Abelia's comment to this photo.) I'm still not sure, but I figure "one of those" is good enough for me! (San Marcos Pass, 7 April 2017)

File.Name: h408a_h408-037.jpg
Notes:
33765601992 Julie noticed this extraordinary Ceanothus Silk Moth (Hyalophora euryalus, Saturniinae) clinging to an outside wall of our house late this afternoon. There happens to be a shrub of Greenbark Ceanothus (Ceanothus spinosus) in the Rhamnaceae plant family close by, which is one of its host plants. I hope this one will lay eggs. (I guess that it is a female with that plump abdomen full of eggs?) I occasionally find their egg-sized silk cocoons on the Ceanothus shrubs, though I don't think I've ever seen a caterpillar. I'm shocked that I have no photos of the cocoons on Flickr, but see this photo at Fotolog where I used to post photos. I guess the moths have been scarce during our drought years. These are large hand-sized moths with wing expanse up to seven inches. Hah, the caterpillars get up to four inches long - and I still haven't seen one! The large moths are beautiful. I'll be looking for this one again tomorrow. (San Marcos Pass, 8 April 2017)

These butterflies are multi-brooded, and the spring and summer forms differ in color - compare this photo from last September. File.Name: h409b_h409-005.jpg
Notes: 33786257762 This sure looks like a little caterpillar - but it's not! This is the larva of a Sawfly Wasp ("Symphyta", Hymenoptera). They are eruciform larvae, which means "caterpillar-like" - with two differences. First, they have simple eye-spots whereas caterpillars usually have a broken ring of eyespots - see this photo. Second, sawfly larvae always have more prolegs - six or more pairs - while caterpillars have five pairs or fewer. There's a useful page on distinguishing them from the Missouri Botanical Garden. Sometimes it's hard to tell them apart, but it might be important. This one is on a twig of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae), and they can become major pests on these trees though they don't get much attention. A couple of years ago, they came close to defoliating my favorite tree at home. At least you should know what's doing the damage! (San Marcos Pass, 9 April 2017)
ScientificName: "Symphyta"; Hymenoptera; eruciform; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae
associatedTaxa: "Symphyta"; Hymenoptera; eruciform; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae

File.Name: h409d_h409-033.jpg
Notes: 7733943041775 This is a different furry little Bee Fly (family Bombyliidae, Diptera) with nice whiskers. It looks a bit like the genus Acreophthiria fly in this photo, but it has strongly patterned wings. Maybe this one is genus Conophorus? It's perched on a flower of Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus, Rosaceae) that looks quite different from the flower in this photo taken at home. The difference is interesting. It turns out that most of the plants east of San Marcos Pass are staminate with a central core of pistils, and most of the plants west of San Marcos Pass are pistillate with no stamens. See this photo for a comparison shot. Sure enough, the Jepson Flora says that most flowers of this plant are unisexual. Maybe this is why I rarely find fruit? Are the plants separate clones? I really don't understand this, but if we could just get the two together we might get some decent berries! (San Marcos Pass, 9 April 2017)
ScientificName: Conophorus
associatedTaxa: Rubus ursinus

File.Name: h410a_h410-023.jpg
Notes:
33121252984 This is love not battle! Here are a mating pair of Crab Spiders (Thomisidae), probably genus Mecaphesa again, on a shiny new leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). The spiders are an example of strong sexual dimorphism - meaning that males and females look totally different. I don't think I've ever seen mating Crab Spiders before, and it's shocking how different they are. Well, as long as they can tell the difference! Yep, I got more photos from the far side and back. I'll save those for hump day Wednesday and BBB Thursday. (San Marcos Pass, 10 April 2017)

Julie and I plan to (finally!) head out to the Carrizo Plain for a couple of days. We should be back on Wednesday evening. We might be late for the mass flower displays, but I'm sure we'll find individual flowers, which I'm really more interested in. See the photos by Marlin Harms, Bill Bouton, Sam McMillan, and Patrick Dirlam - all of the same hill of wildflowers! I'll admit that I'm more interested in the pollinators. Is there an explosion of insects to match this flower explosion or are they all self-pollinated?

I almost forgot that I hit a minor milestone today with 3,000,000+ views of my photos! I was "Treebeard" at Fotolog starting in 2003, but I didn't really start posting at Flickr until 13 February 2012. Thanks!
Locality: finally!) head out to the Carrizo Plain for a couple of days.  We should be back on Wednesday evening.  We might be late for the mass flower displays

File.Name: h411a_h411-043.jpg
Notes:
33828258722 I wish I knew for sure what this photo shows. I got it on a leaf of Poison Oak (Toxicodendron diversilobum, Anacardiaceae). It shows a big yellow Crab Spider (Thomisidae) on the right, probably genus Mecaphesa again with those hairs ("setae") on its abdomen. She's a large lady. But what is that husk on the left? If you look close, it appears to be male with swollen palps. (I'll make a note.) So did the lady eat her mate as the story goes? These spiders show strong sexual dimorphism, the ladies are much larger than the males, see my photo yesterday. Or is she just investigating a cast-off shed skin or exuvia after a molt? It looks kind of like a molt with that shriveled abdomen, see this photo. Could the lady spider really suck the male so dry? Like I said, I wish I knew for sure what this photo shows. It would be a shame to be out there when many of the flowers were closed because of clouds. We'll try again next week. We do have a chance of light rain at home Wednesday and Thursday. Notes: 33827883642 I found this pair of butterfly chrysalises in the woods today, both in small trees of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). What's interesting is that I found it about seven feet up in an oak tree with its Southern Bush Monkey Flower (Mimulus aurantiacus var. pubescens, Phrymaceae) host plant at the base. That's a good climb for a caterpillar. The chrysalis on the left is more interesting. I'm pretty sure it belongs to a California Sister (Adelpha bredowii) butterfly. File.Name: h412a_h412-012.jpg
Notes:
33963291666 This is an uncommon native Fire Poppy (Papaver californicum) in the Papaveraceae plant family. It's the other "California Poppy" that only grows as a fire-follower after recent wildfire burns or (less commonly) after clearings. I found this one in a place that was cleared but didn't burn, and sometimes that's enough to trigger these plants. It's seeds can last for many years waiting for the next fire or disturbance. It really looks like a little Oriental Poppy (Papaver somniferum). Isn't it beautiful! (San Marcos Pass, 12 April 2017)

File.Name: h412b_h412-029.jpg
Notes: 33874980301 This strange-looking critter is the ant-tended caterpillar of a Gossamer-Wing Butterfly (Lycaenidae, Lepidoptera) - the group of Coppers, Blues, Elfins, and Hairstreaks. I found it on catkins of a Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia, Fagaceae). I suspect it will become a California Hairstreak (Satyrium californica) butterfly - but I'm not sure, and photos are scarce on the web. The ants may well be the California Velvety Tree Ant (Liometopum occidentale, Dolichoderinae) which is the most common ant in our woods - and sometimes in our house as well. They tend the caterpillar and keep parasites away in exchange for honeydew - you can see a drop forming in front of the top ant. (I'll add a note.) Uh, is that the rear end of the caterpillar? I think I see prolegs there, which means the wide end is the head. (San Marcos Pass, 12 April 2017)

File.Name: h412c_h410-030.jpg
Notes: 33962789496 This is another photo of mating Crab Spiders (Thomisidae), probably genus Mecaphesa again, on a shiny new leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). See this photo for another shot from the far side. The spiders are an example of strong sexual dimorphism - meaning that males and females look totally different. It's a tender moment ... and complicated! Mating is difficult for these predators who are ready to attack anything that moves. I guess it's always difficult. As I understand it, the male deposits tiny drops of sperm on a special "sperm web" and then inflates the spermaphores on the end of his leg-like palps to pick it up. He then transfers his sperm to the female's epigynum. Of course that description leaves out all the romance! See this photo to photos back for what may be the final outcome of this mating behavior. I'll post one more photo of these spiders for BBB Thursday tomorrow. Happy hump day! (San Marcos Pass, 10 April 2017)

Notes: 33160549164 This small spider is resting for a moment after shedding its old skin - the exuviae in the upper-left. I reckon the webbing helped it get some traction during the act of molting. Maybe the spider is a small Orb-Weaver Spider (Araneidae), though the webbing looks chaotic. I got this on a gloomy morning with a bit of breeze, so I had to use my on-camera flash to freeze the action. I prefer not to use flash, but sometimes it's handy. The shed skin is interesting in light of my [Previous] photo. You can see that the "head" of the exuviae is separated from the rest of it, but this is not true with the unfortunate male spider in that photo. I think this is what Maurice meant in his comment. Happy Web Wednesday! (San Marcos Pass, 7 April 2017)

File.Name: h413a_h413-018.jpg
Notes:
33179385694 This is a mating pair of small beetles (Coleoptera) on a on a leaf of Arroyo Willow (Salix lasiolepis, Salicaceae), with raindrops. Or is it? If you look closer, you can see that they are facing in different directions! I believe these are Leaf Beetles (genus Chrysomela, Chrysomelidae), probably subgenus Macrolina - and maybe species Chrysomela schaefferi. I have another shot of the same beetles really "doing it" in this photo. In both of my photos, there is sexual dimorphism - meaning that males and females look quite different. I'm sure that's the lady below, and something odd is happening at her rear end on the right. Laying eggs? Taking a dump? (San Marcos Pass, 13 April 2017)

Our wannabe storm last night devolved into drizzle this morning. It was breezy all day, becoming downright windy when it finally cleared up in the afternoon. We're under a NWS Wind Advisory tonight, and the breeze will probably continue tomorrow.

The Crab Spider (Thomisidae) is hidden in the new flowers of a Holly-Leaved Redberry (Rhamnus ilicifolia, Rhamnaceae) shrub. Great camouflage. I'm not sure, but the fly might be a Cluster Fly in the genus Pollenia, Calliphoridae, Diptera. I've had great luck finding spiders this week. It's interesting that they are so common in the spring - and also in the fall for Arachtober. (San Marcos Pass, 12 April 2017)

Notes: 33178798584 This is a little Crab Spider (Thomisidae) is hanging on new flowers of native Southern Tauschia (Tauschia arguta, Apiaceae). It's probably genus Mecaphesa again with that hairy abdomen. It's interesting that the flowers are protogynous with female parts appearing before the male parts - i.e. pistils before stamens. The reverse is more typical. The spider has prey that you can't see, but it's an excellent butt shot, so HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 9 April 2017)

File.Name: h414a_h414-013.jpg
Notes:
34041333005 Here is the first flower of native Fairy Lantern (Calochortus albus, Liliaceae) that I've seen this year, but it's growing by Lake Cachuma instead of in the woods at home. Our flowers are close, but not quite ready. They are a kind of upside-down Mariposa Lily. They are a "tender trap" for pollinators - easy for insects to crawl into, but hard to get out past those hairs without carrying a load of pollen to pass on to other flowers. Very beautiful. (Lake Cachuma, Santa Ynez Valley, 14 April 2017)

We had 45-50 mph winds last night. It's been gradually calming down today, but there was still some breeze when I tried to get some photos this morning. I had errands in the Santa Ynez Valley today, which gave me some time in the woods near Lake Cachuma. It was breezy enough to get in the way of my photos. Tomorrow might be better!

File.Name: h414b_h413-024.jpg
Notes: 33229063103 I'm pretty sure this is a Pacific Woodrush (Luzula comosa) in the Juncaceae plant family. Rushes usually grow in wet places, but this one prefers to be dry. The plants have broad grass-like leaves and interesting flower heads that are different from grasses, see this photo. This is one of those plants that might be common, but I rarely notice it. Did you notice the winged aphid on the upper flower head? (San Marcos Pass, 13 April 2017)

File.Name: h414c_h413-020.jpg
Notes: 33655922800 Our mushrooms are mostly done for the year, but I'm still finding a few. I believe this is some kind of Agrocybe mushroom, probably the Spring Agrocybe (Agrocybe praecox). It's also called the Early Agrocybe - which is funny since here it is at the very end of our mushroom season. This one has a brownish cap and an evanescent veil that catches the brown spores. These are growing with the weedy grass along the road in our canyon. Also in the photo is native Bowlesia incana in the Apiaceae plant family, with lobed leaves and flowers as open as they ever are - see this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 13 April 2017)

Notes: 33197795474 Here are two shots of a bristly fly (Diptera) working new flowers of native Southern Tauschia (Tauschia arguta, Apiaceae). I guess from its general bristliness that it's another Tachinid Fly (Tachinidae), likely genus Gonia. What an odd face it has - too broad and too much white forehead. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 9 April 2017)

File.Name: h415a_h415-028.jpg
Notes:
33249459863 This is the first Grass-Skipper butterfly I've managed to photograph this year - surely an Umber Skipper (Poanes melane). It's taking nectar from the flower in a cup-like leaf of Miner's Lettuce (Claytonia perfoliata, Montiaceae). What perfect aim it has! This plant has been having a very good year, producing large clumps of leaves - even in our garden. I'll get a photo one of this days. (San Marcos Pass, 15 April 2017)

We had some residual breeze today - no great gusts, but enough to keep everything in motion. It was interesting that the breeze was mostly from the south. That's our typical rain wind, and we do have another small storm (or two) coming in late Sunday through Tuesday.

File.Name: h415b_h415-004.jpg
Notes: 33676404240 This is an adult Assassin Bug (Reduviidae, Heteroptera, Hemiptera) on a leaflet of native Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus vestitus, Fabaceae). I'm pretty sure it's a Leafhopper Assassin Bug (Zelus renardii), the first adult I've seen this year. You can see its mighty proboscis folded under its head. For some reason, it's missing its left antenna. This one is in a defensive pose with its front legs held high in the air. (San Marcos Pass, 15 April 2017)

File.Name: h415c_h415-018.jpg
Notes: 34060669805 This is "The Blusher" (Amanita novinupta aka Amanita rubescens) mushroom fruiting in the woods today under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) trees. You can see the blushing color on the stalk from my holding it. This mushroom has a ring, but it doesn't have any kind of volval cup on the base. It's the end of our musgroom season, but I also find them in the fall at the beginning of our season, see this photo. This confirms what David Arora says (in Mushrooms Demystified) that they usually fruit twice - "a small flush after the first fall rains, and a larger crop in the late winter and spring." They just are highly variable, compare this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 15 April 2017)

File.Name: h415d_h415-012.jpg
Notes:
33903765322 Here are new flowers of the native perennial grass Small-flowered Melic (Melica imperfecta) in the Poaceae plant family. We don't usually notice grass flowers, but they have stamens and feathery pistils just like all plants. This native grass seems to be fairly common in our mountains - holding it's own against the weedy grasses. It's a tough plant not to overexpose. Did you spot the two aphids? (San Marcos Pass, 15 April 2017)

(If you want to learn more about grasses, you can't do better than the "First Book of Grasses: The Structure of Grasses Explained for Beginners" by Agnes Chase. It's a classic from 1922, revised several times over the years. You can actually find a PDF of the original at the Internet Archive.)
Locality: If you want to learn more about grasses

File.Name: h416a_h416-036.jpg
Notes:
33927254192 Timemas come in all different sizes, shapes, and colors - just like people!

These are almost-mating Timema insects (Timema cristinae, Timematidae, Phasmida) on a flowering stem of Goldencup Oak aka Canyon Live Oak (Quercus chrysolepis, Fagaceae). I found quite a few of them on this oak today. I say "almost mating" because there doesn't seem to be any actual physical contact between them. Maybe they are just snuggling? More likely, this is male "mate guarding" in which males ride on the backs of females for periods up to several days to keep other males away - see BugGuide. We have brown Timemas and green ones and green ones with stripes - and it doesn't seem to matter much. The males (on top) are typically smaller than the ladies. You can see that the males have asymmetric claspers while the females have symmetric cerci. Timemas are atypical walkingstick insects which are endemic to California and neighboring states, and I've been finding lots of them lately. I have a bit more info in my comments on this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 16 April 2017)

It was breezy today, and this photo shows it. It's hard to take my kind of photos when everything is blowing around! We have a chance of rain tonight and tomorrow, but it probably won't amount to much - and maybe nothing at all. We have a better chance of rain on Tuesday morning - followed by a few days of wind. *Sigh*
CommonNames: Timema; Goldencup Oak; Canyon Live Oak
ScientificName: Timema cristinae; Timematidae; Phasmida; Quercus chrysolepis; Fagaceae
associatedTaxa: Timema cristinae; Timematidae; Phasmida; Quercus chrysolepis; Fagaceae

File.Name: h416b_h416-005.jpg
Notes: 33953700741 This is another one of those larva of a Sawfly Wasp ("Symphyta", Hymenoptera) that looks like a caterpillar - but isn't. I have a photo of a different kind here. They are eruciform larvae, which means "caterpillar-like" - with two visible differences. First, they have simple eye-spots whereas caterpillars usually have a broken ring of eyespots - see this photo. Second, sawfly larvae always have more prolegs - six or more pairs - while caterpillars have five pairs or fewer. There's a useful page on distinguishing them from the Missouri Botanical Garden. This one is on a new frond of Western Bracken Fern (Pteridium aquilinum var. pubescens, Polypodiaceae). (San Marcos Pass, 16 April 2017)
ScientificName: "Symphyta"; Hymenoptera; eruciform; Pteridium aquilinum; pubescens; Polypodiaceae

File.Name: h416c_h416-008.jpg
Notes: 34083389735 This is a shy flower of native Canyon Clarkia (Clarkia epilobioides) in the Onagraceaeplant family. It's one of a group of flowers known as Farewell-to-Spring, though this one prefers shady canyons rather than full sun like most of the others. They are considered harbingers of the end of spring, but it doesn't feel that way this year. I reckon the real farewell-to-spring comes when the weedy grasses start to release their nasty seeds that get stuck in your socks and shoes, and it will still be a while before that happens. I hope. (San Marcos Pass, 16 April 2017)
ScientificName: Clarkia epilobioides; Onagraceae

File.Name: h416d_h416-011.jpg
Notes: 33953039251 Here are two plants growing together that you probably don't want to confuse - Poison Oak (Toxicodendron diversilobum) on the left and Basket Bush aka Skunkbush or "Squaw Bush" (Rhus aromatica aka Rhus trilobata) on the right. Both are shrubs in the Anacardiaceae plant family, and both have 3-part leaves, but only the one causes skin irritation. Basket Bush does have a strong smell as it's name suggests, but you don't want to rub a leaf to tell if that's what it is! Poison Oak leaves are sometimes larger and shinier, but don't count on that always being true - especially in the fall. One difference that is always good is to look at the middle leaf. On poison oak, the middle leaf has a distinct stalk, but on Basket Bush the middle leaf narrows down to the base without a separate stalk. You can impress your friends by calmly handling this plant with no ill effect - IF you get the ID right! (San Marcos Pass, 16 April 2017)
CommonNames: Poison Oak; Basket Bush; Skunkbush; Squaw Bush
ScientificName: Toxicodendron diversilobum; Rhus aromatica; Rhus trilobata; Anacardiaceae
associatedTaxa: Toxicodendron diversilobum; Rhus aromatica; Rhus trilobata; Anacardiaceae

File.Name: h417a_h417-005.jpg
Notes:
33977297661 I believe this is a Baja California Chorus Frog (Pseudacris hypochondriaca) with that dark stripe through each eye, but I'm no expert. (It was formerly considered part of the Pacific Chorus Frog (Pseudacris regilla) group and separated in 2006.) It's small and cute. Like Timema insects, they come in both green and brown forms, and I believe they can slowly change color. I found this green one today snuggled in between the basal leaves of a succulent near our "water garden" today. This garden is nothing fancy - just an old fiberglass bathtub filled with water and plants, but these tubs are important for the frogs and other critters. Five years of drought has taken its toll. This is the first frog I've seen this year, but I've been hearing them every night for a few weeks. They are chorus frogs, and they really make a racket! There are sound recordings at CaliforniaHerps.com. (San Marcos Pass, 17 April 2017)

We had gloom and breeze and a bit of drizzle today, but no proper rain. We have a better chance of rain tomorrow morning - followed by a few days of wind.
CommonNames: Baja California Chorus Frog; Pacific Chorus Frog
ScientificName: Pseudacris hypochondriaca
associatedTaxa: Pseudacris hypochondriaca

File.Name: h417b_h417-020.jpg
Notes: 33722401070 This is another Snipe Fly in the genus Rhagio, Rhagionidae, Diptera on a leaflet of native Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus vestitus, Fabaceae) - with raindrops from our drizzle today. This one is a female, large with eggs. These handsome flies are pretty common this year, more often than not on this plant, e.g. here. They are related to the worst biting fly in our mountains - a Rocky Mountain Bite Fly in the genus Symphoromyia of the same Rhagionidae family, photo here. I mention this because Julie said she just got bit by one yesterday. Seems early, but I guess they're here. The fly in this photo is not a biter. (San Marcos Pass, 17 April 2017)

I tentatively identified them as Leaf Beetles (genus Chrysomela, Chrysomelidae), probably subgenus Macrolina - and maybe species Chrysomela schaefferi. File.Name: h417d_h414-020.jpg
Notes: 34100204195 This is a fantastic seedhead of native Silver Puffs (Uropappus lindleyi) in the Asteraceae plant family growing in the woodland near Lake Cachuma. The flowers aren't much to look at, but the seeds are wonderful. Yes, this plant is dandelion-like, but it is no dandelion. It has five silvery pappus scales atop each long seed which are not meant to help the seeds fly away - though they may help them glide down, like a parachute. This is my photo for the Macro Mondays group, with the theme of "Seeds". HMM! (San Marcos Pass, 14 April 2017)

File.Name: h418a_h418-029.jpg
Notes:
33318090693 This Crab Spider (Thomisidae) is waiting on a new flower bud of a Prickly Pear (Opuntia, Cactaceae) cactus in our garden. The hairs on the spider suggest that it is probably genus Mecaphesa again, white rather than yellow like the spider in my [Previous] photo. Are they color-shifters? Look how the cactus has succulent leaves on its flower buds. (San Marcos Pass, 18 April 2017)

It's interesting that I found more spiders than insects today. Maybe the insects were all smart enough to stay out of the rain? We did have 1/4 inch of rain this morning, and it stayed chilly and gloomy for most of the rest of the day. Now the wind will probably pick up again.

The hairs on the spider suggest that it is probably genus Mecaphesa again, yellow rather than white like the spider in my [Next] photo. Are they color-shifters? This one is missing its left-front leg. That's an occupational hazard for these spiders that grab their prey and hang on. I found this one in a flowering shrub of Poison Oak (Toxicodendron diversilobum, Anacardiaceae). (San Marcos Pass, 18 April 2017)

Notes: 34088601106 This is a tiny Trashline Orbweaver (genus Cyclosa, Araneidae) spider, with raindrops. They always have that odd bump on their abdomen like a bottle gourd, but I don't think I've ever seen one so white. Note the cracked surface, magnified by the water drops. It looks like fine porcelain. This one didn't have a web, and it was sitting motionless on its leaflet of native Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus vestitus, Fabaceae). Maybe it's a wandering male, but I couldn't see its palps. (San Marcos Pass, 18 April 2017)

File.Name: h418d_h417-010.jpg
Notes: 33971450672 This is another of those tiny Orb-Weaver Spider (Araneus, Araneidae) in the curl of a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). It looks like one of the large bottlecap-size spiders that collect under the eaves of our house in the fall, though this one is not much more than 1/4 inch long. I've been finding more of these spiders than usual this year, but maybe I'm just learning to find them. This spider is either A. bispinosus or A. montereyensis, and the way to tell them apart is to look at the way the abdomen dips between the two humps. (See Alice Abelia's comment to this photo.) I'm still not sure, but I figure "one of those" is good enough for me! (San Marcos Pass, 17 April 2017)

File.Name: h419a_h419-060.jpg
Notes:
34018986591 Here are more almost-mating Timema insects (Timema cristinae, Timematidae, Phasmida) on a leaf of native Snowdrop Bush (Styrax redivivus) in the Styracaceae plant family. I've found them on this shrub before, see this photo. I found quite a few of them on this shrub today, mostly in pairs like this. There was a shrub of Chamise (Adenostoma fasciculatum, Rosaceae) right next door, and I didn't find any on that shrub. I say "almost mating" because there doesn't seem to be any actual physical contact between them. Maybe they are just snuggling? More likely, this is male "mate guarding" in which males ride on the backs of females for periods up to several days to keep other males away - see BugGuide. We have brown Timemas and green ones and green ones with stripes - and it doesn't seem to matter much, compare this photo. I first thought I hadn't seen a brown female, but look here. The males (on top) are typically smaller than the ladies. If you look close, maybe you can see that the males have asymmetric claspers while the females have symmetric cerci. Timemas are atypical walkingstick insects which are endemic to California and neighboring states, and I've been finding lots of them lately. I have a bit more info in my comments on this photo. Happy almost-hump day! (San Marcos Pass, 19 April 2017)

Today started in a cloud, then turn sunny and nice, and then the wind kicked up in the afternoon. There have been gusts to 40 mph so far. Oh well, the next Macro Mondays challenge is to show motion blur in a macro photo. Usually I do everything I can to eliminate motion blur, but this time the wind might help me get a photo!

File.Name: h419c_h419-023.jpg
Notes: 33337846653 Here is the first new flower I've seen this year of native California Chicory (Rafinesquia californica) in the Asteraceae plant family. The plants look kind of weedy when you first notice them, like non-native Sow Thistle (Sonchus). But if you look closer, you see how elegant they are. I believe the flowers form a proper cyme inflorescence where the top flower opens first, followed by the lower flowers. They are a fire-follower that lingers in disturbed places, though this place was cleared but never burned. The flowers and buds are very similar to the Desert Chicory (Rafinesquia neomexicana) - see this old photo from Fotolog. (San Marcos Pass, 19 April 2017)

File.Name: h420a_h420-026.jpg
Notes:
33357685203 This is a gorgeous Gabb's Checkerspot (Chlosyne gabbii) butterfly perched on a flowering stem of Black Sage (Salvia mellifera, Lamiaceae). The butterflies were working the sage flowers. I don't know much about these butterflies, though I see a few every year. My book says they host on composites and only have a single brood. It's interesting that I found this one in almost the same place as my first photo this year. Maybe they don't travel much - like me. (San Marcos Pass, 20 April 2017)

It was windy all last night and all today with 30+ mph gusts. We're under a NWS Wind Advisory until Friday morning. That's a lot of air going somewhere! It was really too windy for photos, but I got a few. I did play with getting photo in the wind to show motion blur for the Macro Mondays assignment this week. I don't have it yet, but at least this wind is good for something!

File.Name: h420b_h420-035.jpg
Notes: 33783774300 I thought at first these flies were mating. Nope, these photos show a couple of mosquito-like Dance Flies in the family Empididae, probably genus Empis, with their prey. They are hanging on leaves of a vetch and a weedy grass, but both of them were in the air with their prey before they settled down - which is why I thought they were mating. It's surprising to find these flies acting as predators after seeing so many working the flowers of Arroyo Willow (Salix lasiolepis, Salicaceae), see this photo. (Are the same kind?) I can't really see the prey, but they are definitely flies - and they might well be March Flies (Bibio, Bibionidae, Diptera). It's interesting that Rockwolf posted a photo of Empid & Bibio yesterday in the UK. Small world! (San Marcos Pass, 20 April 2017)

Notes: 33783642860 This is another one of those larva of a Sawfly Wasp ("Symphyta", Hymenoptera) that looks like a caterpillar - but isn't. I have another photo of maybe the same kind here. They are eruciform larvae, which means "caterpillar-like" - with two visible differences. First, they have simple eye-spots whereas caterpillars usually have a broken ring of eyespots - see this photo. Second, sawfly larvae always have more prolegs - six or more pairs - while caterpillars have five pairs or fewer. There's a useful page on distinguishing them from the Missouri Botanical Garden. This one is on a stem of Deerweed (Acmispon glaber, Fabaceae) - where I expect to find real caterpillars! (San Marcos Pass, 20 April 2017)

33803644790 A couple of days ago in this photo, I mentioned that "I haven't seen a brown female" Timema insect. Oops, I misspoke. Tomorrow will be another warm one, and I'll have shorts on. I'm cynical enough that I've always avoided Earth Day for having too much corporate hype, but maybe I should attend the March for Science tomorrow?

File.Name: h422a_h422-006.jpg
Notes:
34050617522 This is a Western Side-blotched Lizard (Uta stansburiana elegans) on a rock in the sun today. You can see the dark blue "side blotch" behind its foreleg. They are smaller than our usual Fence Lizards, with smoother scales and different colors. These lizards are fast and usually hard to capture with a camera, but this one posed for me. I was actually looking for Coast Horned Lizards aka "Horny Toads" (Phrynosoma blainvillii) in this place today, but no luck. This lizard was some consolation. (San Marcos Pass, 22 April 2017)

I didn't make it to the March for Science today, but Julie went in my place. As a 30+ year science teacher, I probably should have gone, and I do support the cause. The science policy in place is embarrassing and needs a show of opposition. Julie said the march went well, lots of people and no problems. Ever the hermit, I went for a quiet walk in the woods and even pulled some weeds.

First, they have simple eye-spots whereas caterpillars usually have a broken ring of eyespots - see this photo. Second, sawfly larvae always have more prolegs - six or more pairs - while caterpillars have five pairs or fewer. There's a useful page on distinguishing them from the Missouri Botanical Garden. File.Name: h424c_h424-013.jpg
Notes:
34251931315 This is another caterpillar of a Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly, see the adult here. This on is on a leafy flower head of Woolly Paintbrush (Castilleja foliolosa) in the Orobanchaceae plant family, where I haven't seen it before. It's interesting because older books always refer to its host plants as "scrophs" - members of the old Scrophulariaceae plant family - but this family has been now been split up into multiple new families. I usually find the caterpillars on these plants:

* Southern Bush Monkey Flower (Mimulus aurantiacus var. pubescens) in the Phrymaceae
* Climbing Penstemon (Keckiella cordifolia) in the Plantaginaceae
* Woolly Paintbrush (Castilleja foliolosa) in the Orobanchaceae
* California Figwort (Scrophularia californica) in what's left of the Scrophulariaceae

I wonder if the butterflies recognize a similarity in these plants that the botanists have missed? (San Marcos Pass, 24 April 2017)

File.Name: h426b_h426-007.jpg
Notes:
34161062071 Here are red, white, and blue native flowers - for no particular reason, they just look nice together. I got the red ones today and the other two within the last week. From left to right, here are:

Red - Woolly Paintbrush (Castilleja foliolosa, Orobanchaceae) - I found the first flowers on December 18, see this photo, and they're still going strong;
White - California Mustard (Caulanthus lasiophyllus, Brassicaceae) - it's a true native mustard that grows as a fire-follower on burns and clearings in our mountains;
Blue - Spreading Larkspur (Delphinium patens, Ranunculaceae) - in a shady place along the road in our canyon, just starting to get some morning sun as the sun moves higher in the sky.

Did you notice the little Wild Bee (Anthophila, Hymenoptera) flying into the mustard flowers in the center? (San Marcos Pass, 26 April 2017)

Notes: 34251318296 This is the retreat of a Labyrinth Spider (genus Metepeira, Araneidae) . No doubt the spider is in there - see this photo for another spider in the same shrub of Black Sage (Salvia mellifera, Lamiaceae). These orb-weaver spiders build a proper orb web with a separate little house suspended by a tangle of cobwebs off to the side. They decorate their houses with the bodies of their prey, pirate fashion. This retreat also seem to be decorated with bits of heavier webbing like thread. It looks like the thick white silk that some spiders use for the stabilimentum, but I have no idea what it's doing here. Happy Web Wednesday! (San Marcos Pass, 25 April 2017)

File.Name: h428a_h428-044.jpg
Notes:
33520499413 This is a gorgeous Lorquin's Admiral (Basilarchia lorquini) butterfly perched on a leaf of native Bitter Gooseberry (Ribes amarum, Grossulariaceae) today. It's really guarding a tree of native Arroyo Willow (Salix lasiolepis, Salicaceae) by the creek a few feet away. These butterflies look a lot like the more-common California Sister (Adelpha bredowii ssp. californica) butterfly, but the orange wing-tips go right to the edge of its forewings without a dark border, and the underwings are different - compare this photo. These butterflies don't seem to visit flowers. They just sit and wait - to chase away any other butterfly that comes close to their willow tree. This one let me get closer than usual. I reckon they don't like the wind any more than I do. (San Marcos Pass, 28 April 2017)

Now there's a Santa Ana wind event in the forecast down south, but we don't get those winds in our mountains. Our forecast is only for moderate winds to 15 mph and warmer. We'll see.

Notes: 33965626810 This tattered Common Buckeye (Junonia coenia) butterfly was on the ground interested in this new plant of native Bird's Beak (Cordylanthus rigidus) in the Orobanchaceae (formerly Scrophulariaceae) plant family. The butterfly is probably ovipositing - at least it would be if the darned photographer would leave so it can get on with its business. They are said to use plants in the snapdragon family (Scrophulariaceae) and the plantain family (Plantaginaceae), and the plant in this photo was formerly considered a "scroph". That's interesting because Penstemon plants that used to be considered scrophs are now considered to be in the plaintain family! My head is spinning, I miss the old Scrophulariaceae plant family. See my comment on this photo for more discussion. (San Marcos Pass, 29 April 2017)

File.Name: h430a_h430-026.jpg
Notes:
33562360193 This biting fly is about draw blood from my forearm! I believe it is a Rocky Mountain Bite Fly in the genus Symphoromyia of the Snipe Fly (Rhagionidae) family. It's related to our common Snipe Flies in this photo, but this one has annoying biting habits. I believe this one is a female about to get her blood meal. We actually have (at least) two biting flies in this genus living in our mountains. The other one is orange in color, see this photo. (It may be Symphoromyia sackeni - see this photo at BugGuide.) That one is a real bloodsucker that flies in and out faster than you can swat, but the one in this photo is different. It's slower for one thing. I noticed it on my arm and fumbled around with my tripod and finding a place to get the photo ... and it was still on my arm! It has interesting antennae and impressive mouthparts that I don't understand. If you look close, you can see a little needle-like poker below the pad at the bottom of its mouthpart. I guess that's its sticker. It hurts! (San Marcos Pass, 30 April 2017)

Today was a lovely day - sunny and warm, but still a bit breezy. Maybe one of these days will be just right, but then I'd just get spoiled!

Notes: 34372236005 Here is another female Timema insect (Timema cristinae, Timematidae, Phasmida) on a leaf of native Sacapellote (Acourtia microcephala) in the Asteraceae plant family growing in the mountains. The focus isn't right in this photo, but it's interesting because it shows a new plant and a new color combo. The plant isn't so remarkable because there was a native Chamise (Adenostoma fasciculatum, Rosaceae) shrub nearby and it might have crossed over. We have brown Timemas and green ones and green ones with stripes, and I seem to find them in all combinations - see this photo, but I've never seen one like the current photo. I can tell that it's a lady by her size and the symmetric cerci at the end of her abdomen. The colors of these adorable little insects are highly variable! (San Marcos Pass, 30 April 2017)

File.Name: h430c_h430-037.jpg
Notes: 33987180370 This photo shows the first new flowers of native California Coffeeberry (Rhamnus californica or Frangula californica) in the Rhamnaceae plant family - with a ladybug and a gall. The flowers are similar to the Holly-Leaved Redberry (Rhamnus ilicifolia) in this photo except that they have five petals rather than four. The ladybug is a Convergent Lady Beetle (Hippodamia convergens, Coccinellidae, Coleoptera) with its head stuffed into an open flower. Do these beetles take nectar or is it (more likely) finding little insects like thrips inside the flower? Finally there's the red-colored gall. I'm sure this is a galled flower and not a berry - see "a href="https://www.flickr.com/photos/treebeard/16840903567/">this photo. Like the galls in my [Previous] photo of Holly-Leaved Redberry, I'm still not sure what critter makes these galls, as they are not mentioned in my book by Ron Russo, "Field Guide to Plant Galls" (UC Press, 2006). I suspect they are related insects - if not the same species. (San Marcos Pass, 30 April 2017)

File.Name: h501a_h501-026.jpg
Notes:
34353430836 This is a Convergent Lady Beetle (Hippodamia convergens, Coccinellidae, Coleoptera) on a spike of non-native Foxtail Barley (Hordeum jubatum, Poaceae). Yep, this is the same grass that destroys your socks and cripples wildlife. Nasty stuff. Anyway, we seem to have a convergence of these ladybugs going on! Thousands of them are in the air flying back and forth, and I hope they will stay a while. I haven't see a proper "ladybug rave" (my term) in our canyon since the drought started in 2012. (I did find a small gathering somewhere else in 2012, see this photo.) If you look at that other photo, you will notice that they can have any number of spots, or no spots at all, but they always have those two white lines on the upper-side of their thorax. (San Marcos Pass, 1 May 2017)

Today was a lovely day - sunny and warm, with not much breeze. It's also May Day - a day to celebrate spring, workers, and immigrants. It's also the Gaelic holiday of Beltane - the "cross-quarter day" midway between the vernal equinox and the summer solstice. And if the solstice is also midsummer day - as in William Shakespeare's A Midsummer Night's Dream - then May Day is also the proper first day of summer. Whatever else it is, have a good one!

File.Name: h501b_h501-002.jpg
Notes: 33584892913 Here are new spikelets of native California Brome (Bromus carinatus) grass in the Poaceae plant family growing in a wild place at home. That's a flowering plant of Hummingbird Sage (Salvia spathacea, Lamiaceae) in the background. This native grass is a perennial with flattened spikelets and short awns, which distinguishes it from all the weedy annual bromes like Ripgut Grass (Bromus diandrus) and Red Brome (Bromus rubens) and the rest of their kin that are trying to take over the woodland and grassland. You have to know something about grasses to recognize this one, but it's surprising common in the woodland when you look for it. It seems to be strictly a woodland grass, growing under the oaks. We've been encouraging it for a few years in this place, and it's doing well. (San Marcos Pass, 1 May 2017)

File.Name: h501d_h428-032.jpg
Notes:
34386191035 This is a mass of Braconid Wasp (Braconidae, Hymenoptera) pupal cases on a still-living caterpillar of a Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly. I found this under a new leaf of a Southern Bush Monkey Flower (Mimulus aurantiacus var. pubescens, Phrymaceae) shrub. The tiny wasps deposit their eggs through the skin of the caterpillar. The eggs hatch and the larvae develop inside the caterpillar - like the movie Alien. Finally the larvae leave the caterpillar and spin their silk cocoons. The caterpillar is still alive - I could see it moving - but it didn't last long and dropped away by the next day, see this photo. This must be why the caterpillars are so spiky - to keep the wasps away. It might work as I don't find many of these caterpillars that have been "cocooned" like this. (San Marcos Pass, 28 April 2017)

This is my photo for the Macro Mondays group, with the theme of "Crime" - and indeed a crime has been committed here, at least from the point of view of the caterpillar. (I'm sure the wasps see it differently!) The perpetrator is not to be seen, but the pupal cases are indisputable evidence of its work. HMM!
Locality: I'm sure the wasps see it differently!)  The perpetrator is not to be seen

File.Name: h502b_h502-023.jpg
Notes: 33607511203 On the left is a mushroom-like spring gall of the Live Oak Apple Gall Wasp (Callirhytis quercuspomiformis, Cynipidae) on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). This is what I found at the start of my walk today. On my way back, the same gall had the visitor on the right. I believe this wasp is a Chalcid Wasp (Chalcidoidea), probably in the family Torymidae which favor galls. It's feeling for the larva inside the oak gall to get it with its long ovipositor. (It's not a stinger!) The gall contains a larva that feeds as a parasite on the oak tree, so I reckon this wasp is a hyperparasite - a parasite whose host is a parasite. Actually it should be a hyperparasitoid since it will kill the parasite it infects. I've found a number of these wasps, all on galls - see my photos. I split this photo to get a better view of the gall. (San Marcos Pass, 2 May 2017)

34396686936 I believe this is a Gold-hunter's Hairstreak (Satyrium auretorum) butterfly dipping into a new flower of native California Coffeeberry (Frangula californica aka Rhamnus californica) in the Rhamnaceae plant family. This is the first one I've seen this year. It's the plainest of our "plain brown hairstreaks" - and you mostly recognize it process of elimination. It doesn't have obvious white postmedian lines like the Hedgerow Hairstreak or "white frosting" like the Mountain Mahogany Hairstreak. This is the first one I've seen this year - at least the first one I recognized. I love that name - "gold-hunter's hairstreak". I wonder where it came from? (San Marcos Pass, 3 May 2017)

There was some breeze today, but it was another sunny and warm day. I'm not complaining!

Notes:
33627469043 This is another shot of the mass of Braconid Wasp (Braconidae, Hymenoptera) pupal cases that I found on a still-living caterpillar of a Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly, see this photo. I found this under a new leaf of a Southern Bush Monkey Flower (Mimulus aurantiacus var. pubescens, Phrymaceae) shrub. I got that other photo last Friday - and saved it for the Macro Mondays group. I got this photo the next day, on Saturday. The caterpillar has fallen away, I assume it's dead though I couldn't find it. The tiny wasps deposit their eggs through the skin of the caterpillar. The eggs hatch and the larvae develop inside the caterpillar - like the movie Alien. Finally the larvae leave the caterpillar and spin their cocoons. And in this case, the caterpillar then left the larvae. That's nature! (San Marcos Pass, 29 April 2017)

I thought to post this photo to the Web Wednesdays group - but does it qualify? I'm guessing that those pupal cases are silk though I'm not sure if they count as webbing. I suppose they could also be chitin or keratin or even cotton - they look like little cotton balls! Well, I'll post it anyway and the admins can always delete it without hurting my feelings. Happy Webnesday (?)

File.Name: h504a_h504-042.jpg
Notes:
34327532671 Here is a Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly ovipositing - laying eggs - on a leaf of Climbing Penstemon (Keckiella cordifolia, Plantaginaceae). You can see her yellowish eggs by the tip of her abdomen. It's interesting that this butterfly is using this plant for her eggs while almost all the caterpillars I found earlier this spring were on Southern Bush Monkey Flower (Mimulus aurantiacus var. pubescens, Phrymaceae). As I understand it, the caterpillars emerge in early summer and feed on their host plant. This is when the caterpillars spin their creepy webs, see this photo. Then they form tough skins and drop to the forest floor to over-winter. They emerge in the following spring to finish feeding and pupate into adults. It's a curious cycle, but it works for them. It's interesting that they are using different plants in the summer and following spring. (San Marcos Pass, 4 May 2017)

There was some breeze again today, but it was another sunny and warm day. That's due to change. A small storm system is forecast to bring cooler weather and a good chance of rain on Sunday.

File.Name: h505a_h505-011.jpg
Notes:
33667607593 I believe this pretty butterfly is a female Acmon Blue (Plebejus acmon) - though the Lupine Blue (P. lupini) is very similar. It's perched for just a moment on (or by) a plant of California Fuchsia (Epilobium canum, Onagraceae). Like ducks, the females are less colorful than the males - but still quite handsome. This is only the second one I've seen this year, after the first one yesterday. I guess it's their time. (San Marcos Pass, 5 May 2017)

I wasn't really expecting to find any butterflies today as it was cloudy and breezy and much cooler than it's been - 60s rather than 80s. This one was a nice surprise. The change in weather is the work of a small storm system that will hopefully bring some rain this weekend - just a chance tomorrow, but "rain likely" on Sunday.

Notes: 34317306352 This stunning Orb-Weaver (Araneidae) spider had a web nearby, but it retreated to this perch when I got too close. My first thought was that it is a Labyrinth Spider (genus Metepeira), compare this photo, but it's larger and has no retreat at all. After hitting my books and BugGuide, I believe it is the species Aculepeira packardi. The color patterns on its abdomen are fantastic, like the cloud bands of Jupiter. I can almost see them in motion. (San Marcos Pass, 5 May 2017)

File.Name: h505c_h505-020.jpg
Notes: 34346601011 This is the Butterfly Mariposa Lily (Calochortus venustus) in the Liliaceae plant family growing on a hillside in the mountains. The insides of the flower get the most attention (photo here), but the shape and color of the outside are outstanding. They're not always so colorful. They've been flowering for at least a week. (San Marcos Pass, 5 May 2017)

File.Name: h505d_h430-022.jpg
Notes: 33667145093 This biting fly is drawing blood from my forearm! I believe it is a Rocky Mountain Bite Fly in the genus Symphoromyia of the Snipe Fly (Rhagionidae) family. It's related to our common Snipe Flies in this photo, but this one has annoying biting habits. I believe this one is a female about to get her blood meal. We actually have (at least) two biting flies in this genus living in our mountains. The other one is orange in color, see this photo. (It may be Symphoromyia sackeni - see this photo at BugGuide.) That one is a real bloodsucker that flies in and out faster than you can swat, but the one in this photo is different. It's slower for one thing. I noticed it on my arm and fumbled around with my tripod and finding a place to get the photo ... and it was still on my arm! It has impressive mouthparts that I don't understand, but they are visible in this photo taken at the same time. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 30 April 2017)

File.Name: h506a_h506-009.jpg
Notes:
34367474211 This is looking up at a caterpillar of the Western Tussock Moth (Orgyia vetusta, Lymantriidae, Lepidoptera) on a mostly eaten stem of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) - with raindrops. It was really a crummy day for getting photos, but I managed to get this caterpillar, a spider, mushrooms, and a first flower - nice variety. (San Marcos Pass, 6 May 2017)

We did get some rain last night, but only about 1/2 of 1/10 inch - not much. We were in a cloud all morning with some breeze, then it cleared and the winds started increasing, to 40+ mph. It's been chilly too, mostly in the 40s today. It feels more like a storm moving out than a storm coming in, but there's still a 80% chance of rain tomorrow.

Notes: 34112453040 I believe this is a small Labyrinth Spider (genus Metepeira, Araneidae) out of its house on the left. These spiders build a proper orb web that's sort of visible here, with a separate little house suspended by a tangle of cobwebs off to the side. They decorate their houses with the bodies of their prey, pirate fashion. This one had quite a large house - several inches high, big for a spider that's only about 1/4 inch across. (San Marcos Pass, 6 May 2017)

File.Name: h506c_h506-006.jpg
Notes: 34111951710 I found masses of these mushrooms growing on year-old wood chips in the canyon today after a bit of rain last night. I've seen them before. They appear to be brown-spored and hygrophanous, but I didn't collect them. Maybe they are Tubaria furfuracea which my mushroom book describes as the "quintessential LBM" or "Little Brown Mushroom". These mushrooms make up in number what they lack in interest. (San Marcos Pass, 6 May 2017)

File.Name: h506d_h506-022.jpg
Notes: 34366655231 These are the first new flowers of native Woolly Blue Curls (Trichostema lanatum) in the Lamiaceae plant family that I've found in the canyon this year, though I have seen a few higher in the mountains. They look like one of those "Mexican Sages" in the garden, but these are native Californians. I've always wondered what insect the long pistils and stamens were adapted for, and maybe I found it in this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 6 May 2017)

File.Name: h507a_h507-028.jpg
Notes:
34521986295 This is a golden Syrphid Flower Fly (Syrphidae, Diptera) perched on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) after the rain this morning. Maybe it's Syrphus opinator? This fly got waylaid by chilly weather today and ended up on this leaf, too cold to fly away. See this photo for maybe the same fly in the same circumstances. I believe this is a fly that lives up to the other common name of Hover Fly. I usually find them hanging "motionless" in the air - but this one really was (almost) motionless. I even nudged it into position without it flying away. (San Marcos Pass, 7 May 2017)

We got about 1/3 inch of rain last night - and even a bit of hail this afternoon. It was overcast with moments of breeze - but also moments of calm. It wasn't the best day for new photos, but I got a few nice ones.

Notes: 34135944530 Here is another Snakefly (family Raphidiidae, order Raphidioptera) insect on a leaf of Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus, Rosaceae) - with raindrops. This one is a female with that stout ovipositor. I believe this one is genus Agulla with a "kite-like" head and ocelli present. They have subtle but beautiful colors. (San Marcos Pass, 7 May 2017)

File.Name: h507c_h507-004.jpg
Notes:
33678667874 I believe this caterpillar-like creature is really the larva of a Cimbicid Sawfly (Cimbicidae, Hymenoptera) in the genus Abia - maybe Abia americana. See this photo for the bee-like adult. This one is munching a leaf of native California Honeysuckle (Lonicera hispidula) in the Caprifoliaceae plant family. I seem to find the adults in the early spring, followed by the larvae like this ... and then nothing until the next spring. Do the larvae or pupae just wait out the year? (San Marcos Pass, 7 May 2017)

This is another one of those larva of a Sawfly Wasp ("Symphyta", Hymenoptera) that looks like a caterpillar - but isn't. We seem to have many different kinds. These are eruciform larvae, which means "caterpillar-like" - with two visible differences. First, they have simple eye-spots whereas caterpillars usually have a broken ring of eyespots - see this photo. Second, sawfly larvae like this always have more prolegs - six or more pairs - while caterpillars have five pairs or fewer. There's a useful page on distinguishing them from the Missouri Botanical Garden.
CommonNames: Cimbicid Sawfly; California Honeysuckle; Sawfly Wasp

File.Name: h507d_h507-010.jpg
Notes: 34479787906 These are the first flowers of native California Tea (Rupertia physodes) in the Fabaceae plant family that I've found this year. I learned this plant many years ago as Psoralea physodes. The flowers remind me of a locoweed (Astragalus). This plant isn't common in our mountains, but I find it here and there, mostly in our canyon that I know very well. The plant has clover-like "leaves of three" like Poison Oak but smoother and pointier. I've never tried making tea from the leaves like the common name suggests, but I should. It's one of those natives that could disappear from the woods without leaving a ripple, but here it is! (San Marcos Pass, 7 May 2017)
ScientificName: Rupertia physodes; Fabaceae; Psoralea physodes; Astragalus

File.Name: h508a_h508-034.jpg
Notes:
34544145265 Here is a lovely Brown Elfin (Callophrys augustinus) butterfly on a flower of native Wedge-leaved Horkelia (Horkelia cuneata) in the Rosaceae plant family. At first the butterfly looks plain brown, but when you look closer you see that it really has a rich palette of many colors. Up close it looks like a pointillist painting - many color swatches, including violet and gold, combine to make the eye-catching brown. It's a Hairstreak (family Theclinae) butterfly, but you can hardly tell by looking at it with that stubby little tail. The plant is interesting too. The flowers have flat stamen filaments that remind me of those wide matches that some restaurants used to give out. The plant seems to release a perfume when it rains, though it's hard to pinpoint the smell exactly. Anyway, several different kinds of butterflies like it. (San Marcos Pass, 8 May 2017)

I was expecting our usual winds to kick up after our small rain yesterday, but it wasn't too bad this morning. The wind did kick up in the afternoon, and now we're under a NWS Wind Advisory again tonight. It sounds like the wind might be all done by tomorrow.
ScientificName: Callophrys augustinus; Horkelia cuneata; Rosaceae; pointillist; Theclinae

File.Name: h508c_h508-028.jpg
Notes:
34382596272 Here are two different insects that have nothing in common, except - they are both goofy looking; They are both small, about 1/4 long; they are both on Greenbark Ceanothus (Ceanothus spinosus, Rhamnaceae); I got both photos today; and neither is the best photo. They are both interesting critters! (San Marcos Pass, 8 May 2017)

On the left is a remarkable Chaparral Monkey Grasshopper (Morsea californica) in the Eumastacidae family of the Orthoptera. They are highly variable in color - sometimes orange like this one and sometimes tan or even colored like granite rock, see this photo. I actually found several of them today on this ceanothus shrub.

On the right is a goofy little Planthopper (Fulgoroidea, Auchenorrhyncha, Hemiptera) that is notable for its skinny wings. I believe this one is genus Danepteryx which are just like that. Those narrow wings can't be much good for flying, but maybe they help it steer when it hops. What an odd face it has!
CommonNames: Greenbark Ceanothus; Chaparral Monkey Grasshopper; Planthopper

File.Name: h509a_h509-006.jpg
Notes:
34403070232 This is another mighty California Bee Assassin (Apiomerus californicus) hanging on to a couple of weedy grass stems. It's a large Assassin Bug in the family Reduviidae of the insect order Heteroptera, Hemiptera. You can some of its spike-like proboscis folded under its head. I've found them in the weedy grasses before, see this photo. This is the fourth one I've seen so far this year - more than usual. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 9 May 2017)

We had our first proper "fog winds" this morning. That's my name for the turbulent winds that form at the top of the marine layer fog - when the moist cool air below meets the warmer and drier air above, a classic temperature inversion. This morning started clear, but then a thick layer of "June gloom" fog/stratus spilled over from the ocean side bringing gloomy fog and wind. I went higher in the mountains to get above it. I did find some sun, but not until about 3500 feet. It never did clear out at home.

Interesting news tonight...
Locality: meteorology)">temperature inversion. This morning started clear
ScientificName: Apiomerus californicus; Reduviidae ; Heteroptera; Hemiptera
associatedTaxa: Apiomerus californicus; Reduviidae ; Heteroptera; Hemiptera

Notes: 34523655556 Here are two shots of what I'm pretty sure is a Pacific Woodrush (Luzula comosa) in the Juncaceae plant family. Rushes usually grow in wet places, but this one prefers to be dry. The plants have broad grass-like leaves and interesting flower heads that are different from grasses. This is one of those plants that might be common, but I rarely notice it. (San Marcos Pass, 9 May 2017)

File.Name: h509c_h509-002.jpg
Notes: 34564544445 These are new dreadlock-like staminate catkins and pistillate flowers of a native Tanbark Oak aka Tanoak (Lithocarpus densiflorus) tree in the Fagaceae plant family. The erect staminate catkins distinguishes it from true oaks in the genus Quercus, and you can see the burr-like pistillate flowers that will become acorns below the catkins. I don't think the catkins are releasing pollen yet, but they are getting close. I think of these as June flowers, at least in the shady woods near home. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 9 May 2017)

File.Name: h509d_h509-023.jpg
Notes: 33753966703 This is a new flower of native Thimbleberry (Rubus parviflorus) in the Rosaceae plant family growing in a place where the sun never shines. They are just starting to flower. These plants have maple-like palmate leaves which are quite different from our common Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus). Thimbleberries are common and almost a roadside weed in the Sierras and Oregon, but they are quite uncommon here in the Santa Barbara mountains. I reckon it's a relict plant in our mountains from wetter times in the past that's managed to hold on so far against climate change. It did make it through our five-year drought. I've never found the berries here. (San Marcos Pass, 9 May 2017)
ScientificName: Rubus parviflorus; Rosaceae; Rubus ursinus

33774030003 This is the web and retreat of a different Labyrinth Spider (genus Metepeira, Araneidae) in a shrub of native Chamise (Adenostoma fasciculatum, Rosaceae) that's getting ready to flower - with raindrops. These spiders typically build a proper orb web with a separate little house suspended by a tangle of cobwebs off to the side, though this one seems to have skipped the orb web. Happy Web Wednesday! (San Marcos Pass, 10 May 2017)

The raindrops are really drizzle drops. We've been under a deep marine layer that's been quite drippy - 6/100 of an inch so far in the rain gauge. The NWS forecast discussion mentions that the marine layer depth was about 6,000 feet this morning. I don't mind the gloom, but there was also annoying steady breeze today that made getting new photos difficult. We never saw the sun today, but we might tomorrow.
