These orb-weaver spiders build a proper orb web with a separate little house suspended by a tangle of cobwebs off to the side. I have a closer shot of the same retreat here, but what I like about this photo is that it shows the attached orb web as well. Notes: 34453217071 This spider has its prey. If you look closer, you can see that its prey is another spider - possibly its mate? I believe from its web that the spider is a Sheet Web Weaver spider in the family Linyphiidae. I have more shots of the same kind of spider in this photo and this photo. Does this mean that the story about spiders killing and eating their mates is true? I might have another example in this photo. There is an entry on "Spider cannibalism" at Wikipedia that says that "the theory of the 'sacrificial male' has become greater than the truth. Mating of spiders is not always followed by cannibalism ." But it apparently does happen. (San Marcos Pass, 4 May 2017)

File.Name: h510d_h508-036.jpg
Notes: 33773499713 I believe this is the Caterpillar Phacelia (Phacelia cicutaria var. hispida) in the Boraginaceae plant family growing in the canyon at home. They have pale, washed-out flowers that won't win any awards, but they make it up with those fantastic hairy flower heads which look a bit like "woolly bear" caterpillars - all the more so as they expand with new flowers. The long "hispid" hairs are irritating to the skin. Phacelias are a tricky plant group in our area because we have so many different kinds, but these are distinctive. (San Marcos Pass, 8 May 2017)

File.Name: h511a_h511-018.jpg
Notes:
34603985565 This is a Trirhabda beetle on a furry leaf of Trask's Yerba Santa (Eriodictyon traskiae) in the Boraginaceae plant family. This is the adult that comes from the iridescent beetle grubs in this photo. They are in the group of Skeletonizing Leaf Beetles (Galerucinae, Chrysomelidae, Coleoptera), which describes what the larvae do to the foliage. They definitely inflict some damage! I believe this one is Trirhabda eriodictyonis. The species name shows it's affinity for this shrub. (San Marcos Pass, 11 May 2017)

The fog rolled over the mountains again this morning, but it started to break up in the afternoon - with some "fog wind" in the process. Now we're looking at a windy week ahead. There are NWS rumors of 60 mph winds blowing over the mountains tomorrow night. *Sigh* I'll do what I can for new photos, but today might have been the best photo day for a while - despite the morning fog.

File.Name: h511b_h511-039.jpg
Notes:
34473261061 Here are more almost-mating Timema insects (Timema cristinae, Timematidae, Phasmida) on a flowering branch of native California Coffeeberry (Frangula californica aka Rhamnus californica) in the Rhamnaceae plant family. I say "almost mating" because there's no actual physical contact between them. Probably this is male "mate guarding" in which smaller males ride on the backs of larger females for periods up to several days to keep other males away - see BugGuide. This photo shows a light green male riding a brown female. Hah - look close, the green male has brown legs! We have brown Timemas and green ones and green ones with stripes - and a few oddballs like this one. They are highly variable. Timemas are atypical walkingstick insects which are endemic to California and neighboring states, and I've been finding lots of them lately. I have a bit more info in my comments on this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 11 May 2017)

This is the first time I've ever seen Timemas on a Coffeeberry shrub. They are having a good year! Here are the shrubs I've found them on so far this year:

Chamise (Adenostoma fasciculatum, Rosaceae)
Greenbark Ceanothus (Ceanothus spinosus, Rhamnaceae)
Hairy Ceanothus (Ceanothus oliganthus, Rhamnaceae)
Bigpod Ceanothus (Ceanothus megacarpus, Rhamnaceae)
California Coffeeberry (Frangula californica, Rhamnaceae)
California Peony (Paeonia californica, Paeoniaceae)
Canyon Live Oak (Quercus chrysolepis, Fagaceae)
Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia, Fagaceae)
Snowdrop Bush (Styrax redivivus, Styracaceae)
Chaparral Currant (Ribes malvaceum, Grossulariaceae)
Sacapellote (Acourtia microcephalam, Asteraceae)

File.Name: h511c_h511-025.jpg
Notes:
34441567572 A tale of two tails...

This is looking close at the tails of two almost-mating Timema insects (Timema cristinae, Timematidae, Phasmida) insects on a stem of Bigpod Ceanothus (Ceanothus megacarpus, Rhamnaceae). I say "almost mating" because there's no actual physical contact between them. Probably this is male "mate guarding" in which males ride on the backs of females for periods up to several days to keep other males away - see BugGuide. In my photo, that's the male in the foreground with the female behind. Males are typically smaller than the ladies and have asymmetric claspers. The much larger females have symmetric cerci, those appendages at the end oh her tail. Timemas are atypical walkingstick insects which are endemic to California and neighboring states, and I've been finding lots of them lately. I have a bit more info in my comments on this photo. HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 11 May 2017)

This is the first time I've ever seen Timemas on this Ceanothus. See my [Next] photo for another first.

If I'm right, they usually lose their fur, see this photo. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 11 May 2017)

File.Name: h513a_h513-019.jpg
Notes:
34600447486 Here are two ants sharing *something* on a furry leaf of Trask's Yerba Santa (Eriodictyon traskiae) in the Boraginaceae plant family. They stayed like this for a good minute after I noticed them, hardly moving. To be honest, I rotated this image about 150 degrees counter-clockwise to better fit the screen. They were actually nearly upside-down. I'm pretty sure the ants are the California Velvety Tree Ant (Liometopum occidentale, Dolichoderinae, Formicidae, Hymenoptera) with one nodal spine and a strong unpleasant smell. These are by far our most common ants. Here's another photo. (San Marcos Pass, 13 May 2017)

I was expecting big winds today, but it wasn't too bad - especially in the morning. The winds did pick up in the afternoon again, and we're back under a NWS Wind Advisory with 30+ mph gusts so far. One good thing about the wind is that it blew the fog away. Tomorrow sounds similar to today, and I did get some photos today.

File.Name: h513b_h513-013.jpg
Notes: 34479298062 This is a neat pair of little Treehoppers (family Membracidae) in the order Auchenorrhyncha, Hemiptera on a furry leaf of Trask's Yerba Santa (Eriodictyon traskiae) in the Boraginaceae plant family. Maybe they are Buffalo Treehoppers in the genus Ceresa. The thorax of these insects is expanded to cover the abdomen like a shield. They have remarkable eyes, like all hoppers that have to see where they're hopping. I tried to shift my camera to get them both in focus, but they got away from me. I figure one is better than none! (San Marcos Pass, 13 May 2017)

File.Name: h513c_h513-031.jpg
Notes: 33831350663 This is a small gathering of Convergent Lady Beetles (Hippodamia convergens, Coccinellidae, Coleoptera) on a leaf of native California Coffeeberry (Frangula californica aka Rhamnus californica) in the Rhamnaceae plant family. The shrub is flowering, and there were lots of these ladybugs working the flowers. They can have any number of spots, or no spots at all, but they always have those two white diagonal lines that converge behind the head. This is a small gathering, but sometimes they converge by the thousands. I haven't see a proper "ladybug rave" (my term) in the mountains since the drought started in 2012. (San Marcos Pass, 13 May 2017)

Notes: 34478890802 I guess this is a California Orange-winged Grasshopper (Arphia ramona), the first I've seen this year. It's a Band-winged Grasshopper in the subfamily Oedipodinae of the Acrididae, Orthoptera. This one had brilliant orange wings when it took flight a moment later. If I'm right, it also has blue tibia though I couldn't get a glimpse. It's interesting to think that the tiny Chaparral Monkey Grasshopper nymph in my [Previous] photo would probably fit on this one's eye! (San Marcos Pass, 12 May 2017)

File.Name: h514a_h514-006.jpg
Notes:
34664963555 This is a gorgeous California Tortoiseshell (Nymphalis californica) butterfly perched on a new plant of native Coyote Mint aka White-leaf Monardella (Monardella hypoleuca, Lamiaceae). It must be newly emerged to be so perfect. I usually find a few tattered individuals in mid-winter, like this one, but I've never found one looking so lovely. I guess I shouldn't be surprised since I found caterpillars two months ago in a place not too far away, see this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 14 May 2017)

We've been in and out of the clouds today. We started with morning fog which burned off to sunshine by the time I went out with my camera. Then it got cloudy again with real clouds. I didn't leave the canyon today, but from my perspective the clouds seemed to be forming in a line along the east-west Santa Ynez Mountains. Maybe that's a sign of convection from the ocean/south side? I guess this is the work of a dry front moving in, and the forecast does call for some drizzle later tonight and slow clearing tomorrow.

Notes: 34664722375 I believe this is the caterpillar of a Ceanothus Nola Moth (Nola minna) on a leaf of Greenbark Ceanothus (Ceanothus spinosus, Rhamnaceae). I have to chuckle because none of the caterpillar photos at BugGuide (just four) are actually on Ceanothus. The caterpillars are small but quite lovely with those long hairs. I only recognized this one because Alice Abelia recently posted this photo of a similar beast. Thanks again Alice! (San Marcos Pass, 14 May 2017)

Notes: 34664407625 I found this remarkable "true bug" perched deep in the throat of a new flower of Southern Bush Monkey Flower (Mimulus aurantiacus var. pubescens, Phrymaceae). I'm pretty sure this is Closterocoris amoenus (Miridae, Heteroptera, Hemiptera) - a species that deserves a common name! I mostly find the bugs on White Sage (Salvia apiana, Lamiaceae). They come from remarkable ant-mimic nymphs, see this photo. All stages of this insect seem to be on the sage when it flowers - in a couple of weeks. (San Marcos Pass, 14 May 2017)

This is the thistle with violet flowers, on the right in this photo. The thistles have started to flower up on the steep hillside where I can't get to them. These ladybugs lack the converging white lines of the Convergent Lady Beetles in this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 14 May 2017)

File.Name: h515a_h515-005.jpg
Notes:
34645872856 This is a wonderful new flower of the Elegant Clarkia (Clarkia unguiculata) in the Onagraceae plant family. The petals look like they've been cut with scissors, but that's just how they are. It's a Farewell-to-Spring - a sign that our dry summer is approaching, too fast! I found this one in the woods near Lake Cachuma where they've been flowering for a while. Note how the stamens come in two series - an inner series of four that are are already shriveled up and close to the pistil, and an outer series of four that are bright red and full of pollen. (Lake Cachuma, Santa Ynez Valley, 15 May 2017)

It was really a pretty crummy day for new photos, but I wanted to get at least one. I had an errand in the Santa Ynez Valley, so I stopped along the way for a short walk. It was overcast and breezy - with 25 mph gusts all day. It's also been uncommonly chilly for May, barely to 60∞ today. I'm not complaining, but the weeds are loving the extended growing season.

File.Name: h515b_h514-031.jpg
Notes: 34555416091 I found this large bottlecap-size Orb-Weaver (Araneidae) spider in a low shrub of Nuttall's Snowberry (Symphoricarpos mollis, Caprifoliaceae ). I actually found it a week ago, and I've been trying every day to get a good photo - this one isn't perfect, but it will do. The spider seems intent on staying here, even with me futzing around with my tripod every day. Maybe this spider is Araneus andrewsi or Neoscona crucifera? I believe these are one the spiders that make large webs under the eaves of our upstairs porch in fall - just in time for Arachtober! (San Marcos Pass, 14 May 2017)

Notes: 33843220404 Here are two shots showing top and bottom wings of the same gorgeous California Tortoiseshell (Nymphalis californica) butterfly that I show two photos back, here. It's perched on a new plant of native Coyote Mint aka White-leaf Monardella (Monardella hypoleuca, Lamiaceae). It's stunning when they quickly flash their wings open. The weather turned cloudy and chilly yesterday morning, and I reckon this butterfly got grounded by the change. I left it with wings closed and went on with my walk, and the butterfly was still in the same position when I came back an hour later. (San Marcos Pass, 14 May 2017)

File.Name: h515d_h513-027.jpg
Notes:
34548443191 Into the woods and into a flower...

This is a little Convergent Lady Beetle (Hippodamia convergens, Coccinellidae, Coleoptera) going into a new flower of native California Coffeeberry (Frangula californica aka Rhamnus californica) in the Rhamnaceae plant family. I originally found the beetle head down in the flower and watched it for a while. After a few minutes. I finally poked it to make sure a spider didn't have it! Nope, the beetle shrugged off the interruption, and here it is going back into the flower. See this photo for more info about these beetles. This is my photo for the Macro Mondays group, with the theme of "Into the woods". Hah, the theme was too easy this week - almost all my photos qualify, but HMM anyway! (San Marcos Pass, 13 May 2017)

File.Name: h516a_h516-032.jpg
Notes:
33896842423 I'm pretty sure this is a plain Gold-hunter's Hairstreak (Satyrium auretorum) butterfly working a new flower of native Wedge-leaved Horkelia (Horkelia cuneata, Rosaceae). It's the plainest of our "plain brown hairstreaks" - and you mostly recognize it process of elimination. It doesn't have obvious white postmedian lines like the Hedgerow Hairstreak or "white frosting" like the Mountain Mahogany Hairstreak. I saw several of these today working these flowers, so they seem to be having a good year. I love that name - "Gold-hunter's Hairstreak". I wonder where it came from? (San Marcos Pass, 16 May 2017)

Today is the one-year anniversary of my bypass surgery on May 16, see this photo from a week after. I'm feeling good. That's a year's worth of good natural history photos that might not have been!

Notes: 34706099055 Here is a Gabb's Checkerspot (Chlosyne gabbii) butterfly perched on a stem of Southern Bush Monkey Flower (Mimulus aurantiacus var. pubescens, Phrymaceae). If you compare this photo taken a month ago, you can see that the butterflies are starting to show some signs of wear. It's a wild world out there. This butterfly isn't really interested in the monkeyflower, but it's a handy perch to keep an eye on the Horkelia and Black Sage flowers nearby. (San Marcos Pass, 16 May 2017)

File.Name: h516d_h516-037.jpg
Notes: 33896366043 Here is a Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly on a flower cluster of Golden Yarrow (Eriophyllum confertflorum, Asteraceae). Look close at the butterfly's proboscis - it's covered with sticky pollen. Butterflies don't eat pollen, but they pick it up and transport it from flower to flower to bring about pollination. The pollen surely didn't get that far up the proboscis from these shallow flowers, but there is Prickly-Phlox (Leptodactylon californicum, Polemoniaceae) flowering nearby that is probably the source - see this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 16 May 2017)

It looks different from the Treehoppers in this recent photo. I'm not sure what it is, but maybe the Coast Live Oak Treehopper (Telamona vestita)? Compare this photo at BugGuide. The thorax of these insects is expanded to cover the abdomen like a shield. They have remarkable eyes, like all hoppers that have to see where they're hopping. (San Marcos Pass, 17 May 2017)

It was a pretty crummy day for new photos - gloomy in the morning, then windy once it cleared. There was a moment in between when I got this photo. We're back under a NWS Wind Advisory tonight with wind gusts to 35 mph so far. It sounds like tomorrow will be a nicer day - warmer too.

Notes: 33916002343 Here is a mating pair of little Planthopper (Fulgoroidea, Auchenorrhyncha, Hemiptera) insects on a stem of Greenbark Ceanothus (Ceanothus spinosus, Rhamnaceae). These insects are notable for their skinny wings, which may make them genus Danepteryx which are just like that. Those narrow wings can't be much good for flying, but maybe they help them steer when they hop. This is the first time I've ever caught them in the act. I reckon that's the male of the right and the female on the left - large with eggs. It's an interesting position - quite discreet for a photo. Happy hump day! (San Marcos Pass, 14 May 2017)

This is the partial web of an Orb-Weaver (Araneidae) spider in a shrub of of native California Coffeeberry (Frangula californica aka Rhamnus californica, Rhamnaceae). It may actually be the web of another Labyrinth Spider (genus Metepeira) with its shelter visible in the background just above what should be the center of the web - see this photo. File.Name: h518a_h518-017.jpg
Notes:
33935949133 Here is a mating pair of Sara Orangetip aka Pacific Orangetip (Anthocharis sara) butterflies on a leaf of Mugwort (Artemisia douglasiana, Asteraceae). I saw them get engaged - one butterfly was perched on the leaf, the other flew up ... and *wham bam* they were going at it! There was no courtship at all that I could see, but maybe their romance has been going on for a while? I mostly saw these butterflies in February and March, and now I'm seeing them again. I reckon these are their two broods for the year. Wouldn't you know that I'd get this photo on a Thursday - after Insect Hump Day Wednesday! Oh well, there's always next week. (San Marcos Pass, 18 May 2017)

Today was a lovely day - sunny, warm but not hot, calm morning with some afternoon breeze. Tomorrow should be more of the same, a bit warmer. This is a welcome change after the last difficult week with fog and wind.

File.Name: h518b_h518-034.jpg
Notes: 34613225031 I found this little "blue butterfly" on a leaf of California Coffeeberry (Frangula californica aka Rhamnus californica, Rhamnaceae). It almost looks like it's ovipositing - but it must be a male with that brilliant color. Do you see that it has become spider prey?? Look on the butterfly's belly, under the leaf. I believe the spider is a Jumping Spider (Salticidae) in the genus Phidippus with a barely-visible red body in the shadow. I believe the butterfly is a Pacific Azure aka Echo Azure (Celastrina echo) butterfly - formerly known as the Spring Azure (Celastrina ladon), but that name is now restricted to butterflies from the Appalachian and Ozark ranges. Nature is tough, but one butterfly's loss is a spider's gain. (San Marcos Pass, 18 May 2017)

Notes: 34582248442 This is a Lady Beetle (Coccinellidae, Coleoptera) on a new flower of native Wedge-leaved Horkelia (Horkelia cuneata) in the Rosaceae plant family. It's actually a Seven-spotted Lady Beetle (Coccinella septempunctata) and not the more common Convergent Lady Beetle (Hippodamia convergens) - though it hardly matters. The plant is interesting too. The flowers have flat stamen filaments that remind me of those wide matches that some restaurants used to give out. I've gotten some mileage out of the ladybugs this week - for Macro Monday, Insect Hump Day, and now Beautiful Bug Butt Thursday. HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 16 May 2017)

File.Name: h519a_h519-019.jpg
Notes:
34379657910 Here is a Brown Elfin (Callophrys augustinus) butterfly ovipositing - laying eggs - on the flower buds on a stem of native Chamise (Adenostoma fasciculatum, Rosaceae). You can see an egg coming out if you look close! I haven't found many references to Chamise as a host plant for these butterflies, but here it is! Jeffrey Caldwell does mention it is in his excellent Notes on Lepidoptera Values of California Native Plants list. At first these butterflies looks plain brown, but when you look closer you see that they really have a rich palette of many colors. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 19 May 2017)

Today was a nice day, but there was a steady onshore breeze from the ocean-side of the mountains. Our usual wind is from the north, so this wind from the south was interesting. The temperature did get into the 80s today for the first time in a while.

File.Name: h519b_h519-061.jpg
Notes: 34379325720 Here are photos of two different Gorgon Copper (Lycaena gorgon) butterflies working new flowers of California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum, Polygonaceae). On the left is a fantastic female with heavily patterned wings above. The butterfly on the right is probably a male with copper-colored wings above, but I think their undersides are the same. They do look kind of like a large blue butterfly, but their upper wings tells a different story. I think of this shrub as a summer plant, and it's just starting to flower now. According to my butterfly book, the butterfly's host plant is a different buckwheat - Longstem Buckwheat (Eriogonum elongatum). This was growing nearby, though it's not flowering yet. See this photo. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 19 May 2017)

34602038082 This is "just a fly" (Diptera) in the wide throat of a new flower of Southern Bush Monkey Flower (Mimulus aurantiacus var. pubescens, Phrymaceae). File.Name: h520a_h520-024.jpg
Notes:
33978047943 I believe this is a common European Earwig (Forficula auricularia, Dermaptera) on a leaf of Holly-Leaved Redberry (Rhamnus ilicifolia) in the Rhamnaceae plant family. I say "common" - but these insects are not at all common up here in the mountains. I think of them as household pests, though I occasionally find them in the woods too - see this photo. If you look closer, it's actually an interesting insect. It is built sort of like a Rove Beetle (Staphylinidae, Coleoptera) - see these photos of genus Philonthus at BugGuide. The earwig has leathery front wings and membranous hind wings that are folded up under the front wings. See my [Previous] photo of the wings unfolding. We rarely see earwings at home, but I've been seeing more of them than usual. I hope this isn't the beginning of an infestation. (San Marcos Pass, 20 May 2017)

It was another nice day, and warm - up to 88 degrees! But there was also an annoying breeze from the southwest that got in the way of my photos. It was a nice warm spring day - but kill the breeze!

File.Name: h520b_h520-026.jpg
Notes: 34654113551 I believe this is a common European Earwig (Forficula auricularia, Dermaptera) taking wing from a leaf of Holly-Leaved Redberry (Rhamnus ilicifolia) in the Rhamnaceae plant family. Isn't it remarkable how it manages to fold all that wing surface into the small space under its short wing covers. These aren't the best photos, but I was happy to get them because just this morning I read an article at NPR on how ladybugs fold their wings. This earwig caught me by surprise when it opened its wings - it happened that fast, and it puts them away just as fast. Earwig origami? See my [Next] photo for more info about earwigs in the mountains. (San Marcos Pass, 20 May 2017)

File.Name: h521a_h521-025.jpg
Notes:
34424746680 I believe this is a very dark Fontana Grasshopper (Trimerotropis fontana). It's a short-horned Band-winged Grasshopper (Oedipodinae, Acrididae, Orthoptera). This one is on the edge of our chipseal road in the canyon. A moment later it took wing, and its wings are distinctly greenish in flight. I didn't notice any particular color on it's hind legs, but they should be blue. If I'm right about the ID, I've found it once before in the canyon. (San Marcos Pass, 21 May 2017)

It was another nice warm day, but that annoying breeze from the southwest was back. This isn't our usual wind direction. This isn't a big wind, but it's enough to keep everything in motion which frustrates photography. An interesting web site that gives a big picture of the wind (and other weather) is Ventusky. It's been showing a neat little swirl over Santa Barbara bringing wind from the southwest - exactly what I've noticed.

Freddy Krueger comes to mind, but I'd rather think of Henri Matisse in his later years. This and my next two photos are red, white, and (sort of) blue. No particular reason, I just had them all. File.Name: h521c_h519-008.jpg
Notes:
34810430305 This is a new white flower of California Thistle (Cirsium occidentale var. californicum) in the Asteraceae plant family. I usually call it the "white thistle" to distinguish it from the "violet thistle" that grows in our canyon at home. (That one has already started to flower up on a steep hillside where I can't get to them.) They are the same taxonomically, but they look quite different. And then there's the Red Thistle aka Cobweb Thistle (Cirsium occidentale var. venustum)! All are the same species, but they all look different. See this photo for a side-by-side comparison. This white thistle is the first I've seen in flower this year. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 19 May 2017)

If you do any kind of restoration work - pulling weeds and such - make sure you know the difference between the native and weedy thistles. The non-native Bull Thistle (Cirsium vulgare) is easily confused with the natives when it's just coming up. If in doubt, leave it be!

File.Name: h521d_h519-042.jpg
Notes: 34770433666 Here are new flowers of Chaparral Penstemon (Penstemon heterophyllus) in Plantaginaceae plant family that I've found this year. (It's another of those plants that was moved from the Scrophulariaceae.) You can see most of its four real stamens above, and the non-fertile "staminode" at the bottom - which sticks out like a Rolling Stones tongue. That's the mysterious "fifth stamen" - which gives this "pen-stamen" family it's name. The flowers are beautiful and look blue when you see them, though they do an odd color shift when you try to photograph them, and end up looking more magenta. Another plant that does this is Spreading Larkspur (Delphinium patens, Ranunculaceae). Is it maybe something to do with UV light? Last year I found these flowers in mid-April, but they're just starting to flower this year a month later. (Near Arroyo Burro Saddle, Santa Ynez Mountains, 19 May 2017)

File.Name: h522a_h522-014.jpg
Notes:
34023441323 This is another shot of a Pinacate Beetle (genus Coelocnemis, Tenebrionidae, Coleoptera) walking in a pile of two-year-old wood chips in the canyon this morning. The genus Eleodes beetles are similar, but this one has fine golden-brown hairs on the lower half of the inside margin of the hind tibiae - actually all the legs. It's also known as a Stink Beetle or Stinkbug or Acrobat Beetle or Clown Beetle, but I prefer the old name. Wikipedia says that "the name pinacate is Mexican Spanish, derived from the Nahuatl (Aztec) name for the insect, pinacatl..." Kids like to torment these large beetles - don't let them! The beetles put their heads on the earth, think of them as wise. These beetles are said to release a brew of noxious chemicals from their rear ends, but I can't vouch for that. The real difficulty with photographing these beetles is that they don't stop walking - not fast, but steady. Looking at this photo, I'm not sure why I didn't use it I considered using this photo for my Macro Monday photo. It show the wood chips nicely. (San Marcos Pass, 22 May 2017)

This was a good photo project today because the breeze kicked up again this morning - again from the southwest. It was cooler today, and it will be even cooler tomorrow.
CommonNames: Pinacate Beetle; Stink Beetle; Stinkbug; Acrobat Beetle; Clown Beetle
ScientificName: Coelocnemis; Tenebrionidae; Coleoptera; Eleodes; pinacatl
associatedTaxa: Coelocnemis; Tenebrionidae; Coleoptera; Eleodes; pinacatl

Maybe they are Tubaria furfuracea which my mushroom book describes as the "quintessential LBM" or "Little Brown Mushroom" - but maybe not. File.Name: h524a_h524-049.jpg
Notes:
34712496592 Here are two shots of a female Acmon Blue (Plebejus acmon) butterfly on new flowers of California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum, Polygonaceae) showing its wings above and below. Or is it a Lupine Blue (Plebejus lupini) that uses buckwheat as a host plant? (Yeah, the name is confusing, apparently a reference to the color of lupine flowers.) My usual butterfly book (An Introduction to Southern California Butterflies, 2004 - here) says that "The Lupine Blue (Plebejus lupini) is quite similar and experts question whether the Acmon and the Lupine should be lumped together as a single species, whether those that feed on buckwheats are all Lupine Blues and those that feed on legumes (Lotus and Astragalus) are Acmon Blues, or finally whether there are more than just the two species." It also says that "the females are all but impossible to separate in the field." My book also says about Acmon Blues that "Females vary seasonally: the early spring broods have extensive blue above, while later broods are brown" - but Lupine Blues only have a single brood. I guess I don't really care - it is a beautiful butterfly! (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 24 May 2017)

Today was a changeable weather day - foggy then clear, breezy then calm, cloudy then sunny, cool then warm. It sounds like tomorrow will bring marine layer clouds and cool temperatures with maybe no clearing at all. The marine layer may be thick enough (4,000+ feet predicted) that we miss the fog winds.
CommonNames: Acmon Blue; California Buckwheat; Lupine Blue

File.Name: h524b_h524-024.jpg
Notes:
34875647595 Here are the first new flowers and pinnate foliage of the locally uncommon native False Indigo (Amorpha californica) shrub in the (Fabaceae) plant family. The shrub is deciduous, and just starting to flower for the new year. It has a light and airy look, like mesquite or acacia in the desert, but here it is in the mountain chaparral. This shrub has long odd-pinnate leaves with many leaflets. The indigo flowers are remarkable, and hardly look like legumes at all. This shrub is not common in our mountains, and it really looks more like it belongs in the desert. It occurs near the Santa Ynez River and on the mountain passes - Refugio and Arroyo Burro, but for some reason it skips San Marcos Pass. I find this shrub to be interesting and beautiful! (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 24 May 2017)

Many sources repeat that this is the only food plant of the California Dogface (Colias eurydice) butterfly, but I'm sure that's wrong. This shrub is uncommon, and the butterflies are quite common later in the summer. They must have other host plants.
ScientificName: Amorpha californica; Fabaceae; Colias eurydice
associatedTaxa: Amorpha californica; Fabaceae; Colias eurydice

Notes:
34802776201 This is not an ant! It's a remarkable ant-mimic nymph of a "true bug" in the order Heteroptera, Hemiptera on a shrub of native White Sage (Salvia apiana, Lamiaceae). I believe they are the same Closterocoris amoenus (Miridae) critters that I've been finding inside of Southern Bush Monkey Flower (Mimulus aurantiacus var. pubescens, Phrymaceae) - see this photo. This one is tiny - just 800 pixels across in this 1:1 macro shot , which comes to about 3.1 mm, less than 1/8 inch. (See here for how I figure.) They are remarkable mimics - they look like ants, move like ants, and even have a faux "nodal spine" like some ants have for real. You can see a bit of its long proboscis folded under its body. (San Marcos Pass, 27 May 2017)

I was feeling pretty crummy today with a cold, but it wasn't a bad day for new photos. There were some clouds, some breeze, and it was still cool for the end of May - barely up to 70∞. Tomorrow is expected to be warmer. I hope I'm feeling better.

Notes: 34547200000 This is another tiny Orb-Weaver Spider (Araneus, Araneidae) on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). It looks like one of the large bottlecap-size spiders that collect under the eaves of our house in the fall, though this one is not much more than 1/4 inch long. This spider is either A. bispinosus or A. montereyensis, and the way to tell them apart is to look at the way the abdomen dips between the two humps. (See Alice Abelia's comment to this photo.) I'm still not sure, but I figure "one of those" is good enough for me! (San Marcos Pass, 27 May 2017)

File.Name: h528a_h528-019.jpg
Notes:
34916225106 This is a small Bee Fly (order Bombyliidae, Diptera) approaching a new flower of native Sticky False Gilyflower (Allophyllum glutinosum) in the Polemoniaceae plant family in our canyon. (No relation to gillyweed in Harry Potter 4! I've never actually heard anyone use this name.) It's a remarkable summer plant that's just starting to flower and will probably continue until September or later. It's a remarkable fly too. It looks like a small version of the larger genus Bombylius flies that we get in mid-winter, see this photo, so maybe it's the same genus? At least I guess it is in the same tribe Bombyliini of the subfamily Bombyliinae. The fly won't actually land on the flower, but it will perch on its front legs while keeping in the air - see this photo. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 28 May 2017)

I'm still feeling crummy with a cold, but it was a nice day to feel crummy on! It was sunny all day with temps in the 70s and modest breeze. Tomorrow sounds like more of the same.

File.Name: h528d_h527-017.jpg
Notes:
34915550286 This is a new violet flower of California Thistle (Cirsium occidentale var. californicum) in the Asteraceae plant family. I usually call it the "violet thistle" to distinguish it from the "white thistle" that grows higher in the mountains. They are the same taxonomically, but they look quite different. And then there's the Red Thistle aka Cobweb Thistle (Cirsium occidentale var. venustum)! All are the same species, but they all look different. See this photo for a side-by-side comparison. This one has been flowering for a while up a steep hillside where I can't get to them, but this plant is finally accessible. Unlike our non-native weedy thistles, this one is truly beautiful. All that's missing is the usual butterfly. (San Marcos Pass, 27 May 2017)

If you do any kind of restoration work - pulling weeds and such - make sure you know the difference between the native and weedy thistles. The non-native Bull Thistle (Cirsium vulgare) is easily confused with the natives when it's just coming up. If in doubt, leave it be!
Locality: Cirsium vulgare) is easily confused with the natives when it's just coming up. If in doubt

File.Name: h529a_h527-005.jpg
Notes:
34815940402 This is a tiny spider in its shelter in the cactus patch of our garden. It really is tiny - just 650 pixels high in this 1:1 macro shot , which comes to about 2.55 mm, close to 1/10 inch. (See here for how I figure.) I'm pretty sure it's a Labyrinth Spider (genus Metepeira, Araneidae). These spiders typically build a proper orb web with a separate little house suspended by a tangle of cobwebs off to the side, but I've never seen one so small. Maybe it's just a spiderling, but it has a well-built shelter. It also seems to have a cat face on it's abdomen! This isn't the real Cat-Faced Spider (Araneus gemmoides) - see this photo - but It will do. This is an example of pareidolia, seeing patterns like faces where none really exists, which was the theme for the ">Macro Mondays group this week. All my photos today are outtakes from that challenge. It was a fun search, here is my photo. (San Marcos Pass, 27 May 2017)

I'm still feeling crummy with a cold, but it was another nice day to feel crummy on! It was sunny all day with temps in the upper 70s and modest breeze. The weather might turn to "June Gloom" conditions by Wednesday, if not tomorrow. I'm actually feeling better today. I hope it doesn't get too gloomy tomorrow.

File.Name: h530a_h530-010.jpg
Notes:
34870103141 Here are two shots of a bee-sized fly hovering over the chipseal road in our canyon today. It has amazing eyes. I believe from the iridescent eyes and pointed antennae that this is a Deer Fly (family Tabanidae, order Diptera), probably the same kind I've photographed before here, here, and here. I believe it is genus Stonemyia which are not biters, maybe S. californica or S. fera. This fly would hover in one place for a minute or so and then abruptly move to a new place a few feet away - until I tried to get closer! Then the fly would back away, always maintaining a distance of about 3 feet. It was pretty steady hovering in the air. Wonderful. (San Marcos Pass, 30 May 2017)

We were in and out of the clouds today as the clouds spilled over the mountains and then backed off again - again and again. I never left the canyon today to see, but I reckon the marine layer stratus clouds were hanging right at the level of the mountains. Every time the clouds would spill over, the "fog winds" would kick in - and then disappear when the fog backed off. It sounds like the marine layer will deepen tonight.

Above the spider, you can see the stigma of the flower, like an uvula in the throat of the flower. This is normally fun to play with. You can poke it and watch it fold up - but this one has already been poked, probably by the spider. Compare this photo showing the open stigma. File.Name: h530c_h529-031.jpg
Notes:
34838005602 Why you shouldn't kiss a butterfly:
you don't know where it's been!

This is a little Acmon Blue (Plebejus acmon) butterfly getting *something* from a large turd on the chipseal road in our canyon. A moment before, there was also a Lady (genus Vanessa) butterfly sharing the log, but I managed to scare her off trying to get close. I'm not sure what the butterfly is getting from the turd - minerals I guess, like puddling? I'm also not sure what animal it came from. It's large, about the size of a long hot dog, and quite dry, like it's in a wrapper. I'm sure it's not a dog. It seems too big to be a coyote or a bobcat and too skinny to be a bear. I'm running out of big animals ... could it be a cougar? Darn, I didn't think to get a better photo of just the turd. I went back today and it was gone. (San Marcos Pass, 29 May 2017)

34859146072 This is a an adult Froghopper (Cercopoidea, Auchenorrhyncha, Hemiptera) pulling free from its larval shell on a leaf of Trask's Yerba Santa (Eriodictyon traskiae) in the Boraginaceae plant family. The larvae are the Spittlebugs that make frothy "loogies" of slime on the plant stems. You can see some of the slime still attached to the larval shell. Hah - how did that adult fit in the larva?? The bugs seem to favor this shrub, along with Sawtooth Goldenbush (Hazardia squarrosa, Asteraceae) later in the summer. This froghopper is still soft like a soft-shell crab. It will look quite different when it has a chance to harden. I have this photo of an adult on Goldenbush, but I'm pretty sure this one looks less ornate. Ain't nature grand! (San Marcos Pass, 31 May 2017)

We actually had a trace of rain early this morning! Today went from cloudy to sunny, becoming breezy in the afternoon. It sound like the next couple of days will be very nice. It's still cool for the end of May.

File.Name: h531b_h531-018.jpg
Notes:
34178822394 This is another remarkable ant-mimic nymph of a "true bug" in the order Heteroptera, Hemiptera on new buds of native Toyon (Heteromeles arbutifolia, Rosaceae). I got a sharper photo today, but I like this one because it clearly shows the antennae and proboscis that make it a bug and not an ant. They are remarkable mimics - they look like ants, move like ants, and even have a faux "nodal spine" like some ants have for real. This may be a later instar of the same Closterocoris amoenus (Miridae) nymphs that I showed in this photo, with an adult in this photo. On the other hand, if you compare that other photo of the nymph, you can see that the "nodal spine" is at the rear of the thorax whereas in this one it's at the front of the thorax. So I'm not sure of the ID of this one. There are other families of ant-mimic true bugs, e.g. the family of Broad-headed Bugs (Alydidae). Any advice will be appreciated. (San Marcos Pass, 31 May 2017)

(I'm assuming that this one is not an adult - but it does have short wings. Could I be wrong about this?)

File.Name: h603a_h603-018.jpg
Notes:
35040585126 Here is another clutch of tiny caterpillars of a Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly on a different stem of Climbing Penstemon (Keckiella cordifolia, Plantaginaceae), compare my [Previous] photo. These caterpillars are terrific web-spinners even when they are this small. If you look close, you can see that they have done some damage to the leaves. They may kill a branch or two, but they usually do not kill the whole plant. The little caterpillars spin their creepy webs on the host plants in the summer. Then they form tough skins and drop to the forest floor to overwinter until they reemerge next spring to finish feeding and pupate into adults. It's a strange cycle, but it works for them. Tents now = butterflies next year! See the comments on my [Previous] photo for some more thoughts and questions about these caterpillars. (San Marcos Pass, 3 June 2017)

It was nice today, but there was a steady breeze - just enough to keep everything in motion. It was warm today with temps in the 80s. It sound like the next few days will be more of the same.
ScientificName: Euphydryas chalcedona
associatedTaxa: Keckiella cordifolia

File.Name: h603b_h603-007.jpg
Notes:
34236585934 Here is a clutch of tiny caterpillars of a Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly on a stem of Climbing Penstemon (Keckiella cordifolia, Plantaginaceae). This is actually the same stem that I found a butterfly oviposting on a month ago in this photo, and I have to wonder if these are from her eggs! These caterpillars are web-spinners even when they are this tiny, see my [Next] photo. The little caterpillars spin their creepy webs on the host plants in the summer. Then they form tough skins and drop to the forest floor to over-winter until they reemerge to finish feeding and pupate into adults next spring. It's a strange cycle, but it works for them. Tents now = butterflies next year!

It's interesting that these butterflies lay all their eggs in one pile, and they hatch and live together for the first part of their lives. See this photo for a closer look. Other butterflies seem to spread their eggs around. It's also interesting that they are using Climbing Penstemon as their summer host plant, but they were using Southern Bush Monkey Flower (Mimulus aurantiacus var. pubescens, Phrymaceae) when they emerged in the spring. Why two different plants? I find extensive webbing in the summer crop of caterpillars, but not so much in the spring batch. (I do find some, see this photo. ) I suspect it's because the summer brood caterpillars are social, but the spring caterpillars are on their own. It's a nice thing about these butterflies that all stages of their lives are available without too much hunting. (San Marcos Pass, 3 June 2017)
CommonNames: Variable Checkerspot; Climbing Penstemon; Southern Bush Monkey Flower
ScientificName: Euphydryas chalcedona; Keckiella cordifolia; Plantaginaceae; Mimulus aurantiacus; pubescens; Phrymaceae
associatedTaxa: Euphydryas chalcedona; Keckiella cordifolia; Plantaginaceae; Mimulus aurantiacus; pubescens; Phrymaceae

They should be out right now near their Gooseberry (Ribes) host plants, but I haven't seen them for a couple of years. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 2 June 2017)

File.Name: h604a_h604-003.jpg
Notes:
34973393451 This is a tiny wild bee (Anthophila, Hymenoptera) working the anther on a stamen of native White Sage (Salvia apiana, Lamiaceae). I didn't get this photo at 1:1 magnification, but I measured the open anther at 2 mm, so that can be your ruler. Perhaps this bee is Lasioglossum ovaliceps - compare BugGuide. It sure seems to be collecting pollen on both its abdomen and hind legs. I've photographed this bee before, see this photo from last summer, but I had the name wrong. I thought that bee might be a Cuckoo Bee (genus Sphecodes, Halicticae), Halicticae), but those bees are cleptoparasites that lack pollen-collecting hairs. I'm not so good at identifying bees, bit the genus Lasioglossum seems like a good match. (San Marcos Pass, 4 June 2017)

It was warm today with temps in the 80s and not much breeze. It sound like the next few days will be more of the same. Perfect!
CommonNames: wild bee; White Sage; Cuckoo Bee

File.Name: h604b_h604-023.jpg
Notes: 35103575375 See that drop of blood by the fly's back leg? This biting fly has been drawing blood from the back of my hand. I believe it is a another Rocky Mountain Bite Fly in the genus Symphoromyia of the Snipe Fly (Rhagionidae) family, probably the same as the one in this photo. It's related to our common Snipe Flies in this photo, but this one has annoying biting habits. I believe this one is a female getting her blood meal. We have at least two biting flies in this genus living in our mountains. The other one is orange in color, see this photo. (It may be Symphoromyia sackeni - see this photo at BugGuide.) That one is a real bloodsucker that flies in and out faster than you can swat, but the one in this photo is slower and easier to get. (San Marcos Pass, 4 June 2017)

File.Name: h604c_h604-037.jpg
Notes: 35103326435 This is a handsome "true bug" in the order Heteroptera, Hemiptera on a ripening berry of Greenbark Ceanothus (Ceanothus spinosus, Rhamnaceae). I guess that it is a Leaf-footed Bug (Coreidae) because of its enlarged legs, but that's just a guess. If you look close, you can see that it has its beak fully inserted into the berry. I wonder what it's after? (San Marcos Pass, 4 June 2017)

File.Name: h604d_h604-032.jpg
Notes:
35102866745 I believe this odd creature is the tiny nymph of a Psyllid (Psyllidae, Auchenorrhyncha, Hemiptera) insect. They are on a berry of native Sugar Bush (Rhus ovata) in the Anacardiaceae plant family. I believe it is the genus Calophya, see Chris Mallory's second comment to this photo of a different psyllid. Apparently the whole genus is associated with Rhus/Sumac plants. Alice Abelia has photos of an adult Calophya californica on Lemonade Berry (Rhus integrifolia), a similar shrub that grows lower in the mountains. These critters really are tiny - just 325 pixels long in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to about 1.3 mm, about 1/2 of 1/10 inch. (See here for how I figure.) Apparently, the white tubes are a white waxy honeydew, and they are certainly more conspicuous than the insects themselves! (San Marcos Pass, 4 June 2017)

It's interesting - the berries have a sticky coasting that is sweet, the sugar in its Sugar Bush name. In this photo, it looks like the psyllid has eaten the sugary coating from the end of the berry. I'll have to get back to them and see if its true. BTW, you can eat the berries too - but strictly as a "suck and spit" berry as the taste soon turns bitter. Hah, this is a great design for seed propagation!
ScientificName: Psyllid; Psyllidae; Auchenorrhyncha; Hemiptera; Rhus ovata; Anacardiaceae; Calophya; Calophya californica; Rhus integrifolia

File.Name: h605a_h605-008.jpg
Notes:
34961121532 Here are two large bees (Apidae, Anthophila, Hymenoptera) working new flowers of native White Sage (Salvia apiana) in the Lamiaceae plant family. These bees are both heavy enough that they can force the initially closed flowers into an open state, see my [Previous] photo. This sage plant is on the edge of our garden. On the left is a Large Carpenter Bee (Xylocopa). The female bees nest in the fascia boards of our upstairs deck, so I spend some time studying them every day when I have a beer after my outing. If you look close behind its right wing, maybe you can see that this unfortunate bee is carrying a load of mites. On the right is a large Bumble Bee (Bombus) - maybe a Yellow-faced Bumble Bee (Bombus vosnesenskii). Both of these large bees adopt what I think of as a "copulatory position" when they mount a flower. They are fun to photograph, but challenging too. Both bees fly fast and jump between flowers. It takes me lots of shots to get any acceptable photo. It would be impossible with film! (San Marcos Pass, 5 June 2017)

I was expecting the weather today to be a repeat of yesterday, but it was cooler and breezier. It barely got above 70 degrees with a cooling breeze from the ocean side of the mountains. At least we're out of the marine layer fog belt so far.

File.Name: h605b_h602-006.jpg
Notes: 34960910412 Here are open and closed flowers of native White Sage (Salvia apiana) in the Lamiaceae plant family. The flowers start out in a closed position, like the flowers on the left. It takes a heavy bee like a Carpenter Bee (Xylocopa) or Bumble Bee (Bombus) to lever them open. Once opened, the flowers will stay open so other insects like honeybees can get in, like the flowers on the right. See my [Next] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 2 June 2017)

File.Name: h605c_h605-009.jpg
Notes:
34280667124 *Sigh* The things we do for a photo...

This is other serious biting fly in our mountains doing a blood draw from my right forearm. I believe it is a another Rocky Mountain Bite Fly in the genus Symphoromyia of the Snipe Fly (Rhagionidae) family, though it's different from the one I showed yesterday in this photo. With its orange colors, this one may be Symphoromyia sackeni, see the discussion with this photo at BugGuide. This one is particularly nasty because it can fly in and out faster than you can swat, while the ther species is a bit slower. They'll be bad for a few weeks, then they'll disappear. BTW, this one was settled on my right arm, so I rested that arm on my truck bed and tried to take the photo with my left hand. Hah - that's almost impossible! (San Marcos Pass, 4 June 2017)

File.Name: h605d_h601-010.jpg
Notes: 34273914364 This is an impressive Robber Fly (family Asilidae, Diptera) on a leaf of California Coffeeberry (Frangula californica aka Rhamnus californica) in the Rhamnaceae plant family. It's about an inch long. These flies are predators that catch their prey on the wing between their front legs, like the dragonflies they resemble. They don't visit flowers, so there's no easy way to find them. This is my photo for the Macro Mondays group, with the theme of "Silhouette". It's certainly backlit, but is it a proper silhouette? Usually I try to avoid photos like this and increase the exposure to compensate, so it was interesting to do the opposite. I have another recent photo of a different robber fly with more conventional lighting here. HMM! (San Marcos Pass, 1 June 2017)

File.Name: h606a_h606-021.jpg
Notes:
34761315710 Like starlings on the power line, here are Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterflies jockeying for position on flowers of Trask's Yerba Santa (Eriodictyon traskiae) in the Boraginaceae plant family. Hah - it's kind of a local naturalist-photographers' secret that these butterflies are the easiest of all our butterflies to get close to and photograph. If you've never photographed butterflies before, these critters are a good place to begin. These butterflies are abundant right now, but other butterflies are less so. Those are the ones I'm looking for! (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 6 June 2017)

Today started clear, but a wind kicked up that felt like a "fog wind". I headed up into the higher mountains, and then I could see the thick marine layer fog/stratus knocking on the door of San Marcos Pass. Going higher to escape the winds worked. When I came back down, the clouds were finally backing off, and it warmed up some. I might be going back up the mountains if the "June gloom" fog returns tomorrow.
ScientificName: Euphydryas chalcedona; Eriodictyon traskiae; Boraginaceae
associatedTaxa: Euphydryas chalcedona; Eriodictyon traskiae; Boraginaceae

File.Name: h606b_h606-035.jpg
Notes: 35016550661 This is another female Gorgon Copper (Lycaena gorgon) butterfly working new flowers of native Golden Yarrow (Eriophyllum confertflorum, Asteraceae). I've been finding more of them than usual this year, and mostly females. They look kind of like Blue (Polyommatinae) butterflies on the bottom of their wings, but the topsides are soft and beautifully patterned. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 6 June 2017)

File.Name: h606c_h606-008.jpg
Notes: 35147692965 This is an Anise Swallowtail (Papilio zelicaon) butterfly working a new flower of native Red Thistle aka Venus Thistle or Cobweb Thistle (Cirsium occidentale var. venustum) in the Asteraceae plant family in the mountains today. This is the first of these butterflies and the first of these thistles that I've seen this year. These butterflies are distinctive because the black on their forewings runs along the wing rather than across it. Beautiful. These butterflies use not anise but non-native Fennel (Foeniculum vulgare, Apiaceae) as their host plant. We keep a few of the weedy plants along our road just for the butterflies, and Julie says here are already tiny caterpillars on them. I'll look for them tomorrow. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 6 June 2017)

File.Name: h606d_h606-006.jpg
Notes: 35107302676 This is a bee-sized wild bee (Anthophila, Hymenoptera) working a new flower of native Red Thistle aka Venus Thistle or Cobweb Thistle (Cirsium occidentale var. venustum) in the Asteraceae plant family in the mountains today. This is the first red thistle I've sound in flower this year. I believe the bee is a Leaf-cutter Bee in the family Megachilidae, probably genus Megachile. The bee was going around in circles standing on its head, waggling its tail back and forth. I assume it's getting nectar from the flowers, but it's also collecting pollen on its abdomen. Bees in this family carry pollen on the underside of their abdomen rather than on their hind legs like most other bees. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 6 June 2017)
CommonNames: wild bee; Red Thistle; Venus Thistle; Cobweb Thistle; Leaf-cutter Bee

File.Name: h607a_h607-048.jpg
Notes:
34359143043 This is a mature caterpillar of an Anise Swallowtail (Papilio zelicaon) butterfly munching a leaf of non-native Fennel (Foeniculum vulgare, Apiaceae) plant in the canyon. This plant is a bad weed in our mountains, but we always keep a few plants just for the butterflies - and try to remember to cut the flowers before they make seeds! See this photo from yesterday of the adult butterfly. Most of the caterpillars in our plants are tiny, like the unfortunate one in my [Previous] photo, but there are a few of these larger ones. It was hard to photograph this today with the fog and wind, but I like this as an action shot. (San Marcos Pass, 7 June 2017)

Today was a lousy day for photography, but a good day for caterpillars! We were deep in a "June Gloom" marine layer fog almost all day. It did finally clear up around 4:00 pm - just in time to get cloudy again. "I don't mind the weather so the wind don't blow" - as the old song goes. The real problem with the fog is that we get the "fog winds" - my name for the chaotic winds that happen when the cool fog near the ground meets the warmer air above. It sound like we'll have another day of this tomorrow - before real winds blow the clouds away.
ScientificName: Papilio zelicaon; Foeniculum vulgare; Apiaceae
associatedTaxa: Papilio zelicaon; Foeniculum vulgare; Apiaceae

File.Name: h607b_h607-009.jpg
Notes: 35168302745 I believe this photo shows an early-instar caterpillar of an Anise Swallowtail (Papilio zelicaon) with the pupal case of a Braconid or Ichneumonid wasp larva that emerged from it. I got this photo on a dissected leaf of non-native Fennel (Foeniculum vulgare, Apiaceae) plant in the canyon. (It's a bad weed in our mountains, but we always keep a few plants just for the butterflies.) The pupal case looks like a little loofah sponge or maybe its packed in excelsior. See Alice Abelia's comment on this photo from last year. The caterpillar looked dead when I first found it, and I was surprised when it started to move! Alice says that the caterpillar will linger for a day or two before dying. This is like one of those murder mysteries where the victim confronts his murderer before dying of a slow poison - like the classic D.O.A. film noir. It was hard to photograph this today with the fog and wind, but it is very interesting. I'll try again tomorrow - if we get some sun. (San Marcos Pass, 7 June 2017)
ScientificName: Papilio zelicaon; Foeniculum vulgare; Apiaceae; D.O.A.
associatedTaxa: Papilio zelicaon; Foeniculum vulgare; Apiaceae; D.O.A.

File.Name: h607c_h607-043.jpg
Notes: 35168100345 Here is a clutch of tiny web-spinning caterpillars of a Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly on a stem of Climbing Penstemon (Keckiella cordifolia, Plantaginaceae). The little caterpillars spin their webs over the plant stems and consume the leaves as they are doing here. They will do this all summer or until the shrubs go dormant for summer. Then they form tough skins and drop to the forest floor to over-winter until they reemerge to finish feeding and pupate into adults next spring. It's a strange cycle, but it works for them. Tents now = butterflies next year! See this photo for another shot of the same caterpillars and some more thoughts about them. Yep, these caterpillars make webs too, so Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 7 June 2017)
ScientificName: Euphydryas chalcedona
associatedTaxa: Keckiella cordifolia

File.Name: h607d_h607-047.jpg
Notes: 34322762094 This is a the caterpillar of a Gossamer-Wing Butterfly (Lycaenidae, Lepidoptera) - the group of Coppers, Blues, Elfins, and Hairstreaks - on a unopened flowers of California Coffeeberry (Frangula californica aka Rhamnus californica) in the Rhamnaceae plant family. I'm not sure what butterfly it will become. I think of hairstreak caterpillars as being hairier and more patterned, but Coffeeberry is listed a a host plant for Gray Hairstreak (Strymon melinus) butterflies. When I first found this caterpillar, it was tended by ants. I tried getting a photo, but it was too windy. I continued walking and got this photo on the way back - without the ants. (San Marcos Pass, 7 June 2017)
CommonNames: Gossamer-Wing Butterfly; California Coffeeberry; Gray Hairstreak

File.Name: h608a_h608-045.jpg
Notes:
35148396956 This is a large late-instar caterpillar of an Anise Swallowtail (Papilio zelicaon) butterfly - showing its osmeterium! That's the brilliant orange "thing" on it's forehead that looks like gummy bear antlers. There's some info at Wikipedia. It's an old naturalist trick to tickle its forehead with a grass stem to bring it out. It doesn't always work, but this time it did. (It's a great trick with kids!) If you look close behind the orange osmeterium, you can see the white egg of (probably) a Tachinid Fly (Tachinidae, Diptera). Can that one egg bring down a large caterpillar like this? See this photo and this photo for earlier instars of this caterpillar, and this photo for the adult butterfly. This one is on a stem of non-native Fennel (Foeniculum vulgare, Apiaceae) in the canyon. (San Marcos Pass, 8 June 2017)

Today turned out to be a very nice day. The marine layer fog was deep enough to come into the mountains, but it stayed along the coast for some reason. It was still pretty breezy.
ScientificName: Papilio zelicaon; Tachinidae; Diptera; Foeniculum vulgare; Apiaceae
associatedTaxa: Papilio zelicaon; Tachinidae; Diptera; Foeniculum vulgare; Apiaceae

File.Name: h608b_h608-017.jpg
Notes:
34343378134 This is the middle-stage caterpillar of an Anise Swallowtail (Papilio zelicaon) butterfly on a leaf of non-native Fennel (Foeniculum vulgare, Apiaceae) plant in the canyon. The caterpillars go through a number of stages or instars as they mature starting with the early black and white stage in this photo from yesterday. (I'm simplifying - there actually are several early black and white instars.) Then comes his stage which still has spines. Finally comes the final stage in this photo and my [Next] photo. The caterpillars lose their spines in that stage. Yes, it's complicated. I'll see if I can figure it out. (San Marcos Pass, 8 June 2017)

This plant is a bad weed in our mountains, but we always keep a few plants just for the butterflies - and try to remember to cut the flowers before they make seeds! See this photo of the adult butterfly. The butterflies favor this non-native brood plant. It must have found other Apiacea plants before Fennel was introduced, but which ones are out in mid-summer? Maybe Sanicles or Tauschia, though I've never found a caterpillar on this plant.
ScientificName: Papilio zelicaon; Foeniculum vulgare; Apiaceae; Apiacea

I'm sure it's the same kind of fly as shown in this photo last week. It looks like a small version of the larger genus Bombylius flies that we get in mid-winter, see this photo, so maybe it's the same genus? It might be a Yellow-faced Bumble Bee (Bombus vosnesenskii). These large bees are important for "opening up" the tight sage flowers, see this photo and this photo. This is also a nice butt shot, so HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 5 June 2017)

File.Name: h609a_h609-027.jpg
Notes:
35076350801 This is a hatch of tiny caterpillars of a Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly in a flower head of Climbing Penstemon (Keckiella cordifolia, Plantaginaceae). The caterpillars are just hatching from their eggs and haven't had time to spin their webs yet, see this photo. The butterflies lay their eggs in a group, see this photo. It's interesting than these summer caterpillars are social and here they are all hatching at the same time. I didn't know what I had when I got this photo! (San Marcos Pass, 9 June 2017)

Today was sunny and cloudy; breezy and calm; warm and cool ... an in-between day. There's a late-season frontal boundary moving through, and the wind is picking up. We're under a NWS Wind Advisory tonight with wind gusts to 30 mph already. The next couple of days will probably be breezy.

File.Name: h609b_h609-006.jpg
Notes: 34396858403 Here is the uncommon native Chaparral Orchid aka Denseflower Rein Orchid (Piperia elongata) in the Orchidaceae plant family - in full flower. The basal leaves completely shrivel up by the time they flower, leaving a bare asparagus-like stalk a foot or two feet tall. See this photo for the early basal leaves. These are the first flowers I've seen this year. Believe me - if you drive over San Marcos Pass on Highway 154 in June, the last thing you would think of is orchids and lilies in the summer-dry woods... but here are the orchids, and the lilies are coming! (San Marcos Pass, 9 June 2017)

File.Name: h609c_h609-017.jpg
Notes: 35040833152 I guess you have to take my word about this fly, but this was the only photo I could get today. It's a remarkable Metallic Small-Headed Fly in the genus Eulonchus of the uncommon family Acroceridae of the order Diptera - probably Eulonchus smaragdinus. This one is snuggled into a flower of native Southern Bush Monkey Flower (Mimulus aurantiacus var. pubescens, Phrymaceae). See this photo for another shot of the same fly approaching the flower from the air and note its long proboscis - longer than its body. The flies seem made for these flowers - just the right size to get in, and a long enough proboscis to reach the sweet stuff inside. These flies move so fast that they are very hard to capture - even just coming out of the flower. I'll keep trying! (San Marcos Pass, 9 June 2017)

It's a small Bee Fly (order Bombyliidae, Diptera) working new flowers of native Trask's Yerba Santa (Eriodictyon traskiae) in the Boraginaceae plant family. File.Name: h610a_h610-006.jpg
Notes:
35225950525 Here is another remarkable ant-mimic nymph of a "true bug" in the order Heteroptera, Hemiptera on a shrub of native White Sage (Salvia apiana, Lamiaceae). I believe this is the nymph of the same Closterocoris amoenus (Miridae) critters that I've been finding inside of Southern Bush Monkey Flower (Mimulus aurantiacus var. pubescens, Phrymaceae) - see this photo. I have another shot of this nymph from a couple of weeks ago in this photo, and I can see that it's grown almost twice the size. That one was just 800 pixels across in a 1:1 photo, but this one is 1520 pixels - which comes to about 6 mm or a bit less than1/4 inch. This one also has the beginning of "training wings". These are remarkable mimics - they look like ants, move like ants, and even have a faux "nodal spine" like some ants have for real. You can see its long proboscis folded under its body, which real ants don't have. (San Marcos Pass, 10 June 2017)

Today started in a marine layer cloud, but then it cleared up and was quite nice. At around noon, the clouds rolled back in. Now the wind is picking up again and we are under another NWS Wind Advisory tonight. It was surprisingly cool today. I don't think it ever got out of the 60s.

(See here for how I figure.) Just before I got this photo, the sun disappeared behind a thick mass of clouds, so the exposure is what it is. I've found these tiny dragonfly-like flies before, see this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 10 June 2017)

Notes:
34861778660 I'm pretty sure this is a male Silvery Blue (Glaucopsyche lygdamus) butterfly showing its upper wings on a shrub of native Black Sage (Salvia mellifera, Lamiaceae). See this photo for a shot of the undersides of the wings. The Pacific Azure aka Echo Azure (Celastrina echo) butterfly - formerly known as the Spring Azure (Celastrina ladon) - looks similar above, but I believe that one has a wider dark margin on its upper wings, compare this photo and this photo. These two butterflies are our earliest Blue (Polyommatinae) butterflies. I reckon this is a new brood? (San Marcos Pass, 11 June 2017)

Today was another chilly day for June - overcast in the morning, clear but windy in the afternoon, never out of the 60s. We're under another NWS Wind Advisory tonight, with gusts already to 40+ mph. File.Name: h612d_h608-028.jpg
Notes: 34455535933 Here is foliage of native Poison Oak (Toxicodendron diversilobum) in the Anacardiaceae plant family. I'm not sure why this winter-deciduous shrub is already turning red after a decent rain year, but here it is. I don't believe this plant is really poison, but many people are sensitive to it. It really is a beautiful shrub or vine and important for wildlife. It's pretty to look at, but don't touch it if you are sensitive! For some reason, I don't get it. I hike through Poison Oak constantly, and it doesn't bother me unless I really ask for it. This is my photo for the Macro Mondays group, with the theme of "Poisonous". By the way, some leaves are as large as my open hand, but these leaves were quite small. The entire area in about two inches across. This isn't the photo I wanted for Macro Mondays. I spotted a Black Widow spider on its web a few days ago, but it scurried away before I could get a photo. I've gone back every day since then and haven't seen it again. Oh well, I had a backup. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 8 June 2017)

File.Name: h613a_h613-012.jpg
Notes:
34451088094 Here are two shots of a West Coast Lady (Vanessa annabella) butterfly on new flowers of native Golden Yarrow (Eriophyllum confertflorum, Asteraceae). Yeah, it's a bit over-exposed, but this is a record shot - the first of these butterflies I've seen this year. This is the smallest of our Lady butterflies, and the least common - at least up here in the mountains. The distinguishing characteristic is the first orange bar in the middle of the front edge of the upper wing which is white in other Ladies, though the American Lady (Vanessa virginensis) butterflies in our mountains sometimes have a nearly orange bar in that place, compare this photo. There are other differences as well - It has squared-off wing tips, it has four (or five) eyespots below its hindwings, and each of the eyespots on the hindwing above has a blue center. I think size is usually enough, though I've been seeing some small American Lady butterflies lately - see this photo. This is the first one I've managed to photograph this year. (San Marcos Pass, 13 June 2017)

Today was a very nice day. It was warm but not too hot, high about 80 degrees. There was still some residual wind, but it wasn't too bad this morning. Now we're in for an extended period of HOT weather for the next week.

34926820930 This green-eyed monster is really a Deer Fly (family Tabanidae, order Diptera). Here it is working new summer flowers of native California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum, Polygonaceae). It's about the size of a honey bee, and just as furry. I believe it is genus Stonemyia which are not biters, maybe S. californica or S. fera. Unlike most other tabanid species, none of the females of this genus are known to feed on blood. Today was just about a perfect day with temps in the low 80s and not much breeze. It will get downright hot in the days ahead.

File.Name: h614d_h613-017.jpg
Notes: 34503715413 Here is a clutch of tiny web-spinning caterpillars of a Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly on a stem of Climbing Penstemon (Keckiella cordifolia, Plantaginaceae). It's interesting that the caterpillars appear to represent two different stages or instars. Are they maturing at different rates? Or did another butterfly slip its eggs into the clutch? The little caterpillars spin their webs over the plant stems and consume the leaves as they are doing here. They will do this all summer or until the shrubs go dormant for summer, then they will form tough skins and drop to the forest floor to over-winter until they reemerge to finish feeding and pupate into adults next spring. It's a strange cycle, but it works for them. Tents now = butterflies next year! Yep, spiders aren't the only web spinners, so Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 13 June 2017)
ScientificName: Euphydryas chalcedona
associatedTaxa: Keckiella cordifolia

File.Name: h615b_h615-024.jpg
Notes: 34490146964 I suspect the more you know about insects, the stranger this critter will look. I'm pretty sure it is a male scale Insect or mealy bug (Coccoidea, Sternorrhyncha, Hemiptera) - likely a Giant Mealybug in the family Putoidae, genus Puto. See this photo at BugGuide, and this photo by Alice Abelia. The long white tails are really just long hairs covered by a waxy exudate. I made a grab for one a few years ago, and the white stuff came off in my fingers leaving just the filament. I guess the whole insect is covered with the same waxy exudate. If this is a male, I have no idea what the females look like - but they must be out there. I only see one of these every few years, see this photo for the last one I saw in 2014. File.Name: h616a_h616-017.jpg
Notes:
35185903042 Push, mama, push!

This mama Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly is ovipositing - laying eggs - under a leaf of well-named but non-native Butterfly Bush (Buddleia davidii, Scrophulariaceae or Buddlejaceae) in the garden. These butterflies lay their eggs in a group that will make a social web in the bush, see this photo.

This is also interesting because older books always refer to the butterfly's host plants as "scrophs" - members of the old Scrophulariaceae plant family - but this family has been now been split up into multiple new families. I usually find the caterpillars on Southern Bush Monkey Flower (Phrymaceae) or Climbing Penstemon (Plantaginaceae) or Woolly Paintbrush (Orobanchaceae) or California Figwort in what's left of the Scrophulariaceae. And now here's a butterfly ovipositing on a non-native shrub that's been moved to the Buddlejaceae plant family! I wonder if the butterflies recognize a similarity in these plants that the botanists have missed? (San Marcos Pass, 16 June 2017)

Today was another very nice day. It was feeling hot today with temps in the upper-80s, and there was some breeze from the south. It got to 100∞ down by the river today. It will get downright hot in the days ahead. We're under an NWS Heat Advisory this weekend and on into next week.

File.Name: h618b_h618-044.jpg
Notes:
35355200116 These are new flowers of native Sacapellote (Acourtia microcephala) in the Asteraceae plant family growing in the mountains. It's an unusual perennial composite with an interesting common name. It has heads of pretty two-lipped mint-like flowers that are unlike any other composite I know. Many plants with composite flowers that have the appearance of being a single flower, but these Sacapellote flowers are more like groups of individual flowers. (San Marcos Pass, 18 June 2017)

These might be the only open flowers I see this year in the mountains. Most of the flowers appear to be producing only white pappus hairs, and I'm afraid they will skip flowering - a kind of "cleistogamy", flowering without opening. I found this one flowering plant growing through a shrub of native Holly-leaved Cherry (Prunus ilicifolia) in the Rosaceae. This has happened before, see this photo. On the other hand, I've been wrong about this before. Maybe the flowers are just late.
Locality: Prunus ilicifolia) in the Rosaceae. This has happened before

File.Name: h618c_h614-037.jpg
Notes: 34551516964 These are stunning new flowers of native Scarlet Larkspur (Delphinium cardinale) in the Ranunculaceae plant family. It's a summer wildflower that is not common in our mountains, but it is well worth looking for. The plants grow quite tall, to eight feet in a favorable place like this. Deer seem to relish the plants. It's another plant where you have to pinch yourself and remember that it is native. They are especially striking at this time of year. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 14 June 2017)

File.Name: h618d_h617-042.jpg
Notes: 34551238364 Hah - this is a native plant of Climbing Penstemon (Keckiella cordifolia, Plantaginaceae) growing from the open top of an electrical conduit pipe on the utility pole by our mailbox. I believe it's just a telephone pole, no power. It's about ten feet high, so it's too high to look into - but I wonder what's in there. I think this counts as growing in the gaps! Nature is resilient. (San Marcos Pass, 17 June 2017)

File.Name: h619a_h619-005.jpg
Notes:
35248971322 I found another mama Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly ovipositing - laying eggs - under a leaf of well-named but non-native Butterfly Bush (Buddleia davidii, Scrophulariaceae or Buddlejaceae) in the garden. See this photo of another one I found a few days ago. If you look close under the leaf, you can see that these butterflies lay their eggs in a group that will make a social web in the bush, see this photo. This photo is also interesting because older books always refer to the butterfly's host plants as "scrophs" - members of the old Scrophulariaceae plant family - but this family has been now been split up into multiple new families. I usually find the caterpillars on Southern Bush Monkey Flower (Phrymaceae) or Climbing Penstemon (Plantaginaceae) or Woolly Paintbrush (Orobanchaceae) or California Figwort in what's left of the Scrophulariaceae. All of these plants used to be considered scrophs. And now here's a butterfly ovipositing on a non-native shrub that's been moved to the Buddlejaceae plant family! I wonder if the butterflies recognize a similarity in these plants that the botanists have missed? (San Marcos Pass, 19 June 2017)

Lordy, it was HOT today. I saw 99∞ on the thermometer on our upstairs porch, and a neighbor's weather station at Weather Underground registered 102∞. It was 104∞ down by the river. Needless to say, we didn't get the sea breeze from the south that has helped cut the heat for the last couple of days. It's been muggy too. Tomorrow is expected to be the hottest day of this heat wave. There's good info about the heat and humidity at the California Weather Blog.

File.Name: h619b_h619-009.jpg
Notes: 35028221670 Here are some of the first new flowers of Branching Phacelia (Phacelia ramosissima var. latifolia) in the Hydrophyllaceae or Boraginaceae plant family growing wild on the edge of our garden. You can see a tiny wild bee flying into a flower in the upper-left. This plant will never win any awards for beauty with all those faded flowers which linger on the flower heads, but several different kinds of tiny bees seem to favor it. This phacelia differs from most others of its genus for being perennial. It keeps producing flowers all summer with occasional watering. It figure it's important for pollinators. (San Marcos Pass, 19 June 2017)

File.Name: h619c_h618-031.jpg
Notes: 35248511592 Here is another photo of what I believe are tiny nymphs of Psyllid (Psyllidae, Auchenorrhyncha, Hemiptera) insects. They are on a berry of native Sugar Bush (Rhus ovata) in the Anacardiaceae plant family. I believe they are genus Calophya which are associated with Rhus/Sumac plants, see Chris Mallory's second comment to this photo of a different psyllid. Alice Abelia has photos of an adult Calophya californica on Lemonade Berry (Rhus integrifolia), a similar shrub that grows lower in the mountains. The critters in my photo are tiny, about 1.0 mm. (See here for how I figure.) Apparently, the white tubes are a white waxy honeydew, and they are certainly more conspicuous than the insects themselves! I have another photo which is a tighter crop. Hah - they look like tiny horseshoe crabs or trilobites! (San Marcos Pass, 18 June 2017)

File.Name: h619d_h617-015.jpg
Notes: 35242171622 Here are drops of "milk" on a torn leaf of Showy Milkweed (Asclepias speciosa) in the Asclepiadaceae or Apocynaceae) plant family in our garden. See this photo for the flowers and a butterfly. It's a lovely California native from further north with large nickle-sized flowers that smell like jasmine. The white sap contains a latex which feels sticky, like rubber cement. Keep it away from your eyes! This is my photo for the Macro Mondays group, with the theme of "Drips, Drops and Splashes". HMM! (San Marcos Pass, 17 June 2017)
