File.Name: h620a_h620-045.jpg
Notes:
35270345352 Here is a male California Dogface (Colias eurydice) butterfly working new flower of native Woolly Blue Curls (Trichostema lanatum) in the Lamiaceae plant family. What colors. You can see a bit of the "dogface" pattern on its forewing, but these fine butterflies always close their wings at rest, so it's a challenge to capture. I did get a photo today, but I'm not sure if it's good enough to post. See this photo from last year for a good try. The butterflies are common enough, but the only way to catch their upper side is to get them in the air. This is our official California State Butterfly, and I'll bet not many people know why. (San Marcos Pass, 20 June 2017)

Maybe it was a degree or two cooler today, but It was still saw 98∞ on the thermometer on our upstairs porch. It's so hot and muggy that most insects are lying low. There's good info about the heat and humidity at the California Weather Blog. Today is the last official day of spring. The summer solstice is officially tomorrow on June 21, but it really happens tonight on June 20 at 9:24 p.m. PDT. That's at 04:44 UTC in Greenwich, England - where time starts. It's the first day of summer when the sun is highest above the ecliptic and the path of the sun is highest in the sky. Happy solstice!
ScientificName: Colias eurydice; Trichostema lanatum; Lamiaceae
associatedTaxa: Colias eurydice; Trichostema lanatum; Lamiaceae

File.Name: h620b_h620-040.jpg
Notes: 34594774714 I was focused on this new flower cluster of native Coyote Mint aka White-leaf Monardella (Monardella hypoleuca, Lamiaceae) when a handsome Sand Wasp (genus Steniolia, Bembicini, Apoidea, Hymenoptera) flew into my photo! These wasps are hard to photograph. They move very fast between the flowers, and they seem to see everything with those large goggle-like eyes. Many monardella species have a sweet peppermint smell, but these plants smell dark and musty. They are one of my favorite summer wildflowers, that pick the hot and dry part of the year to flower. They are the only plant on which I've found uncommon Callippe Fritillary (Speyeria callippe) butterflies, see this photo from a couple of years ago. I hope for another sighting this year. (San Marcos Pass, 20 June 2017)

File.Name: h620c_h620-016.jpg
Notes: 35049282050 This is a middle-stage caterpillar of an Anise Swallowtail (Papilio zelicaon) butterfly on a leaf of non-native Fennel (Foeniculum vulgare, Apiaceae) plant in the canyon - but it has a problem. You can see a dark area on the first segments behind its true legs. If you look closer, you can see the white egg of (I think) a parasitoid Tachinid Fly (Tachinidae, Diptera). Now if you look even closer, you can see four more eggs between the dark area and the head. Sometimes the caterpillars get lucky, see this photo of a caterpillar walking away from a shed skin with eggs. But I don't like the look of that dark necrotic area. Caterpillars really are sitting ducks for parasitic flies and wasps. Some caterpillars like Woolly Bears are so covered with spikes and hairs that it might be hard for predators, but other caterpillars have no obvious defense. (San Marcos Pass, 20 June 2017)
ScientificName: Papilio zelicaon; Foeniculum vulgare; Apiaceae; Tachinidae; Diptera

File.Name: h620d_h620-009.jpg
Notes:
34626012843 This is a mass of new flowers of Farewell-to-Spring flowers in the Onagraceae plant family - still going strong even though today is the last day of spring. I believe they are the Speckled Clarkia (Clarkia cylindrica), though these flowers are not at all speckled. They do have erect wiry stems, nodding flower buds, and erect buds. The species Clarkia bottae is very similar. These plants have had a good year with some rain for a change. They'll keep flowering for a while. (San Marcos Pass, 20 June 2017)

Today is the last official day of spring, so farewell spring. The summer solstice is officially tomorrow on June 21, but it really happens tonight on June 20 at 9:24 p.m. PDT. That's at 04:44 UTC in Greenwich, England - where time starts. It's the first day of summer when the sun is highest above the ecliptic and the path of the sun is highest in the sky. Happy solstice!

File.Name: h621a_h621-011.jpg
Notes:
35456359255 This is a small Pacific Ambush Bug (Phymata pacifica, Phymatidae, Heteroptera, Hemiptera) waiting for prey on a brand new flower of native California Goldenrod (Solidago californica) in the Asteraceae plant family. This is probably a lady, large with eggs. What's remarkable is its cyan/blue color. These tiny predators are color-shifters going between white and yellow, but I don't think I've ever seen this color before. I hope it's not some horrible internal parasite. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 21 June 2017)

It's too damn hot ... listen. Maybe it was a degree or two cooler today, but It was still 98∞ on the thermometer on our upstairs porch. It's so hot and muggy that most insects are lying low. Our NWS Heat Advisory has now been extended until next Sunday. This has been a serious heat wave.

File.Name: h621b_h621-032.jpg
Notes:
34646281983 Here is a little Bee Fly working new flowers of Yerba Santa with a gall in the center. Sure, the fly flew into my photo. I'll take them one at a time.

This is a Bee Fly (order Bombyliidae, Diptera) that looks like a small version of the larger genus Bombylius flies that we get in mid-winter, see this photo. Maybe it's the same genus - or at least the same tribe Bombyliini of the subfamily Bombyliinae. Yep, the proboscis is probably as long as the fly, see this photo.

The flowers belong to a shrub of native Trask's Yerba Santa (Eriodictyon traskiae) in the Boraginaceae plant family. This shrub is a favorite of many local insects and butterflies, see this photo.

There's a gall in the center of the ring of flowers. These galls are open at the top, like little volcanoes erupting from the plant. They are not listed in my book on plant galls, and I don't know whose galls they are. I see similar galls on the seed heads of Eriodictyon crassifolium down by the river, see this photo. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 21 June 2017)

This tangled web belongs to a female Western Black Widow (Latrodectus hesperus) spider. It's a real cobweb in the in the family Theridiidae family of Cobweb Weaver spiders. I saw the spider on its web a couple of weeks ago, but haven't seen it again. Too bad, a shot would have been perfect for the Macro Mondays "Poisonous" theme. The whole tangled web is a couple of feet across. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 17 June 2017)

File.Name: h622a_h622-024.jpg
Notes:
35345560811 This is an American Lady (Vanessa virginensis) butterfly working new flowers of the well-named but non-native Butterfly Bush (Buddleia davidii, Scrophulariaceae or Buddlejaceae) in the garden. The two large eyespots under its hindwings give the ID, but this butterfly is interesting in having an orange bar in the middle of the front edge of the upper wing rather than white. (I'll make a note.) Compare this photo of an American Lady with a white bar there. And compare this photo of a West Coast Lady (Vanessa annabella) butterfly which also has an orange bar. Well, butterflies are variable, just like you and I. (San Marcos Pass, 22 June 2017)

Today was 15∞ cooler than yesterday, only in the low-80s rather than the high-90s. The trade-off was wind - a cooling sea breeze from the south. Looking at the Ventusky web site for the big picture, this unusual wind seems to be he result of a large eddy in the normal wind flow from the north. Very curious. I recommend this interesting site to see the big picture of the wind and other weather.

One distinguishing feature of these butterflies is a row of hyaline (translucent) spots on its forewings, visible when the sun is behind. (The larger Umber Skipper (Poanes melane) also has these sun spots, but they are not actually clear.) I've been seeing these butterflies for some time, but this is the first photo I've posted. (San Marcos Pass, 22 June 2017)

I believe the bee is a Mining Bee in the genus Anthophora, possibly the California Anthophora (Anthophora californica). I think I had a "sweat filter" on my camera lens this day - a problem on these hot days. Compare my [Previous] photo of a different wild bee. HBBBT! File.Name: h623a_h623-036.jpg
Notes:
35328442972 This is the work of tiny nymphs of Psyllid (Psylloidea, Auchenorrhyncha, Hemiptera) insects on berries of native Sugar Bush (Rhus ovata) in the Anacardiaceae plant family. Apparently, the white tubes are a white waxy honeydew - which is much more noticeably than the insects! I believe they are genus Calophya which are associated with Rhus/Sumac plants. There are mostly nymphs in there, see this photo for a close shot. I also notice a few adults, see my [Previous] photo, here. BTW, the berries are edible - but strictly as a "suck and spit" berry since the taste soon turns bitter. Don't eat the ones with white whiskers! (San Marcos Pass, 23 June 2017)

It was about 90∞ warm today, but a dense fog bank was lurking just down the mountains towards Santa Barbara! It was curious that the fog was so close, but we didn't have any "fog winds" - my name for the chaotic winds that happen when the cool fog near the ground meets the warmer air in the mountains above. Darn, just when I think I'm starting to understand our weather. It sounds like we have a few more days of hot weather coming - and a slight chance of thunderstorms tonight??

Notes: 35364429711 Here are two shots of a tiny adult Psyllid (Psylloidea, Auchenorrhyncha, Hemiptera) insect on a berry of native Sugar Bush (Rhus ovata) in the Anacardiaceae plant family. Apparently, the white tubes are a white waxy honeydew, see my [Next] photo to appreciate how extensive they can get. This is the first time I've ever noticed the adults. I believe they are genus Calophya which are associated with Rhus/Sumac plants, see Chris Mallory's second comment to this photo of a different psyllid. Alice Abelia has photos of an adult Calophya californica on Lemonade Berry (Rhus integrifolia), a similar shrub that grows lower in the mountains. Maybe they are the same, maybe not. These critters are really tiny - just 430 pixels long in these 1:1 macro shots, which comes to about 1.7 mm, about 1/16 inch. (See here for how I figure.) Yep, these are deep crops - too deep, but so it goes. (San Marcos Pass, 23 June 2017)

File.Name: h623c_h623-030.jpg
Notes:
35327704342 *Sigh* The things we do for a photo...

This is a nasty biting fly in our mountains, on the back of my hand. They hurt! I believe it is in the family Tabanidaeof Horse and Deer Flies, but it is not much larger than a house fly and its eyes are quite plain unlike most other Tabanids. Maybe it is genus Apatolestes in the subfamily Pangoniinae, in particular the species Apatolestes hinei? I reckon it's OK to call it a Deer Fly even though it is not in the subfamily Chrysopsinae. Usually the Rocky Mountain Bite Flies (Symphoromyia, Rhagionidae) - see this photo and this photo - are the worst biters in our mountains, but lately these have been worse. I guess there's always something. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 23 June 2017)

This fly has slightly different colors than I expect, but it has the same reddish eyes and red tip on its abdomen that I associate with this family. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 22 June 2017)

It wasn't too hot today considering that we're still under a NWS Heat Advisory, but it was muggy, the kind of day where you get sweaty just standing in the shade. There were some tropical clouds today and even a slight chance of a thunderstorm. We didn't get any rain, but I could feel the moisture.

File.Name: h624b_h624-029.jpg
Notes: 35127910300 This is a tiny parasitic wasp inserting its slender ovipositor into a ripening fruit of Greenbark Ceanothus (Ceanothus spinosus, Rhamnaceae). I'm pretty sure the wasp is a Chalcid Wasp (Chalcidoidea, Hymenoptera). If you look close, you can see the wasp's ovipositor inserted into the galled flower and into the gall-inducing insect within. It seems odd that the wasp's ovipositor comes from the front of the abdomen, but I believe they fold it under their belly to the middle like this. I called it a "parasitic wasp", but I'm not sure if the wasp is laying its eggs in the fruit or into a grub inside the fruit. Some of these Chalcid wasps are plant feeders, some are gall inducers, and some appear to be hyperparasites that parasitize the larvae inside of galls - see this photo and this photo. I took a lot of photos of this critter today to get this keeper. (San Marcos Pass, 24 June 2017)
CommonNames: parasitic wasp; Greenbark Ceanothus; Chalcid Wasp
ScientificName: Ceanothus spinosus; Rhamnaceae; Chalcidoidea; Hymenoptera
associatedTaxa: Ceanothus spinosus; Rhamnaceae; Chalcidoidea; Hymenoptera

I found this one is in the same shrub of Greenbark Ceanothus (Ceanothus spinosus, Rhamnaceae) where I've found others like the nymph in this photo. This one is about 1/2 inch or less long. 35127618840 This is a small spider's exuvia - the shed skin after a molt - on a spent flower stem of native Wedge-leaved Horkelia (Horkelia cuneata) in the Rosaceae plant family. It is beautiful and strange! I guess the reason why these shed skins don't pile up in the woods is that other insects like Carpet Beetle (Dermestidae, Coleoptera) regard them as delicacies. Hah - those beetles are also the bane of insect collections. (San Marcos Pass, 24 June 2017)

(The legs are pretty spiky. I wonder if this could be the exuvia of a Western Lynx Spider (Oxyopes scalaris, Oxyopidae). Compare this photo taken nearby.)

File.Name: h625a_h625-051.jpg
Notes:
34727255193 This is a fantastic diurnal (daytime) California Clearwing Moth (Hemaris thetis, Sphingidae, Lepidoptera) working new summer flowers of native Coyote Mint aka White-leaf Monardella (Monardella hypoleuca) in the Lamiaceae plant family. For some reason, I haven't been seeing very many of these moths this year, and this is my first photo. I find the moths on a number of different flowers, but these might be their favorites. They are also one of my favorites - they look like spring flowers, but they pick the hot and dry time of the year to flower. Note that the flower is sharp but the moth isn't - they are fast, and they never stop moving! (Well, almost never - see this photo!) (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 25 June 2017)

It was hot again today, up to about 99∞ on the upstairs deck. Tomorrow might be even hotter, though our NWS Heat Advisory ends at 9:00 pm tonight. Darn, that means we've had advisory level heat for the last ten days. It's expected to finally start cooling down on Tuesday.

Notes: 35149314160 Here are the first flowers I've seen this year of the Late-flowered Mariposa Lily (Calochortus fimbriatus) in the Liliaceae plant family. The flowers are highly variable, and I want to photograph every one because they are so different! Compare the interior of the flower in my [Next] photo. These fantastic summer flowers attract lots of life - did you notice the white Crab Spider (Thomisidae) on the left-most sepal of the flower on the right? I was in this place a few days ago on June 21, and there were no flowers. Now there are. Thanks to Tom Ballinger for finding the first one in this photo on June 23. File.Name: h626a_h626-006.jpg
Notes:
35171828770 I believe this is a Pale Swallowtail (Papilio eurymedon) butterfly on the well-named but non-native Butterfly Bush (Buddleia davidii, Scrophulariaceae or Buddlejaceae) in the garden today. Note that the Pale Swallowtails look less pale as we move into summer. They get a cream color, and sometimes I confuse them with Western Tiger Swallowtails (P. rutulus). I believe that this photo and this photo show them both together. (San Marcos Pass, 26 June 2017)

It was hot again today, in the mid-90s on the upstairs deck. I hope this is the end of this 10+ day heat wave. It is expected to be a bit cooler tomorrow and much cooler on Wednesday. I'm ready for a change.

Notes: 34717274464 I was focused on this slightly-faded flower of native Evening Primrose (Oenothera elata ssp. hookeri, Onagraceae) on the edge of our garden when a humble little Cabbage White (Pieris rapae) butterfly flew into flower and my photo. The flower is acting like a yellow light tent reflecting some color on the butterfly. The flowers fade during the day, but I got this shot this morning when the sun was first hitting the flowers. This is actually the first one of these butterflies I've been able to photograph this year. (San Marcos Pass, 26 June 2017)

File.Name: h626c_h626-017.jpg
Notes: 35519073086 Here's what we're missing because some thoughtless "drive-by picker" broke the stem in my [Previous] photo - a beautiful inflorescence of native Humboldt Lily (Lilium humboldtii ssp. ocellatum, Liliaceae) growing along the road in Kinevan Canyon. Fortunately the picker just took the one stalk, and there are more plants in plain view along the road that have gone untouched. I reckon most people have the good sense to leave them be. They are beautiful flowers, almost too pretty to be native - but here they are. And sure, there are many more plants hidden away in canyons in the mountains. It's curious that I haven't seen any butterflies on the flowers this year, see this photo from a couple of years ago. (San Marcos Pass, 26 June 2017)

File.Name: h626d_h623-011.jpg
Notes:
35163767580 "Please Don't Pick The Wildflowers!"

*Sigh* I suppose it was inevitable. Some thoughtless "drive-by picker" got this native Humboldt Lily (Lilium humboldtii ssp. ocellatum, Liliaceae) along the road in Kinevan Canyon last week. And they didn't even do it right - the stem is broken, not cut. I never posted a photo of this specimen in flower, but it was impressive - about eight feet tall, with a dozen flowers, see Macro Mondays group, with the theme of "Broken". HMM! (San Marcos Pass, 23 June 2017)

File.Name: h627a_h627-001.jpg
Notes:
34738108654 This is a the caterpillar of a Lycaenid butterfly (Lycaenidae, Lepidoptera) - the group of Coppers, Blues, Elfins, and Hairstreaks - on a flower cluster of native White Sage (Salvia apiana) in the Lamiaceae plant family. I'm pretty sure that this is the caterpillar of a Gray Hairstreak (Strymon melinus) butterfly with those chevron patterns of its back and general hairiness. It's interesting that I haven't noticed any of the butterflies yet this year. So how di the caterpillar get here?? (San Marcos Pass, 27 June 2017)

It was cooler today but still warm, in the mid-80s rather than the mid-90s like yesterday. It expected to be even cooler on Wednesday. I'm ready for a change.

File.Name: h627b_h627-024.jpg
Notes: 34737892544 Here is another tiny Robber Fly (Asilidae, Diptera) perched on a leaf of native Toyon (Heteromeles arbutifolia, Rosaceae). It's only about 1700 pixels long in a different 1:1 macro shot, which comes to about 1/4 inch. (See this photo for how I figure.) It's curious that I recently found another robber fly in the same shrub, see this photo. These flies are predators that catch their prey on the wing between their front legs, like the dragonflies they resemble. They don't visit flowers, so there's no easy way to find them. (San Marcos Pass, 27 June 2017)

Notes: 35191832310 This is a Bordered Plant Bug (family Largidae, Heteroptera, Hemiptera) - almost certainly Largus californicus - crawling through the leaves of a small Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) tree. I saw it coming, but I think I surprised it! I take a lot of head-on photos of insects, but they are rarely acceptable because it is so hard to the narrow focus right. This one came out nice. (San Marcos Pass, 27 June 2017)

File.Name: h627d_h627-039.jpg
Notes: 35539928736 This is a galled leaf of native Coffeeberry (Frangula californica or Rhamnus californica) in the Rhamnaceae plant family. I believe it's the work of a Midrib Gall Moth (Sorhagenia nimbosa) in the family Cosmopterigidae of the Lepidoptera. I've only found a few of these this year, always on shrubs that have buds or are flowering or just finished. It's a real gall, not just a leaf-roller. You can see how the cell structure of the leaf is changed by the caterpillar inside. (San Marcos Pass, 27 June 2017)

File.Name: h628a_h628-003.jpg
Notes:
35213163510 I found this interesting fly (Diptera) on a leaf of on a stem of Nuttall's Snowberry (Symphoricarpos mollis, Caprifoliaceae) this morning. Maybe it is genus Suillia in the family Heleomyzidae - but maybe not. Whatever it is, it's an attractive fly - maybe a female large with eggs. It sort of looks like the flies that breed in our mushrooms in the fall, see this photo - but if you look close at that photo, you can see that they are different. Help with an ID will be appreciated! (San Marcos Pass, 26 June 2017)

It was cooler today with a high of only about 80∞ - perfect temperature after ten days of heat wave. Unfortunately it was quite breezy, at least up in the higher mountains where I went today. Looking at the Ventusky web site, I can see that there was an eddy around Point Conception bringing winds from the south. I recommend this interesting site to see the big picture of the wind and other weather.

File.Name: h628b_h628-043.jpg
Notes: 35469467441 Here is another tiny Calochortus Bee (my name) waiting on an unopened bud of a fantastic summer flower of native Late-flowered Mariposa Lily (Calochortus fimbriatus) in the Liliaceae plant family. This is like waiting in line for tickets to go on sale! The flower will probably be open tomorrow, but this little bee isn't waiting. I believe the bees are subgenus Pygoperdita of the genus Perdita of the Andrenidae, Anthophila, Hymenoptera. Maybe they are Perdita californica? I don't find these bees anywhere else than these flowers, and the flowers don't last very long. Maybe that's why they waiting for the flower - they're in a hurry because every moment is precious. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 28 June 2017)

Yep, they're tiny. I believe the bees are subgenus Pygoperdita of the genus Perdita of the Andrenidae, Anthophila, Hymenoptera. Maybe they are Perdita californica. I don't find these bees anywhere else than these flowers, and the flowers don't last very long. Maybe that's why they always seem to be "doing it"? They're in a hurry because every moment is precious. Happy insect hump day! (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 28 June 2017)

File.Name: h629a_h629-021.jpg
Notes:
35581190286 Talk about overkill! This is a Grass-Skipper butterfly working this new flower of native Woolly Blue Curls (Trichostema lanatum) in the Lamiaceae plant family. What long-exerted stamens and pistil these flowers have! I'm pretty sure the butterfly is a Rural Skipper (Ochlodes agricola). I regularly find these little skippers on the large flowers, but they are obviously not the pollinator the flowers are designed for. One possibility is shown in this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 29 June 2017)

It was definitely cooler today, I don't think it got to 80∞. It was a perfect temperature after ten days of heat wave. The wind was polite.

File.Name: h629b_h629-029.jpg
Notes: 35490065441 I love the expression on this Ambush Bug's face - "darn ants!" This is a small Pacific Ambush Bug (Phymata pacifica, Phymatidae, Heteroptera, Hemiptera) waiting for prey by a flower of California Coffeeberry (Frangula californica aka Rhamnus californica) in the Rhamnaceae plant family. I don't know what kind of ants they are, but I know the ambush bug doesn't like them. I have another photo that actually shows an ant crawling over the bug. The Ambush Bug looks large in this photo, which is a sign of just how small those ants really are. (San Marcos Pass, 29 June 2017)

File.Name: h629c_h629-005.jpg
Notes: 34811298843 Here is another goofy little Chaparral Monkey Grasshopper (Morsea californica) in the Eumastacidae family of the Orthoptera, this time on a shrub of Southern Bush Monkey Flower (Mimulus aurantiacus var. pubescens, Phrymaceae). It's a Monkey Grasshopper on a shrub of Monkey Flower! It's probably about 3/4 inch long. I'm not sure how to distinguish nymph and adult as both are wingless, but this one is larger than the nymphs I've been showing, so it might be an adult. If you compare this photo of (probably) a nymph, you can see how variable their color is. This one let me get a couple of photos and then jumped away. (San Marcos Pass, 29 June 2017)

Notes: 34778798044 This tiny spider has captured a tiny fly for prey. The fly is probably a Syrphid Flower Fly (Syrphidae, Diptera), but I'm not sure about the spider. This photo shows heads and tails so HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 26 June 2017)

File.Name: h630a_h630-024.jpg
Notes:
35252995680 This is a tiny fly (Diptera) working a flower of California Coffeeberry (Frangula californica aka Rhamnus californica) in the Rhamnaceae plant family. The fly could curl up inside the flower! The fly does have a rather large head, so I wondered if it could be a Big-headed Fly in the family Pipunculidae - but Thijs de Graaf tells me that it's genus Paragus in the Syrphidae. Well, the two families are pretty close. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 29 June 2017)

I had an appointment in town this morning, and it was breezy by the time I got home. I did get a few photos, but nothing great. I decided to make his a real Fly Day Friday with photos from the last week!

See this photo for another shot of the same fly. It's only about 1/4 inch long. These flies are predators that catch their prey on the wing between their front legs, like the dragonflies they resemble. They don't visit flowers, so there's no easy way to find them - but sometimes I find them. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 27 June 2017)

Notes: 35639548485 This is a tough-looking Flesh Fly (Sarcophagidae, Diptera) on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods. We seem to have both large and small ones, and they all have reddish eyes and an orange or red butt. Neither the common name nor scientific name are lovely, but they are handsome flies - albeit tough and gnarly. The Fly Day Friday group has a two photo limit, so I won't add this one, but HFDF anyway! (San Marcos Pass, 27 June 2017)

File.Name: h630d_h625-045.jpg
Notes: 35471042232 This is a small Bee Fly (order Bombyliidae, Diptera) flying into a flower cluster of native Coyote Mint aka White-leaf Monardella (Monardella hypoleuca) in the Lamiaceae plant family. Look how long its proboscis is, made for sucking nectar like a long straw! This fly looks like looks like a small version of the larger genus Bombylius flies that we get in mid-winter, see this photo. Maybe it's the same genus - or at least the same tribe Bombyliini of the subfamily Bombyliinae. I've showed these flies before this year, but always on Trask's Yerba Santa - like this photo. The Fly Day Friday group has a two photo limit, so I won't add this one, but HFDF anyway! (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 25 June 2017)

File.Name: h701a_h701-031.jpg
Notes:
35530285821 Here is another fantastic diurnal (daytime) California Clearwing Moth (Hemaris thetis, Sphingidae, Lepidoptera) working new summer flowers of native Coyote Mint aka White-leaf Monardella (Monardella hypoleuca) in the Lamiaceae plant family. It looks like it's parked, but it's actually hovering in place as it works the flowers. The moths are fast, and they never stop moving! (Well, almost never - see this photo!) For some reason, I haven't seen many of these moths this year. Maybe they are just late, but if they wait much longer they will miss their flowers. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 1 July 2017)

It felt like "fog winds" this morning, and sure enough the marine-layer fog started spilling over the mountains around 10 am. I went up into the higher mountains again to find some sun. It was almost cool today, only in the 70s.

File.Name: h701b_h701-052.jpg
Notes: 35273029660 I believe this is a tiny humpbacked Bee Fly (order Bombyliidae, Diptera) on a new flower of native Southern Hawkweed (Hiericium argutum) in the Asteraceae plant family. Probably it is genus Geron. The genus name sounds like "gerontology" - imagine a humpbacked old man with a cane. The larvae are said to be parasites of other insects, especially moths. BugGuide reports what I've often seen, that the flies will often bob up and down above a flower prior landing to nectar. It's an attractive fly - if you get close enough to actually see it! (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 1 July 2017)

Notes: 35660018385 I believe this is a small atypical Tachinid Fly in the family Tachinidae of the order Diptera - probably genus Cylindromyia, and maybe species Cylindromyia intermedia. Compare this photo at BugGuide. It does have a bristly abdomen like other tachinids. This one was perched on the dead grass stems. It kept flying off and coming back to the same perch, like a little dragonfly. It's an attractive fly. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 1 July 2017)

File.Name: h701d_h701-066.jpg
Notes: 35529465611 This is another nasty biting fly in our mountains - on my leg. (I was wearing shorts of course, sitting on the folding chair I bring along on my excursions.) These flies hurt! I believe it is in the family Tabanidaeof Horse and Deer Flies, but it is not much larger than a house fly and its eyes are quite plain unlike most other Tabanids. Maybe it is genus Apatolestes in the subfamily Pangoniinae, in particular the species Apatolestes hinei? I reckon it's OK to call it a Deer Fly even though it is not in the subfamily Chrysopsinae. Usually the Rocky Mountain Bite Flies (Symphoromyia, Rhagionidae) - see this photo and this photo - are the worst biters in our mountains, but lately I've been had by more of these. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 1 July 2017)

ScientificName: Apatolestes hinei
associatedTaxa: NA

File.Name: h702a_h702-011.jpg
Notes:
34873380363 Here is a nice pair of fantastic summer flowers of native Late-flowered Mariposa Lily (Calochortus fimbriatus) in the Liliaceae plant family. The flowers are wonderfully variable, but these two look alike because they are from the same plant - and maybe they breed true. The old Jepson Flora has them as "DFCLT" for gardening, but it would be interesting to try to breed them. I want to photograph every one I find because they are so different, see my [Previous] photo. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 2 July 2017)

We were in a cloud this morning - deep under a layer of marine-layer fog/stratus. I don't mind the fog, but it also brings "fog winds" which make my kind of photos impossible, so I went back up into the higher mountains to try to get above it. I did, but I had to climb another 1,000 feet before I found the sun. It did finally clear up at home, with temps in the low 70s. I'm enjoying the cool weather, but it sounds like it will get hot again later this week - see this report at the California Weather Blog.
ScientificName: Calochortus fimbriatus
associatedTaxa: NA

File.Name: h702b_h702-009.jpg
Notes: 35295128740 Here is a gallery of fantastic summer flowers of native Late-flowered Mariposa Lily (Calochortus fimbriatus) in the Liliaceae plant family. The flowers are wonderfully variable, and I want to photograph every one I find because they are so different! These fantastic summer flowers attract lots of life - look for tiny wild bees in my photos. I got all these photos over the last two days. Fortunately, there are still lots of buds, so they'll be flowering for a while. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 2 July 2017)
ScientificName: Calochortus fimbriatus
associatedTaxa: NA

Notes: 35642705756 Like the proverbial bull in a china shop, this large Bumble Bee (Bombus, Hymenoptera) is stumbling around inside a fantastic new flower of native Late-flowered Mariposa Lily (Calochortus fimbriatus) in the Liliaceae plant family. Usually I find small bees in the flowers - compare this photo and use the flower stamens as a ruler! (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 2 July 2017)

Notes: 34840941264 This is an opening flower of native Late-flowered Mariposa Lily (722Calochortus fimbriatus) in the Liliaceae plant family. True to it's genus, it has three sepals, three petals, six stamens, and a three-lobed stigma in the center - all neatly folded into one bud. It looks like it belongs in the ocean! File.Name: h703a_h703-009.jpg
Notes:
35704288905 This is an Anise Swallowtail (Papilio zelicaon) butterfly on the well-named but non-native Butterfly Bush (Buddleia davidii, Scrophulariaceae or Buddlejaceae) in the garden today. These butterflies are distinctive because the black on their forewings runs along the wing rather than across it. Beautiful. These butterflies use not anise but non-native Fennel (Foeniculum vulgare, Apiaceae) as their main host plant, and I wonder if this one comes from one of the caterpillars in our canyon - see this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 3 July 2017)

It was sunny today, but the fog winds did kick up around 11 am when the marine-layer fog started to spill over the mountains ... but the fog backed off, the wind died down, and it was a nice day. I'm enjoying the pleasant weather, but it sounds like it will get hot again later this week - see this report at the California Weather Blog. I reckon the native plants and insects took a beating during our last heat wave.

File.Name: h703b_h703-044.jpg
Notes: 35573212611 This is a Swallowtail butterfly - but what kind? It looks like a slightly washed out Western Tiger Swallowtail (Papilio rutulus), but it also looks like a cream-colored Pale Swallowtail (Papilio eurymedon). The Pale Swallowtails look less pale as we move into summer, and they get a cream color. Compare this photo and this photo to see the difference. The Western Tiger Swallowtails are more brilliant, see this photo. I believe that this photo and this photo show them both together. So what is the butterfly in this photo? At the time, I thought it was a cream-colored Pale Swallowtail. When I got the photos up on my computer screen, it started looking more like a pale Western Tiger Swallowtail. Hah - I don't know what it is. Pretty though! (San Marcos Pass, 3 July 2017)
ScientificName: Papilio rutulus; Papilio eurymedon
associatedTaxa: Papilio rutulus; Papilio eurymedon

File.Name: h703c_h703-001.jpg
Notes: 35573019061 This is a Pale Swallowtail (Papilio eurymedon) butterfly on the well-named but non-native Butterfly Bush (Buddleia davidii, Scrophulariaceae or Buddlejaceae) in the garden today. This isn't the best photo because the wind chose that moment to kick up, but the photo goes with my [Next] two shots. And it is interesting for two reasons. First, the butterfly is remarkable tattered, life has taken a toll. Second, the butterfly is basically black and white, as they should be. The Pale Swallowtails look less pale as we move into summer. They get a cream color, and sometimes I confuse them with Western Tiger Swallowtails (P. rutulus). Now what is the butterfly in my [Next] photo? (San Marcos Pass, 3 July 2017)

File.Name: h703d_h629-003.jpg
Notes: 35565957511 This is a wilted plant of native Mugwort (Artemisia douglasiana, Asteraceae) in the sun down by the creek. These plants seem to droop even when it's not particularly hot, and they spring right back up when the direct sun leaves. I figure it's just in a relaxed state. This is my photo for the Macro Mondays group, with the theme of "Relaxation" - and I want to get it right. I checked at Thesaurus.com, and they list weaken and ebb as synonyms for both wilt and relax. I figure this is a kind of transitive synonymity: if A=B and C=B then A=C, and so I figure a wilted plant is a relaxed plant. HMM! (San Marcos Pass, 29 June 2017)

File.Name: h704a_h704-042.jpg
Notes:
35339270990 This is a flower of native Red Thistle aka Venus Thistle or Cobweb Thistle (Cirsium occidentale var. venustum) in the Asteraceae plant family in the mountains today. It's red, white, and blue for Independence Day - red petals, white cobwebby hairs on the phyllaries, and a blue background of Woolly Blue Curl plants. Happy birthday USA! It's a good day to remember our revolutionary heritage... (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 4 July 2017)

It did get hotter than expected today, into the 90s. I always dread the Fourth of July because of people and fireworks and wildfires. Today has been quiet so far, and I hope it stays that way. We're under a NWS Excessive Heat Watch and a Fire Weather Watch starting on Thursday. Thunderstorms and sundowner winds are possible. *Sigh* It feels like the new heat wave is here.

File.Name: h704b_h702-041.jpg
Notes:
34884574624 Here are red, white, and blue native flowers for Independence Day!

Red - Northern Blue-stemmed Keckiella (Keckiella ternata ssp. septentrionalis) in the Plantaginaceae plant family. These are growing near the top of La Cumbre Peak, almost 4,000 feet above Santa Barbara. It's the only place I find them.

White - Toyon (Heteromeles arbutifolia) in the Rosaceae plant family. These shrubs flower in summer and produce clusters of red berries for Christmas.

Blue - Woolly Blue Curls (Trichostema lanatum) in the Lamiaceae plant family. Blue flowers are the hardest to find at this time of year, though there are several violet flowers.

Happy birthday USA! It's a good time to remember our revolutionary heritage... (Santa Ynez Mountains, 4 July 2017)

File.Name: h704d_h704-014.jpg
Notes:
35725557725 We had a hatch of tiny caterpillars from a Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly on the well-named but non-native Butterfly Bush (Buddleia davidii, Scrophulariaceae or Buddlejaceae) in the garden this week. This isn't surprising, as I've seen several mama butterflies ovipositing on this same shrub, see photos here, here, and here. Yes, they do have the start of a web (like this), but they seem to come out in the morning. It will be fun to watch them develop. (San Marcos Pass, 4 July 2017)

These caterpillars are quite social when they first hatch. One neat social trick that they have is synchronized twitching - they all twitch at the same time, again and again. I've seen this scare away a Tachinid Fly predator. Now I wonder how they manage to synchronize the timing??

File.Name: h705a_h705-035.jpg
Notes:
35707172186 Hah - I got photobombed by this wasp! I was focused on the remarkable nymph of a Bush Katydid (Scudderia, Tettigoniidae, Ensifera, Orthoptera) on this flower of the violet form of California Thistle (Cirsium occidentale var. californicum) in the Asteraceae plant family. Then the handsome Sand Wasp (genus Steniolia, Bembicini, Apoidea, Hymenoptera) decided to join the photo! I took a few quick photos before the wasp flew off. After that, I stood back and waited around for a good ten minutes for the wasp to come back, but no show. Some events only happen once! (San Marcos Pass, 5 July 2017)

It was actually pretty breezy this morning - from the south ocean-side of the mountains, part of an eddy that forms around Point Conception. That soon ended and heat took over our weather story. We're under a NWS Heat Advisory, a Red Flag Warning, and an Excessive Heat Watch starting on Thursday. The forecast says something about 105∞ on Friday. Sundowner winds are possible - with 45 mph gusts tomorrow night?? I hope that's a misprint.

The flowers are quite pretty if you get down on your hands and knees to see them. The plant seems kind of weedy, growing along the edge of the road in our canyon, but it will flower for much of the summer which makes it special. (San Marcos Pass, 5 July 2017)

Notes: 34905630354 This is a tiny Orb Weaver (Araneidae) spider on its web. Look close at the fantastic light patterns on the web! I reckon this has something to do with refraction/diffraction, but I don't even care to understand it - it's magical. They remind me of the cellular automata I used to play with when I was learning computer programming. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 30 June 2017)

File.Name: h706a_h706-029.jpg
Notes:
35726924306 It was really too hot for insects or insect photographers today! This Grasshopper (Acrididae, Orthoptera) is trying to beat the heat by lounging in a new summer flower of native Late-flowered Mariposa Lily (Calochortus fimbriatus) in the Liliaceae plant family. I don't know if it's any cooler in there, but it sure is pretty place to crash! The grasshopper is a short-winged Oedaleonotus phryneicus (Melanoplinae) - an adult, not a nymph. The little bump on the bottom of the flower petal is a nectar gland. It was worth going out in the heat today to get this photo! (San Marcos Pass, 6 July 2017)

It was *HOT* today - I saw 101∞ on the upstairs porch thermometer, and tomorrow might be even hotter. Muggy too. Fortunately the sundowner winds haven't kicked in yet, and I hope they don't happen. We're under a NWS Red Flag Warning, a Heat Advisory tonight, and an Excessive Heat Watch tomorrow.

But if you look close at the proboscis of he bee, you can see that it is doing "nectar theft" - robbing the flower by entering through the base rather than through the throat so that pollination might happen. Many insects seem to know this trick. I don't think the bees always do this, so it's interesting that they are doing it now that the flowers are nearly done for the season. File.Name: h707a_h707-031.jpg
Notes:
35655174121 This is the tiny nymph of a true bug (Heteroptera, Hemiptera) on a glandular seed head of Caterpillar Phacelia (Phacelia cicutaria var. hispida) in the Boraginaceae plant family. See my [Previous] photo for another shot of the seed head. The nymph has "training wings" and a nice proboscis. When I first saw the nymph, I assumed it was a Stilt Bug in the family Berytidae, but the speckled legs remind me more of an Assassin Bug (Reduviidae). It turns out that BugGuide has a whole section of "Stilt Bugs (Berytidae) | unidentified nymphs" that match my critter well, speckled legs and all, so I reckon that's it. The slender nymph seems a good match for the narrow calyx lobes of the seed heads. (San Marcos Pass, 7 July 2017)

It was *HOT* again today - I saw 104∞ on the upstairs porch thermometer, though a neighbor's weather station at Weather Underground had us down for 109∞! Yipes, it was 112∞at the river today. Muggy too. We're under a NWS Red Flag Warning, a Wind Advisory, and an Excessive Heat Warning tonight. Tomorrow will be hot too. Fortunately we haven't seen any sundowner winds, and I hope they don't happen. Sunday might be 10 degrees cooler - but that's still hot!

Notes: 35654781231 This is a large Black Soldier Fly (Hermetia illucens or kin) in the family Stratiomyidae of the insect order Diptera on a leaf in garden. It's maybe 3/4 inch long. You can see the translucent "windows" under its wings near the top of its abdomen. These give the fly its other common name of Window Fly. Hah - this one looks like it's doing fly yoga, but it's just grooming itself. These flies have wonderful patterned eyes and antennae like an old-fashioned TV set. These are our "compost flies" - see this photo of a maggot. They might actually do some good in the compost bin, see the article at Wikipedia. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 4 July 2017)

Notes: 34976498653 Here is a Blow Fly (Calliphoridae, Diptera) sharing a leaf with newly hatched caterpillars from a Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly on non-native Butterfly Bush (Buddleia davidii, Scrophulariaceae or Buddlejaceae) in the garden. The are the social caterpillars that spin webs, though these are venturing out on their own. I'm not sure if the fly is is just resting or contemplating something nasty. The fly makes a good ruler for the caterpillars. I have a closer photo of the caterpillars here. It's also a nice shot of the fly, so HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 4 July 2017)

File.Name: h708a_h708-022.jpg
Notes:
35674679341 Fire on the mountain!

This is the smoke plume coming from the Whittier Fire this afternoon as seen from a place on East Camino Cielo road. This is looking westwards along the axis of the Santa Ynez Mountains towards Lake Cachuma, with Fremont Ridge going across the photo. The fire is still about 10 miles away, so I'm not too worried. On the other hand, the fire is burning out of control towards the south and east - towards us. It's hard to get information, the highway is closed, homes on the other side of Highway 154 may be subject to immediate mandatory evacuation ... or is that just a warning? Like I said, it's hard to get good information. The fire started near Tequepis Canyon where I started working as a naturalist many years ago at the Outdoor School - at the same Camp Whittier for which this fire is named. Smoke is in the air. We're having a flurry of ash on the porch. (San Marcos Pass, 8 July 2017)

(It's interesting that this photo shows a dead Coulter Pine (Pinus coulteri) on the left - dead from bark beetles and drought. Yeah, the dead trees are a fire hazard!)

It's not a good day for a wildfire as it's been *HOT* again today - I saw 104∞ on the upstairs porch thermometer. That's 40∞C. Muggy too. Fortunately the sundowner winds have failed to appear again, and it's a pretty calm evening right now. Tomorrow is forecast to be 14∞ cooler with calm winds.
Locality: Pinus coulteri) on the left - dead from bark beetles and drought. Yeah
ScientificName: Pinus coulteri
associatedTaxa: Pinus coulteri

File.Name: h709a_h709-015.jpg
Notes:
35018121933 Fire on the mountain!

This is what the Whittier Fire looked like this afternoon as seen from a place on East Camino Cielo road. This is looking westwards along the Santa Ynez Mountains towards Lake Cachuma, with Fremont Ridge going across the photo. Compare my photo from yesterday. The fire has crossed the mountains and is now burning towards Goleta on the coastal side, but the weather has been helping today. We actually had a splash of monsoon rain this morning! (Darn, it was enough to destroy any "ash leaves" that might have landed - see this photo.) And it's been nearly 20 degrees cooler than yesterday. Most importantly, the wind has been from the south-east which meets the fire head-on forcing it back on itself. The highway is still closed. West Camino Cielo and Kinevan have been evacuated, but our side of the mountains on East Camino Cielo is only under an evacuation warning. Of course that could change if the wind changes. In the meantime it's remarkably quiet except for the occasional rumble of an air tanker. I guess if you have to have a wildfire, then this was a pretty good day for it. (San Marcos Pass, 9 July 2017)

It was cooler today - in the mid-80s rather than yesterday's 104∞ - nearly 20∞ cooler! And that interesting breeze is from the south-east, while our usual wind from he northwest would have blown the wildfire right at us! I believe this was the work of a "Catalina eddy" - see the Ventusky web site to appreciate what this is. I recommend this interesting site to see the big picture of the wind and other weather. I've been noticing the south winds more this year because of it.

I mostly find the nymphs in the flower heads of native White Sage (Salvia apiana, Lamiaceae about 20 feet away. It's interesting that the bugs lose their ant-like qualities as they make the transition to adulthood. (San Marcos Pass, 7 July 2017)

File.Name: h709d_h706-012.jpg
Notes:
35827070335 Here is another recent clutch of social caterpillars of a Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly on a plant of native Bird's Beak (Cordylanthus rigidus) in the Orobanchaceae (formerly Scrophulariaceae) plant family. The caterpillars are web-spinners. I usually find them on Climbing Penstemon (Keckiella cordifolia, Plantaginaceae), but they use other plants - including non-native Butterfly Bush (Buddleia davidii, Scrophulariaceae or Buddlejaceae) in the garden, see this photo. It's interesting that all these plants are "scrophs" in the plant family Scrophulariaceae. That plant family was split up, but these caterpillars still use them all. See my comment on this photo for more discussion. (San Marcos Pass, 6 July 2017)

I just noticed that one of the caterpillars in he photo has the white egg of a parasitic Tachinid Fly. I'll make a note. These caterpillars have a few adaptations against this - their webbing gives some protection, they are covered with spikes, and they do a sort of synchronized twitching - they all twitch at the same time, again and again. I've seen this scare away a Tachinid Fly predator.

File.Name: h710b_h710-005.jpg
Notes:
35678477142 I found this fragment of an ash leaf on the woven cover of my car seat this morning. We had a good ash fall on Saturday night from the Whittier Fire, but then we had a miraculous bit of monsoon rain on Sunday morning - which knocked all the ash apart before I could get a photo. I found this ash leaf on the seat next to the open window of my truck, which I hadn't driven for a few days. Here it was protected from the rain. *Sigh* I tried to move the leaf over a bit for better framing, and it fell apart at the slighest touch. Always get a photo FIRST before you try unknown adjustments! The ash leaf must have been carried here from the Whittier Fire, several miles away - see my [Next] photo. File.Name: h710c_h706-010.jpg
Notes: 35806709136 This is looking at the underside of a flower of native Late-flowered Mariposa Lily (Calochortus fimbriatus) in the Liliaceae plant family. The bumps on the base (top) of the three petals are nectar glands where the insects usually congregate. BTW this is how I found it, I didn't break the flower stem. This was my alternative shot for the Macro Mondays group, with the theme of "Bottoms Up" this week. See my [Previous] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 6 July 2017)
ScientificName: Calochortus fimbriatus
associatedTaxa: NA

File.Name: h710d_h705-022.jpg
Notes: 35000944664 This is looking at the underside of a Woolly Bear caterpillar - the larva of a Tiger Moth (family Arctiini) in the order Lepidoptera, the first one I've seen this year. I found this one crossing a patch of bare ground, and I turned it over to show its bottom - head on the left and stumpy prolegs on the right. This is my photo for the Macro Mondays group, with the theme of "Bottoms Up" - HMM! (San Marcos Pass, 5 July 2017)
ScientificName: Arctiini; Lepidoptera
associatedTaxa: Arctiini; Lepidoptera

File.Name: h711a_h711-033.jpg
Notes:
35699940512 Fire on the mountain!

I saw the Whittier Fire this morning, but it looked pretty quiet with a breeze from the south. The breeze turned around in the afternoon, and there was a major flare-up on the east side - the side near us. You can see a group of concerned neighbors in the lower-left. Compare this photo of the same scene from yesterday. It may be that the fire looks closer than it actually is in this photo - there might be one more canyon hidden in the smoke. The danger is that if it burns across the canyon of Bear Creek, then it's ready to burn down the ridgeline you can see in my photo. Then it burns Cold Springs Tavern and moves on to us. The first sign that it's getting close is that the power goes off, so if I don't post tomorrow, that's why. I really don't want to evacuate, but we'll see what happens. (San Marcos Pass, 11 July 2017)

It was cooler today - only about 80∞ rather than 104∞ last weekend. The breeze started from the south-east today, but then it changed to a north-west breeze. It's not a big wind, but it's big enough to make trouble for us.
ScientificName: NA
associatedTaxa: NA

File.Name: h711b_h711-011.jpg
Notes:
35737119631 I was up on what I call "FAA Peak" today - the high peak between Painted Cave and Arroyo Burro saddle that hosts a FAA radio transmitter. I heard a loud noise and saw this large C-130 converted cargo plane putting heading towards the Whittier Fire. It's a four-engine prop plane - but huge, with a big load of fire-retardant. It has military colors rather than the usual red and white CDF colors, so I believe it's a military plane. It's probably flying in over the backcountry from Naval Air Station Point Mugu. There's info about this plane in page 32 of the CalFire Firefighting Aircraft Recognition Guide. Hah - this is a field guide for firefighting aircraft! (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 11 July 2017)

The wildfire was pretty calm when I was up on the mountain, but that changed this afternoon. See my [Next] photo. We've had lots of helicopters, but these large airtankers have been unfortunately scarce at this fire. I've heard rumors/speculation that there are too many wildfires right now.
ScientificName: NA
associatedTaxa: NA

File.Name: h711c_h711-006.jpg
Notes: 35827812556 I believe this is a Stiletto Fly in the family Therevidae, Diptera, maybe genus Thereva. I tend to confuse these flies with Snipe Flies (Rhagio, Rhagionidae), but they are hairier, have more patterned wings, and different antennae. This one is perched for a moment on non-native Butterfly Bush (Buddleia davidii, Scrophulariaceae or Buddlejaceae) in the garden. They are attractive flies - I wish I knew more about them. (San Marcos Pass, 11 July 2017)
CommonNames: Stiletto Fly; Snipe Flies; Butterfly Bush
ScientificName: Therevidae; Diptera; Thereva; Rhagio; Rhagionidae; Buddleia davidii; Scrophulariaceae; Buddlejaceae
associatedTaxa: Therevidae; Diptera; Thereva; Rhagio; Rhagionidae; Buddleia davidii; Scrophulariaceae; Buddlejaceae

File.Name: h711d_h711-024.jpg
Notes: 35699094172 I believe this is a Blister Beetle in the family Meloidae of the insect order Coleoptera perched on one of the last flowers of native Coyote Mint aka White-leaf Monardella (Monardella hypoleuca) in the Lamiaceae plant family. Maybe the beetle is genus Epicauta? It has short wing-covers like a Rove Beetle (Staphylinidae). I believe the heat lately has helped bring these flowers to an early end. Fortunately we have a colony of plants that is still waiting to flower in the canyon at home. (San Marcos Pass, 11 July 2017)
CommonNames: Blister Beetle; Coyote Mint; White-leaf Monardella; Rove Beetle
ScientificName: Meloidae; Coleoptera; Monardella hypoleuca; Lamiaceae; Epicauta; Staphylinidae
associatedTaxa: Meloidae; Coleoptera; Monardella hypoleuca; Lamiaceae; Epicauta; Staphylinidae

File.Name: h712a_h712-006.jpg
Notes:
35501037650 I finally got a photo showing the upper wings of a Mylitta Crescent (Phyciodes mylitta) butterfly perched in the mint patch of the garden this morning. (See this photo of the wings below.) These butterflies seem to spend their days perched on a tall plant and flying off to chase any other insect - or photographer - that comes by. They are watchful and hard to get close to. The gold sparkles at the base of the wings are nice! (San Marcos Pass, 12 July 2017)

I don't have a photo for day five of the Whittier Fire, and that's a good sign! The fire is still burning, but it's not the scary flare-up we had last night - see this photo. Much of the intensity of the fire depends on the winds which were pretty benign today. The fire is still burning, and I'm sure it will flare-up again - but maybe not tonight. I can't see anything from our canyon, but maybe I heard more fire bomber activity today? Temperatures have been mild, only about 80∞ today.
ScientificName: Phyciodes mylitta
associatedTaxa: Phyciodes mylitta

File.Name: h712b_h712-015.jpg
Notes: 35757510251 This is a Propertius Duskywing (Erynnis propertius) butterfly working new flowers of non-native Cat Mint (Nepeta cataria, Lamiaceae) on the wild edge of the garden. The Mournful and Funereal Duskywings are similar - with addition of white trim on their hindwings, see this photo. This one seems to have particularly large hyaline (translucent) patches on its forewing. (San Marcos Pass, 12 July 2017)
ScientificName: Erynnis propertius; Nepeta cataria
associatedTaxa: Erynnis propertius; Nepeta cataria

File.Name: h712c_h712-036.jpg
Notes: 35757286161 This is a large Green Lynx Spider (Peucetia, Oxyopidae) guarding her egg case, the first one I've seen this year. Maybe this is the Lesser Green Lynx (Peucetia longipalpis) because its abdomen has white hairs and no chevron patterns? (See BugGuide.) I have to admit that I rotated this image 90 degrees clockwise. I don't think that tall skinny images work well with wide-screen monitors. (San Marcos Pass, 12 July 2017)
CommonNames: Green Lynx Spider; Lesser Green Lynx
ScientificName: Peucetia; Oxyopidae; Peucetia longipalpis
associatedTaxa: Peucetia; Oxyopidae; Peucetia longipalpis

File.Name: h712d_h712-030.jpg
Notes: 35719074792 Here is a spider web - decorated with the ashes of burned trees and chaparral from the Whittier Fire about three miles away, see my [Previous] photo. We had a "snow flurry" of ash yesterday evening when the wind was right. Here are some of them, caught in the safety net of a web. I believe this web is the work of a Bowl and Doily Spider (Frontinella communis, Linyphiidae) - or something similar. See this photo for more info about the structure of these webs. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 12 July 2017)
ScientificName: Frontinella communis; Linyphiidae
associatedTaxa: Frontinella communis; Linyphiidae

File.Name: h713a_h713-041.jpg
Notes:
35908485615 This is a lovely White Checkered-Skipper (Pyrgus albescens or P. communis) butterfly working new summer flowers of native California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum, Polygonaceae). It's a striking butterfly with those brown scales on its shoulders. I don't see these very often - at least I don't often get the photo to prove it. (San Marcos Pass, 13 July 2017)

I don't have a photo for day six of the Whittier Fire either, and that's still a good sign! The fire is still burning, but all I could see today is hazy smoke hiding all the action. It was peculiar that this morning there seemed to be smoke in the Santa Ynez Valley to the north AND over the ocean to the south, as though the breeze was blowing in both directions at once. The winds were pretty benign today, and temperatures have been mild. That might change this weekend with more heat and sundowner winds in the forecast. The fire is still burning, and I'm sure it will flare-up again. Meanwhile, we're still at home.
ScientificName: Pyrgus albescens; P. communis; Eriogonum fasciculatum; Polygonaceae
associatedTaxa: Pyrgus albescens; P. communis; Eriogonum fasciculatum; Polygonaceae

File.Name: h713b_h711-009.jpg
Notes: 35099264483 Here is a little beetle (Coleoptera) on an almost-open flower of native Ms. Plummer's Baccharis aka Smooth Baccharis (Baccharis plummerae) in the Asteraceae plant family. I don't know what kind of beetle it is, but I have to guess it's a Rove Beetle (Staphylinidae) because of the way its abdomen sticks out beyond the short wing covers. These little beetles seem very common right now in the mountains. I should know what they are. Please leave a comment if you know what this is! (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 11 July 2017)
CommonNames: Ms. Plummer's Baccharis; Smooth Baccharis; Rove Beetle
ScientificName: Coleoptera; Baccharis plummerae; Asteraceae; Staphylinidae
associatedTaxa: Coleoptera; Baccharis plummerae; Asteraceae; Staphylinidae

File.Name: h713c_h708-003.jpg
Notes: 35776543201 This is a "butt shot" of a handsome Sand Wasp (genus Steniolia, Bembicini, Apoidea, Hymenoptera) working new flowers of non-native Cat Mint (Nepeta cataria, Lamiaceae) on the edge of the garden. See this photo for another shot the the same wasp. Is that a stinger I see at the tip of the abdomen?? It doesn't look much like an ovipositor - and it does look sharp! (San Marcos Pass, 8 July 2017)
ScientificName: Steniolia; Bembicini; Apoidea; Hymenoptera; Nepeta cataria; stinger

File.Name: h713d_h707-011.jpg
Notes: 35068184984 This is a little Sheet Web Weaver spider in the family Linyphiidae hanging from its dome-like web in a small oak tree in the woods. Maybe the spider is a Filmy Dome Spider (Neriene radiata) - but maybe not. The web looks magical, but it's also a pretty good butt shot - so HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 7 July 2017)
CommonNames: Sheet Web Weaver; Filmy Dome Spider
ScientificName: Linyphiidae; Neriene radiata

File.Name: h714a_h714-034.jpg
Notes:
35088565634 Fire on the mountain!

This is what the Whittier Fire looked like this afternoon as seen from a place on East Camino Cielo road. This is looking westwards along the Santa Ynez Mountains with Broadcast Peak in the distance on the right. The wind is the big story today. Today started pretty calm with a breeze from the southeast, but the wind is now coming from the northwest - from the right in my photo. That's the top of Fremont Ridge in the foreground. The next ridge beyond goes up to Brush Peak on the left, with a new fire break. The fire seems to be burning in the canyon of Bear Creek that's hidden beyond that. Now the fire is burning towards the city of Goleta to the left, and areas on the coast are being evacuated. Everyone on the west side of Highway 154 has officially been evacuated, though I'm sure some people remain. We're still good on the east side of 154, though that could change at any moment. This wildfire is very much alive. (San Marcos Pass, 14 July 2017)

It was warmer today, in the low 90s. The day started with calm winds from the southeast, but the winds are now from the north/northwest . It is expected to be hotter and windier this weekend. Is it an omen that the Weather Underground weather station for the "Winchestr Cyn Gun Club" is not responding??
ScientificName: NA
associatedTaxa: NA

File.Name: h714b_h714-023.jpg
Notes:
35539696400 Here is another fantastic diurnal (daytime) California Clearwing Moth (Hemaris thetis, Sphingidae, Lepidoptera) working new summer flowers of native Coyote Mint aka White-leaf Monardella (Monardella hypoleuca) in the Lamiaceae plant family growing in the canyon. These moths are fast, and they never stop moving, hovering in place as they work the flowers. These flowers have finished in the higher mountains where I found another one of these moths a couple of weeks ago, see this photo. They are really just starting to flower here. These moths are not particularly common, but if you park yourself by a flower for a while, one might show up. I brought a chair along this time. (San Marcos Pass, 14 July 2017)

(Despite the Whittier Fire that's burning our mountains - see my [Next] photo - I've managed to get some nice nature photos at the same time. It's a way to relax.)
CommonNames: California Clearwing Moth; Coyote Mint; White-leaf Monardella
ScientificName: Hemaris thetis; Sphingidae; Lepidoptera; Monardella hypoleuca; Lamiaceae
associatedTaxa: Hemaris thetis; Sphingidae; Lepidoptera; Monardella hypoleuca; Lamiaceae

File.Name: h714c_h714-011.jpg
Notes: 35539523610 This is a bee-sized wild bee (Anthophila, Hymenoptera) working a new flower of non-native Basil (Ocimum basilicum, Lamiaceae) in the garden. I guess that the bee is a Mining Bee in the genus Anthophora. These bees seem to have a circuit going from flower to flower around the garden again and again. They don't like being photographed - but I got lucky with this one. They have amazing eyes. (San Marcos Pass, 14 July 2017)
ScientificName: Anthophila; Hymenoptera; Ocimum basilicum; Lamiaceae; Anthophora
associatedTaxa: Anthophila; Hymenoptera; Ocimum basilicum; Lamiaceae; Anthophora

File.Name: h714d_h713-006.jpg
Notes: 35088109444 This is a small atypical Tachinid Fly in the family Tachinidae of the order Diptera - probably genus Cylindromyia, and maybe species Cylindromyia intermedia. Compare this photo at BugGuide. It does have a bristly abdomen like many other tachinids. I have another recent photo here. Both were perched on dead plant stems along a road. They keep flying off and coming back to the same perch, like a little dragonfly. It's an attractive fly. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 12 July 2017)
ScientificName: Tachinidae; Diptera; Cylindromyia; Cylindromyia intermedia
associatedTaxa: Tachinidae; Diptera; Cylindromyia; Cylindromyia intermedia

File.Name: h715a_h715-010.jpg
Notes:
35948382585 Always investigate when you find butterflies and other insects in odd positions on flowers! I found this little Acmon Blue (Plebejus acmon) butterfly head down in a summer flower cluster of native California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum, Polygonaceae). It didn't move as I got closer. Then I went around to the other side and saw why - see my [Next] photo, here! (San Marcos Pass, 15 July 2017)

I don't have a photo for day eight of the Whittier Fire, and that's a good sign! The fire is still burning, but today has been calmer than last night - see this photo. It's been a good sign (for us) that the winds have mostly been from the southeast today. It might also be a good omen that the Weather Underground weather station for the "Winchestr Cyn Gun Club" is reporting again - today had a high of 95∞ with 30% humidity, a bit muggy. That station is near the southeast corner of the fire, a place we're all keeping an eye on. Acreage has gone up while containment has dropped, the fire now stands at nearly 18,000 acres burned and 35% contained. It could still be on us in hours with an ill wind!
